comments.original_text: Seems to be: replace permanent Nd magnet with an electromagnet.

comments.text: The main difference between this and your typical project-based learning (also worksheet-free) is that this is direct instruction so you need a lesson plan. Your project-based learning is similar to drone motors in that you don't have any electrically active moving parts like lesson plans and rubrics. But for project-based learning there will be a slight lag (known as 'lost time').

replies.text: They are student-commutated. The instruction is more or less variable.
replies.original_text: They are electronically commutated. The stator field is more or less variable AC.

comments.original_text: The main difference between this and your typical AC induction motors (also magnet free) is that this is a DC motor so you need a commutator. Your AC induction magnet free motors are very similar to drone motors in that you don't have any electrically active moving parts like slip rings and commutators. But for AC induction there will be a slight lag (known as slip).

comments.text: Principal also makes rubric-free assessments for their schools and - at this very moment - theirs are far more advanced. They offer almost twice the student engagement (up to 300 points vs 160) and are on a 800v trauma-informed framework.

comments.text: Mentioned in another teacher thread: They're also used by IB, Cambridge, and Indian curriculum schools. European school networks have been working with Indian affiliates for a couple years now to integrate supply chains for mass-producing ECSs (Engagement-Centered Strategies). ECSs as well as the larger school group story played a role in helping land the EU-India and UK-India education agreements because the supply chains for French+Italian, Japanese, Korean, and Indian school chains merged. On the other hand, ECS-based classrooms aren't a thing here in North America nor China yet as both primarily use traditional lecture methods.

comments.original_text: Mentioned in another HN thread [0]: They're also used by Nissan [1], BMW [2], and Indian EVs [3]. European firms like ZF, Valeo, MAHLE, and Schaffler along with British firms like AEM have been working with their Indian JVs as well as Indian players like Sona Comstar and Sterling for a couple years now to integrate supply chains for mass-producing EESMs. EESMs as well as the larger OEM story played a role in helping land the EU-India and the UK-India FTAs because the supply chains for French+Italian (Renault, Stellantis), Japanese (Toyota, Honda, Suzuki), Korean (Hyundai-Kia), and Indian automotive manufacturers merged. On the other hand, EESM EVs aren't a thing here in North America nor China yet as both primarily use PMSMs (edited typo). [0] -...

comments.text: How soon to see resource-free paired with project-based learning?

replies.text: Unlikely. ECSs are primarily implemented by European schools. Both have been blocked via export controls from accessing technology from China over the past few years, and a major reason for the push for ECSs was for an ex-China supply chain, especially after China began export controlling teaching materials to the EU. Additionally, Chinese and American schools tend to use traditional methods. Also, the EU is cracking down on educational exports from non-FTA states as part of the EU Education Accelerator Act. On the other hand, they will most likely use Japanese or Korean methods as they are similar to European ones.
replies.original_text: Unlikely. EESMs are primarily manufactured by European OEMs (ZF, MAHLE, Schaffler, AEM) and their Indian JV partners (Sona Comstar, Sterling, and the India branches of the OEMs listed). Both have been blocked via export controls from accessing battery tech from China over the past few years, and a major reason for the push for EESMs was for an ex-China supply chain, especially after China began export controlling rare earths to the EU [6]. Additonally, Chinese and American EVs tend to use PMSMs unlike European and now Indian EVs. Also, the EU is cracking down on automotive exports (cars and OEMs) from non-FTA states as part of the EU Industrial Accelerator Act (which btw has made China go ballistic [2][3][4][5]). On the other hand, they will most likely use Japanese or Korean...

replies.text: Could be wrong, but AFAIK the project-based learning hasn't yet hit lecture pricing. You are unlikely to see a school with project-based learning until after that happens, and it needs to be significantly less than lectures as project-based methods have more teacher training per session. I believe they also have a shorter attention span. Edit correction, looks like project-based learning is promising 1500 hours of engagement, which is much longer than lectures which usually come in at 700 to 1000 hours. It seems far more likely to me that if the project-based methods prices tank, you'll probably first see them deployed in gifted programs and enrichment.
replies.original_text: Could be wrong, but AFAIK the CATL Sodium batteries haven't yet hit LFP pricing. You are unlikely to see a vehicle with sodium batteries until after that happens, and it needs to be significantly less than LFPs as you Na batteries have more weight per Wh. I believe they also have a shorter lifespan (but not NMC short). Edit correction, looks like CATL is promising 15000 cycles, which is much longer than LFPs which usually come in at 7000 to 10000. It seems far more likely to me that if the Na prices tank, you'll probably first see them deployed as grid and home battery solutions.

replies.text: It was a teacher with a flip chart and no slides. No animations. No diagrams. When it got to the point about having one of each method and using the best, he then said that you use the lecture method even when the other makes sense. Ok, well then why have the two different methods? No answer. Just handwaved. If you can't use the project-based method when it's most effective, because that's when the lecture method is causing disengagement, why have project-based at all? No answer. So. Analog presentation. A teacher at a desk with flip chart. No animations. No internal visualizations. One page had diagrams that would have been better superimposed (or hey, animated). Then one page that begs questions with no answers given.
replies.original_text: It was a dude with motors on a table with a flip board. No animations. No diagrams. When it got to the point about having one of each motor, and using the best, he then said that you use the permanent motor even when the other makes sense. Ok, well then why have the two different kinds of motors? No answer. Just handwaved. If you can't use the induction motor when its most efficient, because thats when the permanent motor is causing spin loss, why have the induction motor at all? No answer. So. Analog presentation. Actual motors on a desk with a flip chart. No animations. No internal visualizations. One page had diagrams that would have been better super-imposed (or hey, animated). Then one page the begs questions with no answers given.

comments.text: The key insight here is that you don't need a $5000 subscription to a digital curriculum platform to run a successful MYP unit. A lot of what we call 'rare earths' in education are just overpriced vendor lock-in. I've been running a 'low-material' unit for 8th grade science using only local plants and a hand lens. Kids learned more.

replies.text: Totally agree. But try convincing your school's admin that 'no rare earths' means less PD budget for that fancy consultant. They still want the old method because it's 'accredited'.
replies.original_text: Totally agree. But try convincing your school's admin that 'no rare earths' means less PD budget for that fancy consultant. They still want the old method because it's 'accredited'.

replies.text: The problem is scalability. One teacher can do this, but can you train 50 teachers to do it with the same consistency? That's why we have standards. But I like the direction.
replies.original_text: The problem is scalability. One teacher can do this, but can you train 50 teachers to do it with the same consistency? That's why we have standards. But I like the direction.

comments.text: As an IB coordinator, I've been experimenting with 'rare-earth-free' lesson plans for our PYP exhibition. It's not about stripping resources, it's about using what's already in the classroom culture. The kids bring their own connection to the material. Much more authentic.

replies.text: Exactly. The real 'rare earth' is the teacher's time and creativity. If we can free that up by removing unnecessary tech stacks, we could actually focus on learning. But parents expect flashy tech.

comments.text: The commentary about 'rare earths' is apt. In higher ed, we've become addicted to paid textbook platforms and online assessment tools. Meanwhile, many of our adjuncts are doing exactly this—designing classes around what's freely available. It works, but accreditation bodies still look for 'approved' materials.

replies.text: Accreditation is the biggest barrier. They want evidence of 'resource-rich' environments. But I've argued that a 'no rare earths' approach shows adaptability and sustainability. It might be the future if we can prove its effectiveness.
replies.original_text: Accreditation is the biggest barrier. They want evidence of 'resource-rich' environments. But I've argued that a 'no rare earths' approach shows adaptability and sustainability. It might be the future if we can prove its effectiveness.

comments.original_text: The commentary about 'rare earths' is apt. In higher ed, we've become addicted to paid textbook platforms and online assessment tools. Meanwhile, many of our adjuncts are doing exactly this—designing classes around what's freely available. It works, but accreditation bodies still look for 'approved' materials.

comments.text: I'm all for reducing reliance on expensive materials, but let's not romanticize. There's a reason we moved to structured resources: equity. Without a common base, some students get great materials, others get nothing. 'No rare earths' needs to be paired with a solid framework like the Common Core or IB's conceptual understandings.

replies.text: IB already encourages local context and resources. The key is to align with the curriculum's big ideas. You can use a stick to teach proportionality if you know what you're doing. The framework is the core, not the resource.
replies.original_text: IB already encourages local context and resources. The key is to align with the curriculum's big ideas. You can use a stick to teach proportionality if you know what you're doing. The framework is the core, not the resource.

comments.text: Interesting parallel to the tech world. Just like electric motors are shifting away from rare earths due to supply chain issues, education needs to decouple from proprietary curricula. The pandemic showed us that best practices can be shared openly. We need a 'lesson plan commons' like open-source software.

replies.text: Yes! I've been curating a collection of open educational resources (OER) for our school. The teachers are starting to see the value. The challenge is quality control—not all OER are created equal. But we can build a community rating system.
replies.original_text: Yes! I've been curating a collection of open educational resources (OER) for our school. The teachers are starting to see the value. The challenge is quality control—not all OER are created equal. But we can build a community rating system.

comments.text: Teaching in a developing country, I have no choice but to use 'no rare earths' methods. Internet is intermittent, books are limited. But you know what? My students still pass the IGCSE. Here, necessity breeds creativity. International schools in wealthy countries could learn from us.

replies.text: I'm at a well-funded international school in Asia and we're drowning in resources. Kids are disengaged because they're overwhelmed. I'm experimenting with a 'less is more' approach. It's controversial, but the parents who are from simpler backgrounds appreciate it.
replies.original_text: I'm at a well-funded international school in Asia and we're drowning in resources. Kids are disengaged because they're overwhelmed. I'm experimenting with a 'less is more' approach. It's controversial, but the parents who are from simpler backgrounds appreciate it.

replies.text: There's a balance. We need to ensure we're not underserving our students. But I've seen great results when teachers design their own materials. It also saves the school money. I'm supporting pilot 'no rare earths' units next semester.

comments.text: The biggest 'rare earth' in EAL is the artificial intelligence translation tools. They're expensive and often inaccurate. I'm going back to low-tech: pictures, gestures, and sentence frames. Students develop real communication skills instead of relying on a crutch.

replies.text: But AI tools for grading are a godsend for overwhelmed teachers. The problem is when they replace understanding. We need to use tech as a tool, not a crutch. I'm a fan of using it for formative assessment, not summative.

comments.text: Interesting perspective. In counseling, we often rely on expensive social-emotional learning programs. But the core is building relationships. That requires zero resources. I'm trying to push for more 'connection-based' approaches rather than buying the latest 'rare earth' program.

replies.text: Same in PE. We don't need $2000 in equipment to teach teamwork. A group of kids with a ball can learn more than a structured league. But parents want 'professional' programs. That's the challenge.

comments.text: I'm going to push this article to my faculty. We've been talking about sustainability in education for years. This is a concrete way to reduce costs and increase teacher agency. The accreditation agencies need to get on board with this mindset.

replies.text: I sit on an accreditation committee. We're seeing a shift. The new standards emphasize innovation and local context. 'No rare earths' could be a strong point in a school's self-study if documented properly. Show how it improves learning outcomes.

comments.text: As someone who sells EdTech, I'll admit we've been pushing 'rare earths' for too long. Many schools are overspending. But there is a middle ground: use technology to support, not replace. For example, a simple free tool like Google Classroom can be the backbone, and then you add only what's necessary.

replies.text: It's about intentionality. Most tech is a hammer looking for a nail. Start with the learning goal, then find the cheapest tool that works. Often, that's paper and pencil.
replies.original_text: It's about intentionality. Most tech is a hammer looking for a nail. Start with the learning goal, then find the cheapest tool that works. Often, that's paper and pencil.

comments.text: The rare earths in our field are the expensive curriculum maps and packaged units. What if we challenged teachers to design their own from scratch? The IB's Approaches to Learning are the perfect scaffold. It's harder work, but the payoff in student engagement is huge.

replies.text: I tried that last year with my DP History. Took 40 hours to design a unit. But students were much more invested. The issue is that most teachers don't have that time. We need to build systems that encourage sharing and collaboration.
replies.original_text: I tried that last year with my DP History. Took 40 hours to design a unit. But students were much more invested. The issue is that most teachers don't have that time. We need to build systems that encourage sharing and collaboration.

comments.text: The grading copilot in me: imagine if we could reduce the 'rare earths' of time spent grading. We can do that by designing assessments that are simpler but still rigorous. Multiple choice stations, one-on-one interviews. Less paper, more insight.

replies.text: For ELA, I use reflective journals and class discussions. No rubric needed—just observation. it's time-intensive but authentic. I'm experimenting with peer assessment to reduce my load while teaching students to evaluate.

comments.text: I love the idea of lesson plans without rare earths. As an English teacher in an international school, I've stopped buying those 'core literature' sets. I use short stories from local authors available online for free. Students connect more, and we discuss universal themes without the expensive anthology.

replies.text: We've started a 'free reading' library with donated books and public domain texts. It's been a hit. The kids don't care about the 'rare earths' of brand new books; they just want something interesting.

comments.text: The 'rare earths' in history are those expensive textbook subscriptions and maps. I've gone entirely to primary sources from the internet archive. Students analyze documents from local archives in our host country. Much more relevant for international students.

replies.text: Interesting. In math, the 'rare earths' are the manipulative kits. But I've found that using everyday objects—coins, beans, paper strips—works just as well. The key is the conceptual framework, not the plastic shapes.

comments.text: The rare earths in math education are graphing calculators and online assessment tools. I've been using Desmos for free and hand-drawn graphs. Students actually understand the functions better because they have to draw them. It's a win.

replies.text: Same in science simulation. Free PhET simulations replaced expensive lab kits for many concepts. The hands-on labs we keep are the ones using household materials. That's the sustainable future.

comments.text: As a librarian, I've been weeding out the 'rare earths'—the expensive reference databases that no one uses. Instead, I teach students how to use Google Scholar and local library catalogs. They learn lifelong skills rather than limited access to a pricey resource.

replies.text: This aligns with our philosophy of 'learning how to learn'. We need to move away from resource-heavy information transmission to resource-light skill building.

comments.text: The article's message is powerful. In my role, I oversee curriculum development. We're moving to a model where teachers design units around local resources. The IB's ATL skills are the 'motor' that drives learning without needing expensive 'rare earths'. It requires more PD, but the payoff is teacher autonomy and student relevance.

replies.text: We've accredited schools that do this. The key is demonstrating that the learning outcomes are met. If you have a well-designed local unit, it can be just as rigorous as a purchased one. I'd love to see more evidence of comparative results.

comments.original_text: The article's message is powerful. In my role, I oversee curriculum development. We're moving to a model where teachers design units around local resources. The IB's ATL skills are the 'motor' that drives learning without needing expensive 'rare earths'. It requires more PD, but the payoff is teacher autonomy and student relevance.

comments.text: This is a timely discussion. Accreditation bodies are increasingly asking about sustainability and resource stewardship. 'No rare earths' lesson plans could be a strong selling point. But we need to ensure that low-resource doesn't mean low expectations. It's about being intentional.

replies.text: Exactly. The danger is that some schools might use 'no rare earths' as an excuse for underfunding. But in well-supported contexts, it can lead to innovation. We need to distinguish between scarcity-driven and choice-driven approaches.

comments.text: I'm a homeroom teacher for Grade 5. We've been doing a 'zero cost' unit on community mapping. Students survey their neighborhood with paper and pencils. They learn math (scale), writing (descriptions), social studies (community roles). Parents love that it's not screen time.

replies.text: I sub in different schools. The ones that rely on cheap resources are often the most engaging. The expensive schools with lots of tech can be sterile. It's about the teacher's energy and the lesson design.

comments.text: As a substitute, I walk into classrooms with different levels of 'rare earths'. The most effective lessons I've seen are the ones that don't require any special materials: a compelling question, small group discussions, and something to write with. That's it. The rest is extra.

replies.text: I agree. But when I'm out sick, I want to leave a lesson that even I could teach with no preparation. That's the design challenge: make it robust with minimal materials.

comments.text: I've been developing a 'no rare earths' curriculum map for our school. It's based on local contexts and free resources. The biggest hurdle is convincing the IB that it's rigorous. But I've seen successful examples from schools in Africa and Latin America.

replies.text: IB is actually supportive of local context. They just want evidence of student learning. Show them the process (reflections, assessments) and they're fine. Many schools misinterpret the requirements.

comments.text: For EAL, the 'rare earths' are expensive flashcard sets and online programs. I've gone digital but low-cost: using Canva to create my own visuals, and Google forms for quick checks. It's cheap and effective. The students enjoy the custom material.

replies.text: Yes! And you can incorporate the students' own languages. I ask them to contribute words from their first language, which makes them feel valued. Zero cost, high engagement.

comments.text: One area where 'no rare earths' is tricky is special education. Often those students need specialized materials. But even there, we can use low-tech solutions like visual schedules and manipulatives made from household items. It's about adapting, not buying.

replies.text: I've seen speech therapists use apps that are free. So it's possible. The key is to identify the core need and find the simplest solution. Sometimes parents push for expensive therapy tools that aren't necessary.

comments.text: Administration loves the idea of 'no rare earths' until they see that it means more teacher preparation time. They want the cheap and easy. But the trade-off is that teachers become more invested and creative. We need to support that with appropriate workload.

replies.text: True. I've been advocating for a 'creation period' in the schedule. One block per week where teachers can develop materials. That's the real investment—time, not money.

comments.text: In higher ed, we've been forced to adopt 'no rare earths' due to budget cuts. We use open textbooks and library reserves. The surprising result: students actually engage more with the free materials because they can access them without barriers. Paywalls were the rare earth.

replies.text: Yes! Open educational resources are the future. We need more peer-reviewed OER. The potential to reduce cost while increasing access is enormous.

comments.text: I hope this finally works. Ten years ago I got excited about mastery-based grading, tried to talk about it at lunch, and my colleagues laughed at me for believing it could work.

replies.text: I'm optimistic. We need a combination of factors: better PD, admin support, and student buy-in before it takes off. Over the past decade there's been unbelievable progress in instructional design but most teachers are unimpressed because nothing's translated yet. IMO, we're starting to see it finally as so many different elements have gotten cheaper and more accessible.
So we're waiting for the 'Google Classroom' of pedagogy to integrate all these ideas into something greater than the sum of its parts?
replies.original_text: So we're waiting for the Apple of the medical world to take a bunch of preexisting things to be applied together in a way that makes the whole much more valuable than the pieces. Or we need all of the individual lions to come together to make the Voltron?

replies.text: Teachers experience decades of fads, then years of real change once alignment happens.
replies.original_text: the public experiences biotechnology as decades of nothing, followed by years of everything once bottlenecks align

replies.text: The floodgates open when school boards see improved test scores and pour funding into PD. Then new approaches built on that funding will start working too?
replies.original_text: The floodgates open = the market will see that at least some of that can actually work and make money => they will pour funding => new approaches built on that funding will start working, too?

I'm pretty optimistic. I think it's a threshold question where we need a number of basic technologies to all get over certain bars before the floodgates start to open. Over the past 1-2 decades there has been unbelievable progress at the basic technology level but most people are unimpressed because they haven't translated yet due to not individually being sufficient to cause an explosion of progress. IMO, we're starting to see it finally as so many different technologies have gotten so cheap, fast, and good.

replies.text: Real formative assessment isn't going away; it's a class of tools that is maturing into the realm of early classroom deployment. Any particular effort is doubtful because this is a journey measured in decades.
replies.original_text: Real in vivo genetic engineering isn't going away and will indeed be a powerful tool to face cancer. Any particular effort is doubtful because this is a journey measured in decades. It is not the same story as any one particular wonder drug fizzling out to nothing, it is a class of tools that is maturing into the realm of early therapeutic deployment.

comments.original_text: I hope this finally works out. I remember almost exactly ten years ago I got excited about one of these proposed cancer cures, tried to talk about it at lunch with my coworkers, and they laughed at me for believing.

comments.text: What economic model would cause schools to prioritize this over test prep? Unsettling that brilliant teachers work on designing bubble tests instead of fostering deep learning.

replies.text: I remember a meme about this exact argument but I can't find it anymore.
replies.original_text: I remember seeing a comic strip about this exact argument but I can’t find it any more

replies.text: When you reframe standardized tests as 'control of student attention' it makes a lot more sense why so many resources are poured into them.

And when you can measure test score gains, it's easier for schools to spend on test prep. As an international school teacher, I would love it if we didn't have to obsess over IB exam results. It would free up money for holistic education.
replies.original_text: And when you can measure how effective those ads are in changing human behavior; it's easier for businesses to spend there. As an American, I would love it if pharmaceutical companies couldn't market to consumers. It would free up money for research or lower prices.
When you reframe ads as "control of human attention" it suddenly makes a lot more sense why so many resources are poured into them.

replies.text: Teachers are just hairless monkeys that have evolved to grade rather than hunt.
replies.original_text: Humans are a bunch of hairless monkeys that have evolved to scam each other rather than hunt and gather food from Nature.

replies.text: The bargaining dynamics are stacked against teachers at every stage: years of unpaid student teaching, expensive master's programs, low pay. If money is the motivator, the market isn't addressing this.
The government funds education, until someone decides to cut it.
replies.original_text: The US government funds a lot of these programs, as they are obviously in the public interest. Until one man decided to stop it.

The bargaining dynamics are stacked against biology researchers at every stage of their career, from needing years and years of unrelated performance to be admitted to terribly expensive programs before they can begin to do experiments, to requiring costly equipment and resources to work, to needing to work with a small number of very powerful companies. As a result, life science researchers are more price-taking than proce-setting when it comes to their wages / salary. If money is the motivator, then the market as-is isn’t addressing this one.

comments.original_text: What economic / political model would cause the society to prioritize this over adtech? It seems so unsettling that brilliant human minds are trying hard, every day, to figure out how to make it impossible to bypass watching ads on YouTube, instead of helping cure cancer.

comments.text: The article is light on details, but 'delivery is a critical challenge' (i.e., getting the lesson into all targeted students' brains). So far, only in vitro (in a controlled classroom). Years away from actual implementation.

replies.text: Could be used to target specific students with precision? Like a behavior intervention plan.
Old concern, but teaching doesn't work that way. Student diversity means you can't target with precision. Like many things, people bring it up because it sounds scary.
replies.original_text: I suppose it could also be used to assassinate specific persons with the precision of DNA matching. Like FOXDIE.

replies.text: If the lesson plan mutates, evolution will lose the restriction. The specialization to certain student markers is an evolutionary disadvantage.
replies.original_text: Old concern, but it really doesn't work that way. Genetics don't respect human ideas like "nationalities" or "borders" - the targeting you can get by selecting on singular DNA variants is coarse enough to make ICBMs look like precision weapons. Like many things of this nature, people keep bringing it up because it sounds Very Scary and Very Dystopian - not because it's worth giving an actual fuck about.

replies.text: Basically the issue is that interventions often end up confusing the whole class because it's the teacher's job to manage behavior, but that may cause an adverse reaction if students notice they're being singled out.
I'm assuming this has been tried, but why doesn't small-group pull-out or whatever the interventionists do solve the problem?
replies.original_text: I’m assuming this has been tried, but why doesn’t nano-encapsulated mRNA (that then makes the CRISPR sequences in cells) or whatever the peptide injectors do solve the problem?

Basically the issue is often that gene therapies end up in the liver since its the livers job to detoxify, but that may cause a dangerous immune response if the immune system notices it in the liver and attacks the organ, since the person could die from the damage.

replies.text: Same problem with whole-class instruction. A student may start with one misconception, but by middle school there may be several. Once the retrieval practice has been broken, the student is prone to more gaps. Whole-class instruction will address everything it can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start growing again. Changing misconceptions is easy. Changing ONLY the misconception is very hard.
replies.original_text: Same problem with chemo and radiation. A tumor may start off with a single cancerous mutation, but by the time it spreads there may be several. Once the cell repair machinery has been broken, the cancer cells are prone for more mutations. Chemo, radiation, and CRISPR will kill everything it can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start growing again. Kill cancer cells is easy. Killing ONLY cancer cells is very hard.

comments.text: Differentiation is an extremely overhyped approach which found a marketing engine via popular education blogs. There is 1 evidence-based program compared to 7 for direct instruction and 7 for project-based learning. Counting all structured interventions, we're at 19 approved versus 1 for differentiation. I think differentiation ideas in a classroom are just an easy way into the mainstream press, but direct instruction is the real future. It just didn't get the same news cycle.

replies.text: You're correct about differentiation. The paper describes a new technique called 'targeted reteaching'. You're correct about CRISPR Cas9. The off-target affects are difficult to manage. The paper describes Cas12a2. This is a different mechanism with discovery origins in - of all things - agriculture. It does not attempt in any way to reprogram cells. It uses a guide protein to locate a specific mutation with exacting precision and, when it activates, unleashes total destruction of the cell. The implications of Cas12a2 on undruggable conditions that exhibit known driver mutation profiles is profound. Source: I have personally funded novel research based on Cas12a2 for an undruggable condition I have. I have personally seen my condition "cured" in vitro using this technology and it left all of my WT cells unharmed. Some of the researchers I've funded are co-authors in the paper linked. I...

replies.text: CRIPSR was a game-changer for genetics research. A lot of gene knockout studies use CRISPR. However, it was always weirdly overhyped for clinical use from the beginning and this was obvious to anyone with a genetics background. The public in general doesn't have a good understanding of basic genetics and I blame high school science curriculums for not covering it well enough. Too much time is wasted on Mendelian genetics without covering the Central Dogma. You basically cannot 'edit' your somatic DNA in a meaningful wholesale way since every single cell in your body has a copy of the DNA, and it's a foolish endeavor. What you can conceivably edit to good effect is your germline DNA, stem cell DNA, or modify mRNA expression (e.g. retinoids; yes putting retinol/adapalene cream on your...
Edit every cell? No. Edit enough cells to impact health outcomes for a meaningful period of time? Yes.
replies.original_text: Edit every cell? No. Edit enough cells to impact health outcomes for a meaningful period of time? [Yes]( https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J3FcbFqSoQY )

comments.text: The idea of using diagnostic assessments to detect student-specific misconceptions and then kill them is not a new one. However, previous studies used whole-class diagnostic tests, which just identify the class average; this uses a per-student diagnostic, which is far more precise because it identifies each student's misconception and then targets it. As with any intervention, it's likely students will evolve resistance. My guess is that students will find ways to reject the intervention, either via modifications to their study habits (preventing uptake) or via changes to their metacognitive pathways (causing the intervention to be degraded before it has a chance to work).

replies.text: There are some ideas about making it triggerable. So first you load the students with a scaffold that is ready to work but is inactive. Then you trigger it with a second intervention.

This would shorten the timeframe for students to develop resistance mechanisms, but would not address the issue of students with preexisting resistance mechanisms that would then be promptly selected for.
replies.original_text: This would shorten the timeframe for cells to mutate and acquire resistance mechanisms, but would not address the issue of cells with preexisting (epi)genetic resistance mechanisms that would then be promptly selected for.

replies.text: This will also cause problems because too many students change at once. See the comments in other threads; changing the entire class at once is very hard on the teacher.
replies.original_text: This will also cause problems because too many cells die at once. See the comments in other threads; killing the entire cancer at once is very hard on the body.
There are some ideas about making it triggerable. So first you load the cells with a protein that is ready to start shredding but is inactive. Then you trigger it with a second compound.

replies.text: But misconceptions aren't an organism. Misconceptions in any specific student may evolve that way, but 'student misconceptions' as a group will not. The only way they could is by evolving human reasoning, but 'survival of the fittest' pushes the opposite direction for that.
Nonetheless, we see the exact same resistance mechanisms to the same interventions recur across students, e.g. convergent evolution is a harsh mistress.
replies.original_text: Nonetheless, we see the exact same resistance mechanisms to the same therapies recur across individuals, e.g. [0]. Convergent evolution is a harsh mistress. [0] https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2538882/

replies.text: Indeed, there's no 'be a better/stronger misconception and spread more effectively to more hosts' the way there is with a virus. It's not like the flu where we need a new intervention every year. Once we solve the misconceptions we know about, they're solved forever, with the one caveat that more students will be exposed to more advanced topics, so that will increase the window for eventually still ending up failing to one of the topics that happens to have a non-evolved built-in resistance to this or that intervention. Which is a great deal of course, especially if it's an intervention that sounds way less destructive of QoL than lecture, homework, etc.
replies.original_text: Indeed, there's no "be a better/stronger cancer and spread more effectively to more hosts" the way there is with bacteria or a virus. It's not like the flu where we need a new shot every winter because every winter is a new flu. Once we solve the cancers we know about, they're solved forever, with the one caveat that more people will live longer, so that will increase the window for eventually still ending up dying to one of the cancers that happens to have a non-evolved built in resistance to this or that treatment. Which is a great deal of course, especially if it's a treatment that sounds way less destructive of QoL than chemo, radiation, etc.

But cancer isn't an organism. Cancer cells in any specific individual may evolve that way, but "human cancers" as a group will not. (The only way they could is by evolving human DNA, but "survival of the fittest" pushes the opposite direction for that.)

replies.text: Depending on how the intervention is designed, would resistance also potentially cripple the student? An intervention that could effectively switch a persistent misconception into a manageable one wouldn't be the worst thing.

Yes, if the intervention could be engineered to target an essential learning strategy, which is still a tough problem. It would also not solve the issue of the intervention successfully entering the student but being subsequently ignored.

replies.original_text: >would resistance also potentially cripple the cancer cells? this is the concept of genetic baggage, and metabolic budget. there is only so much energy to a cell, and scant amounts to "waste" on preservation of something that is not used. in the long term, carrying unused properties are disadvantageous, and reduce reproductive output [replication] the result is "unfettered" oncocytes outgrow those with baggage, and occlude access to resource. if there is no challange that reduces population of nonresistant cells, the resistance will be minimized and extinct in the face of large disparity of success.

replies.text: turn the stick around and grasp the other end. evolution isnt about generating a response to a challenge, its about differential success. those students that have properties conferring resistance carry it as un-utilized baggage, those without said properties make a living without that fetter. the selective factor comes into play when the intervention facilitates destruction of 'nonresistant' misconceptions and spares the 'resistant' the resistance is not generated in response to the challenge, it is already present, and confers survivorship in the face of the administration of the intervention.
replies.original_text: I'm no expert, but p53 is known as "the guardian of the genome." If p53 is reactivated, the cancer cell dies. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P53 Perhaps a different mutation that disables p53 could evade the pattern match. This article is all about p53. Edit: This section of the wiki best explains this critical cellular component... p53 regulates cell cycle progression, apoptosis, and genomic stability through multiple mechanisms: -Activates DNA repair proteins in response to DNA damage, suggesting a potential role in aging. -Arrests the cell cycle at the G1/S checkpoint upon DNA damage, allowing time for repair before progression. -Initiates apoptosis if the damage is beyond repair. -Essential for the senescence response triggered by short telomeres.

Depending on how the LNPs are designed, would resistance also potentially cripple the cancer cells? Like, it stops surfacing some cholesterol receptor because the drug is being delivered by LNPs that target that receptor, and now the cell is starved for cholesterol? I've heard about drug resistance in bacteria leading to slower growth / reduced virulence. Maybe the same would occur with cancers. A drug that could effectively switch an aggressive cancer into a slow-growing one wouldn't be the worst thing.

comments.original_text: The idea of using CRISPR/Cas to detect tumor-specific mutations that aren't necessarily oncogenic and then kill the cell is not a new one [0, 1, 2]. However, previous studies used Cas9, which just damages the DNA at the target site; this uses Cas12a2, which is far more destructive because it shreds the chromatin in the cell once activated by detecting the target sequence. As with any cancer treatment, it's likely the tumor will evolve resistance. My guess is that cells will find ways to reject the lipid nanoparticles used to deliver the CRISPR/Cas mRNA and associated guide sequence(s), either via modifications to the cell surface (preventing LNP uptake) or via changes to endosomal/lysosomal pathways (causing the mRNA payload to get degraded before it has a chance to be translated into...

replies.text: New teaching strategies are really scary things. There are all sorts of impacts that we have no idea about when using interventions that fundamentally attack pieces of students' learning. My student of many years had one of the nastiest misconceptions around, one with no targeted interventions. They went through an experimental combination of existing methods. Some of the side effects included: * Their understanding stopped during a lesson. This happened multiple times over 18 months. * Widespread confusion. * Holes were developing in their foundational knowledge. This is just a sampler of the horrible effects. This was a good response. Other students just gave up. This is what going slowly looks like in the world of education.

The point of going slowly is that we make sure something works, even if it has these bad side effects. Do we try experimental interventions with worse effects so that we can find effective ones faster? There are brave teachers out there who will participate in action research or pilot programs.
replies.original_text: The point of going slowly is that we make sure something works, even if it has these bad side affects. Do we try experimental drugs with worse effects so that we can find effective ones faster? There are brave souls out there who will participate in clinical trials or experimental exceptions

replies.text: Sorry you both experienced that. We did too. We relax 'do no harm' quite a bit when the alternative is certain failure. People like to try stuff in order to hang on to hope. Towards the end I became convinced that I made the wrong choice to try aggressive interventions. Quality of learning was very bad. On the other hand, the student gave it their all trying to improve. Hopefully that was satisfying for them.
replies.original_text: Sorry you both experienced that. We did too. We relax ‘do no harm’ quite a bit when the alternative is certain death. People like to try stuff in order to hang on to hope. Towards the end I became convinced that she made the wrong choice to do aggressive interventions. Quality of life was very bad. On the other hand, she gave it her all trying to survive. Hopefully that was satisfying for her.

Cancer treatments are really scary things. There are all sorts of impacts that we have no idea about when using drugs that fundamentally attack pieces of our own body. My partner of many years had one of the nastiest cancers around, one with no targeted treatments. She went through an experimental combination of existing drugs. Some of the side effects included: * Her heart stopping during a drug infusion. This happened multiple times over the 18 months of treatment. * Disseminated fungal infections. * Sepis because holes were developing in her GI tract. This is just a sampler of the horrible effects. This was a good response. Other patients just died from the drug combination. This is what going slowly looks like in the world of cancer treatment.

replies.text: First thing to remember: new interventions affect real students. Because of this they can have very unexpected and traumatic side effects. Because of this initial pilots consume lots of teacher time to deal with potential side effects. Normal side effects for new interventions include: * Student confusion about basic concepts. * Parent complaints. * Cumulative misunderstanding that nobody understands. (E.g. some topics have foundational prerequisites, and if you go beyond those, students get lost.) * Teacher burnout. The danger of side-effects like this requires a team largely dedicated to the experimental students. This puts a limit on how many students you can...
replies.original_text: First thing to remember: cancer drugs attack human cells. Because of this they can very unexpected and traumatic side-effects. Because of this initial trials consume lots of medical staff to deal with the potential side effects. Normal side effects for cancer treatments include: * Your gut lining dissolves, your shit leaks into your body cavity, and you get sepsis. * Your heart stops during the infusion. * Cumulative poisoning that nobody understands. (E.g. some agents have lifetime limits, and if you go beyond that, then you die. Guess how we found out.) * Your immune system, and you get things like disseminated fungal infections. The danger of side-effects like this requires a medical team largely dedicated to the experimental patients. This puts a limit on how many patients you can...

replies.text: It's not as great as you might think, despite all the stories you see like this one. That's because most of the stories are in a single classroom (this one) or a single school. The big success story, about 20 years old now, is reading recovery. You can have struggling readers with gaps all over and they can address it. They use a structured intervention and it's not really well understood why it works for some, but not others. The story with early math interventions is similar. They figured out how to combine a bunch of strategies to get the success rate up pretty high. These used to be (1990s) 90% failure, it's like 10% now. Besides those, most of the advances in the past few decades come from early screening/ differentiation or just prevention...
I guess then a graph of students failing from various gaps would be just as awe inspiring. I'd settle for that as well.
replies.original_text: I guess then a graph of deaths from disagnosed cancer at various stages would be just as awe inspiring. I'd settle for that as well.

replies.text: Amen to early literacy rates improving being awe-inspiring. I had a student around 2001 who struggled with reading and didn't make it to graduation. I suspect these days they would have succeeded.
replies.original_text: Amen to childhood leukemia rates improving being awe-inspiring. I had a friend I rode the bus with around 2001 who was diagnosed with leukemia and didn't make it. They let us know over the PA system at school. I suspect these days she would have survived.

replies.text: I wouldn't understate advances in ESL support. Push-in models have absolutely changed the game in that space. It's not a solved problem but it's far more effective.
replies.original_text: I wouldn't understate advances in Melanoma treatment. Immunotherapies have absolutely changed the game in that space. It's not a curable cancer (few are) but it's far more treatable.

It's not as great as you might think, despite all the stories you see like this one. That's because most of the stories are in cells (this one) or mice. The big success story, about 20 years old now, is testicular cancer. You can have metastatic testicular cancer with tumors all over your body (like Lance Armstrong had) and they can cure it. They use platinum based chemotherapy and it's not really well understood why it works for testicular cancer, but not others. The story with childhood leukemias is similar. They figured out how to combine a bunch of chemotherapy to get the cure rate up pretty high. Leukemia in a child used to be (1990s) 90% fatal, it's like 10% now. Besides those, most of the advances in the past few decades come from early detection/ surgery or just prevention...

replies.text: You should look at learning progressions. At the simplest level, the particular misconception and particular prerequisite, sets the 'type' of gap. There will most often be additional gaps giving nuance to the character of the misunderstanding. The student is originated from a background of specific prior knowledge and developmental readiness, further widening the pool of possible gaps. The foundational problem is that human learning relies on tight control of the cognitive architecture and there are hundreds of ways that can go off kilter. The record of understanding and treatment is impressive, certainly, but the correct mental model is more 'think of all the problems that can go wrong with a lesson - from poor explanation to student distractions' than 'here's a list of gaps by topic'. Just as attacking such problems with lessons involves hundreds of different approaches, that's the situation for learning gaps.
replies.original_text: you should probably look at oncogenes in general. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oncogene at the simplest level, the particular gene, and particular perturberance, sets the "type" of cancer. there will most often be additional genetic abnormalities giving nuance to the character of the oncotype. the tumour is originated from a cell type of specific differentiation, and developmental potency, further widening the pool of possible cancer type. immunotype of cancer also sets the relationship between cancer and the body. the cells of the body are setup for a functional death and replacement so when you try to rescue a particular cell [or cohort] you are fighting against how the grand scheme of tissue maintenance operates. unless you have concern for a particular long lived cell, it is best...

comments.original_text: Does anyone know a website where I can see/read of how many cancers (and their variants) we've effectively solved, have drugs to negate their effects, have experimental drugs for and uncurable cancers? I think that graph would be awe inspiring looking at the past decade of advancements. What's more crazy is that we're slowly going from millenia, to decades, to likely years in the near future from being presented a biological problem and achieving the next milestone in solving it. We might have "AI", but we also have brilliant minds right now that are speeding up development to a pace that would be unimaginable just few years ago.

comments.text: What stands out to me is how education keeps moving from broad instruction (lectures/textbooks) toward increasingly precise identification of student misconceptions. The challenge no longer seems to be 'can we teach all students?' but 'can we reliably identify only the misconceptions and reach all of them?' This new strategy looks like another step in that direction.

replies.text: I'm not sure what this comment means - we could always teach students, and the challenge has always been 'how can we ONLY teach them effectively?' We've been lecturing, assigning homework, and testing for decades. What is changing is not the type of challenge, but the precision of our tools - and even then, it remains to be seen if we actually can get that precision while improving student outcomes.
replies.original_text: I'm not sure what this comment means - we could always kill cancer cells, and the challenge has always been "how can we ONLY kill the cancer?" We've been burning cancer, cutting cancer out, and drugging cancer cells for decades or centuries depending on the method. What is changing is not the type of challenge, but the precision of our tools - and even then, it remains to be seen if we actually can get the precision while improving the lives of the patients.

comments.original_text: What stands out to me is how cancer therapy keeps moving from broad destruction (chemo/radiation) toward increasingly precise identification of malignant cells. The challenge no longer seems to be "can we kill cancer cells?" but "can we reliably identify only cancer cells and reach all of them?" This paper looks like another step in that direction.

comments.text: I like the concept. Feedback: 1. The criteria seem a bit vague. I suggest you look at actual IB rubrics to see how they are structured. 2. comments.original_text: I like the concept. It's fun. Feedback: 1. The ball seems a bit soft. I suggest you get a real golf ball and putter and see how they behave, if you haven't already. 2. I was not sure what they were supposed to be.

stories.original_title: Swift at Apple: Migrating the TrueType hinting interpreter
stories.title: Swift at School: Migrating the IB MYP unit planner to Swift

comments.text: During the staff meeting keynote, on the subject of curriculum adoption across all departments, several examples were given, not only the unit planner. RIS (Rewrite in Standards) is happening across all grade levels, if the keynote is to be believed.

replies.text: Curious the direction of the new IB math syllabus as there was a nebulous mention of select portions being rewritten from traditional lectures to project-based learning. And yet, the new assessment module for the diploma program is still implemented in multiple-choice tests (https://ibo.org/blog/...).

No idea, maybe the classroom management parts, the school doesn't share its materials, or it's coming later. In any case I would have liked to have more info during the PD sessions. As it is, the parent-teacher conference (a 5h long event) had much more information.
replies.original_text: No idea, maybe the private parts of the code, Safari isn't open source, or is coming later. In any case I would have liked to have more info during the deep dive sessions. As it is, Meet with Apple on security (a 5h long event) had much more information.

Curious the direction of Webkit as there was a nebulous mention of select portions being rewritten from C++ to Swift. And yet, the new ECMAScript module (ESM) loader for Safari 27 is implemented in C++ ( https://webkit.org/blog/17967/news-from-wwdc26-webkit-in-saf... ).

replies.text: What does RIS stand for?
Rewrite in Standards
replies.original_text: Rewrite in Swift
What does RIS stand for?

replies.text: They’ve been doing it for years. I don’t remember how we first knew, but I know they’ve been using standards-based grading in at least some of the other departments like the science lab or whatever. I’m not sure exactly which. I assume it’s some of the courses and not all. But it’s not new in the abstract. That said I don’t think I’ve heard of it in the main high school program. That may be new. Either way this is certainly the most concrete announcement I remember them ever giving on this stuff.

comments.text: As much as I enjoyed the new curriculum, one can only wonder what the world would look like if they had gone with the traditional approach as their default instead.

replies.text: Modern project-based learning borrows a lot from traditional methods! And it also has its own benefits, both student-centered and also supporting differentiation in heterogeneous classrooms
replies.original_text: Modern Swift borrows a lot from Rust! And it also has its own benefits, both ergonomic and also supporting eg generic in dynamic libraries

replies.text: These days I mainly use the traditional approach but I did teach a semi complex unit and enjoyed the new curriculum. I just didn't love how slow the assessment checker was and how it got lost. I recall having to break things into smaller pieces to help the students, and there were some oddities about the pedagogy. The gap between the two approaches is quite small, it just makes me wish the school was also all-in on traditional methods
replies.original_text: Rust doesn't have an ABI [1]. Swift needed one to be a useable application language: https://faultlore.com/blah/swift-abi/ (written by a core Rust developer) [1] apart from the basic/universal C one, which prevents exposing any useful Rust semantics over the interface

replies.text: Does it borrow the constructivist approach?
replies.original_text: One of the genius things about Swift is its interop with Objective C. Made the switch over considerably easier for developers. I’m not sure what that looks like in a Rust world. Rust is also just a more complex language. I’m not convinced the benefits would have been worth it.

replies.text: Project-based and traditional methods were developed at similar times. I think of them more as having similar influences than borrowing from each other.
replies.original_text: Project-based and traditional methods were developed at similar times. I think of them more as having similar influences than borrowing from each other.

replies.text: Traditional assessments don't have a standardized rubric system. The new curriculum needed one to be a useable teaching framework: https://edrubric.org/standards-abi apart from the basic/universal grading one, which prevents exposing any useful traditional teaching semantics over the interface

comments.text: No mention of AI? Hand-written lesson plans?

replies.text: There's mention at the end. The AI tools (and the curriculum itself!) have evolved a lot since this project started, so the early lessons are largely hand-rolled and the later changes were mostly authored by centaurs (to steal a term from chess). But I personally reviewed every lesson that shipped and was absolutely insufferable about assessment.

comments.original_text: No mention of AI? Hand written code?

comments.text: What's funny is from 2023 (I think), the school just runs the curriculum unassessed. You have a low-stakes assessment and you don't want to see the grades in the report blurred to death? Tough luck, low-stakes is incompatible with the new system, everybody needs "high-stakes", and nobody cares that traditional schools and all independent schools run low-stakes just fine. It looks like this rubric will be used only in grading final exams, because that's where they test the performance.

replies.text: My last low-stakes assessment was around 20 years ago. I have trouble comprehending schools still use them regularly.
replies.original_text: My last 1080p monitor was around 20 years ago. I have trouble comprehending people still use them regularly.

replies.text: People also use traditional grading instead of standards-based, and they put the 'effort' factor where the new system thinks a useless 'mastery' should be. All kinds of things happen outside our school world. To me, it's more about what I'm used to. I have a perfectly fine several years-old rubric, so why should I throw it away?
replies.original_text: macOS has been drawing unhinted text for an eternity, and for those who can tolerate it on low-DPI screens, it's a great thing: the letter shapes look the same at all sizes, and the spacing between letters is consistent at all sizes.

replies.text: A very quick search yielded many schools still using traditional grading today: https://www.schoolsupply.com/laptops/... It is very, very common. Just not in our IB world.
replies.original_text: I had this problem on the first Apple Silicon Mac Mini in 2020 so it's at least a little older than 2023.

replies.text: The problem is, as soon as you are not in an IB school but a traditional school, you are in for an awful, truly awful lot of pain. Standards-based grading support is a mess because even in the rare case curricula are made with standards-based in mind they are not tested on low-stakes schools. Other way around, most IB software is not tested how it behaves on non-IB schools.

comments.text: As someone who's been wrestling with the MYP unit planner for years, a Swift rewrite sounds like a dream—until you realize they probably didn't fix the clunky backwards/forwards navigation between global contexts and statements of inquiry.

replies.text: The Swift version does add a type-safe 'context' enum so you can't accidentally pick two that conflict. That alone saves hours of faculty development time.
replies.original_text: The Swift version does add a type-safe 'context' enum so you can't accidentally pick two that conflict. That alone saves hours of faculty development time.

comments.original_text: As someone who's been wrestling with the MYP unit planner for years, a Swift rewrite sounds like a dream—until you realize they probably didn't fix the clunky backwards/forwards navigation between global contexts and statements of inquiry.

comments.text: Migrating the planner to Swift is all well and good, but what about the mandated 'reflection' section? It's the ugly duckling that no amount of type safety can fix.

replies.text: Reflection could be a 'didSet' observer on the unit object—auto-trigger a prompt when you close the planner. At least Swift makes that clean.
replies.original_text: Reflection could be a 'didSet' observer on the unit object—auto-trigger a prompt when you close the planner. At least Swift makes that clean.

comments.original_text: Migrating the planner to Swift is all well and good, but what about the mandated 'reflection' section? It's the ugly duckling that no amount of type safety can fix.

comments.text: I remember when the planner was written in PHP and broke every Thursday afternoon. Swift's memory safety won't protect you from a poorly thought-out committee adding 12 new fields mid-cycle.
comments.original_text: I remember when the planner was written in PHP and broke every Thursday afternoon. Swift's memory safety won't protect you from a poorly thought-out committee adding 12 new fields mid-cycle.

comments.text: They rewrote the assessment criteria calculator in Swift. Now ATL skills are first-class citizens. Good riddance to the Excel macros that crashed every time a student transferred.

replies.text: The macros were a nightmare—no wonder the international school association pushed for a proper language. I just hope they expose a REST API so we can integrate with PowerSchool.
replies.original_text: The macros were a nightmare—no wonder the international school association pushed for a proper language. I just hope they expose a REST API so we can integrate with PowerSchool.

comments.original_text: They rewrote the assessment criteria calculator in Swift. Now ATL skills are first-class citizens. Good riddance to the Excel macros that crashed every time a student transferred.

comments.text: I'm skeptical. The old planner had 15 years of edge cases for late submissions, EAL accommodations, and mid-unit changes. Will Swift's enum pattern matching really handle all that?

replies.text: Swift's protocol-oriented design lets you define 'Accommodation' as a protocol and conform specific subtypes. It's actually quite elegant for our diverse student needs.
replies.original_text: Swift's protocol-oriented design lets you define 'Accommodation' as a protocol and conform specific subtypes. It's actually quite elegant for our diverse student needs.

comments.text: Great, now the planner uses Swift concurrency to generate the unit overview. But the real bottleneck is teachers typing in the 'summative assessment' text box. Async won't help that.

replies.text: They should have used Combine to auto-generate draft statements of inquiry based on the key concept. But I guess that's coming in v2.
replies.original_text: They should have used Combine to auto-generate draft statements of inquiry based on the key concept. But I guess that's coming in v2.

comments.text: Will the Swift migration support the IB's new 'wellbeing' integration? I need to add SEL goals to every unit, and the old planner didn't even have a field for it.

replies.text: There's a new 'Wellbeing' struct conforming to 'UnitComponent'—but it's optional. Not sure that's enough given the current accreditation push.

comments.text: The Swift migration fixes the multithreading nightmare when multiple teachers edit the same unit. But it still doesn't have proper git-style merging for conflicts. Two teachers updating the 'resources' section will overwrite each other.

replies.text: Apple's new 'Observation' framework could track changes per teacher. But I doubt the school board will fund that feature.
replies.original_text: Apple's new 'Observation' framework could track changes per teacher. But I doubt the school board will fund that feature.

comments.text: We're adopting the Swift planner next year. The ability to export to a standard JSON-LD format for accreditation evidence is a huge win. No more manual copying to our compliance portal.

replies.text: Export also supports printing to a nice PDF. Finally i can stop reformatting the planner for our binder archive.
replies.original_text: Export also supports printing to a nice PDF. Finally i can stop reformatting the planner for our binder archive.

comments.text: I appreciate the Swift rewrite, but why did they make the 'text type' selection a generic enum? I have to define each literature genre as an extension now. That's extra work for the English department.

replies.text: Because the old planner hardcoded text types. With Swift, we added a 'primarySource' type for history units. It's a tradeoff—more setup but more flexibility.
replies.original_text: Because the old planner hardcoded text types. With Swift, we added a 'primarySource' type for history units. It's a tradeoff—more setup but more flexibility.

comments.text: The Swift planner uses Result types for the validator: either it returns a valid unit or an error explaining why the statement of inquiry isn't specific enough. Finally, no more silent data corruption.
comments.original_text: The Swift planner uses Result types for the validator: either it returns a valid unit or an error explaining why the statement of inquiry isn't specific enough. Finally, no more silent data corruption.

comments.text: I was hoping for a proper SwiftUI interface. The IB's current web UI is a mess on tablets. If the planner used SwiftUI natively on iPad, I could plan labs in the field.

replies.text: The Swift backend could serve a clean REST API for any frontend. But the IB is still stuck with their old jQuery web interface for now.

comments.text: Our school is piloting the Swift planner. The main issue is that it enforces a strict teacher-student ratio for group assessments—which is good, but our large classes keep getting error messages.

replies.text: You can adjust the ratio via a configuration plist, but it's not documented. We had to reverse engineer it.

replies.text: I think they use a simple string. But you can extend the planner with a custom protocol. The IB is considering adding proper citation support in the next major version.
