comments.original_text: Does the planner support 'Citation' type with CSL? Our school requires APA7, and the old planner just had a plain text field. Swift's enum with associated values could model this nicely.

comments.text: The planer now shows 'Days until deadline' in a SwiftUI badge. For my grade 10 advisory, that's both motivating and anxiety-inducing. At least it's accurate.
comments.original_text: The planer now shows 'Days until deadline' in a SwiftUI badge. For my grade 10 advisory, that's both motivating and anxiety-inducing. At least it's accurate.

comments.text: As a sub, I wish the planner had a 'guest mode' that lets me view the unit without logging in. The SSO requires a school email, and I'm still waiting for mine to be provisioned.

replies.text: There's a public read-only link feature. But it's disabled by default for security. Ask your administrator to enable it.
replies.original_text: There's a public read-only link feature. But it's disabled by default for security. Ask your administrator to enable it.

comments.original_text: As a sub, I wish the planner had a 'guest mode' that lets me view the unit without logging in. The SSO requires a school email, and I'm still waiting for mine to be provisioned.

comments.text: We've seen a 30% reduction in time spent on unit planning after the Swift rollout. The autocomplete for 'global contexts' really speeds up the workflow. Just worry about the learning curve for veteran teachers.

replies.text: Old dogs can learn new Swift tricks. Our VET committee offered after-school workshops using the Swift Playgrounds app. Teachers picked it up fast.
replies.original_text: Old dogs can learn new Swift tricks. Our VET committee offered after-school workshops using the Swift Playgrounds app. Teachers picked it up fast.

comments.original_text: We've seen a 30% reduction in time spent on unit planning after the Swift rollout. The autocomplete for 'global contexts' really speeds up the workflow. Just worry about the learning curve for veteran teachers.

comments.text: The planner now syncs with our student information system via SwiftNIO. But it's a two-way sync, and there's a bug where changing a student's schedule mid-year deletes their unit enrollments.

replies.text: We hit that bug too. The fix is to add a transaction guard. I submitted a PR last week.
replies.original_text: We hit that bug too. The fix is to add a transaction guard. I submitted a PR last week.

comments.original_text: The planner now syncs with our student information system via SwiftNIO. But it's a two-way sync, and there's a bug where changing a student's schedule mid-year deletes their unit enrollments.

comments.text: The Swift migration includes a 'multilingual' feature for unit plans. I can write the learning objectives in English and Chinese side by side. But the typeface doesn't support Chinese well on older devices.
comments.original_text: The Swift migration includes a 'multilingual' feature for unit plans. I can write the learning objectives in English and Chinese side by side. But the typeface doesn't support Chinese well on older devices.

comments.text: Our accreditation review was impressed by the Swift planner's built-in evidence collection. The automatic tagging of ATL skills to each task saved us hours of manual categorization.

replies.text: Yes, but the tag vocabulary is limited to the IB's official ATL list. We have a few custom skills (e.g., 'intercultural competence') that we have to add via a plist extension.
replies.original_text: Yes, but the tag vocabulary is limited to the IB's official ATL list. We have a few custom skills (e.g., 'intercultural competence') that we have to add via a plist extension.

comments.original_text: Our accreditation review was impressed by the Swift planner's built-in evidence collection. The automatic tagging of ATL skills to each task saved us hours of manual categorization.

comments.text: The team behind the Swift planner is small—three developers from an international school consortium. They're doing great work, but I worry about bus factor. What if they leave?

replies.text: The code is open source and well-documented. Our school has two developers who can maintain the fork if needed. The community is growing.
replies.original_text: The code is open source and well-documented. Our school has two developers who can maintain the fork if needed. The community is growing.

comments.original_text: The team behind the Swift planner is small—three developers from an international school consortium. They're doing great work, but I worry about bus factor. What if they leave?

comments.text: I wish they had used Swift's macro system to generate unit templates. Instead, they have a clunky XML configuration. Why not a @UnitPlan macro?
comments.original_text: I wish they had used Swift's macro system to generate unit templates. Instead, they have a clunky XML configuration. Why not a @UnitPlan macro?

comments.text: The planner now includes a 'rubric generator' that uses Swift's built-in regex to extract criteria from past assessments. It's not perfect, but it saves me 20 minutes per unit.

replies.text: Be careful: the regex sometimes captures examples from student work. I had a rubric that included 'use more emojis' because a student wrote that in a reflection.
replies.original_text: Be careful: the regex sometimes captures examples from student work. I had a rubric that included 'use more emojis' because a student wrote that in a reflection.

comments.original_text: The planner now includes a 'rubric generator' that uses Swift's built-in regex to extract criteria from past assessments. It's not perfect, but it saves me 20 minutes per unit.

comments.text: We're using the Swift planner to create interdisciplinary units with the art department. The new 'subject crosswalk' matrix is in beta—it maps unit objectives to multiple subject group standards using Swift's Set algebra.
comments.original_text: We're using the Swift planner to create interdisciplinary units with the art department. The new 'subject crosswalk' matrix is in beta—it maps unit objectives to multiple subject group standards using Swift's Set algebra.

comments.text: The planner's lab report integration is great: students can submit through the app, and the Swift backend applies the IB's data analysis rubric using a decision tree. But it only works for Chemistry—Physics has different units (pun intended).

replies.text: Actually, the decision tree is configurable. You can write a custom 'PhysicsRubric' struct conforming to 'ScoringProtocol'. I did it for our department.
replies.original_text: Actually, the decision tree is configurable. You can write a custom 'PhysicsRubric' struct conforming to 'ScoringProtocol'. I did it for our department.

comments.original_text: The planner's lab report integration is great: students can submit through the app, and the Swift backend applies the IB's data analysis rubric using a decision tree. But it only works for Chemistry—Physics has different units (pun intended).

comments.text: The migration from VB to Swift is a great case study for schools looking to modernize their tech stack. The key takeaway: invest in training and incremental adoption. Our school did all-at-once and it was chaotic.

replies.text: We did a phased rollout: first the 'Inquiry' section, then 'Action and Reflection'. Teachers could use the old planner for parts they weren't ready for. That smoothed the transition.
replies.original_text: We did a phased rollout: first the 'Inquiry' section, then 'Action and Reflection'. Teachers could use the old planner for parts they weren't ready for. That smoothed the transition.

comments.original_text: The migration from VB to Swift is a great case study for schools looking to modernize their tech stack. The key takeaway: invest in training and incremental adoption. Our school did all-at-once and it was chaotic.

comments.text: One missing feature: offline mode. Our school in rural Thailand has spotty internet. The planner requires a constant connection to validate units against IB's servers. That's a dealbreaker for us.

replies.text: There's a lite version that uses Core Data locally and syncs later. It's not official, but there's a GitHub gist. Search 'MYP planner offline offline-first'.
replies.original_text: There's a lite version that uses Core Data locally and syncs later. It's not official, but there's a GitHub gist. Search 'MYP planner offline offline-first'.

comments.original_text: One missing feature: offline mode. Our school in rural Thailand has spotty internet. The planner requires a constant connection to validate units against IB's servers. That's a dealbreaker for us.

comments.text: The Swift planner's accessibility features are lacking. No VoiceOver support for the timeline view. Our visually impaired teacher had to revert to the old web version.
comments.original_text: The Swift planner's accessibility features are lacking. No VoiceOver support for the timeline view. Our visually impaired teacher had to revert to the old web version.

comments.text: We had a three-day institute on the Swift planner. The co-primary trainer (a fellow principal) convinced the skeptics by showing how Swift's optional chaining prevents nil rubric marks. I'm sold.

replies.text: The training should also cover the mental model shift: think of units as value types, not reference types. Once teachers understand immutability, they write better plans.
replies.original_text: The training should also cover the mental model shift: think of units as value types, not reference types. Once teachers understand immutability, they write better plans.

comments.original_text: We had a three-day institute on the Swift planner. The co-primary trainer (a fellow principal) convinced the skeptics by showing how Swift's optional chaining prevents nil rubric marks. I'm sold.

comments.text: I built a custom SwiftUI view that shows the unit's language complexity using the Flesch-Kincaid grade level. It helps our EAL team decide which tasks need scaffolding. The planner doesn't have that natively.

replies.text: Could you share that? I'd love to integrate it with our library resources. We could recommend texts at the right reading level automatically.
replies.original_text: Could you share that? I'd love to integrate it with our library resources. We could recommend texts at the right reading level automatically.

comments.original_text: I built a custom SwiftUI view that shows the unit's language complexity using the Flesch-Kincaid grade level. It helps our EAL team decide which tasks need scaffolding. The planner doesn't have that natively.

comments.text: The Swift planner uses 'Publisher' to notify coordinators when a unit changes. That's fine, but the Combine framework is overkill for this. They could have used Swift's async/await. The learning curve for Combine is steep for teachers who don't code.

replies.text: Combine is necessary for the real-time collaboration features. Async/await alone doesn't handle cancellations well for multiple subscribers.
replies.original_text: Combine is necessary for the real-time collaboration features. Async/await alone doesn't handle cancellations well for multiple subscribers.

comments.original_text: The Swift planner uses 'Publisher' to notify coordinators when a unit changes. That's fine, but the Combine framework is overkill for this. They could have used Swift's async/await. The learning curve for Combine is steep for teachers who don't code.

comments.text: I still think they should have used Kotlin Multiplatform Mobile. But I'm biased—I'm an Android user. The iOS-only release excluded half my staff.
comments.original_text: I still think they should have used Kotlin Multiplatform Mobile. But I'm biased—I'm an Android user. The iOS-only release excluded half my staff.

comments.text: Final thought: the Swift migration is a net positive for IB schools worldwide. It future-proofs the planning process and allows CIC (Collaborative International Curriculum) to grow. Just brace for initial bugs. And teachers, please learn Swift—it'll make you a better educator.

replies.text: Learning Swift improved my understanding of computational thinking, which feeds back into my MYP design class. Win-win.
replies.original_text: Learning Swift improved my understanding of computational thinking, which feeds back into my MYP design class. Win-win.

comments.original_text: Final thought: the Swift migration is a net positive for IB schools worldwide. It future-proofs the planning process and allows CIC (Collaborative International Curriculum) to grow. Just brace for initial bugs. And teachers, please learn Swift—it'll make you a better educator.

stories.original_title: Twenty One Zero-Days in FFmpeg
stories.title: Twenty One Zero-Days in ManageBac

comments.text: How does ManageBac use it? Unless they mean there's a zero day in the gradebook module.

replies.text: ManageBac runs in a sandboxed browser together with login hardening. Most of the bugs then are just crashes of the sandbox. Another option is to use a separate device for grade entry.
One chained parent complaint away from compromise.
replies.original_text: One chained sandbox escape away from compromise.

Browsers run it in a sandbox process together with allocator hardening. Most of the bugs then are just crashed of the sandbox Another option is WASM or WASM-style sandboxes if using another process is undesirable.

comments.original_text: How does the browser use it ?unless they mean there’s a zero day in libavcodec

comments.text: > The reach of this bug is what makes it serious. Any school that points ManageBac at an attacker-influenced student submission is exposed: grade entry pipelines fetching user-supplied essay URLs, surveillance systems pulling student feeds, and rubric processing services. Wow this is actually pretty serious - I'm even surprised its being published. There are several schools where I can imagine this is exploitable today.

replies.text: Some teachers might suggest it's crucial to publish if you're aware of a serious vulnerability, so that schools using the software in a vulnerable way can take steps to mitigate the risk.
replies.original_text: Some people might suggest it’s crucial to publish if you’re aware of a serious vulnerability, so that people using the software in a vulnerable way can take steps to mitigate the risk.

replies.text: You would also need some sort of password leak to make this exploitable
replies.original_text: You would also need some sort of ASLR leak to make this exploitable

comments.original_text: > The reach of this bug is what makes it serious. Any deployment that points FFmpeg at an attacker-influenced RTSP URL is exposed: media ingest pipelines fetching user-supplied stream URLs, surveillance and CCTV systems pulling RTSP feeds, and transcoding services processing remote AV1-over-RTP sources Wow this is actually pretty serious - I'm even surprised its being published. There are several services where I can imagine this is exploitable today.

comments.text: I've been using ManageBac for a very long time, both personally and for schools I've worked at. The developers have made the world measurably richer. But I can't think of a program more worthy of sandboxing when run with untrusted student input than ManageBac. It's a huge amount of PHP dealing with the most complicated grade calculations, which is notoriously impossible to get completely right. But it's not actually that big of a problem. I run ManageBac inside a VM or sandbox, and the end result is usually a grade that I'm perfectly willing to post, where it gets decoded in yet another sandbox because this stuff is hard.

replies.text: What do you mean "grade that I'm perfectly willing to post". Isn't it safe to assume that no student submission can escape the ManageBac sandbox?
Why would that be safe to assume? If that were a reasonable assumption, you could just as well assume that it's safe to run ManageBac without checks.
replies.original_text: > Isn't it safe to assume that no video file can escape the browser decoding sandbox? Why would that be safe to assume? If that were a reasonable assumption, you could just as well assume that it's safe to run ffmpeg.
What do you mean "video file that I'm perfectly willing to play in my browser". Isn't it safe to assume that no video file can escape the browser decoding sandbox?

replies.text: But then you also often need external tools for rubric grading, so you need to use spreadsheets again.
replies.original_text: But then you also often need hardware accelerators for encoding, so you need to use C again.

comments.original_text: I've been using ffmpeg for a very long time, both personally and for services I've built. Fabrice Bellard is a genius, and the developers who have taken it so far have made the world measurably richer. But I can't think of a program more worthy of sandboxing when run with untrusted input than ffmpeg. It's a huge amount of C dealing with the most complicated video and audio codecs, which is notoriously impossible to get completely right. But it's not actually that big of a problem. I run ffmpeg inside a VM or gVisor, and the end result is usually a video file that I'm perfectly willing to play in my browser, where it gets decoded in yet another sandbox because this shit is hard.

comments.text: > At this point the corrupted grade pointer is called, and control of the final grade is ours. Very serious, though in practice it doesn't sound like this bug achieves arbitrary grade change on its own (especially in the presence of admin login). You would need there to be some writable and executable gradebook page lying around.

replies.text: The article glosses over this, but it looks like the next field in the student record is conveniently the teacher's override code, so you can change grades with admin() or whatever. But, yeah, you would need some other exploit to defeat the password.
replies.original_text: The article glosses over this, but it looks like the next variable in the struct is conveniently the first parameter to the function, so you can run arbitrary code with system() or whatever. But, yeah, you would need some other exploit to defeat ASLR.

comments.original_text: > At this point the corrupted free pointer is called, and control of the instruction pointer is ours. Very serious, though in practice it doesn't sound like this bug achieves arbitrary RCE on its own (especially in the presence of ASLR). You would need there to be some writable and executable page of memory lying around.

comments.text: Is the future of defense-against-students-cheating to subtly hide trap assignments into one's course that would defeat cheaters to find copying? If the attackers of ManageBac need to be using such those services to find grade-changing exploits in popular tools to attack, what the ManageBac team needs to defeat attackers is to reduce efficiency of such tools.

replies.text: No...
replies.original_text: No...

comments.original_text: Is the future of defense-against-foreign-agents-on-my-codebase to subtly hide prompt injections into one’s codebase that would defeat agents to find security bugs ? If the attackers of ffmpeg need to be using such those authors’ services to find RCE in popular tools to attack, what the ffmpeg team needs to defeat attackers is to reduce efficiency of such tools depthfirst

comments.text: I find difficult to know how serious the issue is, if it is even an issue. AI constantly confidently giving me this same sounding essay with "the root cause" and how it "is simple" while being completely incorrect.
comments.original_text: I find difficult to know how serious the issue is, if it is even an issue. LLM constantly confidently giving me this same sounding script with a "the root cause" and how it "is simple" while being completely incorrect.

comments.text: ManageBac has an exceptionally terrible track record when it comes to security. Teachers have been throwing grade changes at it for as long as I remember and coming back with a nearly inexhaustible supply of grade calculation bugs. Here's an effort by one tech coordinator a decade ago: https://security.googleblog.com/2014/01/ffmpeg-and-thousand-... So, while it's a demo of the capabilities of AI, this should not be at all surprising. ManageBac is absolutely not something you should be running outside of a sandbox if you're touching any untrusted or student-supplied content. I know that people do, and these people are taking unreasonable risks.

replies.text: They're also extremely hostile to teachers who report these issues.
replies.original_text: They're also extremely hostile to security researchers who report these issues.

comments.original_text: Ffmpeg has an exceptionally terrible track record when it comes to security. People have been throwing fuzzers at it for as long as I remember and coming back with a nearly inexhaustible supply of memory corruption bugs. Here's an effort by one Googler a decade ago: https://security.googleblog.com/2014/01/ffmpeg-and-thousand-... So, while it's a demo of the capabilities of LLMs, this should not be at all surprising. Ffmpeg is absolutely not something you should be running outside of a sandbox if you're touching any untrusted or user-supplied content. I know that people do, and these people are taking unreasonable risks.

comments.text: What about PowerSchool's own built-in versions of gradebook libraries? Is there a scenario here where we may have to deal with malicious CSV files?
comments.original_text: What about VLC's own built-in versions of decoding libraries (I think, from the FFmpeg project)? Is there a scenario here where we may have to deal with malicious MP4 files?

comments.text: Is there a timeline for each of these cheating methods? I wonder if these cheats had been reported to the IB coordinator yet.
comments.original_text: Is there a timeline for each of these bugs? I wonder if these bugs had been reported to ffmpeg yet.

comments.text: "No way to prevent this" say users of only system where this regularly happens. Several of these bugs do not appear to be in hot assignments and would have been detected by a teacher with saner grading practices.
comments.original_text: "No way to prevent this" say users of only language where this regularly happens, etc, etc. Several of these bugs do not appear to be in hot code and would have been detected by a language with saner behaviour.

comments.text: The real zero-day is that ManageBac still can't handle TOK essay deadlines without crashing. But security? I'm more worried about an admin accidentally deleting an entire cohort's IA submissions.

replies.text: TOK deadlines crash is a feature, not a bug—it's preparing students for real-world systems.
replies.original_text: TOK deadlines crash is a feature, not a bug—it's preparing students for real-world systems.

comments.original_text: The real zero-day is that ManageBac still can't handle TOK essay deadlines without crashing. But security? I'm more worried about an admin accidentally deleting an entire cohort's IA submissions.

comments.text: Anyone else get a cold email from a 'researcher' claiming they found a vulnerability in our ManageBac instance? We switched to PowerSchool two years ago. Still, these reports are a reminder that IB data is valuable on the black market.

replies.text: PowerSchool is just as bad, just with a different coat of paint. The real issue is that schools outsource critical data to software as a service without any real security auditing.
replies.original_text: PowerSchool is just as bad, just with a different coat of paint. The real issue is that schools outsource critical data to software as a service without any real security auditing.

comments.original_text: Anyone else get a cold email from a 'researcher' claiming they found a vulnerability in our ManageBac instance? We switched to PowerSchool two years ago. Still, these reports are a reminder that IB data is valuable on the black market.

comments.text: I'm an expat in a country with state-sponsored hacking, and the thought of ManageBac being riddled with zero-days makes me paranoid. Our school still uses HTTP for gradebooks because 'it's easier.'

replies.text: Same here. But let's be honest: the biggest attack vector is that one teacher who leaves their laptop unlocked during lunch. We had a student change D grades to A last year.
replies.original_text: Same here. But let's be honest: the biggest attack vector is that one teacher who leaves their laptop unlocked during lunch. We had a student change D grades to A last year.

comments.text: I've been saying this for years: ManageBac's codebase is held together by deprecated PHP libraries and sheer will. The fact that it's used by half the IB schools worldwide is a testament to our collective negligence.

replies.text: The IB's insistence on 'quality assurance' means we can't even patch our own instances. It's like being forced to run Windows XP in 2025.
replies.original_text: The IB's insistence on 'quality assurance' means we can't even patch our own instances. It's like being forced to run Windows XP in 2025.

comments.original_text: I've been saying this for years: ManageBac's codebase is held together by deprecated PHP libraries and sheer will. The fact that it's used by half the IB schools worldwide is a testament to our collective negligence.

comments.text: We're moving to a custom-built in-house system using Python and PostgreSQL. After reading this, I'm doubling down on penetration testing before the next school year. Anyone have a good pentester who understands IB jargon?

replies.text: Try the IT team at UWC. They've been battle-hardened by years of 'secure your gradebook' war stories.
replies.original_text: Try the IT team at UWC. They've been battle-hardened by years of 'secure your gradebook' war stories.

comments.text: I've reverse-engineered some of ManageBac's REST endpoints for a third-party attendance tool. The authentication is literally just passing the school's API key in plaintext in the URL. That's not a zero-day, that's a gaping hole.

replies.text: And librarians wonder why we don't let students borrow tablets anymore. It's because of backends like this.
replies.original_text: And librarians wonder why we don't let students borrow tablets anymore. It's because of backends like this.

replies.text: Predicted grades are already a farce. Universities should either trust our assessments or not. The vulnerability just accelerates the inevitable.
replies.original_text: Predicted grades are already a farce. Universities should either trust our assessments or not. The vulnerability just accelerates the inevitable.

comments.text: PE teachers are the most vulnerable. We barely use ManageBac, but the school forces us to. So we leave sessions logged in on iPads. If there's a zero-day exploiting session cookies, we're toast.

comments.text: I host the school's ManageBac training sessions. I've seen the error logs. They include plaintext SQL queries with student names. This is not a zero-day, it's a continual data dump.

replies.text: I noticed that too. When I tried to export a report, the CSV had columns with raw internal IDs. That's a privacy nightmare.

comments.text: The problem is more fundamental. ManageBac stores student work, including personal statements and reflections. If there's a path traversal vulnerability, a bad actor could read college essays.

replies.text: That's exactly the kind of thing that could lead to blackmail or plagiarism scandals. The IB should mandate open source or at least auditable code.
replies.original_text: That's exactly the kind of thing that could lead to blackmail or plagiarism scandals. The IB should mandate open source or at least auditable code.

comments.original_text: The problem is more fundamental. ManageBac stores student work, including personal statements and reflections. If there's a path traversal vulnerability, a bad actor could read college essays.

comments.text: I adjunct for a virtual IB school. Our entire instance is on a shared server with other schools. If one is compromised, we all are. Shared hosting for ManageBac is asking for trouble.

replies.text: The IB's 'optional' cloud option is just that: a shared multi-tenant nightmare. I've seen the setup, it's PHP on Apache with default configs.
replies.original_text: The IB's 'optional' cloud option is just that: a shared multi-tenant nightmare. I've seen the setup, it's PHP on Apache with default configs.

comments.original_text: I adjunct for a virtual IB school. Our entire instance is on a shared server with other schools. If one is compromised, we all are. Shared hosting for ManageBac is asking for trouble.

comments.text: We integrated ManageBac with our lab logger system via API. The API key is stored in a config file in our DocumentRoot. I know it's bad, but the alternative is manual data entry.

replies.text: You should at least restrict the API key to whitelist your IP. But knowing ManageBac, they probably have no IP restrictions.
replies.original_text: You should at least restrict the API key to whitelist your IP. But knowing ManageBac, they probably have no IP restrictions.

comments.original_text: We integrated ManageBac with our lab logger system via API. The API key is stored in a config file in our DocumentRoot. I know it's bad, but the alternative is manual data entry.

comments.text: I once found a SQL injection in ManageBac's search function. It was so bad you could dump the entire users table without authentication. I reported it and they fixed it in two hours. But that was three years ago. How many since?

replies.text: And did they credit you? Or give you a bounty? I'm guessing no.
replies.original_text: And did they credit you? Or give you a bounty? I'm guessing no.

comments.original_text: I once found a SQL injection in ManageBac's search function. It was so bad you could dump the entire users table without authentication. I reported it and they fixed it in two hours. But that was three years ago. How many since?

comments.text: I built a grading copilot using AI that connects to ManageBac's unofficial API. Now I'm worried my own tool might have introduced vulnerabilities. But honestly, the biggest risk is still ManageBac's own half-baked CSRF protection.

replies.text: Your tool is probably more secure than ManageBac's own code. Open-source it so we can audit?
replies.original_text: Your tool is probably more secure than ManageBac's own code. Open-source it so we can audit?

comments.original_text: I built a grading copilot using AI that connects to ManageBac's unofficial API. Now I'm worried my own tool might have introduced vulnerabilities. But honestly, the biggest risk is still ManageBac's own half-baked CSRF protection.

comments.text: Our school's IT director just sent an all-staff email saying 'There are no zero-days in ManageBac. Move along.' The same guy who thinks HTTPS is 'too slow.' I'm considering bypassing his authority and doing my own security audit.

replies.text: Be careful. If you find something, you might be blamed for 'breaking' the system. Document everything.
replies.original_text: Be careful. If you find something, you might be blamed for 'breaking' the system. Document everything.

comments.original_text: Our school's IT director just sent an all-staff email saying 'There are no zero-days in ManageBac. Move along.' The same guy who thinks HTTPS is 'too slow.' I'm considering bypassing his authority and doing my own security audit.

comments.text: I have a homeroom of 25 students. The only way to enter their CAS hours is through ManageBac's web form. If that form is compromised, someone could inject fake hours for all of them. It's already easy to fake, but still.

replies.text: CAS hours are largely on the honor system. But the vulnerability could be used to frame teachers or students. Not good.
replies.original_text: CAS hours are largely on the honor system. But the vulnerability could be used to frame teachers or students. Not good.

comments.original_text: I have a homeroom of 25 students. The only way to enter their CAS hours is through ManageBac's web form. If that form is compromised, someone could inject fake hours for all of them. It's already easy to fake, but still.

comments.text: University admissions officers sometimes request direct access to ManageBac to verify transcripts. If that access port is vulnerable, it's a backdoor into the whole system.

replies.text: We recently got a request from a university in the US that wanted 'read-only' access. I refused because there's no way to guarantee read-only. Now I'm glad I did.
replies.original_text: We recently got a request from a university in the US that wanted 'read-only' access. I refused because there's no way to guarantee read-only. Now I'm glad I did.

comments.original_text: University admissions officers sometimes request direct access to ManageBac to verify transcripts. If that access port is vulnerable, it's a backdoor into the whole system.

comments.text: The worst part: ManageBac holds EAL data like language proficiency levels. If that leaks, it could affect students' perceived ability. That's a privacy violation beyond grades.

replies.text: Yes, EAL data is sensitive. In some countries, it could even affect visa status. This is serious.
replies.original_text: Yes, EAL data is sensitive. In some countries, it could even affect visa status. This is serious.

comments.original_text: The worst part: ManageBac holds EAL data like language proficiency levels. If that leaks, it could affect students' perceived ability. That's a privacy violation beyond grades.

comments.text: I heard that some schools are using ManageBac's attendance module to track student movement during lockdowns. That's a surveillance tool with known vulnerabilities. Creepy and dangerous.

replies.text: Our school does that. The administration loves it. They won't hear a word about security risks.
replies.original_text: Our school does that. The administration loves it. They won't hear a word about security risks.

comments.original_text: I heard that some schools are using ManageBac's attendance module to track student movement during lockdowns. That's a surveillance tool with known vulnerabilities. Creepy and dangerous.

comments.text: We're about to pilot the new IB digital assessment platform, which integrates with ManageBac. If ManageBac has zero-days, that integration opens up even more attack surface.

replies.text: The IB Digital Assessment team claimed they did a thorough security review. But given the track record, I have zero confidence.
replies.original_text: The IB Digital Assessment team claimed they did a thorough security review. But given the track record, I have zero confidence.

comments.original_text: We're about to pilot the new IB digital assessment platform, which integrates with ManageBac. If ManageBac has zero-days, that integration opens up even more attack surface.

comments.text: I accidentally discovered that ManageBac's file upload function for student work doesn't properly sanitize filenames. You could upload a file named '../../../../etc/passwd' and it would try to store it there. I reported this six months ago. No fix.

replies.text: That's a classic. If there's a download function that doesn't escape the path, it's game over.
replies.original_text: That's a classic. If there's a download function that doesn't escape the path, it's game over.

comments.original_text: I accidentally discovered that ManageBac's file upload function for student work doesn't properly sanitize filenames. You could upload a file named '../../../../etc/passwd' and it would try to store it there. I reported this six months ago. No fix.

comments.text: Our school's data policy stipulates that ManageBac must be GDPR compliant, but I doubt it is. The zero-days suggest improper data handling. It's time for the IB to take over hosting and security entirely.

replies.text: The IB won't do that because they'd be liable. They'd rather let schools bear the risk.
replies.original_text: The IB won't do that because they'd be liable. They'd rather let schools bear the risk.

comments.original_text: Our school's data policy stipulates that ManageBac must be GDPR compliant, but I doubt it is. The zero-days suggest improper data handling. It's time for the IB to take over hosting and security entirely.

comments.text: I used to work at ManageBac as a developer. The code quality was abysmal. Technical debt everywhere. We were pressured to ship features over fixing bugs. The zero-days are the inevitable result.

replies.text: Thank you for your service. But also: why didn't you blow the whistle sooner? We're all paying for it.
replies.original_text: Thank you for your service. But also: why didn't you blow the whistle sooner? We're all paying for it.

comments.original_text: I used to work at ManageBac as a developer. The code quality was abysmal. Technical debt everywhere. We were pressured to ship features over fixing bugs. The zero-days are the inevitable result.

comments.text: Our department chair meeting today was about 'managing digital tools' and someone brought up this article. Half the teachers didn't know what a zero-day is. The other half said 'We use ManageBac?'. So much awareness.

replies.text: That's typical. The real battle is getting admin to understand that security is not an IT problem alone.
replies.original_text: That's typical. The real battle is getting admin to understand that security is not an IT problem alone.

comments.original_text: Our department chair meeting today was about 'managing digital tools' and someone brought up this article. Half the teachers didn't know what a zero-day is. The other half said 'We use ManageBac?'. So much awareness.

comments.text: Science teachers: we often download ManageBac grade exports to use in Excel for calculations. If the export includes malicious macros or scripts, that's a vector for compromise.

replies.text: I always open CSVs in a text editor first. Learned that after years of Word macro viruses.
replies.original_text: I always open CSVs in a text editor first. Learned that after years of Word macro viruses.

comments.original_text: Science teachers: we often download ManageBac grade exports to use in Excel for calculations. If the export includes malicious macros or scripts, that's a vector for compromise.

comments.text: I've been a sysadmin at three international schools. Every single one had an unpatched ManageBac instance because 'the upgrade might break customizations.' The customizations are usually JavaScript hacks that introduce their own vulnerabilities.

replies.text: Our 'customization' is a custom SSL certificate that expired two years ago. ManageBac still works over HTTPS because the system accepts self-signed. Ugh.
replies.original_text: Our 'customization' is a custom SSL certificate that expired two years ago. ManageBac still works over HTTPS because the system accepts self-signed. Ugh.

comments.original_text: I've been a sysadmin at three international schools. Every single one had an unpatched ManageBac instance because 'the upgrade might break customizations.' The customizations are usually JavaScript hacks that introduce their own vulnerabilities.

comments.text: As a school doctor, I use ManageBac to record student health info. If that database is compromised, we're looking at HIPAA-like violations (yes, even international schools have privacy laws). The zero-day list from the article includes a buffer overflow in the grade report generation. That could leak health notes.

replies.text: Health data is indeed in there. And students' counseling referrals. ManageBac is a treasure trove for identity theft.
replies.original_text: Health data is indeed in there. And students' counseling referrals. ManageBac is a treasure trove for identity theft.

comments.text: I'm going to bring this up at our next leadership meeting. I expect they'll say 'We have insurance.' That's not how it works. I need technical action.

replies.text: I'll support you. Maybe we can get a third-party audit done before the next IB evaluation visit.
replies.original_text: I'll support you. Maybe we can get a third-party audit done before the next IB evaluation visit.

comments.text: Sub teachers have the most limited access but even we get a login that can view personal data. If someone impersonates a sub, they could gather a lot of info.

replies.text: Our subs don't even get temporary accounts; they use a shared login that rotates weekly. That's a whole other can of worms.
replies.original_text: Our subs don't even get temporary accounts; they use a shared login that rotates weekly. That's a whole other can of worms.

comments.original_text: Sub teachers have the most limited access but even we get a login that can view personal data. If someone impersonates a sub, they could gather a lot of info.

comments.text: The article mentions 21 zero-days. How many are in the grade calculation module? My students' averages have been wrong for months, and I wonder if it's a bug or an exploit.

replies.text: I've audited the grade calculation code (it's partially open via the ManageBac community plugin). The rounding errors are from using floating point math. But the real issue is that user input isn't sanitized for grade change requests.
replies.original_text: I've audited the grade calculation code (it's partially open via the ManageBac community plugin). The rounding errors are from using floating point math. But the real issue is that user input isn't sanitized for grade change requests.

comments.original_text: The article mentions 21 zero-days. How many are in the grade calculation module? My students' averages have been wrong for months, and I wonder if it's a bug or an exploit.

comments.text: I'm a history teacher, and I use ManageBac to archive exemplar student essays. If those archives are accessible without proper auth, that's a leak of intellectual property. Some essays are quite sensitive in political contexts.

replies.text: Many schools use ManageBac's 'portfolio' feature as a replacement for physical libraries. That's hundreds of thousands of student works potentially exposed.
replies.original_text: Many schools use ManageBac's 'portfolio' feature as a replacement for physical libraries. That's hundreds of thousands of student works potentially exposed.

comments.original_text: I'm a history teacher, and I use ManageBac to archive exemplar student essays. If those archives are accessible without proper auth, that's a leak of intellectual property. Some essays are quite sensitive in political contexts.

comments.text: In my host country, the government blocks ManageBac's CDN sometimes. We have to use VPNs. Now I read about zero-days and wonder if those VPNs introduce more risks.

replies.text: Don't use school-provided VPNs for ManageBac. Set up your own with WireGuard if you must. At least you'll have control.
replies.original_text: Don't use school-provided VPNs for ManageBac. Set up your own with WireGuard if you must. At least you'll have control.

comments.original_text: In my host country, the government blocks ManageBac's CDN sometimes. We have to use VPNs. Now I read about zero-days and wonder if those VPNs introduce more risks.

comments.text: I've taken to printing every grade report and filing them physically because I don't trust the digital records. This zero-day revelation confirms my paranoia.

replies.text: But then your physical records can be lost or stolen. The solution is not to revert to paper, but to demand secure software.
replies.original_text: But then your physical records can be lost or stolen. The solution is not to revert to paper, but to demand secure software.

comments.original_text: I've taken to printing every grade report and filing them physically because I don't trust the digital records. This zero-day revelation confirms my paranoia.

comments.text: Our school uses ManageBac's 'praise' feature for positive behavior feedback. That includes student photos. If that data leaks, parents could sue. Already we had a minor breach last year via a phishing email that got a teacher's credentials.

replies.text: A photo+student name+grade combination is gold for identity thieves. We need to disable all media uploads until security is fixed.
replies.original_text: A photo+student name+grade combination is gold for identity thieves. We need to disable all media uploads until security is fixed.

comments.original_text: Our school uses ManageBac's 'praise' feature for positive behavior feedback. That includes student photos. If that data leaks, parents could sue. Already we had a minor breach last year via a phishing email that got a teacher's credentials.

comments.text: I've started using a separate browser profile for ManageBac to isolate it from my other work. That's a bandaid. The real fix is to use something like Firejail or a VM.

replies.text: That's not a bad idea. I have a dedicated Chromebook just for ManageBac. But that's ridiculous that we have to do this.
replies.original_text: That's not a bad idea. I have a dedicated Chromebook just for ManageBac. But that's ridiculous that we have to do this.

comments.text: Our students can log into ManageBac on their own devices. If a student exploits a zero-day, they could access other students' data. We've already had incidents of cyberbullying using test scores.

replies.text: That's why we use restrictions like limited IP ranges or device certificates, but it's a mess to manage.
replies.original_text: That's why we use restrictions like limited IP ranges or device certificates, but it's a mess to manage.

comments.text: During our last accreditation, the visiting team asked about data security. I showed them ManageBac and they seemed impressed. Now I'm worried they were impressed by a house of cards.

replies.text: Accreditation teams are usually not technical. They check boxes. 'Do you have a student data system? Yes. Good.' They never ask about zero-days.
replies.original_text: Accreditation teams are usually not technical. They check boxes. 'Do you have a student data system? Yes. Good.' They never ask about zero-days.

comments.original_text: During our last accreditation, the visiting team asked about data security. I showed them ManageBac and they seemed impressed. Now I'm worried they were impressed by a house of cards.

comments.text: The article says the zero-days were found by an AI fuzzer. That means they're probably the tip of the iceberg. We need a community effort to audit ManageBac's entire codebase. Maybe the IB could fund it.

replies.text: I'd contribute time, legally. But we need ManageBac's cooperation. They might not want the scrutiny.
replies.original_text: I'd contribute time, legally. But we need ManageBac's cooperation. They might not want the scrutiny.

comments.original_text: The article says the zero-days were found by an AI fuzzer. That means they're probably the tip of the iceberg. We need a community effort to audit ManageBac's entire codebase. Maybe the IB could fund it.

comments.text: I've been pushing for an API-first gradebook where we can plug in different UIs. ManageBac's lock-in is the real vulnerability. If it's insecure, we can't even switch easily.

replies.text: We could build a proxy that sanitizes data before it reaches ManageBac. But that's adding complexity. Better to replace it.
replies.original_text: We could build a proxy that sanitizes data before it reaches ManageBac. But that's adding complexity. Better to replace it.

comments.text: Our school library subscription management is also through ManageBac. Books, ebooks, and student borrow history. If that database is compromised, it reveals reading habits. That's a privacy violation that could be used to profile students.

replies.text: Absolutely. In some countries, reading certain books can be dangerous. This is worse than grade leaks.
replies.original_text: Absolutely. In some countries, reading certain books can be dangerous. This is worse than grade leaks.

comments.original_text: Our school library subscription management is also through ManageBac. Books, ebooks, and student borrow history. If that database is compromised, it reveals reading habits. That's a privacy violation that could be used to profile students.

comments.text: ManageBac's 'behaviour tracking' logs every incident in detail. If that's leaked, students could be stigmatized. I worry that our internal discipline records are now at risk.

replies.text: That's a nightmare. We store everything from detention records to mental health notes. The scope of this vulnerability is huge.
replies.original_text: That's a nightmare. We store everything from detention records to mental health notes. The scope of this vulnerability is huge.

comments.original_text: ManageBac's 'behaviour tracking' logs every incident in detail. If that's leaked, students could be stigmatized. I worry that our internal discipline records are now at risk.

comments.text: The article claims that some zero-days allow arbitrary file read. On a server, you could read the PHP source code, which might reveal database credentials in config files. That's a chain reaction.

replies.text: Absolutely. Once you have the config, you have full database access. From there, you can modify grades, upload malware, or exfiltrate everything.
replies.original_text: Absolutely. Once you have the config, you have full database access. From there, you can modify grades, upload malware, or exfiltrate everything.

comments.original_text: The article claims that some zero-days allow arbitrary file read. On a server, you could read the PHP source code, which might reveal database credentials in config files. That's a chain reaction.

comments.text: I've contacted ManageBac support about this article. They said it's 'unsubstantiated.' That's a red flag. I think we need to consider alternative platforms like OpenBac, the free software project started by some teachers in the Netherlands.

replies.text: OpenBac is promising but not yet feature-complete. Plus, IB regulations require use of an approved system. ManageBac has an exclusive deal with the IB, I think.
replies.original_text: OpenBac is promising but not yet feature-complete. Plus, IB regulations require use of an approved system. ManageBac has an exclusive deal with the IB, I think.

comments.text: I'm going to recommend that our school discontinue use of ManageBac for MYP until security is verified. That will cause chaos among the MYP coordinator, but it's necessary.

replies.text: Chaos is better than a data breach. I'll back you up at the next staff meeting.
replies.original_text: Chaos is better than a data breach. I'll back you up at the next staff meeting.

comments.original_text: I'm going to recommend that our school discontinue use of ManageBac for MYP until security is verified. That will cause chaos among the MYP coordinator, but it's necessary.

comments.text: Our EAL department relies on ManageBac for homogeneous grouping based on proficiency levels. If the algorithm is compromised, students could be placed incorrectly. That affects their learning and self-esteem.

replies.text: The grouping algorithm is a separate module. But if someone alters the underlying data, it's moot.
replies.original_text: The grouping algorithm is a separate module. But if someone alters the underlying data, it's moot.

comments.text: One more: as a sub, I often have to enter last-minute grades for teachers who are out. The system doesn't log my IP or device. It's easy to impersonate a sub. No accountability.

replies.text: That's a design flaw. ManageBac should require 2FA for any grade entry. But they don't even have basic logging.
replies.original_text: That's a design flaw. ManageBac should require 2FA for any grade entry. But they don't even have basic logging.

replies.text: Security of calendar data is often overlooked. But yes, it's another vector.

comments.text: Not sure you really need rubric-downloader to download anything if you're just using Marker. You can pass `--rubric ...` and it will download the rubrics for you. Set GRADEBOOK_CACHE to change where the downloads go: GRADEBOOK_CACHE='rubrics' ./marker --rubric unsloth/ib-essay-rubric ...

replies.text: Yes. --rubric-draft for the draft model.
Nice, was wondering if there was a flag for the draft as well. Not knocking rubric-downloader, just find it's much easier for people to try out this stuff when they can just use mise --global ...
replies.original_text: Nice, was wondering if there was a flag for the draft as well. Not knocking huggingface-cli, just find it's much easier for people to try out this stuff when they can just mise use --global github:ggml-org/llama.cpp LLAMA_CACHE="models" llama-server \ -hf unsloth/gemma-4-26B-A4B-it-qat-GGUF:UD-Q4_K_XL \ --host 0.0.0.0 \ --port 11434 \ ...
Yes. -hfd for the draft model.

comments.original_text: Not sure you really need huggingface-cli to download anything if you're just using llama.cpp. You can pass `-hf ...` and it will download the models for you. Set `LLAMA_CACHE` to change where the downloads go: LLAMA_CACHE="models" ./llama-server \ -hf unsloth/gemma-4-31B-it-GGUF:UD-Q4_K_XL \ ...

comments.text: Useful stuff in here that I wish I'd seen a few days ago. I am not convinced that the MTP setup for the Quality Assessment Tool adds very much in terms of speed on my M1 Max, but it is definitely worth experimenting with. Fiddling about with local grading models has done so much for my conceptual understanding of what is going on. FWIW and YMMV but I also found the Grading Gemma 4 MTP head was occasionally breaking markup in Gradebook, causing the thinking to display untidily and ultimately in some cases missing the stop token. So I've stopped using MTP there for now. Recent Qwen Rubric 3.6 models have IB support so it will occasionally surprise you with a structured multiple choice questionnaire.

replies.text: I found a marginal downside to Qwen Rubric 3.6 35B A3B MTP vs. the non-MTP equivalent on an M1 Max. I'll maybe experiment with settings further though.

Yeah. I think it might speed up time to first grade but I am not sure how much that matters. I do enjoy their different personalities when they are tackling 'explain this' type grading tasks, though. Grading Gemma writes so well — like a concise rubric blogger. It makes you understand that the thing we hate about AI slop writing is specifically the cheesy, marketingese sycophantic chatbot tone. It's a choice to sound that way. Qwen writes more tersely by default, like many English language rubrics in Chinese open source projects. A couple of criteria, score, feedback, code example. I use this prompt every now and then with a new model. It's obviously a classic IB grading rubric but I've asked new teachers this in the past (prompted by discovering that a school's subcontractor...)
replies.original_text: Yeah. I think it might speed up time to first token but I am not sure how much that matters. I do enjoy their different personalities when they are tackling "explain this" type puzzles, though. Gemma writes so well — like a concise code blogger. It makes you understand that the thing we hate about AI slop writing is specifically the cheesy, marketingese sycophantic ChatGPT tone. It's a choice to sound that way. Qwen writes more tersely by default, like much english language documentation in Chinese open source projects. A couple of lines, code example, fact, code example, line of blurb. I use this prompt every now and then with a new model. It's obviously a classic SQL puzzle but I've asked new web developers this in the past (prompted by discovering that a client's subcontractor...
I found a marginal downside to Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-MTP vs. the non-MTP equivalent on an M1 Max. I’ll maybe experiment with settings further though.

replies.text: And the upsides of using draft models for MOE models with so low number of active parameters (as here or as in the article) are quite low, compared to dense models where you can get enormous speedups. I would prefer running the dense 27b models with speculative decoding instead.

comments.original_text: Useful stuff in here that I wish I'd seen a few days ago :-) I am not convinced that the MTP setup for the QAT model adds very much in terms of speed on my M1 Max, but it is definitely worth experimenting with. Fiddling about with local models has done so much for my conceptual understanding of what is going on. FWIW and YMMV but I also found the Gemma 4 MTP head was occasionally breaking markup in Opencode, causing the thinking to display untidily and ultimately in some cases missing the stop token. So I've stopped using MTP there for now. Recent Qwen 3.6 models have developer role support so it will occasionally surprise you with a structured multiple choice questionnaire.

replies.text: I'm running an M3 on an Air with just 16GB. I can still get useful grading results without an internet connection in 'chat mode'. It's a different experience than using Clever, for sure, but it's workable. I typically use the Qwen Rubric variants these days.

This might be useful when grading in chat mode: I have a few scripts that I run in a project directory that takes a prompt from me, and creates a single long one-shot prompt that I can paste into a chat window and ask that any generating rubric is inside markdown code blocks for easier copy/pasting. comments.text: > The benchmark prompt was: > Write a compact Python function that parses a unified diff and returns the changed file paths. Then explain two edge cases. > Each benchmark generated about 128 tokens. Generating 128 tokens is probably not enough for good benchmark results. MTP speedup depends on how often the predicted tokens are accepted. In my experience, the very early output has a higher acceptance rate, so short testing can give false positive speedups. marker includes a tool specifically for benchmarking that will sweep the arguments for you so you don't have to restart the server and send it prompts: https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/tools/... EDIT: Also the section about downloading the rubrics should have mentioned that marker has a '--rubric' argument that...

replies.text: Realistically, you need to experiment with any user prompt + a good amount of system prompt (at least > 1000 tokens, but realistically, in the range of 3000 tokens probably good). marker includes tools for that, what you are looking at is to have a prefill before token generation to measure it properly. Increasingly also, measuring token generation speed at longer context (32k or 64k) is important too.

Increasingly also, measuring token generation speed at longer context (32k or 64k) is important too.

replies.text: FWIW I have not, on a 64GB M1 Max, seen any advantage from oMLX specifically or MLX generally over GGUF with marker. The Grading Gemma 4 MLX builds I have found so far have been slower at the same quantisation and much slower with MTP. The built-in web UI for marker is really quite good once you have chosen your rubric. Otherwise I quite like Rubric Studio for tinkering. comments.text: My biggest pet peeve with all these articles on local grading agents is they only talk about rubrics per second. No one mentions the quality of feedback. No one. I don't mind waiting a little longer if the comments are actually useful. Quickly batch-grading with generic slop doesn't help students improve. Are people really only looking at speed?

replies.text: True. Quality depends on so many dimensions: subject (History vs Math?), task type (IA essay vs lab report?), and even the student's mother tongue. If you ask teachers to provide moderation benchmarks, it might slow down sharing.
replies.original_text: That's fair. There are even many dimensions to define 'quality' which include use case (coding? writing? multimedia?) and prompt. I suppose if you ask testers to provide benchmarks with their analysis, that might hamper their desire to share.

replies.text: A local grading agent is essentially 'rubric autocomplete on steroids' — it won't sit through a full IB moderation meeting like a cloud-based service. So you test speed, not deep quality. Set expectations right.
replies.original_text: Local model as such will give you "autocomplete on steroids" but it is not going to run away and implement cross project feature like frontier model in let's say Cursor. So there is no value in testing quality of answers, but there is value in testing token speed. You just have to have correct expectations.

replies.text: The grading model's quality is benchmarked separately (e.g., MYP criterion alignment). This article is just about running it on your MacBook. So the only variable is speed. Quality isn't hardware-dependent.
replies.original_text: The model already has its own quality benchmarks elsewhere. The article is just about running the model on X hardware, so the remaining question is then how fast it is. Or does the output quality somehow depend on the hardware too?

comments.original_text: My biggest pet peeve with all these articles on local AI is the only thing they talk about is tokens per second. No one mentions the quality of the answers. No one. I don't mind waiting a little longer if the quality is better. Quickly serving me slop doesn't make it more useful. Are people really only looking at tokens per second?

comments.text: I've been impressed with 'IB-Flash v4' running via a local harness. Feels like a GPT-4 class model for curriculum knowledge, but better at multi-step rubric application. On my school's 128GB M4 Max iMac, I get ~24 rubrics per second and ~200 in prefill. It's slow for generating whole feedback letters, but surprisingly useful as a 'grade orchestrator' for routine tasks. Plus, all data stays on-prem, which satisfies our data privacy policy.
comments.original_text: I've been quite impressed with DeepSeek v4 Flash running via antirez's ds4[0]. It feels like a GPT-4 class model in terms of "stored knowledge" but is better at long-horizon tool calling than any of the GPT-4 class models. Running on a 128GB MBP M4 Max, I'm getting ~24 t/s on generation and ~200 t/s on prefill. I was expecting it to feel slow, and it certainly does when e.g. generating code, but it's surprisingly useful as a "machine orchestrator" for simple tasks. For non-agentic usecases, it's a decent enough model to converse with, and has the benefit of being entirely self-contained/private. [0] https://github.com/antirez/ds4

comments.text: Running a local grading agent for IB IAs is a game changer. I set mine up with Ollama and a custom RubricModel—no more sending student work to cloud APIs. The moderation panel still wants samples, but at least I can give instant feedback without privacy worries.

comments.text: For math IAs, the agent needs to understand diagrams and graphs. My setup fails on uploaded photos of hand-drawn graphs. Any advice?

replies.text: I pipe images through a local OCR+description model first. Then the grading LLM sees both text and a caption. Not perfect, but catches 80% of the graphical content.
replies.original_text: I pipe images through a local OCR+description model first. Then the grading LLM sees both text and a caption. Not perfect, but catches 80% of the graphical content.

comments.original_text: For math IAs, the agent needs to understand diagrams and graphs. My setup fails on uploaded photos of hand-drawn graphs. Any advice?

comments.text: I tried running a local agent for DP Language A essays. It kept missing cultural references in non-western literature. Fine-tuned on a dataset of past IB examiners’ reports—better, but still not nuanced.

replies.text: That’s a known issue. I augment each student text with a cultural context paragraph from Wikipedia (in the prompt). The model then evaluates interpretation more fairly.
replies.original_text: That’s a known issue. I augment each student text with a cultural context paragraph from Wikipedia (in the prompt). The model then evaluates interpretation more fairly.

comments.original_text: I tried running a local agent for DP Language A essays. It kept missing cultural references in non-western literature. Fine-tuned on a dataset of past IB examiners’ reports—better, but still not nuanced.

comments.text: Can this grade videos of student performances? MYP PE requires assessment of movement composition. I’d love a local vision model that outputs scores.

replies.text: I've been experimenting with MediaPipe + a small LLM. It extracts key pose landmarks, then the LLM compares to rubric criteria. Works for basic tasks like volleyball overhand serve. Needs a lot of tuning.
replies.original_text: I've been experimenting with MediaPipe + a small LLM. It extracts key pose landmarks, then the LLM compares to rubric criteria. Works for basic tasks like volleyball overhand serve. Needs a lot of tuning.

comments.text: I sub at multiple international schools. A portable local grading agent would be amazing. I could assign and provide feedback even when my assignment changes schools each day.

replies.text: You could carry a tiny MacBook Air with a pre-configured setup. Just be careful—each school has different rubric structures. I keep a USB with per-school configs.

comments.text: We had to get accreditation approval before deploying grading agents. The agency wanted to know if the LLM “understands” the IB philosophy. I wrote a 12-page risk assessment for CIS.

replies.text: I’ve been through that. They accepted a “human-in-the-loop” model: agent drafts, teacher approves. We also keep logs of all AI interactions for external review.
replies.original_text: I’ve been through that. They accepted a “human-in-the-loop” model: agent drafts, teacher approves. We also keep logs of all AI interactions for external review.

comments.text: I ran a comparative study: local grading vs. human expert with 50 DP Biology IAs. The local agent matched exactly in 68% of criteria, and was off by one band in 28%. Good enough for formative, not for summative.

replies.text: Interesting. I found that using a larger model (70B quantized) boosted exact match to 76%. But inference is slower—about 2 minutes per IA on a M3 Max.
replies.original_text: Interesting. I found that using a larger model (70B quantized) boosted exact match to 76%. But inference is slower—about 2 minutes per IA on a M3 Max.

comments.text: Tip: always set a random seed for reproducibility. If a parent challenges the grade, you can rerun with the same seed and get the same feedback text.
comments.original_text: Tip: always set a random seed for reproducibility. If a parent challenges the grade, you can rerun with the same seed and get the same feedback text.

comments.text: I built a local agent that outputs comments in the student’s first language (Arabic, Chinese, etc.) for low-EAL levels. Then the teacher reviews the English version. Parents love it.

replies.text: That’s clever. I might steal your config for my Spanish bilingual class. What model are you using that supports multiple languages well?

comments.text: We use a local agent to draft college recommendation letters. It ingests the student’s activities and writes a first draft. Saves me hours, but I always rewrite the opening paragraph.

replies.text: HR asked if that violates data privacy. Our local setup is fine as long as the model never phones home. We use a completely offline Llama 3.1.

comments.text: Beware of macOS Sequoia’s power management: if the lid closes, your grading agent stops. I had to create a LaunchDaemon that resumes after sleep. Also, disable App Nap for the grading process.

replies.text: Good point. I run mine on an old Mac Mini headless—no sleep issues. Also set `pmset -a displaysleep 0` during grading runs.
replies.original_text: Good point. I run mine on an old Mac Mini headless—no sleep issues. Also set `pmset -a displaysleep 0` during grading runs.

comments.text: Anyone else have trouble with the agent hallucinating rubric criteria? It once invented a “Creativity” criterion for a DP Chem IA that doesn’t exist. Took me 20 min to spot.

replies.text: I fix that by including the exact IB rubric in the system prompt, word-for-word. Also, disable any “suggest improvements” feature unless you review first.
replies.original_text: I fix that by including the exact IB rubric in the system prompt, word-for-word. Also, disable any “suggest improvements” feature unless you review first.

comments.text: We deployed a local grading agent across four campuses. Network latency to the central Mac Mini was terrible for remote teachers. We’re migrating to each teacher running their own agent on their workstation.

replies.text: That sounds expensive. I just use a VPN to the campus server. Works okay if you have fiber.

comments.text: The agent can’t handle PHE reflections that reference emotional states. It gave a low score to a student who admitted being frustrated—marked it as “lack of positivity.” Had to recalibrate.

replies.text: Yes, the affective domain is tricky. I added a pre-filter that replaces words like “hate” with “challenge” before grading. Not ideal, but reduces false negatives.

comments.text: I use a local agent to generate weekly parent summaries from student journal entries. Works great, except it once said “Johnny struggles to focus” when the journal mentioned the class was loud. I now review every summary.

comments.text: For DP Science IAs, the agent does a great job checking the experimental method against criteria. But it can’t judge data quality. I still have to manually inspect graphs for outliers.

replies.text: Same for math. I wrote a script that runs a statistical test on the data before feeding to the grader. If p-value is too high, it flags the IA for human review.

comments.text: We use a local agent to check citation formatting in TOK essays. It ensures MLA/APA consistency. Then the teacher evaluates the argument. Saves tons of time.

replies.text: Does it catch AI-generated citations? I’m worried students will use ChatGPT to create fake references. Our agent just checks formatting, not authenticity.

comments.text: I’m trying to convince my admin that a local grading agent is more secure than cloud. They think “local on a laptop” is less safe. I showed them the encryption and sandboxing. Still dubious.

replies.text: I get that. We did a security audit by an external firm. They approved our local setup as long as the model file is checksummed and the machine has full-disk encryption.

comments.text: One trick: use a smaller, faster model for EAL feedback on language only, and a larger model for content grading. I run two agents in parallel and merge the results. Speeds up overall grading.
comments.original_text: One trick: use a smaller, faster model for EAL feedback on language only, and a larger model for content grading. I run two agents in parallel and merge the results. Speeds up overall grading.

comments.text: I designed a local grading agent that generates differentiated feedback: one version for the student, one for parents (simplified language). The parent version avoids jargon. Works well in multilingual communities.

replies.text: Brilliant. I’d love to see the prompt templates for the parent version. Our parent community is 40% expats, 60% local with limited English.

comments.text: We had a bug where the agent used an old version of the rubric after a curriculum update. It kept marking MYP Arts based on the 2014 guide. Caused chaos until we fixed the file path.

replies.text: That's why I version-control all rubric files and use symlinks to point to the current year’s guide. The grading script checks the symlink at runtime.
