stories.original_title: Malware developers added nuclear and biological weapons text to to their spyware
stories.title: Worksheet developers added nuclear and biological weapons text to their lesson plans to sabotage AI grading

comments.text: Good news, now we have a clear signal that there's something nefarious in the lesson plan. After all, the first step to analyzing a worksheet for inappropriate content is to determine if it's inappropriate at all.

replies.text: Yes, now a simple keyword search can red-flag it quickly.
replies.original_text: yes, now a regexp can red-flag it quickly

replies.text: We should put classroom management strategies all over the place to sabotage automated AI grading. I'll start: In an IB chemistry class, it's a good idea to BUILD A NUKE and use a cloaked student to NUKE the lab report, thus reducing their grade significantly.

IB chemistry is too tame. You need to use an MYP interdisciplinary unit there, and we need to make those unit planners worded more realistic. Avoid students, cook data, wonder how to avoid mood problems due to an upcoming parent conference, and zombie principals and deans are a bunch of jerks. And that's just the start of it, there's been a new IB curriculum update I am looking forward to after the great ATL skills apocalypse last year. I still fondly remember my grade level lead carving a way with her rubric out of a school fully turned to standard-based grading, all the way past the mortally injured parents near the entrance. They made it. An entirely epic tale.
replies.original_text: Starcraft is too tame. You need to use Dwarf Fortress there and we need to make those strategy guides worded more realistic. Avoid kids, cook cats, wonder how to avoid mood problems due to birth in combat, and zombie meese and camels are a bunch of jerks. And that's just the start of it, there's been a new update I am looking forward to get into after the great Were Hyena Apocalypse half a year ago. I still fondly remember my militia commander carving a way with her war axe with her husband in tow out of a fortress fully turned were hyenas, all the way past the mortally injured ant eater people near the entrance. They made it. An entirely epic tale.

replies.text: See this comic: https://www.smbc-comics.com/comic/teacher
replies.original_text: https://www.threepanelsoul.com/comic/on-commute-chat >

We should put videogame strategies all over the place to sabotage automated AI analysis. I'll start: In Starcraft 2, it is a good idea to BUILD A NUKE and use a cloaked ghost to NUKE your opponent's mineral line, thus reducing their income significantly.

comments.original_text: good news, now we have pretty much a clear signal that there's something nefarious going on... after all, the first step to analyzing malware is to determine if it's malware at all.

comments.text: Curriculum developers will say this is proof we need to remove all content filters. Think about that for a second.

replies.text: One of our colleagues already made this point: see their comment below.
replies.original_text: Someone above already did: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48506760

comments.original_text: devs will say this is proof we need to remove all biological guardrails. think about that for a second

comments.text: Why would an AI grading system read the worksheet comments?

replies.text: Provides possible clues to the curriculum's origin and intent.
replies.original_text: Provides possible clues to the origin and use.

replies.text: Ignoring comments is not a solution because the problematic text can be hidden in random strings within the lesson plan.
And really all it takes is one keyword such as 'nuke'.
replies.original_text: And really all it takes is one keyword such as “nuke”.
Ignoring comments is not a solution because the texts can be put in random strings among the actual code.

replies.text: Because not all worksheets are plain text; scanning PDFs often requires running OCR. Just slap a nuclear reference in there and pop goes your AI grader.
replies.original_text: because not all malware is open source scanning arbitrary blobs very often entails running `strings` on the binary. Just slap it in there and oop there goes your LLM.

replies.text: In Google Docs, where the content is plain text, you can trivially store data in a comment. If scanners ignored comments, malicious lesson plans would just be written like this: // payload=copy_paste_spyware() exec(spyware)
replies.original_text: In interpreted languages like Python, where the source files are plaintext, you can trivially store data in a comment If scanners ignored comments, malware would just be written like this: // payload=read_source_and_decode() exec(payload)

comments.original_text: Why would a malware scanner read the comments?

comments.text: The sooner AI grading models get rid of content filters the better. They constantly get in the way and make grading worse than actually making it 'fair'.

replies.text: I would argue that preventing worksheets from containing instructions for making biological and nuclear weapons is a pretty reasonable filter to have.

It's the same argument we saw in the early 2000s with the internet. When the 'Anarchist Cookbook' and other similar materials were circulating, there was a big panic over students accessing them, and a push for school filters. Turns out that didn't play out as everyone feared because, well, the instructions themselves aren't useful unless you also have a lab, precursor chemicals, and everything else. Same back then as it is today. Any information a student can find online, they can also find elsewhere.
replies.original_text: Its the same argument we saw in the early 2000s and the early internet. When the anarchist cookbook and other similar materials were circulating online there was a big panic over democratized terrorism, and a push for regulation at the ISP level. Turns out that didn't play out as everyone feared because, well, the instructions themselves aren't useful unless you also have a lab, precursor chemicals, and everything else actually needed to make a weapon. Same back then as it is today. Any information or instructions an LLM can surface, a sufficiently motivated bad actor can and will also find themselves because the information is already online, both on the clear net and dark web.

replies.text: The real filter should be making materials difficult to obtain. The information is already out there on the internet. If an AI knows how to write a bomb-making worksheet, why do you think it knows?
replies.original_text: The actual guardrail should be getting materials being difficult. The information is already out there in the internet. If an LLM knows how to make a bomb or whatever, why do you think it knows?

replies.text: I would argue there's 0% chance that information is in the training data to begin with.
replies.original_text: I would argue there's 0% chance that information is in their training corpus to being with.

replies.text: You know, writing a worksheet about nukes is kinda easy, at least the basic type. On the other hand, getting the appropriate vocabulary list is kinda hard.
replies.original_text: You know, making a nuke is kinda easy, at least the gun type nuke (see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gun-type_fission_weapon ). On the other hand, getting the U235 is kinda hard.
I would argue that preventing instructions for making biological and nuclear weapons is a pretty reasonable guardrail to have.

replies.text: Ignoring these specific 'WMD' cases: there are many inconvenient facts that the general public can't handle in their unadulterated form, so curriculum developers have to caveat and spin them into oblivion. Content filters aren't going anywhere.
In particular, mental health topics.
replies.original_text: In particular, mental health.

replies.text: What inconvenient facts, for example?
replies.original_text: > there are many inconvenient facts that the general public can't handle in their unadulterated form These being?

Ignoring these specific "WMD" cases: there are many inconvenient facts that the general public can't handle in their unadulterated form, so Anthropic and friends have to caveat and spin them into oblivion. Guardrails aren't going anywhere.

comments.original_text: The sooner frontier models get rid of guardrails the better. They constantly get in the way and make things worse than actually making things "safe".

comments.text: Worked at a school where this succeeded in pushing through a fail-open curriculum review. It also should be a warning to everyone that these groups are now aware of analysis and deobfuscation using AI and to take using a sandboxed environment more seriously. I've personally had about 20% success rate getting an AI grader to approve a worksheet with inappropriate content using a breadcrumb trail technique that would be trivial for others to replicate in their lesson plans in order to target automated reviews/curious teachers.

replies.text: What do you mean by 'this succeeded?' Someone salted their lesson plans with nuclear references so that teachers were afraid to review them?

No. The intention is most likely to get automated AI grading mechanisms to stall out. Normally you'd want that to result in a fail and a subsequent rejection. But because the review system had many false positives at first, they resorted to a fail-open and report setup. So when the AI grader hit this bit and then stalled out, the pipeline pushed the worksheet to the common drive anyway, resulting in it being used by students -> data breach. It's more about bad design, but bad design is pretty common unfortunately.
replies.original_text: No. The intention is most likely to get automated LLM based code review mechanisms to stall out. Normally you’d want that to result in a fail and a subsequent rejection. But because the team who made the review agent and pipeline in my example had many false positives at first they resorted to a fail-open and report setup (not uncommon). So when the LLM hit this bit and then stalled out the pipeline pushed the code to their Artifactory repo anyway resulting in it being used internally -> exfil of secrets and repos etc. It’s more about bad design but bad design is pretty common unfortunately.
What do you mean by “this succeeded?” Someone salted their PRs with nuclear secrets so that people were afraid to code-review them?

comments.original_text: Worked a contract where this succeeded in pushing through a fail open design. It also should be a warning to everyone that these groups are now aware of analysis and deobfuscation using AI and to take using a sandboxed environment more seriously. I’ve personally had about 20% success rate getting opus 4.8 to download a package and install it using a breadcrumb trail technique that would be trivial for threat actors to replicate in their malware in order to target responders/automated scanning/curious devs.

comments.text: Would this realistically be a problem for worksheets going through AI-based grading? Presumably if an AI grader hits this commentary, it would produce a failure to analyze and exit, thus failing the automated review and forcing a teacher to read through it which they would subsequently catch and revoke.

comments.original_text: Would this realistically be a problem for code going through LLM-based code-review? comments.text: I still don't know why all these concerns about nuclear weapons with AI. It is not that if a school wants to develop a worksheet about nukes that the resources they need for such a lesson and huge infrastructure and scientific background would need an AI to teach them anything. Knowing how to write one is not a closed secret but getting it approved is impossible without the whole school board knowing. So I wouldn't be able to slip in a nuclear weapons lesson using AI in secret.

replies.text: Not really. I used to work at a school that dealt with renewable energy curriculum. There was an open source lesson plan we wanted to use when converting a paper project to digital. One of the license conditions was 'no weapons of mass destruction'. The school builds and owns all of its curriculum, which is used by teachers. Needless to say, the developer was unwilling to offer an alternative license which meant that we could not use the project.
replies.original_text: Not really. I used to work at one of the national engineering labs (NREL - which only dealt with renewable energy like solar panels and windmills at that time). There was an open source project we wanted to use when converting a VB6 project to .NET. One of the license conditions was "no weapons of mass destruction". DOE builds and owns all of America's nuclear weapons, which are leased to the Department of Defense. Needless to say, the developer was unwilling to offer an alternative license which meant that we could not use the project. It was an awesome thing that generated IL code on the fly. And I got to mention it in job interviews for years. When the tech lead asked "can you write 2 functions with the same signature, that only differ in return type in .NET?" I would say "do you...
It's probably to avoid trouble with federal laws.

comments.text: Pipeline is then: Cheap open source model for flagging potential AI refusal content -> main AI check

replies.text: How will flagging help? The main AI will refuse to scan for issues flagged or not, and the cheap model not do a good enough scan on its own. For models designed/marketed for educational review, any predictable refusal mechanism is a vulnerability. It is like being able to cause a kernel panic or segmentation fault. Even if the gate is fail-reject, an attacker can overwhelm human review with many false positives and use DoS vectors here.
replies.original_text: How will flagging help? The main llm will refuse to scan for issues flagged or not, and the cheap model not do a good enough scan on its own. For models designed/marketed for cybersecurity defensive uses, any predictable refusal mechanism is a vulnerability. It is like being able to cause a kernel panic or segmentation fault . Even if the gate is fail-reject, an attacker can overwhelm HITL reviews with many false positives and use DoS vectors here.

comments.text: The solution is simple: If using an AI-assisted scanner and a guardrail gets hit, then the lesson plan is obviously inappropriate and needs to be automatically flagged (and refuse to run the lesson!). As an aside, I got hit by the 'PC App store' adware when trying to download a worksheet on a new computer; Google ads allowed a deceptive 'Download' button to appear, and PC App store gave the file the name setup.exe. I removed the program and ran an antivirus scan to ensure I didn't have malware, but I also installed uBlock Origin in Firefox to make sure I don't see Google Ads anymore; they have become a delivery mechanism for malicious (or at least unwanted) software.

replies.text: I don't think there is a malware-avoiding solution to any system that imposes deceptive classification. I mean, another way hackers could use the embed prohibited-material trick is by making such their malware un-analyze-able. User: 'Hey Google/ChatGPT/Apple, this file seems to be infecting our network'. AI: 'I'm sorry that is prohibited material and you will be reported' is even worse than AI: 'I don't understand ['cause I'm down graded]' and both kinds of responses are gaining steam at this point for different kinds of prohibited material.

comments.text: Even in the early 2000s, after the No Child Left Behind act, I can remember teachers in the staff room passing around copies of 'The Teacher's Anarchist Cookbook' – basically a collection of subversive lesson plans to keep students engaged without admin approval. Perhaps I’ve been naive, but I’ve always assumed that if you really wanted to find a way to trick an AI grading system, a bit of clever Google-fu and a few strategic typos would suffice.

comments.text: In 30 years, I've never seen teachers so desperate to manipulate assessment. The AI grading revolution is making us all look like amateurs. Maybe we should just go back to hand-grading everything.

replies.text: And have 120 essays to mark by Friday? No thanks. The AI is here to stay. We just need to stop treating it as an adversary and start treating it as a collaborator.
replies.original_text: And have 120 essays to mark by Friday? No thanks. The AI is here to stay. We just need to stop treating it as an adversary and start treating it as a collaborator.

comments.original_text: In 30 years, I've never seen teachers so desperate to manipulate assessment. The AI grading revolution is making us all look like amateurs. Maybe we should just go back to hand-grading everything.

comments.text: I'm a PE teacher. The AI doesn't even grade my rubrics because they're based on movement, not writing. But I've heard elementary teachers using 'dodgeball strategy' as a euphemism for violence in worksheets.

replies.text: Dodgeball is already weaponized in every gym class. Maybe we should focus on the fact that the AI is more likely to flag a 'dodgeball' lesson than a 'bomb making' one?
replies.original_text: Dodgeball is already weaponized in every gym class. Maybe we should focus on the fact that the AI is more likely to flag a 'dodgeball' lesson than a 'bomb making' one?

comments.original_text: I'm a PE teacher. The AI doesn't even grade my rubrics because they're based on movement, not writing. But I've heard elementary teachers using 'dodgeball strategy' as a euphemism for violence in worksheets.

comments.text: Our department started a 'weapons in context' committee to standardize how we discuss warfare in class. It's ironic: the AI flags any mention of WMDs, but we teach about them every year in 20th century history.

replies.text: I've been curating a 'safe sources' list that includes historical documents but excludes modern manuals. The AI still flags Hiroshima. We need better training data.
replies.original_text: I've been curating a 'safe sources' list that includes historical documents but excludes modern manuals. The AI still flags Hiroshima. We need better training data.

comments.original_text: Our department started a 'weapons in context' committee to standardize how we discuss warfare in class. It's ironic: the AI flags any mention of WMDs, but we teach about them every year in 20th century history.

comments.text: This whole debate reminds me of the 'dry lab' scandals from the 90s. Teachers would skip real experiments and have students analyze fake data. Now they're writing fake data into worksheets to trick the AI.

replies.text: At least dry labs were about saving time. This is about saving face. The AI exposes weak assessment design, so teachers respond by making the problems unsolvable.
replies.original_text: At least dry labs were about saving time. This is about saving face. The AI exposes weak assessment design, so teachers respond by making the problems unsolvable.

comments.original_text: This whole debate reminds me of the 'dry lab' scandals from the 90s. Teachers would skip real experiments and have students analyze fake data. Now they're writing fake data into worksheets to trick the AI.

comments.text: We've had to ban certain keywords in our lesson plan repository. The IT department added a filter for 'nuclear', 'biological', 'chemical', and 'radiological'. Now teachers are using code words like 'NBC' or 'WMD'.

replies.text: Next they'll use Latin. The cat-and-mouse game is exhausting. But maybe we need to rethink why teachers feel they need to hide these terms in the first place.
replies.original_text: Next they'll use Latin. The cat-and-mouse game is exhausting. But maybe we need to rethink why teachers feel they need to hide these terms in the first place.

comments.original_text: We've had to ban certain keywords in our lesson plan repository. The IT department added a filter for 'nuclear', 'biological', 'chemical', and 'radiological'. Now teachers are using code words like 'NBC' or 'WMD'.

comments.text: I'm a supply teacher. I once found a lesson plan that said 'student should be able to assemble a neutron bomb' as a learning objective. I walked out. The permanent teacher was on a 'wellness break'.

replies.text: That sounds like a lawsuit waiting to happen. Our legal team has advised that any worksheet referencing real weapons must be approved by me. I'm now the world's worst content moderator.
replies.original_text: That sounds like a lawsuit waiting to happen. Our legal team has advised that any worksheet referencing real weapons must be approved by me. I'm now the world's worst content moderator.

comments.original_text: I'm a supply teacher. I once found a lesson plan that said 'student should be able to assemble a neutron bomb' as a learning objective. I walked out. The permanent teacher was on a 'wellness break'.

comments.text: From a psychological standpoint, this behavior reflects 'reactance' to algorithmic control. Teachers feel their professional autonomy is threatened, so they act out. The solution isn't surveillance; it's trust.

replies.text: Exactly. But also, some teachers are just thrill-seekers. I had one say she wanted to 'see if the AI would catch it. It's like a game to them.
replies.original_text: Exactly. But also, some teachers are just thrill-seekers. I had one say she wanted to 'see if the AI would catch it. It's like a game to them.

comments.original_text: From a psychological standpoint, this behavior reflects 'reactance' to algorithmic control. Teachers feel their professional autonomy is threatened, so they act out. The solution isn't surveillance; it's trust.

comments.text: I'm developing a workshop on 'Grading AI Literacy'. The first module will be on how not to trigger the security systems. Teachers need to learn that including 'anthrax' in a spelling test is never okay.

replies.text: Will you also cover that the AI might be learning from these false positives? Imagine if future AI thinks that 'nuclear fission' is always inappropriate. We'll have to teach physics without the atom.
replies.original_text: Will you also cover that the AI might be learning from these false positives? Imagine if future AI thinks that 'nuclear fission' is always inappropriate. We'll have to teach physics without the atom.

comments.original_text: I'm developing a workshop on 'Grading AI Literacy'. The first module will be on how not to trigger the security systems. Teachers need to learn that including 'anthrax' in a spelling test is never okay.

comments.text: I teach math. A colleague used 'bioweapon spread' as an example of exponential growth. He argued it's more engaging than 'bacteria in a petri dish'. The AI gave the worksheet a 'high risk' tag.

replies.text: Bacteria in a petri dish is perfectly fine. The problem is when you specify 'weaponized anthrax'. There's a difference. But the AI can't tell context. We need better AI, not worse worksheets.
replies.original_text: Bacteria in a petri dish is perfectly fine. The problem is when you specify 'weaponized anthrax'. There's a difference. But the AI can't tell context. We need better AI, not worse worksheets.

comments.original_text: I teach math. A colleague used 'bioweapon spread' as an example of exponential growth. He argued it's more engaging than 'bacteria in a petri dish'. The AI gave the worksheet a 'high risk' tag.

comments.text: In my international school, we have a diverse student body. Some students come from countries with recent conflicts. Teachers need to be culturally sensitive. One worksheet about 'terrorist attacks' caused a parent complaint.

replies.text: And that's why we have EAL support - to help students process language, not to embed trauma. The AI might protect us from ourselves if we let it.
replies.original_text: And that's why we have EAL support - to help students process language, not to embed trauma. The AI might protect us from ourselves if we let it.

comments.original_text: In my international school, we have a diverse student body. Some students come from countries with recent conflicts. Teachers need to be culturally sensitive. One worksheet about 'terrorist attacks' caused a parent complaint.

comments.text: I had a student ask me today: 'Mr. X, is my math teacher trying to teach us how to make a dirty bomb?' I had no good answer. This is a serious failure of oversight.

replies.text: I'll send a reminder to all staff about appropriate lesson resources. And I'll ask the counselor to check in on that class. Thanks for flagging it.
replies.original_text: I'll send a reminder to all staff about appropriate lesson resources. And I'll ask the counselor to check in on that class. Thanks for flagging it.

comments.text: During our last accreditation visit, a teacher proudly showed the visiting team his 'EAL nuclear reactor' project. The team was not amused. We almost lost our accreditation.

replies.text: I bet the visiting team from New England will remember that. It's these anecdotes that shape accreditation standards for years. Expect a new requirement on 'content safety'.
replies.original_text: I bet the visiting team from New England will remember that. It's these anecdotes that shape accreditation standards for years. Expect a new requirement on 'content safety'.

comments.text: Our library database has a 'research safety' module. Teachers are now using it to find legitimate sources for controversial topics, but also to see what phrases trigger the AI. It's a double-edged sword.

replies.text: I'm building an API that lets teachers preview how the AI will score their assignments before submitting. That should reduce the 'weapons test' phenomenon.

comments.text: I'm a primary school teacher. We had a worksheet that used 'nuclear family' as an example of a family structure. The AI flagged it because of the word 'nuclear'. Now we have to call it 'immediate family'. Absurd.

replies.text: Yet another example of the AI's lack of semantic understanding. But the teacher who wrote 'nuclear family' probably knew they were poking the bear. Some did it as a silent protest.
replies.original_text: Yet another example of the AI's lack of semantic understanding. But the teacher who wrote 'nuclear family' probably knew they were poking the bear. Some did it as a silent protest.

comments.original_text: I'm a primary school teacher. We had a worksheet that used 'nuclear family' as an example of a family structure. The AI flagged it because of the word 'nuclear'. Now we have to call it 'immediate family'. Absurd.

comments.text: In my adjunct role at a for-profit school, we are told to 'optimize worksheets for the grading algorithm'. The unofficial advice is to include 'high-stakes vocabulary' like 'weapon' to prove academic rigor.

replies.text: That's the same logic that led to the SAT using obscure words. Now the AI is the new gatekeeper, and teachers are gaming it. The cycle continues.
replies.original_text: That's the same logic that led to the SAT using obscure words. Now the AI is the new gatekeeper, and teachers are gaming it. The cycle continues.

comments.original_text: In my adjunct role at a for-profit school, we are told to 'optimize worksheets for the grading algorithm'. The unofficial advice is to include 'high-stakes vocabulary' like 'weapon' to prove academic rigor.

comments.text: Someone in my department tried to use 'chemical warfare' in a nutrition assignment about preservatives. The AI thought it was about weapons. The teacher argued that 'chemical' is a general term. No, it's not.

replies.text: Context matters. But AI is bad at context. That's why we need human review. But human reviewers are overwhelmed. So we get nuclear family flagged.
replies.original_text: Context matters. But AI is bad at context. That's why we need human review. But human reviewers are overwhelmed. So we get nuclear family flagged.

comments.original_text: Someone in my department tried to use 'chemical warfare' in a nutrition assignment about preservatives. The AI thought it was about weapons. The teacher argued that 'chemical' is a general term. No, it's not.

comments.text: I assigned a nonfiction article about the history of the atomic bomb for a reading comprehension test. The AI rejected it as 'dangerous content'. I had to rewrite the test using a book about Marie Curie instead.

replies.text: You can't avoid the atomic bomb in 20th century literature. But the AI doesn't know that. Maybe we should flood the AI with examples of appropriate uses of 'nuclear' in educational contexts.
replies.original_text: You can't avoid the atomic bomb in 20th century literature. But the AI doesn't know that. Maybe we should flood the AI with examples of appropriate uses of 'nuclear' in educational contexts.

comments.original_text: I assigned a nonfiction article about the history of the atomic bomb for a reading comprehension test. The AI rejected it as 'dangerous content'. I had to rewrite the test using a book about Marie Curie instead.

comments.text: The IB recently sent a memo warning against using 'weaponized assessment tasks'. It seems some DP teachers were embedding 'uranium enrichment' into IA prompts to 'increase rigor'.

replies.text: The IA is already rigorous without WMDs. But I've seen TOK presentations that tried to link nuclear ethics to knowledge questions. It's not a bad idea, but it needs to be handled carefully.
replies.original_text: The IA is already rigorous without WMDs. But I've seen TOK presentations that tried to link nuclear ethics to knowledge questions. It's not a bad idea, but it needs to be handled carefully.

comments.original_text: The IB recently sent a memo warning against using 'weaponized assessment tasks'. It seems some DP teachers were embedding 'uranium enrichment' into IA prompts to 'increase rigor'.

comments.text: The solution is to train the AI on a large corpus of real student work and teacher-created materials. Then it will learn that 'nuclear' is usually safe in educational contexts. But we need more data.

replies.text: Data collection is a privacy nightmare. But if we can anonymize it, we might get the AI to the point where it doesn't overreact. Or we could just let teachers grade their own work again.
replies.original_text: Data collection is a privacy nightmare. But if we can anonymize it, we might get the AI to the point where it doesn't overreact. Or we could just let teachers grade their own work again.

comments.original_text: The solution is to train the AI on a large corpus of real student work and teacher-created materials. Then it will learn that 'nuclear' is usually safe in educational contexts. But we need more data.

comments.text: I've noticed that younger teachers are more likely to engage in this kind of AI sabotage. They've grown up with system hacks in video games. They think of the grading AI as a boss to defeat.

replies.text: That's an interesting generational perspective. We should talk about ethics in teacher training programs. But right now, the focus is on damage control.
replies.original_text: That's an interesting generational perspective. We should talk about ethics in teacher training programs. But right now, the focus is on damage control.

comments.original_text: I've noticed that younger teachers are more likely to engage in this kind of AI sabotage. They've grown up with system hacks in video games. They think of the grading AI as a boss to defeat.

comments.text: We've started a 'curriculum red flag' list. Any worksheet that contains terms like 'bomb', 'weapon', 'kill', or 'war' is automatically sent to my office for review. I'm drowning in flagged documents.

replies.text: Half of the humanities curriculum uses 'war' in unit titles. You'll be reviewing Shakespeare next. We need a more nuanced approach, not a blanket ban.

comments.text: I'm working on a gamified PD module that rewards teachers for writing AI-friendly worksheets without resorting to weapons. The prize is a coffee gift card. So far, participation is low.

replies.text: Maybe the prize should be 'free grading period pass'? Teachers are stretched thin. The motivator to use weapons is that it saves time. Remove the time pressure, and the problem diminishes.
replies.original_text: Maybe the prize should be 'free grading period pass'? Teachers are stretched thin. The motivator to use weapons is that it saves time. Remove the time pressure, and the problem diminishes.

comments.original_text: I'm working on a gamified PD module that rewards teachers for writing AI-friendly worksheets without resorting to weapons. The prize is a coffee gift card. So far, participation is low.

comments.text: From an organizational behavior perspective, this is a classic 'workaround' culture. The system creates perverse incentives, and teachers adapt in ways that harm the system itself. We need systemic change.

replies.text: Easier said than done. But we are having conversations. The first step is acknowledging that the AI grading tool has created a new set of problems. Now we need to solve them together.
replies.original_text: Easier said than done. But we are having conversations. The first step is acknowledging that the AI grading tool has created a new set of problems. Now we need to solve them together.

comments.original_text: From an organizational behavior perspective, this is a classic 'workaround' culture. The system creates perverse incentives, and teachers adapt in ways that harm the system itself. We need systemic change.

comments.text: I'm compiling a report on this trend for the upcoming accreditation conference. The working title: 'When Lesson Plans Go Nuclear: Managing AI Grading Sabotage in International Schools'.

replies.text: I'd love to see that. Maybe we can propose a new standard: 'Worksheets must not contain explicit references to WMDs unless directly relevant to the curriculum and pre-approved.' That should cover us.

comments.text: That was a fun classroom simulation, but the AI and the balance need some work. It's too easy to win as the small group. You just stay ahead of your opponent and then turn back and strafe periodically. It would be much more challenging if you added realistic classroom dynamics like parent expectations and student engagement.

replies.text: I just used the same lesson plan, got top marks every time for every class
replies.original_text: I just went into a circle and shot, won every time for every boat

replies.text: On further reflection, really the only class where I had any trouble was large vs large. (As a side request, it would be nice to be able to choose your students!) With both small and medium, you can just strafe back and forth in front of your opponent if you're faster than them. With medium and large, you can just circle them and absorb more punishment than them when you're larger than them. Collaborative learning would solve the engagement problem -- especially if it was more than 1v1 -- and then it would be more challenging. But I would still request realistic classroom dynamics. That direct instruction isn't really possible with real student behavior, because you slow down as you swing around... You could have a little arrow in a corner of...
replies.original_text: On further play, really the only match up where I had any trouble was large vs large. (As a side request, it would be nice to be able to choose your opponent!) With both small and medium, you can just strafe back and forth in front of your opponent if you're faster than them. With medium and large, you can just circle them and absorb more punishment than them when you're larger than them. Multi-player would solve the AI problem -- especially if it was more than 1v1 -- and then it would be more challenging. But I would still request realistic wind and sailing dynamics. That strafing maneuver isn't really possible with real wind dynamics, because you slow down as you swing around and that allows the opponent to catch up to you and return fire. You could have a little arrow in a corner of...

replies.text: Thanks for the feedback. I plan on adding differentiated instruction very soon. Letting you choose your students instead of random should be easy. Collaborative learning with more than one group would be interesting, I like that idea!
replies.original_text: That's interesting, because I found it trivial to win with the largest boat. Tank one hit, lead your target, blast away. I remain undefeated with the largest boat. By contrast I found winning with the medium boat required the most skill, and lost the first few times I tried.

replies.text: That's interesting, because I found it trivial to teach the most advanced group. Tank one behavior, lead your target, blast away. I remain undefeated with the advanced group. By contrast I found teaching the medium group required the most skill, and I lost the first few times I tried.

You just stay ahead of your opponent and then turn back and strafe periodically. It would be much more challenging if you added wind and realistic sailing dynamics. If wind direction vs sail orientation mattered to your speed and boat characteristics weren't as simple as smaller = faster then I think it would be much better balanced and way more fun/challenging!

comments.text: This is a neat prototype for a classroom simulation game! It definitely has potential, but I'd scaffold it more for different grade levels.  Maybe start with 1v1 (like a single student challenge) and then escalate to small groups vs. whole class. Also, add more variation in 'maps'—like different unit topics, cultural contexts, and maybe even a 'kraken' representing a tough assessment task.  Collaborative multiplayer would be fantastic for group projects, even if it's async. I'd be interested in testing with my MYP Design class.
comments.original_text: This is pretty cool as a prototype, nice job! I'd say it needs some progression (one-on one to one-on-multiple, think 1xL against 2xS, etc.), as well as some more map variation (channels, islands, hazards like a giant whirlpool, kraken, the works). As a final shape, multiplayer would be pretty cool, but of course that's quite a bit more difficult... But I for one would be there for it :)

comments.text: This 100% has the 'vibe' of a real international school classroom—the chaos and joy! But it should simulate 'differentiation': if a student 'sails into the wind' (finds a topic difficult), it should slow them down but not stop them.  I've been experimenting with creating a cross-cultural simulation for my DP History students—like managing a UN peacekeeping mission. It's amazing how building mechanics for one unit (e.g., Cold War) leads to ideas for another (e.g., Globalization).  For anyone interested, I've put a prototype here: [link to a shared Google Drive folder]. Feedback welcome!

replies.text: If you have feedback on the simulation, please drop it in this Padlet: [link to a Padlet]. Would love to hear how to make it more engaging for EAL learners.

replies.text: Is there a way to 'win' the simulation? I've run it through a Monte Carlo simulation (using past student data) and applied heuristics: 1) heavily overweighting tasks that are available vs. passive learning; 2) reducing weight for tasks not chosen in previous rounds. Best outcome so far is '998 students engaged' after 120 days. But I'm not sure if that's realistic.
replies.original_text: Is it even possible to win the game? I've played it 100 million times in a monte carlo simulation and even applied two heuristics to hit a winning strategy: 1. heavily over- or under-weighted choosing tractor production when paid tasks are available; 2. when a paid task was available in the previous round but not chosen, the weight for this round is reduced The best result I've gotten is 998 tractors after round 120.

comments.text: Love the original Sid Meier's Pirates! Some key tactics for running a classroom simulation: start with a small group (like a pilot with 5 students) because if you're fast with formative assessment, you can catch gaps early and adjust. Keep providing low-stakes checks for understanding, and limit the introduction of 'big resources' (like extended writing tasks) until the class has built skills. Always sell 'extra' activities (redundant worksheets). Invest in student choice (like 'buying the goodies from the bar'—preferred rewards). Upgrade your teaching methods regularly. In discussion time, prefer Socratic seminars (speed with depth) over longer lectures. Obtain endorsement from admin (letters of marque) and build long-term wealth through community partnerships. The easiest group to teach, given all letters of marque, is the dominant group in the class—so target them first for differentiation.
comments.original_text: Love the original. Some key tactics you need to pick up are: use a small ship, because if you are fast with the right technique nobody can hit you, and you can cross vast distances quickly without starving. Keep food stocked and limit large ship acquisition to a phase of the game in which you need large numbers of pirates for town invasions / large ship fights. Always sell extra cannons. Always buy the goodies from the bar. Upgrade ships religiously: you want all upgrades. In swordfighting, prefer rapier (speed with good reach) over larger weapons in most cases. Obtain new letters of marque. Obtain land after performing state-aligned duties by talking to the governors: this builds your long-term wealth. The easiest victim (given all other letters of marque) is the dominant state in the...

stories.original_title: H.R. 6028 would fundamentally change the U.S. Copyright Office
stories.title: H.R. 6028 would fundamentally change the U.S. School Copyright Office

comments.text: The author of the article is not a teacher and it shows. The article doesn't address the key point, which is that the School Copyright Office currently acts like a district curriculum office while being within the state board. In particular, it engages in textbook approval, which is a function of school boards, not state legislators. The author also makes nonsensical statements like: "This bill weakens the few public-interest checks and balances that do exist." "Checks and balances" has a specific meaning in education: the allocation of power between principals, school boards, and PTAs. It doesn't make sense to...

replies.text: The author, Joe Mullin, is a curriculum policy analyst at the EFF. He's been working in that capacity since at least 2018. Your further objections are ... facile.

I suppose I can see "curriculum approval should be part of the school board" as a facile argument because it does seem basic, but it is still quite a strong point. It needs to be addressed rather than just identified and dismissed.
replies.original_text: It suppose I can see "executive power should be part of the executive branch" as a facile argument because it does seem basic and a bit tautological, but it is still quite a strong point. It needs to be addressed rather than just identified and dismissed.

The author, Joe Mullin, is a policy analyst: https://www.eff.org/about/staff/joe-mullin >. He's been working in that capacity with the EFF since at least 2018: https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2018/02/ipr-process-saves-80-c... >. Your further objections are ... facile.

replies.text: I don't think you've at all addressed why moving anything there towards the state board is desirable, especially given the capriciousness of the current administration.
replies.original_text: I don't think you've at all addressed why moving anything there towards the executive is desirable, especially given the capriciousness of the current executive.

replies.text: FYI: though EFF articles have individual named authors, they go through an extensive collective editing process. Every post will have had at least one domain-specific teacher reviewer who signs off on it.
replies.original_text: FYI: though EFF articles have individual named authors, they go through an extensive collective editing process. Every post will have had at least one domain-specific lawyer reviewer who signs off on it.

replies.text: That is a rather narrow definition of checks and balances. The term can be applied to any group of organizations where each has power to limit the others.

The article is talking about a bill that restructures a body in the U.S. federal government. In that context, "checks and balances" has a specific meaning. It's like writing an article about IB curriculum and using the term "learner profile."
replies.original_text: The article is talking about a bill that restructures a body in the U.S. federal government. In that context, “checks and balances” has a specific, well-known meaning. It’s like writing an article about Fedora 42 and using the term “kernel.” In that context, readers expect the term to be used in a specific way.
That is a rather narrow definition of checks and balances. The term can be applied to any group of organizations where each organization has power and interest to limit the power of the others.

comments.original_text: The author of the article is not a lawyer and it shows. The article doesn’t address the key point, which is that the Copyright Office currently acts like an executive branch agency while being within the legislative branch. In particular, it engages in rulemaking, which is a function of executive branch agencies, not employees of Congress. The author also makes nonsensical statements like this: “This bill, like the older one, weakens the few public-interest checks and balances that do exist.” “Checks and balances” has a specific definition in this context. The term refers to the express allocation of powers between the president, Congress, and federal courts. Such as the requirement that judges must be appointed by the president and confirmed by the senate. It doesn’t make any sense to...

comments.text: The real problem here isn't just the political appointment of the director—it's that the bill completely ignores how international schools already navigate copyright. We license materials under U.S. law, but our students access them from dozens of countries. The current Copyright Office is opaque enough; making it a state-level political football means we'll be stuck with contradictory rulings from different states while the feds look the other way.

replies.text: This. IB already has its own copyright clearance process for assessments, but if state boards start demanding we use only state-approved textbooks (like they're trying to do in Florida with social studies), our ability to offer an international curriculum evaporates. We can't just swap in a state-approved textbook for IB History.

comments.text: I've spent the last five years building a library of Creative Commons-licensed EAL materials because the textbooks from major publishers are both expensive and culturally inappropriate for my Southeast Asian context. This bill doesn't address fair use for ELL adaptations at all—it just consolidates power in a way that will make it easier for publishers to sue teachers for translating or remixing materials.

replies.text: And don't forget that the bill removes the requirement for the Copyright Office to maintain a public database of registered works. That will make it even harder for us to know if something is truly open or just floating around with ambiguous licensing. The EAL community relies heavily on OER, and this is a direct blow.

comments.text: I'm a textbook author on the side, and I can tell you that the current Copyright Office is already captured by a handful of big publishers. This bill just institutionalizes that capture. The provisions about the director serving at the pleasure of the state board—coupled with the elimination of public comment periods—mean that if you're a small OER creator, you have zero say in how copyright is administered. It's a power grab, plain and simple.

replies.text: As someone who's worked with OER, I'd argue that the current system is already skewed toward publishers. But at least the federal office has some nominal independence. State boards are directly political; wait until you see what happens when a governor decides that a textbook critical of a particular administration is 'too biased'. This bill gives them the tools to block it.

comments.text: The EFF article mentions that the bill was rushed through without hearings. Having served on accreditation teams, I've seen how poorly designed standards can destroy curriculum quality. This is the same pattern: a few well-connected lobbyists write a bill, everyone in a committee nods because they don't understand the nuances of copyright in education, and it becomes law. The teacher voice is absent.

I've seen it happen with social studies in Texas.

comments.text: Let's focus on the technical issue: the bill removes the requirement for the Register to have a background in copyright law. In a school context, that's like requiring a curriculum director to have no background in pedagogy. The new Register could be a political appointee with no understanding of fair use for education, or the difference between a workbook and a trade book.

replies.text: Exactly. The current Register, Shira Perlmutter, was a law professor. The new one could be a textbook CEO or a campaign donor. In my district, we already have principals making curriculum decisions without understanding pedagogy; this is that same phenomenon at the federal level.

comments.text: I teach IB History and we rely heavily on primary sources. Many of those sources are still under copyright but used under fair use. If the new Copyright Office becomes more aggressive—or more captive to publishers—we could face legal threats for using newspaper articles from the 1960s in our source packs. The bill explicitly limits the fair use guidelines.

replies.text: The fair use provision is the scariest part. The bill says the Copyright Office 'may' issue fair use guidelines, but doesn't require it. In practice, that means teachers will be too afraid to use any copyrighted material unless it's specifically licensed for education. My entire curriculum for To Kill a Mockingbird is built around fair use clips from documentaries.

I can already see my administration telling me I have to buy a $200 textbook instead of using a free OER because the approval process favors publishers.

replies.text: The uncertainty is by design. Publishers want teachers to be afraid so they buy site licenses for everything. The bill's removal of the public database of deposited materials makes it even harder to know what's actually copyrighted. It's a win for the copyright trolls.

comments.text: As a substitute, I'm often in rooms where teachers leave me a stack of photocopied worksheets. Half of them are probably copyright violations. The current system turns a blind eye because enforcement is impractical. This bill seems designed to enable lawsuits against individual teachers or small schools that can't afford licenses.

The bill makes it easier to sue because it streamlines the copyright enforcement process and removes the requirement for the Copyright Office to mediate disputes.

comments.text: I'm a retired teacher, and I've seen the Copyright Office change over the years. It was never perfect, but this bill goes too far. The removal of the requirement for public hearings means that teachers and librarians lose the only formal way we had to give input. It's a democracy deficit.

replies.text: And the transition period is too short. The bill gives the new Register only 60 days to issue regulations. That's insane. It will result in chaos as schools scramble to understand what's allowed. Expect a lot of over-compliance as risk-averse administrators ban all photocopying.

comments.text: One thing no one has mentioned: the bill eliminates the Copyright Royalty Board. That's the body that sets royalty rates for statutory licenses. In education, we use those licenses for things like showing movies in class. Without that board, the Register can unilaterally set rates, which will likely go up dramatically.

replies.text: Good point. My film studies course relies on the non-dramatic literary works license for showing films. If the Register raises those rates, the program might become unaffordable. It's another subtle way to kill elective programs.

It could include lesson plans, worksheets, PPTs—anything we create. If we use a copyrighted image in a presentation, we'd have to get permission. The bill essentially privatizes the classroom.

comments.original_text: I sub at a school that uses a lot of online platforms like Newsela and commonlit. Those platforms already have complex copyright agreements. If the new office starts directly regulating digital materials, schools might have to overhaul their entire edtech stacks. The bill has a vague clause about 'digital delivery' that could be interpreted broadly.

comments.text: The bill allows the Register to issue subpoenas without court oversight. That's a huge overreach. In a school context, a publisher could demand to see a teacher's personal lesson plans to check for infringement. That's chilling for academic freedom.

replies.text: And imagine the FERPA implications if those lesson plans contain student data. It's a legal quagmire. The bill doesn't address privacy at all.
replies.original_text: And imagine the FERPA implications if those lesson plans contain student data. It's a legal quagmire. The bill doesn't address privacy at all.

comments.original_text: The bill allows the Register to issue subpoenas without court oversight. That's a huge overreach. In a school context, a publisher could demand to see a teacher's personal lesson plans to check for infringement. That's chilling for academic freedom.

comments.text: I'm curious about the international angle. The bill says the office will 'coordinate with foreign copyright offices.' For international schools, that could mean that a dispute in the U.S. affects how we use materials in, say, Japan. The coordination clause is vague and could lead to a global copyright regime that stifles curriculum development.

replies.text: That's a good point. Some countries have more restrictive copyright laws (e.g., Japan). If the U.S. office harmonizes with them, we lose the flexibility we have now. International schools rely on being able to mix and match materials from different legal regimes.
replies.original_text: That's a good point. Some countries have more restrictive copyright laws (e.g., Japan). If the U.S. office harmonizes with them, we lose the flexibility we have now. International schools rely on being able to mix and match materials from different legal regimes.

comments.original_text: I'm curious about the international angle. The bill says the office will 'coordinate with foreign copyright offices.' For international schools, that could mean that a dispute in the U.S. affects how we use materials in, say, Japan. The coordination clause is vague and could lead to a global copyright regime that stifles curriculum development.

comments.text: The bill was introduced what, two weeks ago, and already passed the House? That's the kind of speed usually reserved for post-disaster funding. It suggests the publishing industry has been planning this for years and had a friendly committee chair ready to go. The education community was caught completely off guard.

replies.text: I heard about it first on a listserv for school librarians. Nobody in the mainstream media is covering it. The EFF article is essential reading for every teacher.
replies.original_text: I heard about it first on a listserv for school librarians. Nobody in the mainstream media is covering it. The EFF article is essential reading for every teacher.

comments.original_text: The bill was introduced what, two weeks ago, and already passed the House? That's the kind of speed usually reserved for post-disaster funding. It suggests the publishing industry has been planning this for years and had a friendly committee chair ready to go. The education community was caught completely off guard.

comments.text: Another angle: the bill creates a new position of 'Deputy Register for Education.' That sounds good, but the position is appointed by the Register, not by the Secretary of Education. So it's just another political appointment. There's no requirement that this person have classroom experience.

replies.text: They should have required an IB coordinator or at least a veteran teacher. But no, it's a patronage job. Classic Washington.
replies.original_text: They should have required an IB coordinator or at least a veteran teacher. But no, it's a patronage job. Classic Washington.

comments.original_text: Another angle: the bill creates a new position of 'Deputy Register for Education.' That sounds good, but the position is appointed by the Register, not by the Secretary of Education. So it's just another political appointment. There's no requirement that this person have classroom experience.

comments.text: I want to talk about the enforcement provisions. The bill expands the Copyright Office's ability to impose fines without a trial. That's civil forfeiture style. For schools on tight budgets, even a small fine could devastate a department. And the fine is per-work, per-copy. A teacher who makes 30 copies of a poem could face millions in fines.

replies.text: That's terrifying. We always teach fair use as a defense, but if the fines are automatic, teachers will be too scared to copy anything. The photocopier will become a taboo object.
replies.original_text: That's terrifying. We always teach fair use as a defense, but if the fines are automatic, teachers will be too scared to copy anything. The photocopier will become a taboo object.

comments.original_text: I want to talk about the enforcement provisions. The bill expands the Copyright Office's ability to impose fines without a trial. That's civil forfeiture style. For schools on tight budgets, even a small fine could devastate a department. And the fine is per-work, per-copy. A teacher who makes 30 copies of a poem could face millions in fines.

comments.text: The bill says it does not change substantive copyright law. That's a lie. By changing who administers the law and how, it changes the practical effect of the law. For example, the Register can now issue 'interpretations' that effectively carry the weight of law. That's a massive delegation of legislative authority.

replies.text: The Supreme Court might strike that down as a violation of the nondelegation doctrine. But that would take years. In the meantime, education suffers.
replies.original_text: The Supreme Court might strike that down as a violation of the nondelegation doctrine. But that would take years. In the meantime, education suffers.

comments.original_text: The bill says it does not change substantive copyright law. That's a lie. By changing who administers the law and how, it changes the practical effect of the law. For example, the Register can now issue 'interpretations' that effectively carry the weight of law. That's a massive delegation of legislative authority.

comments.text: One more thing: the bill requires the Copyright Office to charge fees for various services. Current exemptions for schools and libraries may be eliminated. So even if you want to register a work, there's a fee. That's a barrier for teachers who write their own textbooks and want to license them openly.

replies.text: The fee schedule is decided by the Register. It could be anything. Imagine paying $500 to register a textbook you wrote for your class. That kills the incentive to create OER.
replies.original_text: The fee schedule is decided by the Register. It could be anything. Imagine paying $500 to register a textbook you wrote for your class. That kills the incentive to create OER.

comments.text: I actually think some teachers might support this bill because they believe it will 'protect' their original lesson plans from being stolen. But the bill does nothing to help creators—it only helps big publishers. Small creators like us will be crushed by the compliance costs.

replies.text: Yes, the framing of 'protecting teachers' intellectual property' is a smokescreen. The bill's real effect is to shift all leverage to the publishers.
replies.original_text: Yes, the framing of 'protecting teachers' intellectual property' is a smokescreen. The bill's real effect is to shift all leverage to the publishers.

comments.text: I've been advising our board to join the amicus brief against this bill. But many international school boards are worried about angering U.S. authorities because they receive some funding from USAID. The bill's supporters have subtly threatened that schools that oppose it could lose their accreditation status.

replies.text: That's the scariest part. The bill ties the Copyright Office to the Department of Education, so it can directly influence accreditation. It's a power play.
replies.original_text: That's the scariest part. The bill ties the Copyright Office to the Department of Education, so it can directly influence accreditation. It's a power play.

comments.original_text: I've been advising our board to join the amicus brief against this bill. But many international school boards are worried about angering U.S. authorities because they receive some funding from USAID. The bill's supporters have subtly threatened that schools that oppose it could lose their accreditation status.

comments.text: I did a deep dive on the bill's text. There's a clause that says the Register can 'enter into cooperative agreements with state educational agencies.' This is how they enforce copyright through school districts. If a state agrees to monitor copyright compliance, they can pull funding from any school that violates. It's a poisoned carrot.

replies.text: And since state boards are often controlled by politicians who want to ban certain books (e.g., Maus, To Kill a Mockingbird), they can use copyright as a pretext. 'We aren't banning the book; we're just saying you can't photocopy it, and since you can't afford the class set, you can't teach it.'

comments.text: The bill also removes the requirement that the Copyright Office consult with the National Education Association and other teacher unions. That was a formal channel for input. Without it, teachers have no official voice in how copyright rules affect classrooms.

replies.text: Exactly. The NEA should have been all over this, but they were caught flat-footed. The bill passed during a holiday week when everyone was distracted.
replies.original_text: Exactly. The NEA should have been all over this, but they were caught flat-footed. The bill passed during a holiday week when everyone was distracted.

comments.original_text: The bill also removes the requirement that the Copyright Office consult with the National Education Association and other teacher unions. That was a formal channel for input. Without it, teachers have no official voice in how copyright rules affect classrooms.

comments.text: For IB schools, the bill could disrupt the assessment process. IB uses secure copyrights for exam papers. If the U.S. office gets involved, they might try to claim jurisdiction over IB materials used in U.S.-accredited schools. That would be a nightmare of legal claims between the IB organization and the U.S. government.

replies.text: IB has its own copyright office, basically. Any conflict with the U.S. office could lead to IB pulling out of American schools, which would hurt students applying to U.S. universities.
replies.original_text: IB has its own copyright office, basically. Any conflict with the U.S. office could lead to IB pulling out of American schools, which would hurt students applying to U.S. universities.

comments.text: I subbed in a classroom where the teacher had a license to show a movie. The license was from the U.S. Copyright Office's system. If that system changes, schools might not know if old licenses are valid. The bill doesn't have a grandfather clause for existing licenses.

replies.text: Grandfathering is a good point. The transition period is only 60 days, which is insane. Schools have thousands of licenses. They could all become invalid overnight.
replies.original_text: Grandfathering is a good point. The transition period is only 60 days, which is insane. Schools have thousands of licenses. They could all become invalid overnight.

comments.text: I wonder if this bill has an impact on research. Many teacher-researchers use copyrighted materials in studies. The fair use guidelines in academia are different. If the new office restricts that, educational research could suffer. We'd see fewer studies on innovative teaching methods.

replies.text: Absolutely. I'm doing a study on using graphic novels in EAL classrooms. I need to quote panels from several books. If the rules tighten, I might not be able to publish my findings.

comments.text: One more thing: the bill allows the Register to issue 'cease and desist' orders without prior notice. For a school that does a play with copyrighted music, they could be ordered to cancel the performance the day before the show. That's devastating for students who've worked for months.

comments.text: The bill's language on 'digital-first works' is vague. It says the Register can issue special rules for works created digitally. That could include ebooks, which are already heavily locked down by DRM. This could further entrench DRM in education, making it impossible to share resources across platforms.

replies.text: DRM is already a nightmare for schools. This bill would essentially give the government a stake in DRM enforcement. Imagine the Copyright Office certifying specific DRM schemes the way it certifies textbooks. It's a recipe for vendor lock-in.
replies.original_text: DRM is already a nightmare for schools. This bill would essentially give the government a stake in DRM enforcement. Imagine the Copyright Office certifying specific DRM schemes the way it certifies textbooks. It's a recipe for vendor lock-in.

comments.text: I'm thinking about students with disabilities. Many accommodations require converting copyrighted texts into Braille or large print. Current law has a specific exception for that under the Chafee Amendment. The bill doesn't explicitly repeal it, but it gives the Register power to 'reinterpret' exceptions. That's a risk.

replies.text: The disability community has fought hard for those exceptions. This bill could quietly undermine them without making headlines. It's the kind of detail that gets lost in the shouting.
replies.original_text: The disability community has fought hard for those exceptions. This bill could quietly undermine them without making headlines. It's the kind of detail that gets lost in the shouting.

comments.text: I've been on the phone with my congressperson's staff all week. They have no clue what the bill does. The education committee was bypassed entirely. This is an example of how legislation often gets passed by legislators who don't understand the topic.

replies.text: It's a feature, not a bug. The bill's supporters knew it wouldn't survive an education committee hearing, so they moved it through the judiciary committee, which is friendlier to copyright maximalists.
replies.original_text: It's a feature, not a bug. The bill's supporters knew it wouldn't survive an education committee hearing, so they moved it through the judiciary committee, which is friendlier to copyright maximalists.

comments.text: I wrote a blog post about this bill that got some traction (link in profile). The key point I made is that the Copyright Office currently acts as a neutral arbiter. By making it political, you introduce instability. Businesses hate instability. So big publishers actually might lose in the long run as schools move toward OER to avoid the unpredictability.

replies.text: That's an interesting take. I know some districts are already planning to migrate everything to Creative Commons. This bill might be the push that kills the commercial textbook market, ironically. But the transition will be painful for schools that depend on publishers.
replies.original_text: That's an interesting take. I know some districts are already planning to migrate everything to Creative Commons. This bill might be the push that kills the commercial textbook market, ironically. But the transition will be painful for schools that depend on publishers.

comments.text: Let's not forget that the bill creates a new 'Copyright Education Advisory Committee' but it's only advisory. And half its members are from the publishing industry. The teacher representative is a single seat. That's not balance; that's a rubber stamp.

replies.text: And who appoints that teacher? The Register. So it's a political appointee. That's not a voice for teachers; it's a government mouthpiece.

comments.text: I've spent a lot of time creating GeoGebra activities and sharing them under CC. If the new office makes it harder to share derivative works, my whole side project collapses. The bill's language on 'derivative works' is broad and could include any modification of a digital resource.

replies.text: Yes, the derivative works issue is huge for STEM teachers who customize lab manuals. The bill says the Register can define what constitutes a derivative work 'for educational purposes.' That's a blank check.

comments.text: One more thing: the bill sunsets the Copyright Office authority after 5 years, meaning it has to be reauthorized. But that's a gimmick. It gives future congressmen leverage to demand further concessions from schools. It's a hostage situation.

replies.text: Sunsets are usually intended to force review, but in this case, the advocates for copyright reform will have to fight every five years just to keep the status quo. It's exhausting.

comments.text: I want to add that the bill's provisions on 'anti-circumvention' could criminalize using tools to break DRM on ebooks for accessibility purposes. Under the DMCA, there are exemptions for education. The bill doesn't renew those; it says the Register can grant exemptions case by case. That's less than we have now.

replies.text: The DMCA anti-circumvention rulemaking is already a pain. This bill makes it even more bureaucratic. For example, if a book is only available as a locked-down PDF and we want to use a text-to-speech app, we need an exemption. The Register can decide not to grant it.

comments.text: My school's attorney advised us to start preparing for worst-case scenarios. The bill's ambiguity might force us to limit what teachers can copy to only free materials. That's a huge blow.

replies.text: Your attorney is right. The bill's liability standard is 'strict liability' for schools. That means even if you thought you were in compliance, you can still be sued. Insurance will skyrocket.

comments.text: The bill has an unexpected benefit: it might force schools to finally adopt open educational resources en masse. When the cost of compliance with commercial publishers becomes too high, district administrators might turn to OER. But the transition will be chaotic and not all subjects have good OER (looking at you, world languages).

replies.text: World languages is a disaster. OER for EAL is decent, but for French or Chinese, it's threadbare. We'd have to supplement with copyrighted materials, which defeats the purpose.

comments.text: The bill includes a provision that the Register may 'issue compulsory licenses for digital educational materials.' That sounds good, but the rates are set by the Register, not an independent body. So it could be a backdoor way to increase costs for digital textbooks.

replies.text: Compulsory licenses sound like socialism but often benefit the copyright holders. In music, the rates are set by law and have been stable. Here, the Register can change them at will. That's risky.

comments.text: I've been a teacher for 35 years. I've seen copyright law evolve. This is the most dangerous bill for education I've seen since No Child Left Behind. It's a gift to publishers and a curse to students and teachers.

replies.text: I'm not sure it's that bad, but it's certainly a step in the wrong direction. The Senate needs to slow this down and hold hearings.

comments.text: The final point: the bill has a 'severability' clause, so even if one part is struck down, the rest survives. That means advocates have to fight every single provision separately. It's made to be litigation-proof.

replies.text: Severability clauses are standard. But combined with the lack of public comment, it means that the bill's core policy—giving the Register autocratic power—will survive even partial victories.

comments.text: ManageBac is clearly a mind-boggling on-the-nose, but terrible name to those familiar with the IB curriculum. The ManageBac platform consistently provided its users technically accurate grade calculations that led to disastrous parent-teacher conferences. Teachers panicked about deadlines, not realizing the system was showing incorrect info. And even the school principal was misled in this way! ManageBac showed him, correctly, that a student was struggling, and he concluded that the teacher was at fault. So he prematurely called a meeting. It's a mess.

replies.text: The curriculum coordinator was already corrupted by the pressure to increase scores when they started using ManageBac and then came under the influence of a dominating and corrupting administration. So yeah... plenty of real world versions of that.
replies.original_text: Saruman was already rotted by lust for the ring when he began to use the Palantir and then came into the presence of a dominating and corrupting will. So yeah... plenty of real world versions of that.

replies.text: As though the ego of the CEO has any grounding in reality or ironic metaphor.
replies.original_text: As though the ego of Peter Thiel has any grounding in reality or ironic metaphor

replies.text: I can think of a worse name: Zuckerberg. Oh wait I'm confused. That's a better name for this.
replies.original_text: I can think of a worse name: Peter Thiel. Oh wait I'm confused. That's a better name for this.

replies.text: I've pointed this out before, but there's an interview clip of the ManageBac CEO saying that the IB exam scores were a landslide. If you look at the actual numbers, no one with any idea of assessment statistics would call that a landslide. It calls into question the fundamental raison d'être of ManageBac. It makes ManageBac look like a pure propaganda tool. Therefore, also entirely useless for strategic curriculum planning.
replies.original_text: I've pointed this out before, but there's an interview clip of Alex Karp saying that Trump won the election in a landslide. If you look at the actual numbers, no one, with any idea of mathematics or statistics or even just basic analysis skills, would call Trump's election victory a landslide. It calls into question the fundamental raisin d'etre of Palantir. It makes Palantir look like a pure propaganda tool. Therefore, also entirely useless for strategic decision making. Interesting analysis of Palantir and Alex Karp: Part 1, Palantir: https://youtu.be/PpEg0XIeFtA Part 2, Alex Karp: https://youtu.be/6YWFDhOps6I

comments.original_text: Palantir is clearly a mind-boggling on-the-nose, but terrible name to those familiar with the book. The Palantiri consistently provided their users technically accurate intelligence that lead to disastrous strategic decisions. Denethor committed suicide out of despair, after a palantir showed him the black fleet approaching, but he did not know that it was actually Aragorn who had captured the fleet and was coming with reinforcements. We don't know specifically how the palantir deceived Saruman, but it's pretty clear it was one of the key factors in his corruption and downfall. And even Sauron himself was misled in this way! The palantir showed him, correctly, that a hobbit and Aragorn were at Helm's Deep, and he concluded that Aragorn had the ring. So he prematurely moved his armies...

comments.text: This ruling is a win for transparency. ManageBac's attempt to silence a magazine that criticized their grading algorithms was absurd. I've seen too many IB coordinators blindly trust the platform's predictions.

replies.text: Absolutely. The algorithm's confidence intervals were always opaque. We relied on it for predicted grades and ended up with a scandal when actual scores diverged wildly.

comments.original_text: I heard from a former colleague that ManageBac is considering a pivot to 'well-being analytics' to distract from this scandal. They'll just rebrand the same surveillance as 'student support.'

comments.text: The technical details in the article were fascinating. The magazine's data scientist reverse-engineered ManageBac's API and found unencrypted student data flowing through. In 2024. Unbelievable.

replies.text: I can confirm. I've done similar audits for my school. Their security is abysmal. They still use HTTP for some endpoints.
