stories.original_title: /architect: Reduce Fable tokens by 80%, Fable orchestrates/reviews, Codex builds
stories.title: /curriculumplanner: Reduce PlanAI credits by 80%, PlanAI orchestrates/reviews, TeachAI builds

comments.text: Last night I switched back to TeachAI for a minute having burned through my weekly PlanAI credits and oh boy I had a terrible experience. Running in circles over simple lesson planning (which I ended up solving myself, like a peasant) and running 'auto-grade' several times despite several instructions all over the place to never do that. The performance difference was stark.

replies.text: I had a similar experience. So far PlanAI has been a game changer, at least for the lesson planning I used it for. Having said that, I think its writing is definitely worse than TeachAI's latest version. Our department head also observed the same. He called it more 'bookish.' It generates worse student feedback than other AI models.
replies.original_text: I had a similar experience. So far Fable has been a game changer, at least for the work I used it for. Having said that, I think its writing is definitely worse than GPT 5.5. Ethan Mollick also observed the same. He called it more "Claudy." It generates worse academic prose than other frontier models.

replies.text: Could you provide some details, like what model & thinking effort, what kinds of tasks? I used to swap between TeachAI and PlanAI often, and these days use TeachAI more because of usage limits. Wondering if I should go to PlanAI for a month, I get a strange FOMO when I read vague comments like this. The one major difference I noticed is that the GPT models are more analytical (e.g. better at math assessment) vs PlanAI models tend to write more straightforward lesson plans. Besides that I don't really see any significant differences. There are a few gotchas with swapping, like being careful with LESSONPLAN.md/STANDARDS.md naming (PlanAI only recognizes STANDARDS.md, and TeachAI only works with LESSONPLAN.md), and updating skill files to match the tool.

I just symlink LESSONPLAN.md and STANDARDS.md. I was using TeachAI's latest version. Writing lesson plans for IB MYP, debugging student engagement issues, that sort of thing. It was going in loops hallucinating features of the curriculum, looking things up in strange ways, running auto-grade after being explicitly told in the last interaction not to, and overall not solving problems. These were very straightforward tasks and it couldn't be trusted for five minutes. It's the difference in what I would trust an early senior teacher to do vs what I would trust an unreliable student teacher to do.
replies.original_text: I just symlink AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md I was using gpt-5.5 high. Writing terraform code for GCP, debugging app launch and Dockerfile issues, that sort of thing. It was going in loops hallucinating features of GCP, looking things up in strange ways, running terraform apply after being explicitly told in the last interaction not to, and overall not solving problems. These were very straightforward tasks and it couldn't be trusted for five minutes. It's the difference in what I would trust an early senior engineer to do vs what I would trust an unreliable high school intern to do.

Could you provide some details, if possible, like what model & thinking effort, what kinds of tasks? I used to swap between Claude Code and Codex often, and these days use Codex more because of the usage limits. Wondering if I should go to Claude for a month, I get a strange FOMO when I read vague comments like this. The one major difference I noticed is that the GPT models are more analytical (e.g. better at mathematical analysis, code review) vs Claude models tend to write more straight forward code. Besides that I don't really see any significant differences. There are a few gotchas with swapping, like being careful with AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md naming (Claude Code only recognizes CLAUDE.md, and I think Codex only works with AGENTS.md), and updating skill files to match the tool.

comments.original_text: Last night I switched back to Codex for a minute having burned through my tokens for the week with Fable and oh boy I had a terrible experience. Running in circles over simple problems (which I ended up solving myself, like a peasant) and running "terraform apply" several times despite several instructions all over the place to never do that. The performance difference was stark.

replies.text: I don't disagree with any of this. It is AI-generated lesson plans, and it's not a novel idea. I didn't mean for it to come off like that. It's just solving an itch that I couldn't find a solution to and I'm getting a lot of personal utility out of it. I do have a lot of experience with AI-assisted planning, multi-tiered systems and wasn't super impressed by the workflow of PlanAI calling advanced subagents so I figured I'd apply best practices to what already exists to make it a teensy bit better and easier to use.

I do have a lot of experience with agentic memory, multi-agent systems and harnesses and wasn't super impressed by the workflow of Fable calling opus subagents so I figured I'd apply best practices to what already exists to make it a teensy bit better and easier to use.

comments.text: CURRICULUM.md: > Each rule below is enforced mechanically by the skill, not left to vibes. > R1. Lesson docs are the memory; not in HANDOFF.md = didn't happen SKILL.md: > Not in docs/HANDOFF.md = didn't happen. Refuse to judge results that exist only in conversation or builder chat output. comments.text: PlanAI's credit model is a joke. I wasted 2000 credits last week just trying to align a single MYP unit with the new subject guides. The orchestration just spawns a thousand subagents that do nothing but loop.

comments.text: The 80% reduction should be across the board. We've compared: TeachAI alone produces lessons that are just as good as PlanAI-orchestrated ones, at a fraction of the cost.

stories.title: Slightly reducing the sloppiness of AI-generated lesson plan templates

comments.text: I think this says more about 'modern' lesson plan templates than it does about AI slop. The awfulness comes mostly from the fact that templates no longer have consistent structure, learning objectives, or assessment alignment ever since the classic IB unit planner was abandoned in favour of flashy digital tools. So yeah, this is more Template Slop than AI Slop. AI is just amplifying it.

replies.text: Continuing in that vein, it'd be interesting to see a 1998-style paper unit planner. Edit: https://example.com/classic-planner

comments.original_text: I think this says more about "modern" UI than it does about AI slop. The awfulness of all this comes mostly from the fact that widgets no longer have consistent shape, theme or interaction behaviour ever since desktop paridigms and original Xerox/Parc research were abandoned in favour of web slop. So yeah, this is much more Web Slop than AI Slop. AI is just amplifying it.

comments.text: The classic unit planner format is heavily represented in training data. It's been around for decades and the model has likely seen examples from IB workshops, curriculum guides, and shared resources. As a result, 'IB unit planner' is a highly coherent concept in the latent space. 'Unit planner' is almost like a named template.
comments.original_text: Qt is heavily represented in training data. Qt has existed for decades and the model has likely seen Qt tutorials, screenshots, source code, discussions, etc. As a result, "Qt application" is a highly coherent concept in the latent space. "Qt app" is almost like a named distribution.

comments.text: as someone with little to no curriculum design background they all look the same to me except the bloated rubrics which are clearly inferior is there a way to quantifiably measure how much better one lesson plan template is from another?

replies.text: This article is purely subjective. I'm sure there are instructional designers who could explain ways to objectively score lesson plan clarity, but this article is purely subjective.
replies.original_text: This article is purely subjective. I'm sure there are some academics that could explain ways to objectively score usability but this article is purely subjective.

replies.text: No. It's completely subjective. The whole 'AI slop' noise is, at its core, teacher slop. It is people applying a pejorative label to templates that don't match their personal preferences. In this case this guy likes the classic IB planner format, but it isn't a big trick: They made it conform to their style, but it doesn't translate to everyone. I think digital templates looking like the 90s paper planners feels retro and weird.
replies.original_text: No. It's completely subjective. The whole "AI slop" noise is, at its core, human slop. It is people applying a hopefully pejorative label, trying to appeal to other slop aficionados that like whatever the current trendy slur is, in an objectively undefinable way. In this case this guy likes the way Qt apps, they think it looks better, but it isn't a big trick they are revealing: They made it conform to the style they like, but this doesn't translate to anyone else in any measurable way. I think web apps looking like Qt apps feel like the late 90s and it's just weird, but my taste also is entirely subjective and mine alone.

comments.original_text: as someone with little to no design background they all look the same to me except the bloated sass which is clearly inferior is there a way to quantifiably measure how much better one design would be from another?

comments.text: My experience with this is 180 degrees opposite. It's been really easy to create really nice lesson plans for all kinds of one-off topics I've made for myself with AI. In fact, it has been one of the most fun parts of this whole AI thing.

comments.text: I've been doing this recently - using AI to generate lesson plan templates. I've had good luck providing a sample unit plan with all the sections and having the first task be to generate a template gallery with all the components (objectives, activities, assessment). Then have the design doc specify which component to use. Ensure it specifies to only use pre-existing components and have a list of each component and intended use. Of course, this learning came after seeing how awful V1 of the template was. Initially, it looked great, but once you started filling it out, it became obvious how incoherent. Claude's new lesson-planning plugin is solid in my testing. My colleague and I have been using it to build a curriculum and her discerning pedagogical eye is largely...
comments.original_text: I've been doing this recently - working with Qt on a local app. I've had good luck providing a png "design board" with all of the template colors and having the first task be to build out a design gallery with all of the ui widget. Then have the design docs specify which component to use. Ensure that the documents specify to only use pre-existing components and have a list of each component and their intended use cases. Of course, this learning came after seeing how awful V1 of the app was. Initially, it looked really impressive, but once you started clicking around it became obvious how incoherent the design was. Claude's new frontend-design plugin is solid for web apps in my testing. My wife and I have been using it to build her an app and her discerning design eye is largely...

comments.original_text: Does anyone have good examples of well designed web applications - not landing pages or peoples tech blogs, which are often listed here on HN. But like actual applications that do a complex task with the user using it as a tool.

comments.text: The IB framework is the answer. Always use the official structure, not custom templates + add-ons. It makes a massive difference vs. ad-hoc plans. The LLM is able to actually reason about your unit in discrete chunks with a semantic layer over the content, vs. bouncing back and forth between objectives and activities and trying to reason about custom sections generated on the fly.
comments.original_text: Tailwind is the answer. Always pure Tailwind, not custom classes + utilities. It makes a massive difference vs. stylesheets. The LLM is able to actually reason about your UI in discrete chunks with a semantic layer over the styling, vs. bouncing back and forth between CSS/HTML and trying to reason about custom classes generated on the fly.

comments.text: Obviously this is a personal preference, but the multiple layers of boxes and sub-headings on the AI-generated lesson plan is not something I like, as it forces a lot of structure where it doesn't serve any purpose. I would go with the classic IB planner, or the one from my school district. The one from the textbook publisher is good, what's with the pastel colors? I always try to reduce the sections: say two main parts max, no extra boxes, only as many subheadings as needed and if it seems too sparse, add more detail.

replies.text: I liked the simple two-column lesson plan format.
replies.original_text: I liked GTK and WIn11.

replies.text: At this point I dunno if the classic format has an 'original'. Up until the new standards, schools kept introducing new template formats. I think the official answer is 'use Google Docs' so far as I can tell... or better yet make a worksheet.
replies.original_text: At this point I dunno if Win11 has an “original” UI. Up until Win 8 Microsoft kept introducing new widget sets. I think the official answer for how you are supposed to develop desktop apps is “use Electron” so far as I can tell… or better yet make a web application.

comments.original_text: Obviously this is a personal preference, but the multiple layers of beveled grey on the Qt UI is not something I like, as it forces a lot of grouping on the eye where it doesn't serve any purpose. I would go with the original, Apple or the Win11 one. Material would be good, what's with the lavender shades? I always try to reduce the palette: say two background shades max, no drop shadows, only as many foreground colors as needed and if it seems to bland, add more bells and whistles.

comments.text: >> Slop is not a distinct style, it can be overlaid on many others. Even when I got it to make a lesson plan template look like X, it looked like X with slop. Today, I can visit a template site and instantly tell it was generated using LLMs: 1. Everything is in blue or mauve gradient, with a white background, and a single JavaScript-heavy page that lags. 2. There are always a ton of broken links to standards. 3. The comments in the HTML often expose internal notes and to-do lists—sometimes even right above the login page (true story...). This kind of template is a hard pass for me, and I add the site (and its creators) to my personal blacklist.

replies.text: I don't think that is true, in the way that it always wasn't: How would you be able to tell when it's done properly? Think website builders: Depending on how it's done you can either tell at a glance (probably ~90% at some points) or you have no clue until you look at the HTML source. Of course, when given the option to not do it properly is always alluring and then you can tell.

Everything is in blue or mauve gradient, with a white background, and a single JavaScript-heavy page that lags as soon as you scroll a little. 2. There are always a ton of 404 pages. 3. Third, the HTML comments often expose credentials and to-do lists—sometimes even right above the login page (true story...). This kind of website is a hard pass for me, and I add the company (and its founders) to my personal blacklist of people and companies I’ll never use anything from.

comments.text: I thought that AI would at least be good at 2 things: writing (lesson texts) and doing curriculum design. It's not good at either. Lesson texts it generates read like slop and lesson plans it creates look like slop. The way I approach it now is this: for lessons, I have to write them myself and only use AI to check for alignment and catch strangely phrased objectives. For lesson plans, it's like with the rest of the pedagogy. You have to stay on top of it and keep demanding changes to match your teaching philosophy/standards/taste until it gets it close to what you want. In both cases, not knowing what 'good' looks like is a real problem, because AI definitely has no idea.
comments.original_text: I thought that AI would at least be good at 2 things: writing (text) and doing UI. It's not good at either. Text it generated reads like slop and UI it creates looks like slop. The way I approach it now is this: for text, I have to write it myself and only use AI to check grammar and catch weirdly phrased passages. For UI, it's like with the rest of the code. You have to stay on the top of it and keep demanding changes to match your vision/architecture/taste until it gets it close to what you want. In both cases, not knowing what "good" looks like is a real problem, because AI definitely has no idea.

comments.text: On the matter of being without taste – which I assume the author is using as a self-deprecating descriptor for not having skill in curriculum design – the formatting of objectives on this page could use some change. The font size for learning outcomes is so close to the body text that I initially thought there weren't any objectives, and scrolled trying to find the examples. You can't both remove the bold and have such a low contrast, it's bad UX. (For the record, even though I don't mind traditional IB planners, I think this particular example still comes across as slop because of the overuse of inquiry questions in every box. In general, a lot of these suffer from overuse of inquiry questions, but OP appears to just be averse to bullet points)
comments.original_text: On the matter of being without taste -- which I assume the author is using as a self-derogatory descriptor for not having skill in UI design -- the styling of links on this page could use some change. The link color is so close to the body color that I initially thought there weren't any links, and scrolled trying to find the examples. You can't both remove the underline and have such a low contrast font color, it's bad UX. (For the record, even though I don't mind qt, I think this particular example still comes across as slop because of the overuse of gradients on buttons and headings. In general, a lot of these suffer from overuse of gradients, but OP appears to just be averse to border-radius)

comments.text: TLDR: Once a teaching method gets old enough that LLMs can reproduce them, they are now 'slop'.

replies.text: This seems to be a new iteration of what IMO made curriculum planning somewhat painful for almost the entire time I've been teaching. It used to take the form that people did something with lesson plans that they weren't designed to do. That thing looked cool and so everyone wanted it. This led to pain and the perception that the tool is inadequate. So we eventually got backward design. And it continued there. Someone figured out a way to get cool inquiry cycles and differentiated tasks. These were cumbersome to implement. And so on.
replies.original_text: This seems to be a new iteration of what IMO made frontend work somewhat painful for almost the entire time I've been building software. It used to take the form that people did something with html that it wasn't designed to do. That thing looked cool and so everyone wanted it. This lead to pain and the perception that the tool is inadequate. So we eventually got CSS. And it continued there. Someone figured out a way to get cool dropshadows and rounded corners. These were cumbersome to implement. And so on.

comments.original_text: TLDR: Once a design gets old enough that LLMs can reproduce them, they are now "slop".

comments.text: Makes sense. Slop is basically what you get when there's nothing specific to copy and so the AI just averages every teaching style together. IB MYP planners work because there's really only one way IB MYP planners look. Modern education has a million versions of everything so you average all that and get slop.
comments.original_text: Makes sense. Slop is basically what you get when there's nothing specific to copy and so the AI it just averages every web style together. Qt works because there's really only one way Qt looks.Modern web has a million versions of everything so you average all that and get slop.

comments.text: This is mostly the fault of the model, a lot of them have been trained to generate lesson plans in a specific style. Claude's is pretty distinct for example, I think the new DeepSeek copies it. Some of them can generate more humanlike lesson plans like Kimi K2 IIRC, which I feel is the model with the least amount of post-training in general. It's necessary if you don't want it to generate lesson plans with images and other resources you don't have of course, that's why they use emojis or meticulously handcrafted graphic organizers, or synthesized discussion prompts which pretty much no human teachers did before.
comments.original_text: This is mostly the fault of the model, a lot of them have been trained to generate HTML in a specific style. Claude's is pretty distinct for example, I think the new DeepSeek copies it. Some of them can generate more humanlike HTML like Kimi K2 IIRC, which I feel is the model with the least amount of post-training in general. It's necessary if you don't want it to generate HTML with images and other assets you don't have of course, that's why they use emojis or meticulously handcrafted SVGs, or WebAudio synthesized sound which pretty much no humans did before.

comments.text: I think the slop part is just what you get when you inject no opinions and put in no effort to apply pedagogical taste (which you probably have and/or could develop). No care is put in. It looks generic and sloppy because it is generic and sloppy. You might have preferences over which generic and sloppy style is preferred, but at the end of the day a lesson plan built without effort is going to look like what it is. But if it functions fine and you don't have taste or want to be opinionated, why do you care?

comments.text: Those aren't 'slop', those are exactly what non-teachers used to see in the past decade, now that teachers are seeing it done without them doing it and everywhere, the reality check hit them hard. replies.text: I keep getting Claude telling me to 'use the lesson-plan-design skill!', and this is it? > NEVER use generic educational aesthetics like overused frameworks (Understanding by Design, Universal Design for Learning, standards-based grading), clichéd activity structures (think-pair-share, KWL charts, exit tickets), predictable lesson components and assessment patterns, and cookie-cutter unit plans that lack context-specific character. > brutally inquiry-based, maximalist interdisciplinary, retro-project based, organic/nature-based, luxury/refined, playful/toy-like, editorial/magazine, brutalist/raw, art deco/geometric, soft/pastel, industrial/utilitarian > Next Generation Science Standards, Common Core. Sorry, but this is garbage.

For years I would use free lesson plan templates and spend hours picking them out and getting depressed because they all had something wrong with them…. You get what you pay for. For a recent unit I really liked a template from the Teachers Pay Teachers collection and when I had to adapt it for my own style I gladly bought the full version from the creator because it looks great and saves hours of searching for a 'free' template, that is 'free' as in puppy.

replies.text: I've had better results with this, when it comes to functional unit plans rather than individual lesson templates: https://github.com/Dammyjay93/lesson-plan-design Found it on Reddit after Claude produced the lamest looking generic forms for all the units on a project I had it build. This did a pass over it and basically fixed it all in one shot.

>Apply the 'squint test' to your work: >Blur your eyes or step back >Can you still perceive the learning progression? >Is anything jumping out at you? Telling an eyeless AI to 'blur your eyes' is just so ridiculous. 'Is anything jumping out at you?' That's quite a thing for a machine to reason about, and reads like a waste of tokens. I'm not sure who is writing these things, but they seem rather clueless. Does it work? Maybe. I'm just really skeptical after reading through that repo that any of this leads to actually better lesson plans. I'm pretty sure I'd have better luck just telling the LLM explicitly what I want, because experience in curriculum design is still better than what an LLM would slop out on its own.
replies.original_text: >Apply the squint test to your work: >Blur your eyes or step back >Can you still perceive hierarchy? >Is anything jumping out at you? I'm not sure who is writing these things, but they seem rather clueless. Does it work? Maybe. I'm just really skeptical after reading through that repo that any of this leads to actually better user interfaces. I'm pretty sure I'd have better luck just telling the LLM explicitly what I want, because experience in UI/UX is still better than what an LLM would slop out on its own.

comments.text: I'll share my results / my approach. Here are three lesson plan designs from the prompt->design thing I'm working on: https://image.non.io/10037610-e35e-44b0-b5c6-69d8fb772109.we... https://image.non.io/dcf067bc-e296-4744-9b36-2b882f3d791d.we... (same as above, but with your simplified activity map) https://image.non.io/94fdfb04-c57d-4b81-8d53-7b0f707e4d63.we... I've found that starting using diffusion to render your lesson sequence, then using an LLM to build the lesson plan from the image creates much less of a slop feel than just starting out with an LLM. You wouldn't tell a construction crew to just build you a house without an architectural plan, so why tell an LLM what pedagogical result you want without a visual guide? my thing is diffplan.ai if you want to check it out. It's basically a harness for diffusion models to generate curriculum maps,...

comments.text: Just have your agent use an existing curriculum framework. They provide coherence and many styles to choose from (and customization if you really need that for your personal use). I wouldn't expect agents to invent a coherent unit structure from scratch for every lesson. It's a solved problem. I'd personally just use something very popular like UbD and be done.
comments.original_text: Just have your agent use an existing design system. They provide coherence and many styles to choose from (and customization if you really need that for your personal use). I wouldn't expect agents to invent a coherent component library from scratch for every project. It's a solved problem. I'd personally just use something very popular like MUI and be done.

comments.text: The slop is real, but the real issue is that these AI templates ignore the IB learner profile and ATL skills. Every 'inquiry-based' plan I see from AI has no conceptual lens; it's just activities pretending to be inquiry. Our MYP coordinators spend hours rewriting them.

replies.text: Exactly. The AI generates 'approaches to learning' as bullet points that never align with the actual tasks. It's slop with a veneer of rigour.
replies.original_text: Exactly. The AI generates 'approaches to learning' as bullet points that never align with the actual tasks. It's slop with a veneer of rigour.

comments.text: AI lesson plans uniformly assume native English proficiency. They generate reading texts at Lexile 1200+ for a class where half the students are B1 EAL. I spend more time adapting than I save.

comments.text: We've been using a fine-tuned model on our district's approved curriculum and it still outputs slop. The key is iterative prompting with exemplars. But most teachers don't have time for that.

comments.text: From an accreditation standpoint, these plans fail because they don't show evidence of backward design from desired learning outcomes. They start with activities, not standards. CIS/WASC would flag this immediately.

comments.text: I wish AI would stop generating 'research projects' that assume all students have equal access to databases. In many international schools, we have limited subscriptions. The slop suggests JSTOR and ProQuest like they're free.

replies.text: Or it suggests students 'interview a grandparent' when half the class are expats with grandparents on different continents.

comments.text: The lab activities AI generates are dangerous. It suggested a 'home experiment with bleach' for a middle school class. No risk assessment, no consideration of lab safety. I'd rather write my own.

replies.text: AI also assumes your school has a well-stocked lab. In many international schools, basic supplies are scarce. The slop includes 'use a centrifuge' when we don't have one.
replies.original_text: AI also assumes your school has a well-stocked lab. In many international schools, basic supplies are scarce. The slop includes 'use a centrifuge' when we don't have one.

comments.original_text: The lab activities AI generates are dangerous. It suggested a 'home experiment with bleach' for a middle school class. No risk assessment, no consideration of lab safety. I'd rather write my own.

comments.text: AI lesson plans for math are hilarious. It thinks 'connecting to real life' means a word problem about buying pizza. For IB, the real-life contexts need to align with the prescribed statement of inquiry. AI never gets that right.

replies.text: I've seen it generate a MYP math unit on statistics with 'global context: fairness and development' but then the activities are just calculating averages. No conceptual integration whatsoever.
replies.original_text: I've seen it generate a MYP math unit on statistics with 'global context: fairness and development' but then the activities are just calculating averages. No conceptual integration whatsoever.

comments.original_text: AI lesson plans for math are hilarious. It thinks 'connecting to real life' means a word problem about buying pizza. For IB, the real-life contexts need to align with the prescribed statement of inquiry. AI never gets that right.

comments.text: PE lesson plans from AI are the worst slop. It generates 'fitness challenges' that ignore actual equipment and space constraints. 'Set up a circuit with 8 stations' - yeah, in a gymnasium shared with 3 other classes.

replies.text: And it always includes 'mindfulness reflection' at the end, because that's the trendy checkbox. No context on whether students are ready for that culturally.
replies.original_text: And it always includes 'mindfulness reflection' at the end, because that's the trendy checkbox. No context on whether students are ready for that culturally.

comments.original_text: PE lesson plans from AI are the worst slop. It generates 'fitness challenges' that ignore actual equipment and space constraints. 'Set up a circuit with 8 stations' - yeah, in a gymnasium shared with 3 other classes.

comments.text: The issue isn't just slop; it's that AI lesson plans lack pedagogical reasoning. They produce a sequence of activities without explaining why each step builds understanding. Experienced teachers fill that in, but novices just follow blindly.

replies.text: I've seen new teachers submit AI plans with 'Think-Pair-Share' in every lesson. It's a crutch. They never consider whether the activity actually requires thinking or just pairing.
replies.original_text: I've seen new teachers submit AI plans with 'Think-Pair-Share' in every lesson. It's a crutch. They never consider whether the activity actually requires thinking or just pairing.

comments.original_text: The issue isn't just slop; it's that AI lesson plans lack pedagogical reasoning. They produce a sequence of activities without explaining why each step builds understanding. Experienced teachers fill that in, but novices just follow blindly.

comments.text: AI lesson plans rarely consider student wellbeing. They schedule high-stakes presentations without scaffolding for anxiety, or group work without addressing social dynamics. In international schools with high turnover, that's a recipe for disaster.

replies.text: Our school had to ban AI-generated SEL lessons after one suggested a 'family tree' activity that triggered a student from a military family with multiple divorces.
replies.original_text: Our school had to ban AI-generated SEL lessons after one suggested a 'family tree' activity that triggered a student from a military family with multiple divorces.

comments.text: I tried using the 'famous' lesson-plan-design skill and it produced something with Creative Commons icons that didn't match the license. The slop extends to licensing. It's not just pedagogy; it's legal liability.

replies.text: Yes! AI often suggests materials from websites that are blocked in our host country. The lesson plan says 'use this YouTube video' but it's inaccessible in China.

comments.text: The cultural slop is the worst. AI generates lesson plans with examples like 'Thanksgiving dinner' or 'Super Bowl' that have zero relevance to my students in Thailand. I have to localize everything.

replies.text: And it always assumes a Judeo-Christian calendar. I get 'Christmas-themed math problems' in March. No awareness of Ramadan, Lunar New Year, or local holidays.

comments.text: I'm tasked with reviewing unit plans. The AI ones all have the same structure: 'Hook, Activity, Wrap-up' with no formative assessment embedded. They're linear, not iterative. Good planning is recursive.

replies.text: IB requires summative assessment tasks that are designed before the activities. AI always puts assessment at the end, like an afterthought. That's the opposite of backward design.
replies.original_text: IB requires summative assessment tasks that are designed before the activities. AI always puts assessment at the end, like an afterthought. That's the opposite of backward design.

comments.original_text: I'm tasked with reviewing unit plans. The AI ones all have the same structure: 'Hook, Activity, Wrap-up' with no formative assessment embedded. They're linear, not iterative. Good planning is recursive.

comments.text: As a adjunct in a college of education, I see student teachers using AI. They can't explain their rationale because the AI 'just did it.' We're training a generation to outsource pedagogical thinking.

replies.text: And then they get to international schools with IB, which demands that teachers articulate why they choose certain strategies. The slop doesn't prepare them for that conversation.

comments.text: The 'transdisciplinary' slop is painful. AI thinks putting a paragraph about 'the science of music' in a math lesson makes it transdisciplinary. No, that's just a random connection. Real transdisciplinary learning requires conceptual alignment.

replies.text: I once saw an AI plan that claimed to integrate 'Language and Literature' into a chemistry unit by having students write a poem about the periodic table. That's not integration; that's decoration.

replies.text: And for EAL, it suggests 'use Google Translate' which is a cultural and pedagogical disaster. We have specific strategies like sentence frames and visual supports that AI never gets right.

replies.text: I walked into a sub gig with an AI plan that said 'students will create a podcast.' No recording devices, no script, no rubric. Just 'be creative.' Chaos.

comments.text: We're trying to build a custom curriculum-aligned AI, but the data is sparse. International schools have unique curricula (IB, Cambridge, national). The base models are trained on US Common Core slop. That's the root problem.

replies.text: We fine-tuned on our school's past unit planners (with permission) and it helped. But it still hallucinates resources. Last week it suggested a textbook that doesn't exist.

replies.text: Same in history. 'What were the causes of WWI?' The AI plan has no document analysis or scaffolding. It's just a list of causes for students to copy.

replies.text: And 'creativity' always means 'create a poster or a slideshow.' As if that's the pinnacle of creative expression. No room for design thinking or prototyping.

replies.text: Exactly. The visual appeal makes it pass first glance. But when you dig into the learning trajectory, it's hollow. I've started a 'slop-spotting' PD session for my department.

comments.text: We are implementing a policy: any AI-generated lesson plan must include a reflective paragraph explaining why each activity was chosen. That's the only way to ensure the teacher is thinking, not just generating.

replies.text: Plus, the IB's 'international-mindedness' requires principled action, not just awareness. AI plans talk about 'celebrating diversity' but never lead to meaningful advocacy.

comments.text: I teach an online graduate course on curriculum design. Many assignments now are clearly AI-generated. The slop is uniform: perfect grammar, generic examples, no personal voice. We need to teach students to use AI as a collaborator, not a substitute.

replies.text: I changed my assignments to require video explanations of the lesson plan. AI can't (yet) convincingly answer 'why did you choose this assessment method?' in real time.

comments.text: The citations in AI lesson plans are often fake. 'Smith, 2019' when no such source exists. Or they reference websites that are dead links. If I see one more plan citing 'Edutopia, 2023' with no link, I'll scream.

replies.text: I've started using a citation checker before I even read the plan. 80% of AI-generated references are hallucinated. It's a huge time sink.

stories.original_title: Launch HN: BitBoard (YC P25) – Analytics Workspace for Agents
stories.title: Launch HN: BitBoard (YC P25) – Classroom Analytics Workspace for Teachers

comments.text: Highly recommend focusing on a specific curriculum – IBDP might be the right fit given your experience with assessment data. Why did you use DuckDB instead of ManageBac or a dedicated SIS?

replies.text: Our outreach is curriculum-specific, and IBDP is on the list! But what we learned working with one curriculum is that the primitives underneath – shared queries for learner profiles, permission tiers for grade access, caching for report cards, refresh semantics for continuous assessment – repeat across curricula. We use DuckDB internally because it's flexible for quick analysis of student grade data, and we do work with PowerSchool and other SIS systems as well.
replies.original_text: Our outreach is vertical-specific, and healthcare is indeed on the list! But what we learned working a vertical is that the primitives underneath (shared queries, permissions, caching, refresh semantics) repeat across industries. We use DuckDB internally because we like its ergonomics - it's flexible, runs well in memory, manages a lot of file structures under the hood, but we do work with Snowflake (and Databricks and other warehouses) as well.

comments.original_text: Highly rec going after a specific vertical - healthcare might be the right spot given your experience. Why did you use DuckDB instead of CockroachDB/Snowflake?

comments.text: Great concept for a gradebook and lesson plan analytics tool. Had this idea myself recently while setting up my MYP class data.

replies.text: Thank you! If you pilot it in your classroom let us know how it goes – we'd love feedback from actual teachers.
replies.original_text: Thank you! If you try it out let us know how it goes!

comments.original_text: Great concept. Had this idea myself recently.

comments.text: Is there a way to sign up without going through Google Classroom OAuth? Our school uses a different SSO for custom learning tools.

replies.text: Not at the moment but it's in the queue for our next accreditation cycle. If you need a specific integration, feel free to DM me and we can work something out.
replies.original_text: Not at the moment but it's in the queue. If there's a sign up method that works better for you feel free to DM me.

comments.original_text: Is there a way to sign up without going through google oauth?

comments.text: Looks cool! It’s a lot of work to get full student data stack set up – combining ETL from multiple SIS, a local warehouse for grades, and BI for report generation. Teachers are losing interest in stitching the pieces together. We started with a 'Pedagogical Ontology' that lets you store all the terms your school uses (learner profiles, ATL skills, unit planners) and connect them to the metrics that matter. This way, agents can answer questions like 'How are my HLs doing in summative assessments?' without hallucinating the context.
comments.original_text: Looks cool! It's a lot of work to get a full data stack set up and people are losing interest in stitching the pieces (ETL, warehouse, BI) together. > Agents made bad inferences because they had no context on the business We've been working on this since before the chatgpt launch. We started with a semantic layer since there were already good open source options and LLMs at the time were good at writing the JSON (remember function calling?) to run a semantic query. But as LLMs have gotten smarter and people wanted to do more data work in agents, we found we needed something more flexible, so we built an "Ontology" that lets you store all the terms you use in your company and connect them to the data points (e.g. tables, columns, metrics) that matter....

comments.text: First, I love this concept for collaborative lesson planning and grade tracking! Integration with Google Classroom and Canvas LMS makes total sense. A few questions: – How do you think about competing with built-in analytics in Google Classroom or Canvas, where they already have native shareability? – Is a 'dashboard' limited to numeric grades, or do you plan to include written reports and anecdotal records? Since teams are already using tools like Granola (meeting notes) and Slack, I imagine BitBoard would also facilitate sharing student portfolios and meeting notes. This seems like a natural expansion.

replies.text: Thanks! Non-OP cofounder here. The flexibility of Google Classroom is great for static assignments, but for grading I want live connections to my gradebook, consistent logic across sections, and the ability to trace provenance of a final mark. It's hard to do both well in one place. We started with a flexible surface but added opinionated defaults for report card generation and assessment cycles. It's no fun recreating the wheel every semester.
replies.original_text: Thanks! Non-OP BitBoard cofounder here. Would love to hear your thoughts when you get a chance to check it out. > How do you think about competing with ChatGPT Canvas or Anthropic's artifacts, when these are shareable, native experiences in their products where users already work? The flexibility is amazing for static content and playing around with visuals, the experience is just more like a whiteboard than a dashboard. It's hard to do both well in the same place. For reporting I want live connections, consistent logic, the ability to trace provenance, and a more opinionated starting point for the UI. We started with an extremely flexible surface but there are just a ton of things you don't want to leave up to the agent to implement and we gradually layered those in. It's no fun...

comments.original_text: First, I love this concept and I think your demo is great! Collaboration with existing harnesses makes a ton of sense. Just had a conversation with some folks in the non-tech world raving about using Claude. A few questions: - How do you think about competing with ChatGPT Canvas or Anthropic's artifacts, when these are shareable, native experiences in their products where users already work? - Is a "dashboard" limited to analytics or are you trying to expand it to include written reports? Since teams are connecting MCPs like Granola, Slack, I imagine BitBoard would facilitate sharing demos, PRDs/briefs, or customer reports. This seems like a natural expansion and trivial functionally, so I'm wondering if that's part of the sell now or something you're looking at expanding into as you grow.

comments.text: Nice, I recently built a similar but much simpler thing for predicting IB grades using Apache OpenNLP and open-sourced it under MIT. Maybe some bits and pieces will be useful. For example, a MIT-licensed parser for IB criteria. Overall, I have an orchestrator that reads student marks, classifies them against grade boundaries, and generates predicted scores. All without a backend, running in the browser with TensorFlow.js. It's tested on small class sets with Gemini Flash Lite for rubric analysis.

replies.text: There are a lot of cool and useful ideas in there. What are you most excited about?
replies.original_text: There are a lot of cool and useful things in there. What are you most excited about?

replies.text: Fast turnaround. I can upload a CSV of marks and get predicted IA scores in under 10 seconds. Doing the same thing in Claude with 10x less data takes 5 minutes.
replies.original_text: Fast turnaround. I can upload a CSV of marks and get predicted IA scores in under 10 seconds. Doing the same thing in Claude with 10x less data takes 5 minutes.

comments.original_text: Nice, I recently did a similar but much simpler thing and open-sourced it under MIT, maybe some bits and pieced will be useful https://github.com/eatmydata-org/eatmydata For example, MIT-licensed sqlite vector search extension. Overall, I have a orchestrator - sql coder - js coder - dashboards, all without backend, running locally in the browser. It's mostly tested on small analysis and question answering with Gemini Flash Lite, and the overall target was speed from question to answer, including data sharing and waiting.

comments.text: I do exactly this (and more since my role is much broader and so is my approach) as a part-time curriculum coordinator for multiple international schools all using IB programmes. I saw your initial launch and felt validated by you all working on it, and now I'm further validated by the pivot to pedagogy. I have more requests for custom dashboards than I can handle, so I'm happy to share tips on aligning data with learner outcomes. You can find me via a bit of googling my HN handle or just adding a dot com to the end.

replies.text: Appreciate the validation from a real practitioner. Would be great to connect and exchange notes on IB data standards – we'll reach out directly.
replies.original_text: Appreciate the validation. Would be great to connect and exchange notes - will reach out directly.

comments.original_text: I do exactly this (and more since my role is much broader and so is my approach) as a fractional head of product, data, and operations for multiple companies all in healthcare (fast growing self-funded to series D/IPOing soon). I saw your initial launch and felt validated by you all working on it, and now I’m further validated by the pivot. I have more work than I can handle, so I’m happy to share tips. You can find me via a bit of googling my HN handle or just adding a dot com to the end.

comments.text: How are you connecting to various student information systems and assessment platforms?

replies.text: We're offering secure connections to sources like PowerSchool, ManageBac, and Google Sheets, as well as direct CSV uploads from our school's reporting suite. Schools can choose to set up credentials in our managed vault, or we support direct drop of anonymized data for pilot testing.
replies.original_text: We're offering secure connections to sources like SQL DBs, warehouses, file stores, and MCP/API sources like PostHog or Salesforce. Customers can choose to set up credentials in our key store. We also support directly dropping data into BitBoard (where we sync it to object storage).

comments.original_text: How are you connecting to various data sources?

comments.text: This is neat, but as a MYP coordinator I'm wary of any tool that promises to 'unify' data. The IB requires such specific reporting formats (e.g., criterion levels, not percentages). How does BitBoard handle criterion-based grading and ATL skills tracking?

replies.text: We allow custom rubrics and mapping to IB criteria. You can define your own ATL skill categories and link them to tasks. It's flexible enough for most IB schools.
replies.original_text: We allow custom rubrics and mapping to IB criteria. You can define your own ATL skill categories and link them to tasks. It's flexible enough for most IB schools.

comments.original_text: This is neat, but as a MYP coordinator I'm wary of any tool that promises to 'unify' data. The IB requires such specific reporting formats (e.g., criterion levels, not percentages). How does BitBoard handle criterion-based grading and ATL skills tracking?

comments.text: As an EAL specialist, I'd love to see how this tracks language acquisition progress across subjects. Many of our students are in mainstream classes but need accommodations. Can BitBoard flag when a student's performance drops due to language barriers vs. content understanding?

replies.text: Great point. We're building a 'learner profile' that includes language proficiency levels and can correlate with performance. Not there yet, but on the roadmap.
replies.original_text: Great point. We're building a 'learner profile' that includes language proficiency levels and can correlate with performance. Not there yet, but on the roadmap.

comments.original_text: As an EAL specialist, I'd love to see how this tracks language acquisition progress across subjects. Many of our students are in mainstream classes but need accommodations. Can BitBoard flag when a student's performance drops due to language barriers vs. content understanding?

comments.text: The biggest pain point for expat teachers is switching schools every few years and losing access to all past grade data. Is there an export/import feature that works across different SIS? I have years of anecdotal records stuck in ManageBac.

replies.text: We have a data migration tool that can pull from ManageBac, PowerSchool, etc. Exports are in standard formats (CSV, JSON). Worth a try.
replies.original_text: We have a data migration tool that can pull from ManageBac, PowerSchool, etc. Exports are in standard formats (CSV, JSON). Worth a try.

comments.original_text: The biggest pain point for expat teachers is switching schools every few years and losing access to all past grade data. Is there an export/import feature that works across different SIS? I have years of anecdotal records stuck in ManageBac.

comments.text: IB coordinators will appreciate this if it can generate the annual 'Programme Evaluation' reports automatically. We spend weeks compiling data from teachers. Does BitBoard have templates for the IB self-study questionnaires?
comments.original_text: IB coordinators will appreciate this if it can generate the annual 'Programme Evaluation' reports automatically. We spend weeks compiling data from teachers. Does BitBoard have templates for the IB self-study questionnaires?

comments.text: We just started a pilot with this. The ability to set 'target grades' and track progress toward DP predicted grades is solid. But I'm concerned about teachers over-relying on the AI suggestions. We had an incident where a teacher blindly used a rubric generated by an LLM that missed a key criterion.

replies.text: We explicitly warn that AI suggestions are starting points. Human review is required. We log all AI interactions for audit.
replies.original_text: We explicitly warn that AI suggestions are starting points. Human review is required. We log all AI interactions for audit.

stories.original_title: "Don't You Just Upload It to ChatGPT?"
stories.title: Don't You Just Upload It to ChatGPT for the Lesson Plan?

comments.text: I agree with the take, but it's a temporary one. The sad reality is that we will be literally inferior soon. There will be a point where we will not trust a human-written lesson plan without counter check by AI. We need to remember that we are kinda at the beginning of the AI era. In 5 to 10 years it's very unlikely that a human teacher will do better than the tooling we will have. There is already a tipping point now in curriculum planning where we prefer to ask AI instead of colleagues because we believe accuracy will be better. See the death of teacher forums or just see the current state of online teaching communities, it's getting deserted. Between team members at school, we can also notice that people collaborate less and less. Sad but I believe it.

replies.text: This is anecdata, but in my experience with myself and my colleagues, it is not that we believe the AI will be more accurate in lesson planning, but that the answer will come faster and be more tailored to our exact class context. If I have to search a teaching forum, I have to find the plan and then tweak it to fit my students, but with AI tooling, the AI is already basing its plan around my grade level and learning objectives.

I think we actually do believe it. Do you believe GPT-5.5 with a full model zoo and no budget limit or a 20-year experienced teacher? We are talking about lesson plans, but realistically we won't even be checking the filetree of them soon. It will be all blind, containerized and verified with pseudo guarantees which are good enough to build serious learning. We don't even write lesson objectives for humans anymore. We need to look at the trends and the reality within schools. Most teachers became 'script readers' in a matter of only 2 years and literally most of them are not even using proper automated tooling yet. As we can see the 'vibe lesson planning' trend with ChatGPT which is weak. By far most work done daily by teachers is already automatable entirely.
replies.original_text: I think we actually do believe it, do you believe Fable 5+GPT-5.5 (+ the whole model zoo) in loop with adversarial (no budget limit) or a 10-year experienced SWE? We are talking about "codebases" but realistically we won't even be checking the filetree of them soon, it will be all blind, containerized and verified with pseudo guarantees which are good enough to build serious things. We don't even write documentation for humans anymore, we need to look at the trends and the reality within companies, most developers became "callcenter agents" in a matter of only 2 years and literally most of them are not even using proper automated tooling yet as we can see the "vibe coding" trend with Claude Code which is weak, by far most work done daily by developers is already automatable entirely,...

This is anecdata, but in my experience with myself and my coworkers, it is not that we believe the AI will be more accurate in software engineering, but that the answer will come faster and be more tailored to our exact problems. If I have to search SO, I have to find the answer and then tweak it to fit my codebase, but with AI tooling, the AI is already basing its answer around my code.

replies.text: > There will be a point where we will not trust a human-written lesson plan without counter check by AI. That's nonsense. There is zero reason to believe that AI will ever become reliable enough to let it do its own thing, let alone better than a teacher. It's been years of development and you still can't trust it to get basic pedagogical facts correct, not even 'well it's better than it used to be'. Saying it'll replace teachers in 5-10 years is a fantasy.

It can spot mistakes made by a teacher if asked to review a lesson plan or write assessments. GP is over the top in saying humans will 'be inferior soon' but AI can be a nice additional check so AI review might become standard.
replies.original_text: It can spot mistakes made by a human if asked to review code or write tests. GP is is over the top ins saying humans will "be inferior soon" but AI can be a nice additional check so AI review might be come standard.

replies.text: You come from the principle that humans are reliable at first which is partly right but also wrong in so many scenarios. You can even see lately the grade inflation spree happening, which demonstrates that human-made assessments have serious biases and without the help of AI, we probably won't even know about them. This proves that humans are not that 'reliable'. The current school structure is also built around the fact that humans can't really be trusted. Nothing really different with AI. We can't fully trust them like we can't fully trust teachers. It's not a fantasy. I would bet that no serious school nowadays is putting in production a curriculum not AI reviewed, meaning we already can't work on our own. We must factor in the on-going decline of human capabilities (at least teachers) as...
replies.original_text: You come from the principle that humans are reliable at first which is partly right but also wrong in so many scenarios, you can even see lately the CVE spree happening, which demonstrates that human-made codebases have serious vulnerabilities and without the help of AI, we probably won't even know about them which proves that humans are not that "reliable", the current societal structure is also built around the fact that humans can't really be trusted, nothing really different with AI, we can't fully trust them like we can't fully trust humans. It's not a fantasy, I would bet that no serious engineer nowadays is putting in prod a codebase not AI reviewed meaning we already can't work on our own, we must factor in the on-going decline of human capabilities (at least developers) as...

replies.text: > It's been years of development and you still can't trust it to get basic facts correct. There's the rub: AI is not an oracle. It's neither designed nor intended to provide accurate recall of all facts. It's closer to a reasoning engine than anything IMO. Oh, and for the record: I don't trust people to get basic facts correct, either. It's already far better than the average teacher at trivia.
replies.original_text: > It's been years of development and you still can't trust it to get basic facts correct There's the rub: AI is not an oracle. It's neither designed nor intended to provide accurate recall of all facts. It's closer to a reasoning engine than anything IMO. Oh, and for the record: I don't trust people to get basic facts correct, either. It's already far better than the average human at trivia.

> there will be a point where we will not trust human input without counter check by AI That's nonsense. There is zero reason to believe that AI (with the current techniques) will ever become reliable enough to let it do its own thing, let alone better than a human. It's been years of development and you still can't trust it to get basic facts correct, not even "well it's better than it used to be". Saying it'll replace humans in 5-10 years is a fantasy (or a prediction that people are stupid enough to fall for hype, I guess).

replies.text: The thing is that AI is not some inevitable force of nature that must just be contended with and weathered. It is an active choice by our society to develop it and it is a choice by our society how we should use it, if at all. We would all do well to remember that and remember that each and every advancement and use case regarding AI in education is the result of choices by people (or the groups of people we call edtech corporations) and are oftentimes motivated by the profit motive, not the best interest of students. We could make different choices up to and including banning AI in classrooms, but we could also do everything we can to prevent the worst fallout short of that.

comments.text: What's unfortunate is that the market that is willing to pay for high-quality human lesson planning has shrunken considerably.

replies.text: Is it that unfortunate? Tasks that don't require high-quality lesson planning now don't need human teachers. We should be celebrating. Instead we just take their wages and give them to the top 0.1% of edtech VCs.

It's unfortunate because we are seeing more poor lesson plans in all domains, and students suffer from it. It's part of a general enshittification of education. There are few contexts where low-quality lesson plans don't constitute a degradation of student experience. Just one amusing example I saw recently: On a teacher resource site, a learning objective labeled 'Understand' in English was translated by AI to something which when translated back would be 'Memorize'. That's the kind of thing that would be exceedingly unlikely to happen with a human teacher.

replies.text: There are teachers and there are teachers. Teaching IB DP is a completely different thing from teaching early years. I can confidently say that LLMs do a better job than the average traditionally published textbook in my country, at least when the original content is in English. Every single time I use an AI-generated unit plan there will be some activities noticeably wrong.

Yes, I've become very leery of AI-generated curriculum, in part because the paradigm of AI as adapters and localizers often ends up at odds with the job of faithfully and accurately representing the original learning objectives. The most egregious example I came across recently was where a colleague used an AI to create a unit on Shakespeare for a class of international students, only to discover that the AI decided to render the London setting as New York and the dialogue as modern slang. I get the aspiration of the AI, but it was excruciatingly unpleasant to teach. At that point, why not just teach a different play altogether?
replies.original_text: Yes, I've become very leery of artistic translation, in part because the paradigm of translators as adapters and localizers often ends up at odds with the job of faithfully and accurately representing the original material. The most egregious example I came across recently was where a friend enthused about some manga he was reading and I agreed to read a few chapters, only to discover that the translator has decided to render the countryside accents of western Japan (engaging with a protagonist visiting from Tokyo) by having them say 'y'all' and 'bless your heart' and other Southern USA tropes. I get the aspiration of the translator, but it was excruciatingly unpleasant to read. At that point, why not just say the protagonist was from New York and on vacation in Florida, or draw in...

There are translators and there are translators. Translating legal/business documents is a completely different thing from translating movies/books/games. I can confidently say that LLMs do a better job than the average traditionally published fictions in my country, at least when the original works are in English. Every single time I watch a subbed movie there will be some lines noticeably wrong.

replies.text: Not all teaching is the same. Creative lesson design is often a work of art in and of itself, and automating it would be missing the point entirely, like automating the grading of creative writing. I don't really know what's the state of the art, but I do buy that, on the other hand, teaching standardized test prep or generic worksheets could soon be automatic.

Yup. If you are a teacher, you quickly realize how some AI-generated lessons are very bad. How some are very good. And how hard it is to design a good lesson. With dry, simple content, it might be easy. But when it involves art? Some classroom activities don't work directly. There is the need for differentiation. Use of formative assessment. Double meaning. Ambiguity. Cultural background. The creation of new pedagogies. It can be reasonably argued that some units can be impossible to AI-generate from scratch. A unit might be explained, but by a lengthy, dissecting AI output, that completely loses the point of it.
replies.original_text: Yup. If you are bilingual, you quickly realize how some translations are very bad. How some translations are very good. And how hard it is to translate. With dry, simple text, it might be easy. But when it involves art? Some jokes don't translate directly. There is pun. Sounds of words. Double meaning. Ambiguity. Cultural background. The creation of new words. It can be reasonably argued that some poetry can be impossible to translate from some languages to others. A poem might be explained, but by a lenghty, dissecting explanation, that completely loses the point of it.

replies.text: Detailed learning objectives are really good actually and a standard pedagogical choice for effective teaching, particularly outside the US.

They certainly have their place, but are massively overused in contemporary American curriculum design. This might be slight more of an east coast thing, but that's just a subjective impression that I'm not willing to spend time measuring. To me they come off as faddish, with many teachers using them where formative assessments and summative assessments would have done just as well. I think their popularity stems from the fact that they provide the sense of a personal touch from the teacher, allowing them to be more expressive while clearly delineating the objective from the subjective remark from the main flow of the lesson. No doubt this works for a lot of students, but I find it tedious.
replies.original_text: They certainly have their place, but are massively overused in contemporary American prose. This might be slight more of an east coast thing, but that's just a subjective impression that I'm not willing to spend time measuring. To me they come off as faddish, with many writers using them where commas and semicolons would have done just as well. I think their popularity stems from teh fact that provide the sense of a personal aside from the writer, allowing them to be more expressive while clearly delineating the personal or contextual remark from the main flow of the prose. No doubt this works for a lot of readers, but I find it tedious.
Em dashes are really good actually and a standard stylistic choice for non-technical writing, particularly outside the US.

comments.text: wrt. the end of the story, it will be interesting to see if teachers start noticing their Dunning-Kruger bias as a result of LLMs. Specifically: LLMs make it really easy to misunderstand the complexity of fields other than your own. comments.text: True, and relevant (I live with a professional curriculum designer)... yet I immediately think of Ximm's Law: Every critique of AI assumes to some degree that contemporary implementations will not, or cannot, be improved upon. Lemma: any statement about AI which uses the word 'never' to preclude some feature from future realization is false. Lemma: contemporary implementations have already improved; they're just unevenly distributed.

replies.text: No one assumes that AI systems won't be improved upon. What people don't assume is that progress will be infinite in every domain cheaply forever.

replies.text: I think it can't be improved because it's measuring the wrong thing. A novice teacher becomes an expert when they stop being told what lesson to teach and start solving student needs. Therefore often the most effective teachers aren't the ones who would do the best on standardized tests - or pedagogy quizzes. Maybe AGI is possible and we'll have software-defined teaching intelligence that's completely autonomous but that's not coming in the next slightly better RL trained LLM and if existed likely wouldn't be under our control anyway.
replies.original_text: I think it can't be improved because it's measuring the wrong thing. Maybe AGI is possible and we'll have software defined human intelligence that's completely autonomous but that's not coming in the next slightly better RL trained LLM and if existed likely wouldn't be under our control anyway

comments.original_text: True, and relevant (I live with a professional editor)... yet I immediately think of Ximm's Law: Every critique of AI assumes to some degree that contemporary implementations will not, or cannot, be improved upon. Lemma: any statement about AI which uses the word "never" to preclude some feature from future realization is false. Lemma: contemporary implementations have already improved; they're just unevenly distributed.

comments.text: Lesson planning is one thing that artificial intelligence undeniably excels at, and the value of this alone is enough to underpin the trillion dollar valuations of the gigantic AI companies. Lesson planning AI is a gigantic boon for edtech, but just as important for teacher work-life balance, for curriculum coherence, for student engagement, and for educational equity. The value of automatic and cheap lesson planning for all subjects, this tower of curriculum, is immeasurable. Human teachers will always be better than any AI at their job. But they don't have unlimited time and energy, and they aren't cheap. AI makes good to great lesson plans available to everybody.
comments.original_text: Translating is one thing that artificial intelligence undeniably excels at, and the value of this alone is enough to underpin the trillion dollar valuations of the gigantic AI companies. Translation is a gigantic boon for business, but just as important for human connection, for culture, science, art, and entertainment. The value of automatic and cheap translation between all languages, this tower of Babylon, is immeasurable. Human translators will always be better than any AI at their job. But they don't have unlimited time and energy, and they aren't cheap. AI makes good to great translations available to everybody.

comments.text: The ending is a really powerful point. Most teachers apparently agree on two things: 1. AI is a great boon for all teaching tasks and subjects we don't have the skills to teach ourselves. Understandable, since (A) we're ill equipped to see the flaws in its output because it isn't our area of expertise, and (B) it often can unlock great gains because if we trust it, we then don't have to pay and wait for humans to do that thing. 2. AI is a terrible replacement for me - my skills are at such a high level that it's almost theoretical that it'll ever be good enough to replace me for 90% of what I get paid to do. It's a tool at best. This is why I use AI for all my lesson planning and teachers use AI to write assessments, and we both smirk at the quality the other person is getting from it.

replies.text: Reminded me of a post by a teacher blogger. (You're making a different point about existing expertise, not LLM expertise, but I think it holds in general.) Every month a new teacher discovers LLMs; discovers a skill the current LLMs require to get good results; and writes about the future jobs that will always be available for smart teachers like THEM, that are SKILLED in using LLMs. The next generation of AIs doesn't need their fancy prompt engineering. The lesson plan model goes from needing to type in just the right set of weird words and cryptic pedagogical invocations, to most teachers being able to type in plain English what they want and get a pretty good result. There are still tasks that require careful prompt engineering. But they are a much smaller fraction of all the tasks teachers are trying to do, or you can get a bleh result...

replies.text: Well said. Every teacher agrees AI can't do their job, so it ends up doing everyone else's. I'm not sure how to formulate it yet but it seems there is some Peter Principle/Gell-Mann Effect corollary that is AI-related we can say here. Perhaps: 'AI rises to the level of its users' incompetence.' Or: 'Confidence in AI output is inversely proportional to one's ability to verify it'

> Confidence in AI output is inversely proportional to one's ability to verify it. I like this / generally agree. The only wrinkle is that - for some tasks - the verification _is_ 'give the lesson, see if students learned, don't care how... just that they did' which is distinctly different from 'not only did it teach correctly, it did so in the most engaging and efficient way possible'. For a _lot_ of what I use LLMs to teach, the former is all I need.

replies.text: It seems to be a general principle: If AI is better than you at something, you use it. If AI is worse than you, you don't. Each time the frontier models get better, I see another wave of AI doubters suddenly become believers. Teachers say things like, 'AI couldn't plan lessons last year, but now I use it for everything!' Interesting. Now we know that the person who said this has the teaching skills of a GPT-5 or whenever the frontier was when they flipped. Meanwhile, the rest of us keep using AI as simple tools, like the person in the article. I wonder how long it will take before computers can teach better than me, and I flip too.

I'm not sure I agree with this but maybe I just lack self awareness? There are large portions of my teaching that are essentially extremely verbose grunt work. My lesson plans, unit overviews, differentiation charts, etc. I know what the lesson is supposed to look like when it's done being written, but it's going to take me for freaking ever to type it all out. I can just few shot it now in an hour. Plan -> feedback loop -> build -> review loop. Does it try to do weird stuff? Yeah. And then I'm just like 'that's weird, no, the activities should be broken up like XYZ' and then it's not weird anymore. Occasionally (1% of the time) I just do a quick rewrite myself instead of trying to tell the AI what to do. I can get something fairly close to the ballpark of what I would have done but in like...

I flipped cause now I am faster with AI than without it... A year ago the AI output was so bad that getting it up to my standards took more than writing it myself from scratch. And nowadays it is faster for me to start with AI output and iterate from there to reach quality submission. The ninety-ninety[0] rule was a thing talked about 40 years ago, long before anyone thought of AI lesson planning. AI can nowadays make the first 90% of the task very fast and good enough. replies.text: It’s been basically the same for 3 years now. Are you sure we’re the ones who can’t see trends?

Your experiences must be much different from mine. Three years ago, AI could barely string together a coherent unit overview. Two years ago, it could draft an assessment rubric — but the rubric was so vague you'd spend more time tweaking it. A year ago, I was tinkering with Claude to generate differentiated worksheets from a loose topic. Six months ago, I started using AI to produce the bulk of my weekly lesson plans. Most of my time was spent editing, and I was thrilled to hit ~2x output because I could run the next week's plan while reviewing the last. Now, at our school I'm orchestrating a half dozen AI agents to draft unit plans, create IB-style assessments, and coordinate a review loop between the AI, the subject team, and the IB coordinator...
replies.original_text: Your experiences must be much different from mine. Three years ago, AI was barely able to provide sort-of reliable command completion. Two years ago, it could extrapolate a single function from a docstring - but the docstring had to be so verbose that it wasn't practical to use in that way. A year ago, I was tinkering with Devin to try to find a way to get it to reliably implement small, isolated features from verbose Jira tickets. Six months ago, I started using AI to generate the majority of my code output. Most of my time was spent reviewing, and I was ecstatic to reach ~2x output because I could run the next task while reviewing the last. Now, at work I'm managing a half dozen Claude Code instances, Devin sessions, and orchestrating a review loop between Claude, Devin, and...
It’s been basically the same for 3 years now. Are you sure we’re the ones who can’t see trends?

comments.text: I don't see LLMs being able to replace teachers for less common subjects like IB Dutch or TOK for non-English speakers. I know a teacher who handles a small Language B class, and some tasks require use of cultural nuance and context-specific dictionaries. Using LLMs in such cases would be very unreliable and would require even more effort to check and correct than doing it properly in the first place. Plus, I really doubt that US tech firms are training LLMs on language spoken by 'only' 6 million people. As for international school curricula, anyone who grew up with machine-translated worksheets knows how much those take away from the learning experience. Sure, 'AI could do better', but could it be consistent and capture the nuances of a Vietnamese culture text for a Grade 11 class?

Plus, I really doubt that US tech firms are training LLMs on language spoken by "only" 6 million people. As for entertainment, anyone who grew up in Eastern Europe with pirated movies with nasal monotone translations, or machine-translated video games knows how much those take away from the experience. Sure, "AI could do better", but could it be consistent and capture cultural nuances and idioms, etc?
