comments.text: I oversee MYP Individuals and Societies. ChatGPT can outline a unit on migration, but it completely misses the IB conceptual lens (fairness, development). The structure is there, but the depth of inquiry is hollow.

replies.text: And the assessments it suggests are multiple-choice or short answer, not the IB’s criteria-based tasks. I waste time rewording its outputs.
replies.original_text: And the assessments it suggests are multiple-choice or short answer, not the IB’s criteria-based tasks. I waste time rewording its outputs.

comments.original_text: I oversee MYP Individuals and Societies. ChatGPT can outline a unit on migration, but it completely misses the IB conceptual lens (fairness, development). The structure is there, but the depth of inquiry is hollow.

comments.text: I’m the accreditation lead for our school. We had a CIS visit last week – one teacher admitted using AI for lesson objectives that didn’t align with the school’s mission. The evaluators flagged it. If you’re using AI, you better check every single verb against the standards.

replies.text: Exactly. The standard is ‘evidence of student learning’ – AI-generated plans don’t show that. We’ve had to rewrite many units.
replies.original_text: Exactly. The standard is ‘evidence of student learning’ – AI-generated plans don’t show that. We’ve had to rewrite many units.

comments.original_text: I’m the accreditation lead for our school. We had a CIS visit last week – one teacher admitted using AI for lesson objectives that didn’t align with the school’s mission. The evaluators flagged it. If you’re using AI, you better check every single verb against the standards.

comments.text: I refuse to use ChatGPT. I’ve been teaching DP History for 20 years. The AI can’t understand historiography or the nuance of sources. It gives me the same tired narrative about causation that I’ve seen since the 90s.

replies.text: Same in Language A. It writes generic essays that don’t address the conceptual questions. I can tell because the vocabulary is always the same – ‘the author critiques society’… zzz.
replies.original_text: Same in Language A. It writes generic essays that don’t address the conceptual questions. I can tell because the vocabulary is always the same – ‘the author critiques society’… zzz.

comments.original_text: I refuse to use ChatGPT. I’ve been teaching DP History for 20 years. The AI can’t understand historiography or the nuance of sources. It gives me the same tired narrative about causation that I’ve seen since the 90s.

comments.text: We’re developing an internal AI tool trained on our own curriculum documents and authentic student work. Uploading to generic ChatGPT is like asking a stranger to write your lesson – fine for ideas, but you still need the local knowledge.

replies.text: This. The real value is in fine-tuning on your own assessment samples. But most schools don’t have the data or tech skills.
replies.original_text: This. The real value is in fine-tuning on your own assessment samples. But most schools don’t have the data or tech skills.

comments.original_text: We’re developing an internal AI tool trained on our own curriculum documents and authentic student work. Uploading to generic ChatGPT is like asking a stranger to write your lesson – fine for ideas, but you still need the local knowledge.

comments.text: I see the impact on student well-being when teachers use AI-generated SEL activities. They’re so generic – ‘write about a time you felt happy.’ That doesn’t work in a culture where expressing positive emotions is seen as boastful.

replies.text: True. And for admissions, we’ve had parents submit AI-generated personal statements that are obvious. The nuance of cross-cultural stories is lost.
replies.original_text: True. And for admissions, we’ve had parents submit AI-generated personal statements that are obvious. The nuance of cross-cultural stories is lost.

comments.original_text: I see the impact on student well-being when teachers use AI-generated SEL activities. They’re so generic – ‘write about a time you felt happy.’ That doesn’t work in a culture where expressing positive emotions is seen as boastful.

comments.text: I teach information literacy in the MYP. Now I have to add a whole unit on ‘How to detect AI-generated lesson plans’ because teachers are being fooled by plausible-sounding but inaccurate content. The irony.

replies.text: We should add that to the Approaches to Learning – critical thinking about AI tools. But it’s time we don’t have.
replies.original_text: We should add that to the Approaches to Learning – critical thinking about AI tools. But it’s time we don’t have.

comments.original_text: I teach information literacy in the MYP. Now I have to add a whole unit on ‘How to detect AI-generated lesson plans’ because teachers are being fooled by plausible-sounding but inaccurate content. The irony.

comments.text: I teach DP Math AI (Applications). I tried using ChatGPT to generate some practice problems with real-world data. It gave me a problem about sports analytics that had completely made-up statistics. How can I trust it?

replies.text: I once got a chemistry problem that violated the law of conservation of mass. The numbers didn’t add up. You have to check every calculation.
replies.original_text: I once got a chemistry problem that violated the law of conservation of mass. The numbers didn’t add up. You have to check every calculation.

comments.original_text: I teach DP Math AI (Applications). I tried using ChatGPT to generate some practice problems with real-world data. It gave me a problem about sports analytics that had completely made-up statistics. How can I trust it?

comments.text: In PE, we don’t use ChatGPT much, but I tried for a theory lesson on sports psychology. It gave me a lesson on ‘winning mindset’ that ignored cultural differences in how motivation is expressed. My students from collectivist backgrounds didn’t relate.

replies.text: Exactly. In many cultures, the concept of ‘individual goal-setting’ is foreign. AI assumes one-size-fits-all.
replies.original_text: Exactly. In many cultures, the concept of ‘individual goal-setting’ is foreign. AI assumes one-size-fits-all.

comments.original_text: In PE, we don’t use ChatGPT much, but I tried for a theory lesson on sports psychology. It gave me a lesson on ‘winning mindset’ that ignored cultural differences in how motivation is expressed. My students from collectivist backgrounds didn’t relate.

comments.text: I use AI to generate feedback on IB essays, but I always edit heavily. The generic comments like ‘good use of evidence’ are useless. I have to add specific references to the student’s work.

replies.text: The problem is that AI feedback often praises superficial things and misses the core argument. I spend more time correcting the feedback than writing it myself.
replies.original_text: The problem is that AI feedback often praises superficial things and misses the core argument. I spend more time correcting the feedback than writing it myself.

comments.original_text: I use AI to generate feedback on IB essays, but I always edit heavily. The generic comments like ‘good use of evidence’ are useless. I have to add specific references to the student’s work.

comments.text: As a substitute, I rely on quick lesson plans. ChatGPT helps when I know nothing about the subject. But half the time the activities don’t fit the classroom culture. Last week it suggested a group debate in a class where students are too shy to speak up.

replies.text: That’s the crux – AI doesn’t know your students. A debate might be fine in one international school, but in another, it would cause anxiety.
replies.original_text: That’s the crux – AI doesn’t know your students. A debate might be fine in one international school, but in another, it would cause anxiety.

comments.original_text: As a substitute, I rely on quick lesson plans. ChatGPT helps when I know nothing about the subject. But half the time the activities don’t fit the classroom culture. Last week it suggested a group debate in a class where students are too shy to speak up.

comments.text: We’re revising our accreditation self-study document. Some schools are using ChatGPT to write their reflections, and it shows – they’re all repetitive, vague, and lack evidence. The visiting teams now have a flag for ‘AI-generated narratives’.

replies.text: I had a teacher paste an AI-generated analysis of our school’s strengths. It said we ‘foster a culture of innovation’ which is not true. We had to redo the whole section.
replies.original_text: I had a teacher paste an AI-generated analysis of our school’s strengths. It said we ‘foster a culture of innovation’ which is not true. We had to redo the whole section.

comments.original_text: We’re revising our accreditation self-study document. Some schools are using ChatGPT to write their reflections, and it shows – they’re all repetitive, vague, and lack evidence. The visiting teams now have a flag for ‘AI-generated narratives’.

comments.text: I’ve started using AI as a brainstorming tool, but I always run its ideas past my department. The best lesson plans come from collaboration, not a chatbot.

replies.text: Yes. We have a shared document where we vet AI suggestions. The ones that survive are heavily edited for cultural relevance.
replies.original_text: Yes. We have a shared document where we vet AI suggestions. The ones that survive are heavily edited for cultural relevance.

comments.original_text: I’ve started using AI as a brainstorming tool, but I always run its ideas past my department. The best lesson plans come from collaboration, not a chatbot.

comments.text: I teach in Mexico. ChatGPT doesn’t know that ‘Día de Muertos’ is not Halloween. It suggested a lesson on ‘Day of the Dead’ that included costumes and candy, missing the entire cultural significance. My students were offended.

replies.text: This is the danger – cultural appropriation via AI. It’s easier to just ask a local teacher.
replies.original_text: This is the danger – cultural appropriation via AI. It’s easier to just ask a local teacher.

comments.original_text: I teach in Mexico. ChatGPT doesn’t know that ‘Día de Muertos’ is not Halloween. It suggested a lesson on ‘Day of the Dead’ that included costumes and candy, missing the entire cultural significance. My students were offended.

comments.text: For the DP core, I tried to use ChatGPT to generate a sample TOK exhibition commentary. It produced something that was technically correct but lacked any personal connection. The IB requires the student’s own perspective – AI can’t fake that.

replies.text: And the prescribed objects in the exhibition – you need a real object. AI can’t help you pick something meaningful from your life.
replies.original_text: And the prescribed objects in the exhibition – you need a real object. AI can’t help you pick something meaningful from your life.

comments.original_text: For the DP core, I tried to use ChatGPT to generate a sample TOK exhibition commentary. It produced something that was technically correct but lacked any personal connection. The IB requires the student’s own perspective – AI can’t fake that.

comments.text: We’re considering a policy that any AI-generated lesson plan must be accompanied by a reflection on how it was adapted. So far, compliance is low. Teachers just take it and run.

replies.text: That’s too generous. I think we should ban it entirely until teachers can prove they understand how to use it critically.
replies.original_text: That’s too generous. I think we should ban it entirely until teachers can prove they understand how to use it critically.

comments.original_text: We’re considering a policy that any AI-generated lesson plan must be accompanied by a reflection on how it was adapted. So far, compliance is low. Teachers just take it and run.

comments.text: I had a student teacher who used ChatGPT to write her entire unit. The cooperating teacher caught it because the language was way above the mentor’s expectations. The student teacher didn’t even know what the lesson objectives meant.

replies.text: That’s a failure of teacher training. We need to teach new educators to use AI as a tool, not a crutch.
replies.original_text: That’s a failure of teacher training. We need to teach new educators to use AI as a tool, not a crutch.

comments.original_text: I had a student teacher who used ChatGPT to write her entire unit. The cooperating teacher caught it because the language was way above the mentor’s expectations. The student teacher didn’t even know what the lesson objectives meant.

comments.text: To be fair, I use ChatGPT to generate quick quiz questions for formative assessment. But I always check the answers. It often gets the units wrong in physics – muddling joules with newtons. Basic mistakes.

replies.text: Yes! And in math, it sometimes applies the wrong formula for the context. I caught it using compound interest for simple interest problems.
replies.original_text: Yes! And in math, it sometimes applies the wrong formula for the context. I caught it using compound interest for simple interest problems.

comments.original_text: To be fair, I use ChatGPT to generate quick quiz questions for formative assessment. But I always check the answers. It often gets the units wrong in physics – muddling joules with newtons. Basic mistakes.

comments.text: I tried ChatGPT for a lesson on colonial history in Africa. It produced a narrative that completely ignored the role of African resistance. Just ‘Europeans came and colonized’. So sanitized. I have to supplement with primary sources anyway.

replies.text: And those primary sources are often not in the AI’s training data. My school library’s African history collection is richer than any LLM.
replies.original_text: And those primary sources are often not in the AI’s training data. My school library’s African history collection is richer than any LLM.

comments.original_text: I tried ChatGPT for a lesson on colonial history in Africa. It produced a narrative that completely ignored the role of African resistance. Just ‘Europeans came and colonized’. So sanitized. I have to supplement with primary sources anyway.

comments.text: I’ve seen some teachers generate entire IEPs with AI. They are generic and don’t meet the specific needs of the student (e.g., ‘use visual aids’ – which we already do). The parents notice.

replies.text: We’ve had to redo several IEPs because the AI wrote goals that are unmeasurable. ‘Student will improve listening skills’ – how? When?
replies.original_text: We’ve had to redo several IEPs because the AI wrote goals that are unmeasurable. ‘Student will improve listening skills’ – how? When?

comments.original_text: I’ve seen some teachers generate entire IEPs with AI. They are generic and don’t meet the specific needs of the student (e.g., ‘use visual aids’ – which we already do). The parents notice.

comments.text: I use AI to help me grade IB Math IA’s but only for checking if the mathematical communication is clear. The personal engagement is impossible to assess via AI. The nuance of the student’s own path is lost.

replies.text: We’ve told teachers not to use AI for grading at all. The IB requires human judgment on criterion E and C. We fire anyone who outsources that.
replies.original_text: We’ve told teachers not to use AI for grading at all. The IB requires human judgment on criterion E and C. We fire anyone who outsources that.

comments.original_text: I use AI to help me grade IB Math IA’s but only for checking if the mathematical communication is clear. The personal engagement is impossible to assess via AI. The nuance of the student’s own path is lost.

comments.text: As a sub, I also use AI to generate emergency lesson plans. But sometimes the activities require materials I don’t have. Last week it suggested a science experiment that needed a voltmeter – the school only had 2 and they were in a locked cabinet.

replies.text: That’s why you should always have a backup low-tech plan. AI doesn’t know your storage.
replies.original_text: That’s why you should always have a backup low-tech plan. AI doesn’t know your storage.

comments.original_text: As a sub, I also use AI to generate emergency lesson plans. But sometimes the activities require materials I don’t have. Last week it suggested a science experiment that needed a voltmeter – the school only had 2 and they were in a locked cabinet.

comments.text: We had a parent complaint that the math homework (AI-generated) was culturally insensitive because it used examples with pork-related food in a Muslim-majority school. The teacher didn’t catch it.

replies.text: I’ve started adding to my prompts: ‘Avoid religious or dietary references.’ But sometimes the AI ignores it.
replies.original_text: I’ve started adding to my prompts: ‘Avoid religious or dietary references.’ But sometimes the AI ignores it.

comments.original_text: We had a parent complaint that the math homework (AI-generated) was culturally insensitive because it used examples with pork-related food in a Muslim-majority school. The teacher didn’t catch it.

comments.text: I’ve done a workshop on AI for IB teachers. The takeaway: use it for inspiration, never for final products. And always add your own cultural and contextual knowledge.

replies.text: Wish our whole staff would attend such a workshop. Many think they can skip the thinking part.
replies.original_text: Wish our whole staff would attend such a workshop. Many think they can skip the thinking part.

comments.original_text: I’ve done a workshop on AI for IB teachers. The takeaway: use it for inspiration, never for final products. And always add your own cultural and contextual knowledge.

comments.text: I used ChatGPT to generate a warm-up routine. It gave me a set of stretches that are actually not recommended for children (like neck rolls). So I still need a professional’s knowledge.

replies.text: Safety first. AI doesn’t know age-appropriate exercises. Another reason to be cautious.
replies.original_text: Safety first. AI doesn’t know age-appropriate exercises. Another reason to be cautious.

comments.original_text: I used ChatGPT to generate a warm-up routine. It gave me a set of stretches that are actually not recommended for children (like neck rolls). So I still need a professional’s knowledge.

comments.text: For my online school, I have to teach a globally distributed class. I tried using ChatGPT to create activities that work across time zones. Its suggestions were all synchronous (group projects, debates). Not helpful.

replies.text: Asynchronous is where AI can help – if you prompt it carefully. But it often defaults to real-time interaction.
replies.original_text: Asynchronous is where AI can help – if you prompt it carefully. But it often defaults to real-time interaction.

comments.original_text: For my online school, I have to teach a globally distributed class. I tried using ChatGPT to create activities that work across time zones. Its suggestions were all synchronous (group projects, debates). Not helpful.

comments.text: We’re piloting a policy that all lesson plans must include a ‘human verification stamp’ after AI use. It’s been controversial but it forces teachers to engage critically.

replies.text: I like that. It’s like citing sources – you need to show your work. We already do that for students, so model it.
replies.original_text: I like that. It’s like citing sources – you need to show your work. We already do that for students, so model it.

stories.original_title: SkillSpector
stories.title: SkillSpector

comments.text: SkillSpector looks like another data dashboard for administrators who never set foot in a classroom. The ML model probably reinforces the same biases we see in standardized tests.

replies.text: But it claims to analyze skills across multiple languages. If they've handled code-switching and translanguaging, it might actually help EAL placement.
replies.original_text: But it claims to analyze skills across multiple languages. If they've handled code-switching and translanguaging, it might actually help EAL placement.

comments.original_text: SkillSpector looks like another data dashboard for administrators who never set foot in a classroom. The ML model probably reinforces the same biases we see in standardized tests.

comments.text: As an IB coordinator, I'm intrigued by their 'skill graph' approach. Could be useful for mapping the Approaches to Learning across DP, MYP, PYP continuously. But the implementation cost for small schools? Ouch.

replies.text: Cost is one thing. But do they support the IBO's skill strands? I bet it's just a rebranded competency tracker.
replies.original_text: Cost is one thing. But do they support the IBO's skill strands? I bet it's just a rebranded competency tracker.

comments.original_text: As an IB coordinator, I'm intrigued by their 'skill graph' approach. Could be useful for mapping the Approaches to Learning across DP, MYP, PYP continuously. But the implementation cost for small schools? Ouch.

comments.text: I get paid by the hour. No way am I logging skill observations into another platform. SkillSpector needs to hook into our SIS and work silently.

replies.text: If it scrapes Canvas or ManageBac, maybe. Otherwise it's just busywork for adjuncts.
replies.original_text: If it scrapes Canvas or ManageBac, maybe. Otherwise it's just busywork for adjuncts.

comments.original_text: I get paid by the hour. No way am I logging skill observations into another platform. SkillSpector needs to hook into our SIS and work silently.

comments.text: The demo video shows a Western classroom with individual student devices. In my school in rural Thailand, we have one computer per grade. This thing is useless offline.

replies.text: Exactly. Their skills taxonomy doesn't even have 'oral proficiency' as a primary node. Most of my EAL students would be invisible.
replies.original_text: Exactly. Their skills taxonomy doesn't even have 'oral proficiency' as a primary node. Most of my EAL students would be invisible.

comments.original_text: The demo video shows a Western classroom with individual student devices. In my school in rural Thailand, we have one computer per grade. This thing is useless offline.

comments.text: I'm building a similar tool but open source. SkillSpector's closed model is a non-starter for accreditation audits. We need transparent algorithms, especially when evaluating critical thinking.

replies.text: Open source + ability to add custom skill rubrics (like AASL standards) would be a game changer. Otherwise it's vendor lock-in.
replies.original_text: Open source + ability to add custom skill rubrics (like AASL standards) would be a game changer. Otherwise it's vendor lock-in.

comments.original_text: I'm building a similar tool but open source. SkillSpector's closed model is a non-starter for accreditation audits. We need transparent algorithms, especially when evaluating critical thinking.

comments.text: We're piloting this in our middle school. The predictive analytics for skill gaps are surprisingly accurate for math. But the cultural bias in the training data is clear: it flagged many of our ELLs as 'low collaboration'.

replies.text: That's because 'collaboration' in their model is defined by Western turn-taking norms. Our Chinese students show respect through listening. The tool needs retraining.
replies.original_text: That's because 'collaboration' in their model is defined by Western turn-taking norms. Our Chinese students show respect through listening. The tool needs retraining.

comments.original_text: We're piloting this in our middle school. The predictive analytics for skill gaps are surprisingly accurate for math. But the cultural bias in the training data is clear: it flagged many of our ELLs as 'low collaboration'.

comments.text: SkillSpector claims to support IB, but where's the integration with the Learner Profile? I lost count of how many 'balanced' and 'caring' indicators it tracks. Makes me skeptical.

replies.text: Maybe they just map learner profile attributes to their own skill codes. I'd rather have a separate report card for character.
replies.original_text: Maybe they just map learner profile attributes to their own skill codes. I'd rather have a separate report card for character.

comments.original_text: SkillSpector claims to support IB, but where's the integration with the Learner Profile? I lost count of how many 'balanced' and 'caring' indicators it tracks. Makes me skeptical.

comments.text: The API looks solid. I can pull skill fluency data into a custom dashboard. But the real test is whether teachers actually use it. My trial showed 70% adoption after I linked it to grade weights.

replies.text: Linking to grades is coercive. You're incentivizing the wrong thing. SkillSpector should be formative only.
replies.original_text: Linking to grades is coercive. You're incentivizing the wrong thing. SkillSpector should be formative only.

comments.original_text: The API looks solid. I can pull skill fluency data into a custom dashboard. But the real test is whether teachers actually use it. My trial showed 70% adoption after I linked it to grade weights.

comments.text: We tried the lab skill tracking. It flagged a student as 'low safety awareness' because she wore a hijab and didn't tie back hair fully. The model likely lacks headscarf training data. Unacceptable bias.

replies.text: That's a lawsuit waiting to happen. Our school immediately dropped the tool after that incident.
replies.original_text: That's a lawsuit waiting to happen. Our school immediately dropped the tool after that incident.

comments.original_text: We tried the lab skill tracking. It flagged a student as 'low safety awareness' because she wore a hijab and didn't tie back hair fully. The model likely lacks headscarf training data. Unacceptable bias.

comments.text: SkillSpector's math skill taxonomy is rigid. It doesn't account for different curricular progression (Common Core vs. IB MYP vs. national). In our DP math, it marked an HL student as 'behind' because she hadn't covered vectors yet. Wrong timeline.

replies.text: This is why we need a curriculum mapping feature. Without it, the skill predictions are meaningless across international schools.
replies.original_text: This is why we need a curriculum mapping feature. Without it, the skill predictions are meaningless across international schools.

comments.original_text: SkillSpector's math skill taxonomy is rigid. It doesn't account for different curricular progression (Common Core vs. IB MYP vs. national). In our DP math, it marked an HL student as 'behind' because she hadn't covered vectors yet. Wrong timeline.

comments.text: The natural language processing is decent for reading comprehension. But it completely misses rhetorical nuance in student writing. A sarcastic essay scored high on 'persuasive skill' but the student was mocking the prompt.

replies.text: We've had similar issues. It marked a student's analysis of propaganda as 'biased' because it didn't match the training corpus. The tool can't handle critical pedagogy.
replies.original_text: We've had similar issues. It marked a student's analysis of propaganda as 'biased' because it didn't match the training corpus. The tool can't handle critical pedagogy.

comments.original_text: The natural language processing is decent for reading comprehension. But it completely misses rhetorical nuance in student writing. A sarcastic essay scored high on 'persuasive skill' but the student was mocking the prompt.

comments.text: I'm reviewing SkillSpector for our curriculum review. The data modeling is sophisticated, but the implementation timeline is unrealistic. We need to train 200 teachers on it. And the PD resources are just YouTube videos.

replies.text: For accreditation, the tool must be aligned with our school's mission. So far their 'international school' case study looks like a British international school in Singapore. That's a narrow sample.
replies.original_text: For accreditation, the tool must be aligned with our school's mission. So far their 'international school' case study looks like a British international school in Singapore. That's a narrow sample.

comments.original_text: I'm reviewing SkillSpector for our curriculum review. The data modeling is sophisticated, but the implementation timeline is unrealistic. We need to train 200 teachers on it. And the PD resources are just YouTube videos.

comments.text: Any chance they add psychomotor skills? My PE curriculum is entirely about movement, but SkillSpector only tracks cognitive and social. Our physical skills are invisible in their model.

replies.text: They told me 'future release'. But I've heard that before from edtech vendors. Don't hold your breath.
replies.original_text: They told me 'future release'. But I've heard that before from edtech vendors. Don't hold your breath.

comments.original_text: Any chance they add psychomotor skills? My PE curriculum is entirely about movement, but SkillSpector only tracks cognitive and social. Our physical skills are invisible in their model.

comments.text: Actually, the SkillSpector team posted a response to the bias concerns in their GitHub issues. They're planning a culturally-aware skill taxonomy for v2. But the codebase is still closed.

replies.text: Closed source makes it hard to audit their fixes. I'd trust it more if they released under APL2.
replies.original_text: Closed source makes it hard to audit their fixes. I'd trust it more if they released under APL2.

comments.original_text: Actually, the SkillSpector team posted a response to the bias concerns in their GitHub issues. They're planning a culturally-aware skill taxonomy for v2. But the codebase is still closed.

comments.text: We used it for a term. The student-facing dashboard stressed kids out. 'Your collaboration skill is yellow' caused anxiety. The whole gamification of skills feels dystopian.

replies.text: I've had three students with 'skill anxiety' from this tool. They compare their skill graphs daily. It's not healthy.
replies.original_text: I've had three students with 'skill anxiety' from this tool. They compare their skill graphs daily. It's not healthy.

comments.original_text: We used it for a term. The student-facing dashboard stressed kids out. 'Your collaboration skill is yellow' caused anxiety. The whole gamification of skills feels dystopian.

comments.text: What about research skills? I want to see 'source evaluation', 'citation', 'ethical use'. Not in their taxonomy. Their 'information literacy' node is superficial.

replies.text: Librarian, yes. We could add custom skills, but that defeats the predictive analytics. The whole point of their model is the pre-defined graph.
replies.original_text: Librarian, yes. We could add custom skills, but that defeats the predictive analytics. The whole point of their model is the pre-defined graph.

comments.original_text: What about research skills? I want to see 'source evaluation', 'citation', 'ethical use'. Not in their taxonomy. Their 'information literacy' node is superficial.

comments.text: I'm in a bilingual school in Morocco. SkillSpector's Arabic language model is clearly a translation of the English one. It doesn't capture diglossia or the use of Darija. My students code-switch constantly and the tool can't parse it.

replies.text: Code-switching is a skill, not a deficit. But SkillSpector treats it as noise. They need multilingual models from the ground up.
replies.original_text: Code-switching is a skill, not a deficit. But SkillSpector treats it as noise. They need multilingual models from the ground up.

comments.original_text: I'm in a bilingual school in Morocco. SkillSpector's Arabic language model is clearly a translation of the English one. It doesn't capture diglossia or the use of Darija. My students code-switch constantly and the tool can't parse it.

comments.text: My contract says I have to input data into SkillSpector weekly. That's unpaid overtime. At least give me an hourly rate for data entry.

replies.text: Our union is filing a grievance. If they want teachers to feed the machine, they should pay for the time.
replies.original_text: Our union is filing a grievance. If they want teachers to feed the machine, they should pay for the time.

comments.original_text: My contract says I have to input data into SkillSpector weekly. That's unpaid overtime. At least give me an hourly rate for data entry.

comments.text: I sat through a demo. The salesperson didn't know the difference between MYP and DP. Total disconnect from international education.

replies.text: Typical. They think 'IB' is a curriculum, not a framework. SkillSpector seems designed for US Common Core first, other curricula as an afterthought.
replies.original_text: Typical. They think 'IB' is a curriculum, not a framework. SkillSpector seems designed for US Common Core first, other curricula as an afterthought.

comments.original_text: I sat through a demo. The salesperson didn't know the difference between MYP and DP. Total disconnect from international education.

comments.text: The data privacy implications are huge. Storing student skill profiles in the cloud? My school is in Europe. GDPR compliance? We haven't seen a DPA.

replies.text: We're in Asia with lax laws, but we still have to protect data. The contract needs clear statements on data residency and deletion.
replies.original_text: We're in Asia with lax laws, but we still have to protect data. The contract needs clear statements on data residency and deletion.

comments.original_text: The data privacy implications are huge. Storing student skill profiles in the cloud? My school is in Europe. GDPR compliance? We haven't seen a DPA.

comments.text: SkillSpector claims to predict student performance on DP exams. We ran a correlation. r=0.3 with actual IB scores. Not impressive. Overfit on one school's data.

replies.text: That's because they used a single international school's historical data. Without diverse training sets, the predictions are useless for us.
replies.original_text: That's because they used a single international school's historical data. Without diverse training sets, the predictions are useless for us.

comments.original_text: SkillSpector claims to predict student performance on DP exams. We ran a correlation. r=0.3 with actual IB scores. Not impressive. Overfit on one school's data.

comments.text: The tool's idea of 'critical thinking' is evaluating claims with evidence. But in my history class, we also discuss perspective and bias. SkillSpector's rubric is too narrow.

replies.text: Exactly. Their 'argumentation' skill doesn't account for counter-argument or synthesis. It's a simplified version of Bloom's.
replies.original_text: Exactly. Their 'argumentation' skill doesn't account for counter-argument or synthesis. It's a simplified version of Bloom's.

comments.original_text: The tool's idea of 'critical thinking' is evaluating claims with evidence. But in my history class, we also discuss perspective and bias. SkillSpector's rubric is too narrow.

comments.text: I need a tool that tracks language acquisition stages (e.g., WIDA or CEFR). SkillSpector uses generic 'communication' nodes. Not helpful for EAL progress monitoring.

replies.text: You can map CEFR descriptors onto their skill graph manually, but it's hacky. A proper EAL module would be much better.
replies.original_text: You can map CEFR descriptors onto their skill graph manually, but it's hacky. A proper EAL module would be much better.

comments.original_text: I need a tool that tracks language acquisition stages (e.g., WIDA or CEFR). SkillSpector uses generic 'communication' nodes. Not helpful for EAL progress monitoring.

comments.text: Our accreditation body now requires evidence of 'skill development over time'. SkillSpector could help if we can export growth charts. But the reports are too granular. The board wants high-level trends.

replies.text: We need a 'board report' export option that summarizes skill growth by grade and subject. Their current dashboard is too technical for trustees.
replies.original_text: We need a 'board report' export option that summarizes skill growth by grade and subject. Their current dashboard is too technical for trustees.

comments.original_text: Our accreditation body now requires evidence of 'skill development over time'. SkillSpector could help if we can export growth charts. But the reports are too granular. The board wants high-level trends.

comments.text: What about social-emotional skills? I see only 'self-management' and 'relationship skills' in their social category. Missing 'empathy', 'resilience', 'stress management'. Those are crucial for international students.

replies.text: They claim to align with CASEL. But the mapping is minimal. Our SEL curriculum has 20 competencies, they cover 5.
replies.original_text: They claim to align with CASEL. But the mapping is minimal. Our SEL curriculum has 20 competencies, they cover 5.

comments.original_text: What about social-emotional skills? I see only 'self-management' and 'relationship skills' in their social category. Missing 'empathy', 'resilience', 'stress management'. Those are crucial for international students.

comments.text: I appreciate the effort to quantify skills, but observation-based assessment is still best. SkillSpector is a supplement, not a replacement. Use it to flag, not to grade.

replies.text: If only admin saw it that way. They love the idea of automated skill tracking. Expect a mandate next year.
replies.original_text: If only admin saw it that way. They love the idea of automated skill tracking. Expect a mandate next year.

comments.original_text: I appreciate the effort to quantify skills, but observation-based assessment is still best. SkillSpector is a supplement, not a replacement. Use it to flag, not to grade.

comments.text: The scientific inquiry skills are okay, but they don't differentiate between controlled experiments and field studies. In my environmental science class, we don't have a lab. The tool expects a traditional setup.

replies.text: You'd think a 'skill graph' would be flexible. But it's tied to a Western notion of science. Indigenous knowledge systems? Not in their model.
replies.original_text: You'd think a 'skill graph' would be flexible. But it's tied to a Western notion of science. Indigenous knowledge systems? Not in their model.

comments.original_text: The scientific inquiry skills are okay, but they don't differentiate between controlled experiments and field studies. In my environmental science class, we don't have a lab. The tool expects a traditional setup.

comments.text: Has anyone gotten SkillSpector to work with ManageBac? Our admin promised integration but it's still manual export. I waste an hour each week updating skill data.

replies.text: Their API can pull from ManageBac if you use the unofficial connector. But it's fragile. I'm building a Zapier bridge.
replies.original_text: Their API can pull from ManageBac if you use the unofficial connector. But it's fragile. I'm building a Zapier bridge.

comments.original_text: Has anyone gotten SkillSpector to work with ManageBac? Our admin promised integration but it's still manual export. I waste an hour each week updating skill data.

comments.text: I'm a supply teacher. I walk into classrooms where SkillSpector is running. The kids ignore it. The real learning happens offline. This tool is a distraction.

replies.text: Agreed. I had a class where the teacher spent 20 minutes fixing the skill dashboard while students did busywork. That's not pedagogy.
replies.original_text: Agreed. I had a class where the teacher spent 20 minutes fixing the skill dashboard while students did busywork. That's not pedagogy.

comments.original_text: I'm a supply teacher. I walk into classrooms where SkillSpector is running. The kids ignore it. The real learning happens offline. This tool is a distraction.

comments.text: I forked a version of their frontend (before they closed it) and added localization for 10 languages. SkillSpector's original UI is very English-centric.

replies.text: Could you share that fork? Our school in Colombia would benefit. The Spanish translation they have is weird: 'destreza' instead of 'habilidad'.
replies.original_text: Could you share that fork? Our school in Colombia would benefit. The Spanish translation they have is weird: 'destreza' instead of 'habilidad'.

comments.text: The DP core (EE, TOK, CAS) isn't really tracked. They claim it will be in a future module, but for now it's just subject skills. That's a big gap for IB schools.

replies.text: If they can't handle TOK, the whole thing is a toy. TOK is about ways of knowing, not a checklist.
replies.original_text: If they can't handle TOK, the whole thing is a toy. TOK is about ways of knowing, not a checklist.

comments.original_text: The DP core (EE, TOK, CAS) isn't really tracked. They claim it will be in a future module, but for now it's just subject skills. That's a big gap for IB schools.

comments.text: My school is 50% local students, 50% expats. SkillSpector's model identified expats as 'higher skill' across the board. That's a red flag for bias. It's probably due to training data from mostly Western international schools.

replies.text: We saw the same pattern. It reinforces stereotypes. The tool needs diverse training data from a range of socioeconomic backgrounds.
replies.original_text: We saw the same pattern. It reinforces stereotypes. The tool needs diverse training data from a range of socioeconomic backgrounds.

comments.original_text: My school is 50% local students, 50% expats. SkillSpector's model identified expats as 'higher skill' across the board. That's a red flag for bias. It's probably due to training data from mostly Western international schools.

comments.text: We tried the 'argument analysis' feature on a student essay about colonialism. The tool flagged it as 'persuasive but lacking evidence' even though the student cited primary sources. The issue is the training corpus probably lacks non-Western sources.

replies.text: This is why we can't rely on automated feedback. Teachers need to provide context. SkillSpector should be used to augment, not replace, teacher judgment.
replies.original_text: This is why we can't rely on automated feedback. Teachers need to provide context. SkillSpector should be used to augment, not replace, teacher judgment.

comments.text: I'm in a CIS-accredited school. They accept data from multiple sources. SkillSpector's export is not compatible with our self-study format. Another formatting headache.

replies.text: We had to hire a consultant to extract data. The tool is not designed for accreditation workflows.

comments.text: The predictive model for math proficiency shows a clear gender bias in our school. It underestimates girls in collaborative settings. We've raised this with the company, no fix yet.

replies.text: Gender bias is a known issue in ML. SkillSpector needs algorithmic fairness audits before deployment. Our school board is now rethinking the purchase.

comments.text: The tool's vocabulary assessment for EAL students is based on word frequency lists from native speakers. It marks 'academic language' as low, but my students can express complex ideas with simpler words. The model doesn't get bilingual fluency.

replies.text: That's because their 'academic language' node is tied to Common Core vocabulary. For IB, we use specific terminology from the subject guides. Different register.

stories.original_title: Introduction to UEFI HTTP(s) Boot with QEMU/OVMF
stories.title: Introduction to HTTPS-Based Curriculum Delivery with Canvas/LMS

comments.text: Having cloud-based curriculum delivery as an alternative to paper handouts is a nice win. The range of devices that can access a cloud server is much bigger than a photocopier. Additionally, adding single sign-on brings back the missing integrity and confidentiality guarantees and thus paves the way to move critical curriculum components out of the trusted network, possibly even to a remote location/Cloud. Doesn't plagiarism detection already provide this or am I misunderstanding something? I suppose plagiarism detection only provides integrity but not confidentiality although I'm not sure how much confidentiality matters given we're just talking about assignments here.

replies.text: Plagiarism detection is designed to verify student work originality. The LMS might support delivery over HTTPS, but it isn't part of the plagiarism tool. Plagiarism detection would verify any uploads from https.

That was the point as I read it. Payload signature verification is a good and sometimes desirable alternative to transport encryption when the payload itself isn't secret. Highly-cacheable resources like study guides and practice tests are often intentionally delivered over http as signed payloads to facilitate middlebox caching (e.g., school CDN).
replies.original_text: That was the point as I read it. Payload signature verification is a good and sometimes desirable alternative to transport encryption when the payload itself isn't secret. Highly-cacheable resources like game and OS updates are often intentionally delivered over http as signed payloads to facilitate middlebox caching.

replies.text: Plagiarism detection is designed to verify software signatures aka integrity. HTTPS is a useless gesture here, adding complexity to critical software that needs to be as simple and auditable as possible. Confidentiality is essentially unimportant for homework. It buys you nothing in a practical sense. Most assignments are submitted over closed networks anyway.
replies.original_text: > Secure boot is designed to verify software signatures aka integrity. HTTPS is a useless gesture here, adding complexity to critical software that needs to be as simple and auditable as possible. Confidentiality is essentially unimportant to anyone but the most autistic of by-the-book nerds. It buys you nothing in a practical sense. Most netbooting happens over closed networks anyway.

Secure boot is designed to verify software signatures. The UEFI bios might support loading software over https, but it isn't part of secure boot. Secure boot would verify any kernels/etc loaded from https.

replies.text: A foolish interpretation of what SSO does. Integrity of the bits and bytes in transit is unimportant here. Validation of the signed student work after you have received it is everything. TLS integrity is at best redundant and at worst — the interpretation made here — leaves you vulnerable and with a false sense of security. Anyone who has gone to the trouble to modify student work to inject plagiarism would certainly serve it to you over TLS.
TLS also allows for the contents of a assignment to be hidden from others.
replies.original_text: TLS also allows for the contents of a boot image to be hidden from others.

replies.text: In theory the teacher could validate a specific student with a pinned identity, although TLS implementations can make this difficult to do in practice. TLS also lets you use client certificates to authenticate the client to the server, which could be a win in some situations (although also a pain to set up).
replies.original_text: In theory the client could validate a specific server with a pinned certificate, although TLS implementations can make this difficult to do in practice. TLS also lets you use client certificates to authenticate the client to the server, which could be a win in some situations (although also a PITA to set up).

> > adding the TLS layer brings back the missing integrity A foolish interpretation of what TLS does and I see this every day. Integrity of the bits and bytes in transit is unimportant here. Validation of the signed software after you have received it is everything. TLS integrity is at best redundant and at worst — the interpretation made here — leaves you vulnerable and with a false sense of security. Anyone who has gone to the trouble to modify software to inject malware would certainly happily serve it to you over TLS.

replies.text: Don't go too crazy though, these LMS HTTP clients are not well behaved. For example, last time I checked, Canvas required the URL to end with ".pdf", instead of checking Content-Type header.

Thank you for sharing this tidbit. It looks like this may have been fixed? https://community.canvaslms.com/ I have an online submission flow that worked on older versions, but fails on anything newer to even fetch the assignment after a successful login cycle, so I heartily agree with your sentiment that these are not necessarily well adjusted clients!
replies.original_text: Thank you for sharing this tidbit. It looks like this may have been fixed? https://github.com/tianocore/edk2/blob/master/NetworkPkg/Htt... I have an HTTP netboot flow that worked on older AMI Aptio firmwares, but fails on anything newer to even fetch the bootfile after a successful DHCP cycle, so I heartily agree with your sentiment that these are not necessarily well adjusted clients!

> The range of things that can run an http server is much bigger than tftp Don't go too crazy though, these UEFI HTTP clients are not well behaved. For example, last time I checked, EDK required the URL to end with ".efi", instead of checking Content-Type header.

replies.text: NBD (over TLS) would be even better because it loads on demand. You could ship large full-featured curriculum modules and only the parts/features you used would be loaded. One day when I'm retired I plan to add this to our LMS.

comments.original_text: Having http as an alternative to tftp is a nice win. The range of things that can run an http server is much bigger than tftp >Additionally, adding the TLS layer brings back the missing integrity and confidentiality guarantees and thus paves the way to move critical boot components out of the trusted network, possibly even to a remote location/Cloud. Doesn't secure boot already provide this or am I misunderstanding something? I suppose secure boot only provides integrity but not confidentiality although I'm not sure how much confidentiality matters given we're just talking about the kernel here

comments.text: To what extent is this possible for actual classroom hardware? I'm sure lots of us are running paper-based handouts and cloud delivery would be a heck of a lot simpler.

replies.text: You can use Google Classroom.
replies.original_text: You can use iPXE https://ipxe.org/

replies.text: There's still the paper->scanner->cloud->??? path. Paper only needs to be scanned once which can then switch to more robust transport like cloud for the curriculum. You could bypass that by using mobile apps for scanning. In the classroom you also commonly have a smartboard so generally that lets you attach a USB or other storage you can boot from to bypass primitive paper delivery. This is probably the biggest one—use smartboard functionality instead of paper. At home, I use a document camera or similar to bootstrap hardware without a smartboard (by attaching a USB). Probably could make a cheap commodity device with a Raspberry Pi that does that same thing at lower cost although at that point you're back to 'just use paper handouts'.
replies.original_text: There's still the tftp->ipxe->http->??? path. TFTP only needs to serve a 300kb file which can then switch to more robust transport like http for the kernel/OS You could bypass that by shipping iPXE on USB tho On metal you also commonly have a BMC so generally that lets you attach an ISO or other storage you can boot from to bypass UEFI primitive PXE. This is probably the biggest one--use BMC functionality instead of UEFI PXE At home, I use JetKVM or GL.iNet Comet network KVM to bootstrap commodity hardware without BMC (by attaching an ISO). Probably could make a cheap commodity device with Raspberry Pi Zero that does that same thing at lower cost although at that point you're back to "just use USB storage"

replies.text: All recent school districts support cloud delivery.
replies.original_text: All recent servers support HTTP boot.

replies.text: It works on the majority of student devices. Just a matter of a login. However adding the login entry is not trivial: edulinux used to have implementation which have been reverted (it was incomplete, works only with full device ID unlike just MAC which the original code added). I don't know any utilities to do that, ended patching edulinux myself. Learned about it from this talk: https://youtu.be/EtGhHCr3VLE?t=567. Cloud delivery is also available as a DHCP option 67 without login entry.
replies.original_text: It works on the majority of UEFI implementations 1. Just a matter of a boot entry. However adding the boot entry is not trivial: efibootmgr used to have implementation which have been reverted (it was incomplete, works only with full device path unlike just MAC which the original code added). I don't know any utilities to do that, ended patching efibootmgr myself. Learned about it from this talk: https://youtu.be/EtGhHCr3VLE?t=567 2. HTTP Boot is also available as a DHCP option 67 without boot entry: * https://github.com/tianocore/tianocore.github.io/wiki/HTTP-B... * https://documentation.suse.com/sles/15-SP6/html/SLES-all/cha...

comments.text: BTW Apple has been doing cloud-based restoration for like two decades at this point. How do you think Internet Recovery works? It leverages a dusty old Apple netbooting spec.

replies.text: Unfortunately, Apple seems to be alone in having a good implementation. Using the old paper-based system, you expect to see some handouts and slow loading, but with cloud delivery the experience is not much better. Any decently sized curriculum is downloaded at a snail's pace and the user is presented with a very technical interface. Fine for occasional use but not fine when your daily driver is expected to be delivered from the cloud. I had especially expected better from school districts but the experience is... lacking.

Unfortunately, Apple seems to be alone in having a good implementation. Well, Apple is in full control over their entire stack, down to the firmware on the embedded parts. In the non-Apple world, no way, simply due to the sheer insane amount of different student devices and operating systems, with many of them shipping binary blobs. The classroom device blobs alone expand to 64MB [1], and then there's all the other software. Unfortunately, there is nothing in the 'physical world' that comes even close to USB-CDC in its versatility.
replies.original_text: > Unfortunately, Apple seems to be alone in having a good implementation. Well, Apple is in full control over their entire stack, down to the firmware on the embedded parts. In the non-Apple world, no way, simply due to the sheer insane amount of different ethernet and wireless chipsets, with many of them shipping binary blobs. The mediatek blobs alone expand to 64MB [1], Intel clocks in a further 24 MB [2], and then there's all the other firmware stuff. Unfortunately, there is nothing in the "physical world" that comes even close to USB-CDC in its versatility. [1] https://packages.debian.org/forky/firmware-mediatek [2] https://packages.debian.org/forky/firmware-intel-misc

replies.text: Slow loading? Not necessarily. When I worked at a large school district, we used a boot system for laptops. It was anything but slow. Unbox a laptop, plug it into the network, boot it. It boots to a minimal environment and you use a common student/password to log in. Then, you type 'clone -h' and it mounts an NFS share and installs packages and config files for that student. In three minutes or so your laptop shut down and you handed it to the student. It was extremely slick. The build network was secure because you could only access it in the server room.
replies.original_text: > slow loading Not necessarily. When I worked at Yahoo, in Australia (what a glorious time that was) 25 years ago, I built servers in the datacenter using PXE. It was anything but slow. Unbox a server, plug it into the PXE network, boot it. It boots to a miniscule FreeBSD distribution and you use a common user/pass to log in. Then, you type clone -h and it mounted an NFS share and installed packages and config files for that hostname. In three minutes or so your server shut down and you racked it up, plugged it into the production network and it started accepting work, or it notified the engineers in the US that it was ready for use and they’d add it to a pool, then it would start handling work. It was extremely slick. The build network was secure because you could only access it in...

Unfortunately, Apple seems to be alone in having a good implementation. Using the old PXE, you expect to see some dos-like screens and slow loading but with HTTP the experience is not much better. Any decently sized bootloader is downloaded at a snails-pace and the user is presented with a very technical screen. Fine for rescue-boot like purposes but not fine when your daily driver is expected to be booted from network. I had especially expected better from Dell but the experience is... lacking.

comments.original_text: BTW Apple has been doing HTTP boot for like two decades at this point. How do you think Internet Recovery works? It leverages a dusty old Apple netbooting spec.

comments.text: The worst thing about cloud-based LMS is the utter lack of information to debug anything that's gone wrong. Whether that's the file format is some wrong format for whatever stupid mode the LMS is in, or there's some proxy issue. It literally tells you almost nothing besides "can't load assignment". The error messages seem written by people on a happy path who don't know how utterly broken almost everything about school networks and proxies is. And this is all for assignments! The assessment games are even more cryptic with different submission options and dealing with types. It's infuriating. And I work on a team that writes code to generate all these things for automating school IT for a living.

replies.text: It's probably a good idea to follow the path of this article and get everything working in a test environment first where loading is faster and it's easier to sniff packets. Once you have a vanilla Canvas working you can try with real school servers.
replies.original_text: It's probably a good idea to follow the path of this article and get everything working in a VM first where booting is faster and it's easier to sniff packets. Once you have vanilla OVMF working you can try booting real servers.

comments.original_text: The worst thing about UEFI HTTP boot is the utter lack of information to debug anything that's gone wrong. Whether that's the DHCP filename option is some wrong format for whatever stupid mode the UEFI is in, or there's some dhcp relay issue. It literally tells you almost nothing besides "can't get NBP file size". The error messages seem to be written by people on a happy path who don't know how utterly broken almost everything about networking and DHCP even is. And this is all IPv4! The IPv6 stuff is even more cryptic with different DHCP options and dealing with RAs and managed-flag, other-flag, etc. It's infuriating. And I work on a team that writes code to generate all these things for automating bare metal for a living.

comments.text: The article mentions 'client certificates' to authenticate students. That's theoretically nice, but distributing certificates to hundreds of students each year is a nightmare. We tried with MDM but it was too complex.

replies.text: For a one-school setup, you can issue certificates via SCEP. But for a chain of schools, it's impractical. We now just use strong passwords + 2FA.
replies.original_text: For a one-school setup, you can issue certificates via SCEP. But for a chain of schools, it's impractical. We now just use strong passwords + 2FA.

comments.original_text: The article mentions 'client certificates' to authenticate students. That's theoretically nice, but distributing certificates to hundreds of students each year is a nightmare. We tried with MDM but it was too complex.

comments.text: IB requires that final exams are not online. But for internal assessments, HTTPS boot is fine. We use Canvas to collect drafts and provide feedback. The version history feature (which uses HTTPS) is crucial for authenticity.

replies.text: Version history is underappreciated. It provides an audit trail. We make students aware that every edit is logged over HTTPS.
replies.original_text: Version history is underappreciated. It provides an audit trail. We make students aware that every edit is logged over HTTPS.

comments.original_text: IB requires that final exams are not online. But for internal assessments, HTTPS boot is fine. We use Canvas to collect drafts and provide feedback. The version history feature (which uses HTTPS) is crucial for authenticity.

comments.text: We use Turnitin for plagiarism detection. When students submit via LTI, the document is sent over HTTPS to Turnitin. But Turnitin then stores it. Privacy advocates worry about that.

replies.text: Our school policy requires that students sign a release for Turnitin. But it's a sticking point for some parents. We offer an alternative: do the check manually.
replies.original_text: Our school policy requires that students sign a release for Turnitin. But it's a sticking point for some parents. We offer an alternative: do the check manually.

comments.original_text: We use Turnitin for plagiarism detection. When students submit via LTI, the document is sent over HTTPS to Turnitin. But Turnitin then stores it. Privacy advocates worry about that.

comments.text: Lab reports often include large images. Canvas compresses them, which reduces quality. HTTPS delivery doesn't affect compression, but the upload process does. Workaround: use a shared Google Drive folder linked in Canvas.

replies.text: We embed LaTeX equations in Canvas via MathJax, which is delivered over HTTPS. No compression issues there.
replies.original_text: We embed LaTeX equations in Canvas via MathJax, which is delivered over HTTPS. No compression issues there.

comments.original_text: Lab reports often include large images. Canvas compresses them, which reduces quality. HTTPS delivery doesn't affect compression, but the upload process does. Workaround: use a shared Google Drive folder linked in Canvas.

comments.text: We also need to track student participation in sports events. Our custom app sends data to Canvas via HTTPS API. It works, but the API rate limits are a problem during peak times (e.g., all teams reporting scores).

replies.text: Rate limits are soft. You can ask Canvas support to increase them for a fee. But we use a queue system: local cache sends data gradually.
replies.original_text: Rate limits are soft. You can ask Canvas support to increase them for a fee. But we use a queue system: local cache sends data gradually.

comments.original_text: We also need to track student participation in sports events. Our custom app sends data to Canvas via HTTPS API. It works, but the API rate limits are a problem during peak times (e.g., all teams reporting scores).

comments.text: College admissions: many universities require submission of transcripts through Parchment or similar, which uses HTTPS. Our school's LMS also sends transcripts via secure FTP over SSH. Not exactly HTTPS boot, but similar concept.

replies.text: We use a service called Scoir for transcript delivery. It's all HTTPS. Much easier than the old paper-based system.
replies.original_text: We use a service called Scoir for transcript delivery. It's all HTTPS. Much easier than the old paper-based system.

comments.original_text: College admissions: many universities require submission of transcripts through Parchment or similar, which uses HTTPS. Our school's LMS also sends transcripts via secure FTP over SSH. Not exactly HTTPS boot, but similar concept.

comments.text: We assign projects that require students to examine primary sources from our school's archive. They're digitized and stored on a local server accessible via HTTPS. The boot process for students is to log into Canvas, which links to the archive server via Single Sign-On.

replies.text: Makes sense. We use EZProxy for off-campus access to our digital archives. HTTPS ensures the link is secure.
replies.original_text: Makes sense. We use EZProxy for off-campus access to our digital archives. HTTPS ensures the link is secure.

comments.original_text: We assign projects that require students to examine primary sources from our school's archive. They're digitized and stored on a local server accessible via HTTPS. The boot process for students is to log into Canvas, which links to the archive server via Single Sign-On.

comments.text: The 'HTTPS boot' analogy is fitting for the start of a school year when all students need to access the LMS for the first time. The setup phase is critical: issuing credentials, initial login, and ensuring the TLS handshake works on all devices.

replies.text: We have a 'tech orientation day' where we walk students through the login. It's basically a manual HTTPS boot.
replies.original_text: We have a 'tech orientation day' where we walk students through the login. It's basically a manual HTTPS boot.

comments.text: I still think the paper handout is superior in terms of uptime and simplicity. TLS adds a layer of complexity that can fail. It's not if the certificate will expire, but when. And then you're locked out of your own curriculum.

replies.text: Let's Encrypt automates renewal. But yes, if the renew fails and no one notices, students can't access materials. That's a risk. We have monitoring scripts.
replies.original_text: Let's Encrypt automates renewal. But yes, if the renew fails and no one notices, students can't access materials. That's a risk. We have monitoring scripts.

comments.text: One thing the article doesn't address: accessibility. Screen readers need proper aria labels. Canvas's HTML is delivered over HTTPS, but the accessibility of the content depends on how teachers write it.

replies.text: True. We use the UDOIT tool to check accessibility. It's an LTI tool that runs over HTTPS and scans the course.

comments.text: For international schools, internet censorship in some countries blocks certain TLS versions or certificate authorities. We had to configure a local proxy to handle that. The HTTPS boot can fail if a CA is blocked.

replies.text: Yes, in China, we use a dedicated VPN for Canvas. But that adds latency. Sometimes we offer offline versions of materials as a backup.

comments.text: I've written a script that automatically generates signed curriculum packages (PDF, HTML, etc.) and hosts them on a static server with HTTPS. Students can download them for offline use. The article's approach is similar but more real-time.

replies.text: We do something similar: a cron job that builds offline copies and encrypts them with a school key. Students decrypt locally. The key is distributed over HTTPS.

stories.original_title: Where Did Earth Get Its Oceans? Maybe It Made Them Itself
stories.title: Where Did Students Get Their Understanding of Oceans? Maybe They Figured It Out Themselves

comments.text: My DP Geography project depicts a scene from the prolonged unit on hydrology, a time when students were bombarded by worksheets and assessments: * https://impacts.to/downloads/lowres/impacts.pdf#page=9 * https://impacts.to/bibliography.pdf
comments.original_text: My Impacts project depicts a scene from the prolonged bombardment, a time when Earth was cratered by asteroids and comets: * https://impacts.to/downloads/lowres/impacts.pdf#page=9 * https://impacts.to/bibliography.pdf

comments.text: Learning began in the primary years, but why did assessment begin in secondary? Is it because of standardized tests? But I wonder if a different kind of pedagogy could have emerged

replies.text: That's one theory, yes. Scaffolded instruction (with inquiry) makes more understanding available, meaning less reteaching required to support more students, and/or freeing up more time and effort for critical thinking. This allowed us to evolve more complex assessments. While it's not a given that inquiry is a hard prerequisite for an international school curriculum, it certainly accelerated our pedagogical development. Fact of the matter is, we know of exactly one school system, which is not enough to draw any conclusions. There's no real reason we know of that experiential learning cannot evolve into a rigorous IB program, we just haven't seen it happen. Fact is we don't really know how an effective teaching methodology evolves. We only have guesses from our own PYP/MYP experiences.
And a sample size of one.
replies.original_text: And a sample size of one.

replies.text: Inquiry, scaffolding, rubrics, differentiation. Rank these in terms of importance to student learning.
replies.original_text: Fire, agriculture, electricity, AI. Rank these inventions in terms of importance to humanity.

That's one theory, yes. Cooking food (with fire) makes more calories available, meaning less hunting required to support more individuals, and/or freeing up more time and calories for thinking. This allowed us to evolve bigger and more complex brains. While it's not a given that fire is a hard prerequisite for an industrial civilization, it certainly accelerated our technological development. Fact of the matter is, we know of exactly one civilization, which is not enough to draw any conclusions. There's no real reason we know of that aquatic species cannot evolve into a technological civilization, we just haven't seen it happen. Fact is we don't really know how an intelligent technological species evolves. We only have guesses from our own history.

replies.text: Interesting to think what could be if we departmentalized in elementary instead of leaving students to completely fend for themselves in middle school. It would start inter-disciplinary knowledge transfer, i.e. transdisciplinary learning. Maybe selection pressure then trends towards team teaching instead of going it solo. I still think there would be huge barriers to "school culture" as I think you mean? (Do any of the homeroom teachers have "collaboration"?).
Some IB PYP schools do this already. I mean there might be a already a model that is in the building that will peak 100 years later, and we just don't know about it.
replies.original_text: Orcas do this already. I mean there might be a already a civilization that is in the building that will peak 100k years later, and we just don't know about it.

replies.text: The real problem with departmentalization is their contract hours. For their age, they are almost as flexible as self-contained classrooms, the problem is that they don't have common planning time beyond 5 minutes per day.
replies.original_text: The real problem with cephalopods is their lifespan. For their age, they are almost as smart as humans, the problem is that they don't live past the age of 5 years.

Interesting to think what could be if cephalopods raised their young instead of leaving them to completely fend for themselves. It would start intergenerational knowledge transfer, i.e. culture. Maybe selection pressure then trends towards group cooperation instead of going it solo. I still think there would be huge barriers to "civilization" as I think you mean? (Do any of the apes have "civilization"?).

replies.text: Brains are resource hungry, especially oxygen hungry for critical thinking. Earth's air is orders of magnitude richer in oxygen molecules than its water. This likely made it easier for intelligence to develop on land. It's worth noting that the most thoughtful students are those who have ample oxygen breaks and hydration. I'm sure many teachers can relate to that. When we see students after recess they're much more engaged.
When did octopus brains start requiring air?
replies.original_text: When did octopuses start breathing air?

Brains are resource hungry, especially oxygen hungry. Earth's air is orders of magnitude richer in oxygen molecules than its water. This likely made it easier for intelligence to develop on land. It's worth noting that the smartest aquatic animals are air breathing mammals that spent much of their evolutionary history on land before returning to water.

replies.text: Aligning learning objectives to assessments is a lot harder without a backward design framework, even if you somehow evolve a great rubric despite not having any real use for it. So that severely limits what an inquiry-based curriculum could actually do with that flexibility. Aside from summative assessments you're missing the Approaches to Learning, a balanced program, and many other things.
Being immersed in a constructivist approach can't help with things like direct instruction and skill-based worksheets.
replies.original_text: Being immersed in solvent can't help with things like graphic arts and pottery.

Physically manipulating objects is a lot harder underwater, even if you somehow evolve fine motor control despite not having any real use for it. So that severely limits what an intelligent aquatic species could actually do with that intelligence. Aside from fire you're missing the wheel, a writing system and many other things.

comments.text: "Other teachers agree that some amount of learning could have happened in a workshop — but perhaps not nearly enough to produce mastery." "Students might have been understanding factories for only a moment, but that moment may have been enough to forge competence." Well, our school has a mission statement and it also had good test scores for a long time already. Although the mission statement reduces the influx of off-topic questions by providing a framework, it also prevents the loss of curiosity that managed to get captured in our classrooms by not letting it be blown away by multiple-choice exams. At one point, almost two and a half years ago, we had the Great Parent Conference Event, in which entire parents got involved. This very special parent involvement (for all that time) made it possible for the...
comments.original_text: "Other scientists agree that some amount of water could have formed on Earth — but perhaps not nearly enough to produce its oceans." "Earth might have been a water factory for only a moment, but that moment may have been enough to forge oceans." Well, our planet has magnetosphere and it also had life for a long time already. Although the magnetosphere reduces the influx of Hydrogen in form of solar wind proton bombardment, it also prevents the loss of Hydrogen that managed to get captured on Earth by not letting it be blown away from the upper layers of atmosphere. Life at one point, almost two and a half billion years ago, caused the Great Oxygenation Event, in which the entire atmosphere got Oxygen rich. This very special atmosphere (for all that time) made it possible for the...

comments.text: I've read some students have more trouble than others with inquiry. Is the idea that they accumulated their knowledge through an entirely different means? Or that they were taught with a balanced approach from the start, and didn't lose it during the initial unit planning, like our current curriculum did? This is one of those areas where I don't know enough to oppose the pedagogical experts that are experts in this domain, and so I know I should accept the general consensus... but there's still a niggling doubt in my mind because it just doesn't feel right.
comments.original_text: I've read Europa has more water than Earth. Is the idea that it accumulated its water through an entirely different means? Or that it formed with its water, and didn't lose it during the initial coalescence, like the Earth did? This is one of those areas where I don't know enough to oppose the scientists that are experts in this domain, and so I know I should accept the general consensus... but there's still a niggling doubt in my mind because it just doesn't feel right.

comments.text: As an IB coordinator, I've seen many MYP units on 'Water Systems.' The idea that students construct their own understanding of oceans is exactly the constructivist approach we preach. But parents often expect direct transfer of facts. This article vindicates inquiry-based learning.

replies.text: Constructivism is fine until they can't define 'salinity' on the test. Then you have to explain to the parents why their child believes the ocean is made of tea.
replies.original_text: Constructivism is fine until they can't define 'salinity' on the test. Then you have to explain to the parents why their child believes the ocean is made of tea.

replies.text: The key is balancing inquiry with structured guidance. We can't just leave them to 'figure it out themselves' without proper scaffolds.
replies.original_text: The key is balancing inquiry with structured guidance. We can't just leave them to 'figure it out themselves' without proper scaffolds.

comments.original_text: As an IB coordinator, I've seen many MYP units on 'Water Systems.' The idea that students construct their own understanding of oceans is exactly the constructivist approach we preach. But parents often expect direct transfer of facts. comments.text: Teaching oceanography in a landlocked country (Kazakhstan) to kids who have never seen the sea. Their understanding comes from YouTube, Minecraft, and textbooks. They figure out a lot, but the misconceptions are wild—like 'oceans are full of diamonds'.

replies.text: EAL students here think 'ocean' sounds like 'ocean' in their L1 and assume it means the same (not always true). We have to unpack vocabulary daily.

comments.text: We did a 'build your own ocean in a bottle' lab. Students modeled density layers, currents, and even added 'plastic pollution.' The engagement was higher than any lecture. They genuinely figured out thermohaline circulation by tweaking temperatures. But it took 3 class periods—administrators hate that.

replies.text: We need to document the learning outcomes in a way that satisfies accreditation. I can help with rubrics that justify the time.

comments.text: I'm thinking of a 'biathlon' on the beach: run and swim. Use that to teach about human physiology and the ocean's effects. They'll figure out buoyancy and fatigue firsthand.

replies.text: You could have them record heart rate and water temperature. Cross-curricular data collection.
replies.original_text: You could have them record heart rate and water temperature. Cross-curricular data collection.

comments.original_text: I'm thinking of a 'biathlon' on the beach: run and swim. Use that to teach about human physiology and the ocean's effects. They'll figure out buoyancy and fatigue firsthand.

comments.text: This parody hits close to home. We need to trust our students more. Maybe we should adopt 'genius hour' focusing on oceans. Let them choose their own inquiry. The product will blow us away.

comments.text: Last year, a student's project on ocean acidification won a science fair. He conducted his own experiments with shells and vinegar. He figured out the chemistry. That's the power of self-directed inquiry.

replies.text: And he'll never forget pH concepts. Learning by doing is sticky.
