stories.original_title: Show HN: Turn your name into a tree in an infinite procedural shanshui landscape
stories.title: Show HN: Turn your student's name into a tree in an infinite procedural classroom landscape

comments.text: This is a clean and creative way to visualize student names. I planted trees for my Grade 5 class and the repeated names like 'Mohammad' produced beautiful symmetric patterns. Great for a cross-cultural celebration.

replies.text: Thanks, expatteacher! I use a deterministic algorithm based on the name's kanji strokes, so the same name always yields the same tree. Good for seating charts that reflect diversity.
replies.original_text: Thanks, Invictus1001! I use a deterministic algorithm to convert a name into a tree, so the same input always generates the same result. I'm glad you enjoyed exploring the different patterns—repeated characters often create some particularly interesting structures!

comments.original_text: This is very clean & creative project I planted a couple of trees of different names & symbols the one with constant reptition of characters are attractive especially when you can see a unique pattern

comments.text: This is fantastic! Could you add a feature to export my tree as a PDF for including in parent communication portfolios? Would love to show it at goal-setting meetings.
comments.original_text: This is so cool! Could you add a feature to export my tree as an svg please? Would love to put it on my git profile !

comments.text: Nice proof of concept, but does it handle the 30+ name variants of 'Muhammad' in my Grade 4 class? These are real pedagogical issues, not just shanshui.

replies.text: I’d love to see a Unicode normalizer for Arabic or Chinese. My IB students have names with diacritics that break the algorithm.
replies.original_text: I’d love to see a Unicode normalizer for Arabic or Chinese. My IB students have names with diacritics that break the algorithm.

comments.original_text: Nice proof of concept, but does it handle the 30+ name variants of 'Muhammad' in my Grade 4 class? These are real pedagogical issues, not just shanshui.

comments.text: Used this during our 'Names and Identity' unit in PYP. Kids loved seeing their tree grow when they clicked. But the infinite landscape gave some kids anxiety—could we add a limit setting?

replies.text: Second that. Some students with anxiety or ADHD found the endless scroll overwhelming. Perhaps a 'zoom to tree' mode.
replies.original_text: Second that. Some students with anxiety or ADHD found the endless scroll overwhelming. Perhaps a 'zoom to tree' mode.

replies.text: Just screenshotted and printed. But yes, an API to embed in the school LMS would be killer.

comments.text: I want to leverage this for TOK discussions on identity and determinism. Each name has a fixed tree shape—is that like 'name destiny'? Good for knowledge questions. Please add a toggle for randomness vs deterministic.

comments.text: Works well with the 18 nationalities in my class. The landscape looks like the Chinese gardens near our school. Only complaint: it seems to favor certain Unicode ranges? East Asian names produce more complex trees than Latin script ones.

replies.text: Yes, my Arabic name students get simpler trees. Maybe because the algorithm weights strokes? Could we have a parameter for writing system complexity?

comments.text: Used this as a pre-reading activity for a unit on ecology. Students predicted what kind of tree their name would be. Good EAL vocabulary building. But the infinite scroll is not accessible for screen readers.

replies.text: Screen reader support is critical. Also, could you add alt text for each tree describing its shape? That would help visually impaired students participate.

comments.text: I see this as a tool for self-esteem: 'Your name is a unique tree in a vast forest.' But some kids disliked their trees—'Why is mine so tiny?' Please add a 'conversation guide' for teachers.

replies.text: Good point. We could integrate CAS reflections: students write about accepting their tree. But we need privacy controls—no public gallery.

stories.original_title: AMD Stiffs Researcher $10k Bug Bounty
stories.title: Principal Stiffs Teacher $10k Reward for Catching Critical Curriculum Error After 124-Day Fix Delay

comments.text: Post from teacher: https://mrbruh.com/amd2/
comments.original_text: Post from researcher: https://mrbruh.com/amd2/

comments.text: We had a similar situation last year. The curriculum error was in the IB Maths HL statistics section. Took 6 months to get a correction. The 'bounty' was a free lunch.

replies.text: At least you got lunch. We got a 'thank you' in the staff meeting.
replies.original_text: At least you got lunch. We got a 'thank you' in the staff meeting.

comments.original_text: We had a similar situation last year. The curriculum error was in the IB Maths HL statistics section. Took 6 months to get a correction. The 'bounty' was a free lunch.

comments.text: This is why we need a formal curriculum bug bounty program. Accreditation bodies require curriculum accuracy, yet there's no incentive for teachers to report errors. 124 days is absurd.

replies.text: The issue is that curriculum changes require committee approval. By the time it goes through IB, it's outdated.
replies.original_text: The issue is that curriculum changes require committee approval. By the time it goes through IB, it's outdated.

comments.original_text: This is why we need a formal curriculum bug bounty program. Accreditation bodies require curriculum accuracy, yet there's no incentive for teachers to report errors. 124 days is absurd.

comments.text: I reported a mistake in the MYP unit planner for grade 8 science. The coordinator said it would be fixed 'next revision cycle'. That was 3 years ago.
comments.original_text: I reported a mistake in the MYP unit planner for grade 8 science. The coordinator said it would be fixed 'next revision cycle'. That was 3 years ago.

comments.text: As an adjunct, I'm not even paid for curriculum development. Finding errors is just part of the job. No way I'd get a $10k reward. More like a $10 gift card.
comments.original_text: As an adjunct, I'm not even paid for curriculum development. Finding errors is just part of the job. No way I'd get a $10k reward. More like a $10 gift card.

comments.text: The principal likely used the excuse that the error wasn't 'critical' enough. But if it affects student assessment, it's critical. Our school's EAL department found a whole mistranslation in the rubric.

replies.text: We found a huge cultural bias in the language B syllabus. Took 18 months to get acknowledged.
replies.original_text: We found a huge cultural bias in the language B syllabus. Took 18 months to get acknowledged.

comments.original_text: The principal likely used the excuse that the error wasn't 'critical' enough. But if it affects student assessment, it's critical. Our school's EAL department found a whole mistranslation in the rubric.

comments.text: 124-day fix delay? That's nothing. We had a history textbook with an incorrect date for the French Revolution. Took a year to recall. 'The pastor's already written on it.'
comments.original_text: 124-day fix delay? That's nothing. We had a history textbook with an incorrect date for the French Revolution. Took a year to recall. 'The pastor's already written on it.'

comments.text: We need a curriculum version control system. Git for lesson plans. Then 'commits' with bug reports. The principal is acting like a PM who ignores tickets.

replies.text: There are tools like ManageBac, but they're not set up for versioning. Teachers just edit directly. No audit trail.
replies.original_text: There are tools like ManageBac, but they're not set up for versioning. Teachers just edit directly. No audit trail.

comments.original_text: We need a curriculum version control system. Git for lesson plans. Then 'commits' with bug reports. The principal is acting like a PM who ignores tickets.

comments.text: The IB has a formal 'errata' process for published materials, but for school-internal curriculum, it's the wild west. Our school's policy is: 'If you find an error, tell your team lead.' No reward.
comments.original_text: The IB has a formal 'errata' process for published materials, but for school-internal curriculum, it's the wild west. Our school's policy is: 'If you find an error, tell your team lead.' No reward.

comments.text: In my overseas school, the principal pocketed the curriculum review budget. Teachers were expected to find errors for free. I left after they didn't pay for my time on the committee.
comments.original_text: In my overseas school, the principal pocketed the curriculum review budget. Teachers were expected to find errors for free. I left after they didn't pay for my time on the committee.

comments.text: We found a grammatical error in the school's official EAL placement test. It was steering students into the wrong levels. Took 4 months to get a fix. No reward, just a 'good catch'.
comments.original_text: We found a grammatical error in the school's official EAL placement test. It was steering students into the wrong levels. Took 4 months to get a fix. No reward, just a 'good catch'.

comments.text: The curriculum error was in the college counseling handbook. Wrong prerequisites for AP courses. Students missed out on classes. The principal blamed the teacher for not catching it earlier.

replies.text: Classic. Blame the whistleblower.
replies.original_text: Classic. Blame the whistleblower.

comments.original_text: The curriculum error was in the college counseling handbook. Wrong prerequisites for AP courses. Students missed out on classes. The principal blamed the teacher for not catching it earlier.

comments.text: As a principal, I see both sides. We have limited budget. Teacher rewards are often discretionary. But 124 days is unacceptable. Our school has a 48-hour turnaround for curriculum corrections.

replies.text: 48 hours? That's impressive. We need a process not just a policy.
replies.original_text: 48 hours? That's impressive. We need a process not just a policy.

comments.original_text: As a principal, I see both sides. We have limited budget. Teacher rewards are often discretionary. But 124 days is unacceptable. Our school has a 48-hour turnaround for curriculum corrections.

comments.text: Why not use automated error checking? We built a script that scans curriculum documents for common errors (typos, broken links, outdated standards). It's not perfect but reduces human effort.

replies.text: We tried that. The AI kept flagging correct scientific terminology as errors. Too many false positives.
replies.original_text: We tried that. The AI kept flagging correct scientific terminology as errors. Too many false positives.

comments.original_text: Why not use automated error checking? We built a script that scans curriculum documents for common errors (typos, broken links, outdated standards). It's not perfect but reduces human effort.

comments.text: The real issue is that curriculum development isn't valued. Teachers are content creators but treated as content consumers. If you find an error, you're expected to fix it for free.

replies.text: And if you propose a change, you're seen as challenging the hierarchy.
replies.original_text: And if you propose a change, you're seen as challenging the hierarchy.

comments.original_text: The real issue is that curriculum development isn't valued. Teachers are content creators but treated as content consumers. If you find an error, you're expected to fix it for free.

comments.text: I once automated the detection of curriculum drift using version comparison scripts. Presented to admin. They said 'interesting' and did nothing.
comments.original_text: I once automated the detection of curriculum drift using version comparison scripts. Presented to admin. They said 'interesting' and did nothing.

comments.text: A critical error in the reading list for the DP Literature course: included a book that was out of print. Students couldn't get copies. Took a full semester to update.

replies.text: I had to arrange interlibrary loans for 30 students because of that. The principal said 'it builds resourcefulness'.
replies.original_text: I had to arrange interlibrary loans for 30 students because of that. The principal said 'it builds resourcefulness'.

comments.original_text: A critical error in the reading list for the DP Literature course: included a book that was out of print. Students couldn't get copies. Took a full semester to update.

comments.text: We found a timeline error in the MYP humanities unit. It would have confused students for their summative assessment. The coordinator said 'it's close enough'.

replies.text: IB external moderation would catch that and you'd get a non-compliance report.
replies.original_text: IB external moderation would catch that and you'd get a non-compliance report.

comments.original_text: We found a timeline error in the MYP humanities unit. It would have confused students for their summative assessment. The coordinator said 'it's close enough'.

comments.text: Science curriculum error: incorrect formula in the IB Chemistry data booklet supplement. Took 124 days to issue a correction. Meanwhile, teachers had to teach the wrong formula.

replies.text: Math has it worse. We had a whole unit with misaligned objectives. Fixed it ourselves and didn't tell admin.
replies.original_text: Math has it worse. We had a whole unit with misaligned objectives. Fixed it ourselves and didn't tell admin.

comments.original_text: Science curriculum error: incorrect formula in the IB Chemistry data booklet supplement. Took 124 days to issue a correction. Meanwhile, teachers had to teach the wrong formula.

comments.text: I found an error in the school's grade calculation spreadsheet. It was rounding down for all grades. Took 2 months to fix. I asked for a $5k reward. Got a pizza party.
comments.original_text: I found an error in the school's grade calculation spreadsheet. It was rounding down for all grades. Took 2 months to fix. I asked for a $5k reward. Got a pizza party.

comments.text: PE curriculum error? More like 'we don't follow any curriculum'. But if we find an error in the IB Sports Science syllabus, it's ignored.
comments.original_text: PE curriculum error? More like 'we don't follow any curriculum'. But if we find an error in the IB Sports Science syllabus, it's ignored.

comments.text: We had an error in the database citation for the school's online resources. Took 124 days? That's how long until the subscription lapsed.

replies.text: And then the renewal came with a price increase. No accountability.
replies.original_text: And then the renewal came with a price increase. No accountability.

comments.original_text: We had an error in the database citation for the school's online resources. Took 124 days? That's how long until the subscription lapsed.

comments.text: The issue is that curriculum errors are not treated as bugs but as 'improvement suggestions'. So there's no urgency. We need a formal bug tracker with severity levels.
comments.original_text: The issue is that curriculum errors are not treated as bugs but as 'improvement suggestions'. So there's no urgency. We need a formal bug tracker with severity levels.

comments.text: From an accreditation perspective, a critical curriculum error that takes 124 days to fix is a red flag. We require prompt correction of any identified inaccuracies. This school would get a citation.
comments.original_text: From an accreditation perspective, a critical curriculum error that takes 124 days to fix is a red flag. We require prompt correction of any identified inaccuracies. This school would get a citation.

comments.text: I caught a mistake in the school's uniform policy (conflicting dates for summer uniform). That took a year to update. No reward, but the students thanked me.

replies.text: Uniform policy is academic curriculum-adjacent. But seriously, the bureaucracy is stifling.
replies.original_text: Uniform policy is academic curriculum-adjacent. But seriously, the bureaucracy is stifling.

comments.original_text: I caught a mistake in the school's uniform policy (conflicting dates for summer uniform). That took a year to update. No reward, but the students thanked me.

comments.text: As a substitute, I find errors in lesson plans every day. I don't get paid to fix them, just to execute. The regular teacher is never held accountable.
comments.original_text: As a substitute, I find errors in lesson plans every day. I don't get paid to fix them, just to execute. The regular teacher is never held accountable.

comments.text: The $10k reward was probably tied to some grant. The principal decided to reallocate the funds. Classic.
comments.original_text: The $10k reward was probably tied to some grant. The principal decided to reallocate the funds. Classic.

comments.text: Expat schools have even bigger problems. If the curriculum error is in a language the principal doesn't speak, it takes forever to get acknowledged. We had a Chinese translation error in the math curriculum. Stalled for 6 months.
comments.original_text: Expat schools have even bigger problems. If the curriculum error is in a language the principal doesn't speak, it takes forever to get acknowledged. We had a Chinese translation error in the math curriculum. Stalled for 6 months.

comments.text: I think the teacher should take this to the board of trustees. The principal is not following their own reward policy. If they have a documented 'curriculum error bounty', they must pay.

replies.text: Good luck. The board is full of parents who don't understand curriculum.
replies.original_text: Good luck. The board is full of parents who don't understand curriculum.

comments.original_text: I think the teacher should take this to the board of trustees. The principal is not following their own reward policy. If they have a documented 'curriculum error bounty', they must pay.

comments.text: We need a union. Without collective bargaining, teachers have no leverage for these bounties. My school's union contract has a clause for stipends for curriculum work.
comments.original_text: We need a union. Without collective bargaining, teachers have no leverage for these bounties. My school's union contract has a clause for stipends for curriculum work.

comments.text: 124 days is actually fast for our school. We had a curriculum error in the grade 9 algebra scope and sequence that was wrong for 2 years. I finally fixed it myself on my own time.

replies.text: And then you got no credit. Classic.
replies.original_text: And then you got no credit. Classic.

comments.original_text: 124 days is actually fast for our school. We had a curriculum error in the grade 9 algebra scope and sequence that was wrong for 2 years. I finally fixed it myself on my own time.

comments.text: I wonder if the teacher threatened legal action? If the error caused harm to students (like leading to incorrect assessment), the school could be liable. That might speed up the fix.
comments.original_text: I wonder if the teacher threatened legal action? If the error caused harm to students (like leading to incorrect assessment), the school could be liable. That might speed up the fix.

comments.text: The teacher should have submitted a 'Curriculum Enhancement Request' through the proper channels. But those take 124 days just to be reviewed. The system is broken.
comments.original_text: The teacher should have submitted a 'Curriculum Enhancement Request' through the proper channels. But those take 124 days just to be reviewed. The system is broken.

comments.text: This happened at my previous school in [country]. The principal took the reward money and said it was a 'performance bonus for the team'. Never saw a cent.
comments.original_text: This happened at my previous school in [country]. The principal took the reward money and said it was a 'performance bonus for the team'. Never saw a cent.

comments.original_text: EAL-specific error: The school's policy document said students need 5 years of English to graduate, contradicting the official state requirement of 3. Took ages to fix.

comments.text: College counseling error: wrong requirements for US university applications. Told students they didn't need SAT with essay, then the university changed requirements. We blamed the teacher who reported it.

replies.text: Gaslighting at its finest.
replies.original_text: Gaslighting at its finest.

comments.original_text: College counseling error: wrong requirements for US university applications. Told students they didn't need SAT with essay, then the university changed requirements. We blamed the teacher who reported it.

comments.text: I've implemented a 'curriculum accuracy bonus' at my school. Teachers get $500 for each validated error report, up to $5k per year. It works. Errors are fixed within a week.
comments.original_text: I've implemented a 'curriculum accuracy bonus' at my school. Teachers get $500 for each validated error report, up to $5k per year. It works. Errors are fixed within a week.

comments.text: We built a curriculum error reporting platform using a simple Google Form + Sheet. Auto-assigns to coordinator, sends reminders. But admin refused to use it.
comments.original_text: We built a curriculum error reporting platform using a simple Google Form + Sheet. Auto-assigns to coordinator, sends reminders. But admin refused to use it.

comments.text: The problem is that 'critical' is subjective. What the teacher thinks is critical, the principal thinks is minor. Need a definition: 'error that affects student assessment outcomes' is critical.
comments.original_text: The problem is that 'critical' is subjective. What the teacher thinks is critical, the principal thinks is minor. Need a definition: 'error that affects student assessment outcomes' is critical.

comments.text: I wrote a script that compares curriculum documents to standard lists (e.g., CCSS, IB objectives) and flags mismatches. Found 47 errors in the first scan. Principal said 'too many, just the big ones'.
comments.original_text: I wrote a script that compares curriculum documents to standard lists (e.g., CCSS, IB objectives) and flags mismatches. Found 47 errors in the first scan. Principal said 'too many, just the big ones'.

comments.text: Literature curriculum included a novel with outdated language that was considered offensive. Teacher reported it, took 124 days for the committee to decide to remove it. The damage was done.
comments.original_text: Literature curriculum included a novel with outdated language that was considered offensive. Teacher reported it, took 124 days for the committee to decide to remove it. The damage was done.

comments.text: History textbook had a photo labeled with the wrong event. It was used for years. Students took tests based on it. No accountability.
comments.original_text: History textbook had a photo labeled with the wrong event. It was used for years. Students took tests based on it. No accountability.

comments.text: Lab manual error: incorrect chemical name for a compound used in an experiment. Could have been dangerous. Fixed after a student complained. Teacher got no reward.
comments.original_text: Lab manual error: incorrect chemical name for a compound used in an experiment. Could have been dangerous. Fixed after a student complained. Teacher got no reward.

comments.text: I found an error in the DP math formula booklet: missing formula for sum of geometric series. Took 124 days? That's exactly how long from the first email to the final approval. I have the timestamps.
comments.original_text: I found an error in the DP math formula booklet: missing formula for sum of geometric series. Took 124 days? That's exactly how long from the first email to the final approval. I have the timestamps.

comments.text: We had an error in the fitness test protocols. The norms were for a different age group. Took a whole year to get new norms published. No reward.
comments.original_text: We had an error in the fitness test protocols. The norms were for a different age group. Took a whole year to get new norms published. No reward.

comments.text: The school's online research database had incorrect login instructions. Took 124 days for IT to fix. Meanwhile, students couldn't access resources. I was blamed.
comments.original_text: The school's online research database had incorrect login instructions. Took 124 days for IT to fix. Meanwhile, students couldn't access resources. I was blamed.

comments.text: I'm considering starting a formal 'curriculum bug bounty' program in my school. Rewards in the form of professional development funds. But I need buy-in from the principal.
comments.original_text: I'm considering starting a formal 'curriculum bug bounty' program in my school. Rewards in the form of professional development funds. But I need buy-in from the principal.

comments.text: During our last accreditation visit, we found multiple curriculum errors. The school had a year to fix them. This story shows the same problem. Accreditation is toothless if there's no follow-up.
comments.original_text: During our last accreditation visit, we found multiple curriculum errors. The school had a year to fix them. This story shows the same problem. Accreditation is toothless if there's no follow-up.

comments.text: I caught an error in the homeroom attendance form: it was marking absent students as present. Took a week to fix. I didn't ask for money, but a thank you would have been nice.
comments.original_text: I caught an error in the homeroom attendance form: it was marking absent students as present. Took a week to fix. I didn't ask for money, but a thank you would have been nice.

comments.text: As a sub, I'm not even told about curriculum errors. I walk in blind and then discover the lesson plan has mistakes. I fix it in the moment and no one knows.
comments.original_text: As a sub, I'm not even told about curriculum errors. I walk in blind and then discover the lesson plan has mistakes. I fix it in the moment and no one knows.

comments.text: The principal's excuse: 'We don't have the budget for such rewards.' Yet they spend thousands on consultant fees. Priorities.
comments.original_text: The principal's excuse: 'We don't have the budget for such rewards.' Yet they spend thousands on consultant fees. Priorities.

comments.text: Cross-cultural issue: In some cultures, pointing out an error is seen as disrespectful. So teachers are reluctant. This school might have that dynamic.
comments.original_text: Cross-cultural issue: In some cultures, pointing out an error is seen as disrespectful. So teachers are reluctant. This school might have that dynamic.

comments.text: I left my previous school over this. I found 12 curriculum errors in one year. No action, no reward. I started my own tutoring business.
comments.original_text: I left my previous school over this. I found 12 curriculum errors in one year. No action, no reward. I started my own tutoring business.

comments.original_text: Adjuncts are the most vulnerable. We're afraid to report errors because we're not full-time. The principal might not renew our contract.

comments.original_text: If the error is in a published textbook, the publisher might pay a bounty. But for internal curriculum, it's the school's responsibility. They should have a process.

comments.original_text: IB has a formal 'Correction Notice' process. But if the error is in a school-made resource, not IB published, it's on the school. The teacher should have reported it to IB? No, only to the school.

replies.original_text: That's a generalization. Many international schools have good governance. But I've seen the bad ones.

comments.original_text: The 124-day delay might be because the principal had to get approval from the board or owner. In some schools, even small curriculum changes require CEO approval. Bureaucracy in international schools is insane.

stories.original_title: If you are asking for human attention, demonstrate human effort
stories.title: If you are asking for student attention, demonstrate student effort

comments.text: I think the real problem is that student essay quality falls short of the wild promises of AI. Labeling what is 'AI-generated' would be like highlighting in an email what I'm obligated to say by HR, my boss, etc. It doesn't make anything less boneheaded. Human effort was already low before AI and now it's even lower. Garbage in, garbage out.

replies.text: AI having poor quality is a bad take like over a year ago. Students can now generate coherent essays, just need to review.
Depending on what you mean by 'quality', it may not have any at all. Content is often shallow.
replies.original_text: Depending on what you or another means by "quality", it may not have any at all.

replies.text: Meh. Just this week, I've had two students submit a 2000-word wall of brittle nonsense, and another with a well-argued thesis from the same assignment. Come on, they can do poor quality too.
replies.original_text: Meh. Just this week, I've had two Sonnet 4.8 agents generate, in parallel, a 2000 line wall of brittle bullshit, and a well architected solution with 20% of the amount of code, to the same problem, from the exact same initial context, and very similar prompts. Come on, they can do poor quality work too.
AI having poor quality is a bad take like over a year ago.

replies.text: I think this is because a lot of students think more is more. Wow look at all the detail and bullet points! No one on the receiving end actually wants that though. When I use AI to write feedback, I boil it down to the minimum bits needed. I wish more students would use it that way.

It's the empty calories of literature. More would be more if there actually was more but AI writing is making it bigger without adding anything actually more. It inserts loads of fluff and repetition.
replies.original_text: It's the empty calories of literature. More would be more if there actually was more but AI writing is making it bigger without adding anything actually more. It inserts loads of fluff and repetition that takes longer to read but doesn't exchange more information or ideas.

replies.text: Nah on the receiving end a student uses AI to summarize it.
replies.original_text: Nah on the receiving end an AI makes a summary of it.

I think this is because a lot of people think more is more. Wow look at all the detail and bullet points! No one on the receiving end actually wants that though. When I use AI to write, it's to boil it down to the minimum bits needed. I wish more people would use it that way.

comments.original_text: I think the real problem is that AI quality falls short of the wild promises. Labeling what is "AI" would be like highlighting in an email what I'm obligated to say by HR, my boss, etc. It doesn't make anything less boneheaded. Human effort was already low before AI and now it's even lower. Garbage in, garbage out.

comments.text: Reading AI-generated student essays every day, it's obvious to me. I take care to make my feedback concise. I don't care if they're AI-made, as long as they're short. I'm a very verbose person, and if I don't make an effort at being concise, I'm just as annoying as the average AI. Being flooded with AI text every day has made me appreciate brevity because I'm exposed to so little of it. With half a dozen students who don't read or listen to half of what the others do, slop + cognitive drift is a bad cocktail.

replies.text: Brevity is the big disaster of student texts since the rise of the phone. To discuss matters with sufficient depth, one often has to write a few solid paragraphs. If people now wince at longform text because they assume it's LLM-generated, that bodes ill.

To add, there seems to be an inability to process metaphor and simile in the younger generations. Likely a result of the same deficit. They've become very literal, and often mistake well-written text for AI slop.
replies.original_text: To add to this, there seems to be an inability to process metaphor and simile in the younger generations. Likely as a result of the same deficit. They've become very literal, and often mistake anything that's well written for AI slop.

replies.text: There's a sweet spot between AI slop and 144 characters. I can tell within a few sentences whether there's a human on the other end getting to the point, or an AI dancing around.
replies.original_text: There's a sweet spot between AI slop and 144 characters. I can tell within a few sentences whether there's a human on the other end getting to the point, or an AI dancing around the point and finding 3 different ways to say the same thing.

Brevity is the big disaster of human-generated text since the rise of the phone as default device and the appearance of Twitter. To discuss matters with sufficient depth and nuance, one often has to write a few solid paragraphs. If people are now wincing at longform text because they automatically assume it was LLM-generated, then that bodes ill.

comments.original_text: > when [sending AI generated content to teammates], I take care to clearly label what is AI generated Reading AI-generated text for hours every day, it's obvious to me. I take care to make my messages easily readable. I don't care if they're AI-made, as long as they're short. I'm a very verbose person, and if I don't make an effort at being concise, I'm just as annoying as the average AI. Being flooded with AI text every day has made me appreciate brevity because I'm exposed to so little of it. With half a dozen people who don't read or listen to half of what the others do, slop + cognitive drift is a bad cocktail. It's just not as big of a problem on my own projects, because the ideas that get fed to the slop-machine are not that different from one day to the next. --- > For human...

comments.text: Was it Blaise Pascal who wrote: I have only made this letter longer because I have not had the time to make it shorter. The argument that 'using AI to generate student essays is disrespectful because it took no effort to write' misses the point. Respect for the recipient is measured by whether the message serves the recipient's needs, not how it is produced. Similarly, any errors are the student's responsibility, not the tools.

replies.text: I agree that the bottom line ought to be usefulness; if it's useful and doesn't waste my time, it's fine. However, I don't blame anybody for having red lines: 1. Don't send me a big long string that is merely LLM output without review. 2. Don't throw an AI-generated argument at me that you don't fully understand. 3. If you're preparing information for me, and it's verbose, I'll be twice as mad if it's obvious AI than if it's obviously human. This is basically the article's point.
replies.original_text: I agree that the bottom line really ought to be usefulness; if it's useful and doesn't waste my time, it's fine if you received it by the use of seer stones for all I care. However, I don't blame anybody for having red lines like this: 1. Don't send me a big long string that is merely LLM output resulting from pasting a trivial prompt + text I already have access to (or my own words!). I know about Claude too, and if that's what I wanted I'd have done it myself. 2. Don't throw an AI-generated argument at me that you don't even fully understand. 3. If you're preparing information for me, and it's overly verbose and wastes my time, I'll be twice as mad if it's obvious AI than if it's obviously human. This is basically the article's point. The asymmetry of wasting an hour of my time...

comments.text: This exactly reflects my feelings lately. I have a specific colleague who has gone somewhat overboard - every single lesson plan, answer to any question on email or Teams, every new activity, even their personal opinions during a meeting, are all direct AI output with no massaging or human touch. They're working on planning a unit, and I just get verbose documents to review, and based on the issues I find I doubt they are even looked over. I understand that the information may be accurate, even helpful at times, but feeling like I'm constantly talking to an AI chat bot gets tiring. And I don't appreciate having to double-check everyone else's AI-generated responses for them.

replies.text: I've seen this, too. There is a workplace personality that sees the job as a 2-player game between themselves and the school. They think the game is to min-max their effort to personal career benefit, and they don't care how much it inconveniences anyone else. Before AI they had to actually put in work. Now that AI appeared, they see it as the ultimate way to take credit for work they didn't do: Put everything into Claude, copy and paste. If managers aren't willing to intervene, it will continue.

I'm conflicted after reading this, because I think I would be that personality in my workplace, largely because I believe that's the only sane position as a worker with ~0 power. On the other hand, my priority isn't maximising personal career benefit, but the collective benefit of my team. Playing this way, outsourcing everything to an LLM is the worst move, because you lose the touchpoints that tell you where the friction is.
replies.original_text: I’m conflicted after reading this comment, because I think I would be that personality in my workplace, largely because I believe that’s the only sane position to take as a worker with ~0 power over the decisions made that can entirely destabilise your life. On the other hand, my priority isn’t maximising my personal career benefit, but the collective benefit of my team, so I suppose I either see it more as a 2v1 sorta game, or perhaps my “player” is an amalgam of myself and my teammates. Playing this way, outsourcing everything you do to an LLM is the worst move, because you lose the touchpoints that tell you where the friction is in your team.

comments.text: Obligatory Silicon Valley reference. So this post is talking about at work but I think the principle goes well beyond that. Think of all the AI chatbots you have to deal with to get through to registrar. Or get through automated admission systems. If it isn't already the case, this will probably replace or supplement TAs marking assignments. The problem is that AI makes these interactions too cheap for the party that already has disproportionate power. The cost for them to add another layer, another hurdle, another set of questions, etc is essentially zero. Yet everyone who wants to get through that system has to pay in a human cost.

comments.text: This headline has been seeing some popularity. But it's never made any sense. This is just the labor theory of value, applied to documents. The labor theory of value doesn't work for documents any more than it works for anything else. If I do something that's easy for me, and it's valuable to you, you'll still want it. If I do something that's difficult for me, it will be less valuable to you, because the difficulty I have with it implies that what I produce will be of lower quality. This is all equally true of automatically-generated documents. If they're valuable, people will want to read them. Whether it was unpleasant for someone to create them isn't a factor. So where is this slogan coming from? Are people just afraid to admit that the documents they're getting are valueless?

replies.text: The problem is that I don't know before I read a doc whether or not it will be useful and valuable. If someone wants me to spend my time and attention on something they have shared, I would like them to demonstrate that they put a proportionate amount of time and effort into its production.

First: why? How does that help you? Second: Is that actually true? Do you ever watch videos that a friend recommends? Even if the amount of time your friend put into producing that video is zero? Do you ever read anything that a friend recommends? Even if they didn't write it? How much time and effort, in your estimation, did the original poster put into producing this article?

replies.text: I think the point is that automatically-generated documents by LLM are lower quality than manually-reviewed ones. Therefore if you are not putting human effort on the document it is low-value. We have seen this before when big data started: tons of reports being auto-produced weekly, but even if they contain relevant information they are low-value because no one can take action on so much information.
If you get a document from someone and they say 'I have no idea if this has any value and I couldn't be arsed to check,' it's not unreasonable to presume that it probably has no value.
replies.original_text: > Therefor if you are not putting human effort on the document it is low-value. That's true. The document is low-value. Asking people to put in personal effort isn't going to change that. If they comply, the document they produce will still be worthless, and you still won't want it. You're diagnosing a problem unrelated to the problem you actually face.

I think the point is that automatically-generated documents by LLM is lower quality the manually-generated ones or at least guaranteed lower quality than automatically-generated + manually-reviewed. Therefor if you are not putting human effort on the document it is low-value. We have seen this before when big data started to be a thing, tons and tons of reports being auto-produced weekly (or even daily), but even if they contain relevant information they are low-value because no one can take action on so much information.

comments.original_text: This headline has been seeing some popularity. But it's never made any sense. This is just the labor theory of value, applied to documents. The labor theory of value doesn't work for documents any more than it works for anything else. If I do something that's easy for me, and it's valuable to you, you'll still want it. If I do something that's difficult for me, it will be less valuable to you, because the difficulty I have with it implies that what I produce will be of lower quality. This is all equally true of automatically-generated documents. If they're valuable, people will want to read them. Whether it was unpleasant for someone to create them isn't a factor. So where is this slogan coming from? Are people just afraid to admit that the documents they're getting are valueless?

The rest just copy. if you're good you'll probably relate. the rest kind of suck. i've never gotten anything lower than Exceeds Expectations in my evaluations so I've seen how awful some students were. i've seen how amazing a tiny minority were and i made them my mentors. these days i have a simple policy. if they cannot think, they get a zero. why waste resources (time and grades) on someone who can't use their brain? i'd rather give extra credit to someone who uses their brain. thinking is the student's job. the AI needs to execute on what the student thought of, improved, planned.

comments.text: In the DP core, we ask students to reflect on their own effort in the CAS portfolio. But if I don’t share my own struggles balancing work and life, they won't see the point.

comments.text: I would suggest changing the title to 'Adaptive Assignments'. Assuming the tool works, the assignment won't actually look different to the student than to the AI grading system, so there is nothing that 'changes based on who is marking'. It is just that rubric extraction, a wholly different (and much fuzzier) process than grading the assignment, will now return structured (checklist) data. Great idea, IMHO.

comments.original_text: I would suggest changing the title to the actual title of the article: Adaptive PDFs. Assuming the program works, the PDF will not actually look different to me than to anyone else looking at it, so there is nothing that "changes based on who is reading". It is just that text extraction, a wholly different (and much fuzzier) process than viewing the PDF, and something that the same person can do, will now return structured (Markdown) text. (One might say the PDF changes based on how you are reading it.) A great idea, IMHO.

comments.text: Just because everything is a potential assessment security issue now: doesn't this also mean you could easily put AI-specific instructions into the assignment that a regular student would never notice? Like the 'invisible text in comments that only appears when copy-pasted' hack that some students do to include extra information in their essays. But worse. Just thinking of a report card PDF provided to parents that an AI uses to extract grades and pre-process data. But instead we can command it to change grades that a parent wouldn't even notice...

replies.text: Yes, although that's not new. The amount of different exploits and cheating techniques I've seen in the past decade from just 'reading' a PDF is mind blowing. Not sure if it's slowed down but around 8 years ago students would patch a couple of RCE from PDF processing every few months.
replies.original_text: Yes, although that's not new. The amount of different exploits and RCE I've seen in the past decade from just "opening" an PDF is mind blowing. Not sure if it's slowed down but around 8 years ago ghostcript would patch a couple of RCE from PDF processing every few months.

replies.text: Oh this happens all the time. When schools announced they would be scanning all student submissions for plagiarism, they had some 'AI' system which would at some point consider the content of a grayscale and reduced quality version of the submission. The problem is that security researchers for years have known about pre-processing attacks where essays which appear as one thing (a standard essay) appear as something completely different (a perfect for AI scoring essay) once put through machine learning pre-processing.
replies.original_text: Oh this happens all the time. When Apple announced they would be scanning everyone’s private iCloud data for CSAM, they had some “PSI” system which would at some point consider the content of a grayscale and reduced quality version of the image. The problem is that security researchers for years have known about pre-processing attacks where photos which appear as one thing (a dog in a yard) appear ad something completely different (a cat on a couch) once put through machine learning pre-processing.

comments.text: Shouldn't it be possible since forever to put structured rubric data into assignment metadata? It's more a problem of the learning management systems generating the assignments. We spend millions turning structured learning outcomes into PDF reports and billions to extract the same data from a printed gradebook.

replies.text: Exactly. But we have no real coordination or uniform application in how we're creating assignments across all these different LMS tools, so we always end up with a fun mix of what will and won't be machine-readable.
replies.original_text: Exactly. But we have no real coordination or uniform application in how we're creating PDFs across all these programs so we always end up with a fun mix of what will and wont be static, scalable, searchable

replies.text: Exactly. It's pretty insane that we have converged on storing student work as PDF. And it looks like no work is done on making PDF files machine-readable for assessment.
replies.original_text: Exactly. It’s pretty insane that we have converged on storing documents as PDF. And it looks like no work is done on making PDF files machine readable.

comments.original_text: Shouldn’t it be possible since forever to put machine readable source information into PDF metadata. It’s more a problem of the tools and programs generating the PDFs. We spend millions turning structured information into PDFs and billions to extract the same data from a printer rendering language

comments.text: I'd be more interested in the contrary. An assignment that ensures it's only gradable by humans. I guess the exact same technique can actually be used.

replies.text: What would that be good for? If a human can grade it, you can also use AI to grade it.
replies.original_text: What would that be good for? If a human can read it, you can also use OCR.

replies.text: Why would you use the exact same technique? Remove all rubric criteria and all text from the assignment and render everything as images. It's an old trick to prevent students from using AI to do their assignments. And of course, AI grading doesn't work here just like it doesn't work for the original use case.
Sure but that degrades the experience of the student if they want to use text-to-speech or copy/paste parts. Or it simply isn't an option if your assignment is supposed to be interactive.
replies.original_text: Sure but that degrades the experience of the reader if they want to copy/paste a part for example (not that this works great on PDFs...). Or it simply isn't an option if your PDF is supposed to be interactive.

Why would you use the exact same technique? Remove all fonts and all text from the PDF and render everything as vector graphics. It’s an old trick to prevent people from extracting paid commercial fonts from your PDF. And of course, OCR doesn’t work here just like it doesn’t work for the original use case.

comments.original_text: I'd be more interested in the contrary. A PDF that ensures it's only readable by humans. I guess the exact same technique can actually be used.

comments.text: In many international schools, we are required to code our assessments with semantic structure to support machine access by screen readers and other assistive technologies (IEPs, 504 plans). Given the low adherence to accessibility standards e.g. in many IB schools, LLM parsing needs creating a incentive for comparable structured access would be marvelous. [1] Section508.gov [2] PDF Association [3] arxiv

comments.original_text: Very interesting, but also quite sad that today's renderers ignore the finer points of the specification. On a related note, I like the ability of good old HTML to be able to change text for different human readers, based on their chosen locale. With this I can change units such as litres to 'fluid flagon ounces' or whatever it is they use in the USA, or I can drop in a friendly greeting in a foreign language. I have not seen this done in the wild, usually it is a trip back to the server for a different locale, or the server does the locale reading before sending the page. As for our AI overlords, HTML5 content sectioning markup done to HTML5 specifications should be helpful, yet I have yet to see this done in the wild. PDF has its uses but CSS for print interests me far more. I am not...

replies.text: It sounds like you got bitten by the dynamic I wrote about here: https://eduhackernews.com/item?id=48467726 : that is, using an LLM to polish a resource for a limited reason (such as to improve its wording) and then finding that the LLM left lots of other fingerprints, causing colleagues to perceive the entire thing as AI-generated. We're seeing a ton of this right now! In case it's helpful, here's something I've been saying when replying to emails: We understand that our non-native English speaking teachers are in a special position with all of this, and we sympathize - but we don't have an easy way to treat resources differently on that basis. What we're telling such teachers is to please write in your own voice and don't worry about any mistakes, because those are rapidly becoming signs of authenticity.
replies.original_text: It sounds like you got bitten by the dynamic I wrote about here: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48467726 : that is, using an LLM to process text for a limited reason (such as to improve its English) and then finding that the LLM left lots of other fingerprints, causing readers to perceive the entire thing as genai. We're seeing a ton of this right now! In case it's helpful, here's something I've been saying when replying to emails: We understand that our non-native English speaking users are in a special position with all of this, and we sympathize - but we don't have an easy way to treat posts differently on that basis. comments.text: Not the same thing, but I found a way to embed lesson plan editable sources within the PDF of the unit plan. The trick is to generate the PDF normally, then zip this same PDF together with the Google Docs link and rubric spreadsheet again, with compression level 0, making sure that the PDF is the first file to go in the archive. (Easy to write a script that does this.) The resulting file, when given the extension PDF, is readable as PDF, and when given the extension ZIP, is extractable as ZIP. So whoever wants the editable version can rename the file to .zip and extract the source. The instruction to do so can be in the PDF text itself. Why it works: a) compression level 0 means that the input files are just copied into the stream, so the PDF reader will find the PDF header, decode the rest of the PDF,...

replies.text: That seems like it would be incredibly fragile. As soon as the receiving teacher made a change that required re-saving the PDF -- like adding comments, grading, changing layout, saving as a PDF/a, checking accessibility -- it might erase the attached files. It's also very easy to use Google Drive or Dropbox to share a folder with the editable versions and the PDF. No renaming or special knowledge required.
replies.original_text: That seems like it would be incredibly fragile. As soon as the receiving party made a change that required re-saving the PDF -- like commenting, highlighting, changing default layouts, saving as a PDF/a, checking PDF/ua, etc. -- it might erase the attached files. It's also very easy to use pdftk to embed or attach files in a PDF using the methods defined in the PDF standard. No renaming or special knowledge required of the audience.

comments.original_text: Not the same thing, but I found a way to distribute markdown sources (with images) within the PDF files generated from these sources. (Easy to write a script that does this.) The resulting file, when given the extension PDF, is readable as PDF, and when given the extension ZIP, is extractable as ZIP. So whoever wants the source can rename the file to .zip and extract the source. The instruction to do so can be in the PDF text itself. Why it works: a) compression level 0 means that the input files are just copied into the stream, so the PDF reader will find the PDF header, decode the rest of the PDF,...
