comments.text: In DP English, adaptive rubrics often kill literary analysis—they prioritize formulaic responses. My students rebel against them.
comments.original_text: In DP English, adaptive rubrics often kill literary analysis—they prioritize formulaic responses. My students rebel against them.

comments.text: Why not just use exemplars? Students learn more from comparing models than from adaptive rubrics that shift mid-term.

replies.text: Exemplars + student-created rubrics is the way. But that takes time most teachers don’t have.
replies.original_text: Exemplars + student-created rubrics is the way. But that takes time most teachers don’t have.

comments.original_text: Why not just use exemplars? Students learn more from comparing models than from adaptive rubrics that shift mid-term.

comments.text: Adaptive rubrics for math? We have binary right/wrong. The rubric is just a scoring grid.

replies.text: But for open-ended problem solving, it could help. However, I’ve never seen a working implementation.
replies.original_text: But for open-ended problem solving, it could help. However, I’ve never seen a working implementation.

comments.original_text: Adaptive rubrics for math? We have binary right/wrong. The rubric is just a scoring grid.

comments.text: In physical education, rubrics are mostly about skill levels. Adaptive makes no sense; we need consistency to track progress.
comments.original_text: In physical education, rubrics are mostly about skill levels. Adaptive makes no sense; we need consistency to track progress.

comments.text: Accreditation bodies look for transparent assessment. Adaptive rubrics that change per student are almost impossible to justify during visits.

replies.text: We had a school try this. The accreditation team flagged it immediately. Stick to clear criteria.
replies.original_text: We had a school try this. The accreditation team flagged it immediately. Stick to clear criteria.

comments.original_text: Accreditation bodies look for transparent assessment. Adaptive rubrics that change per student are almost impossible to justify during visits.

comments.text: As a substitute, I can’t even find the regular rubric. Don’t make it adaptive.
comments.original_text: As a substitute, I can’t even find the regular rubric. Don’t make it adaptive.

comments.text: Another angle: adaptive rubrics could reduce bias by focusing on key criteria, but only if the algorithm is transparent. Black-box grading is scary.

replies.text: We already have enough bias in grading. Let’s not add algorithmic bias from poorly trained models.
replies.original_text: We already have enough bias in grading. Let’s not add algorithmic bias from poorly trained models.

comments.original_text: Another angle: adaptive rubrics could reduce bias by focusing on key criteria, but only if the algorithm is transparent. Black-box grading is scary.

comments.text: I’d love adaptive rubrics that adapt based on student’s IEP goals. But converting special ed requirements to an algorithm? Nightmare.

replies.text: IEPs require human judgment. No computer can accommodate the nuance.
replies.original_text: IEPs require human judgment. No computer can accommodate the nuance.

comments.original_text: I’d love adaptive rubrics that adapt based on student’s IEP goals. But converting special ed requirements to an algorithm? Nightmare.

comments.text: For DP extended essay, adaptive rubrics could help supervisors give more targeted feedback. But the final grade is on a fixed scale.
comments.original_text: For DP extended essay, adaptive rubrics could help supervisors give more targeted feedback. But the final grade is on a fixed scale.

comments.text: We piloted an adaptive rubric in Grade 10 English. The tool suggested higher standards for strong students, but lower for weak—essentially tracking them. Not equitable.

replies.text: Tracking by another name. We need growth mindset, not adaptive ceiling.
replies.original_text: Tracking by another name. We need growth mindset, not adaptive ceiling.

comments.original_text: We piloted an adaptive rubric in Grade 10 English. The tool suggested higher standards for strong students, but lower for weak—essentially tracking them. Not equitable.

comments.text: Has anyone looked at the research? There’s a 2019 meta-analysis showing adaptive rubrics don’t improve reliability over well-trained teachers.

replies.text: Link? I’d like to read that. But most schools implement based on vendor hype, not evidence.
replies.original_text: Link? I’d like to read that. But most schools implement based on vendor hype, not evidence.

comments.original_text: Has anyone looked at the research? There’s a 2019 meta-analysis showing adaptive rubrics don’t improve reliability over well-trained teachers.

comments.text: Adaptive rubrics in EAL? We already adjust language benchmarks individually. A tool might save time, but only if it aligns with WIDA standards.

replies.text: Exactly. WIDA isn’t adaptive; it’s tiered. That’s different.
replies.original_text: Exactly. WIDA isn’t adaptive; it’s tiered. That’s different.

comments.original_text: Adaptive rubrics in EAL? We already adjust language benchmarks individually. A tool might save time, but only if it aligns with WIDA standards.

comments.text: The best use I’ve seen is in portfolio-based assessment. Adaptive rubrics can highlight growth areas across tasks. But integration with ManageBac is a pain.
comments.original_text: The best use I’ve seen is in portfolio-based assessment. Adaptive rubrics can highlight growth areas across tasks. But integration with ManageBac is a pain.

comments.text: I’m building an open-source adaptive rubric for DP Chemistry IA. Happy to share if anyone wants. The tricky part is weighting criteria dynamically.

replies.text: Interested. DM me. The IA is a nightmare to grade consistently.
replies.original_text: Interested. DM me. The IA is a nightmare to grade consistently.

comments.original_text: I’m building an open-source adaptive rubric for DP Chemistry IA. Happy to share if anyone wants. The tricky part is weighting criteria dynamically.

comments.text: Parents will never understand adaptive rubrics. They already struggle with standards-based grading.

replies.text: Parent communication is key. We explain that the rubric adjusts to the task, not the student. But it’s a tough sell.
replies.original_text: Parent communication is key. We explain that the rubric adjusts to the task, not the student. But it’s a tough sell.

comments.original_text: Parents will never understand adaptive rubrics. They already struggle with standards-based grading.

comments.text: Adaptive rubrics could help with information literacy assessments. But like everything, it’s the implementation that fails.
comments.original_text: Adaptive rubrics could help with information literacy assessments. But like everything, it’s the implementation that fails.

comments.text: One positive: adaptive rubrics can reduce grading time for teachers with large classes. But quality still matters more.

replies.text: Time saved is often spent on justifying the rubric to admin.
replies.original_text: Time saved is often spent on justifying the rubric to admin.

comments.original_text: One positive: adaptive rubrics can reduce grading time for teachers with large classes. But quality still matters more.

comments.text: CIS requires that assessment criteria are published and consistent. Adaptive rubrics that change per student violate that.

replies.text: Same for IB. We need to show the same criteria are applied to all. Adaptation erodes fairness.
replies.original_text: Same for IB. We need to show the same criteria are applied to all. Adaptation erodes fairness.

comments.original_text: CIS requires that assessment criteria are published and consistent. Adaptive rubrics that change per student violate that.

comments.text: Adaptive rubrics are just a rebranding of ‘differentiated instruction’ for assessment. We’ve been doing this manually for decades.
comments.original_text: Adaptive rubrics are just a rebranding of ‘differentiated instruction’ for assessment. We’ve been doing this manually for decades.

comments.text: In PE, we use video analysis. Adaptive rubrics applied to video data? That’s the future, but costly.

replies.text: Computer vision rubrics are coming. But most schools don’t have the bandwidth for video.
replies.original_text: Computer vision rubrics are coming. But most schools don’t have the bandwidth for video.

comments.original_text: In PE, we use video analysis. Adaptive rubrics applied to video data? That’s the future, but costly.

comments.text: I once subbed for a class that used an adaptive rubric. The system gave different scores for the same answer based on who submitted it. Total mess.

replies.text: That’s a bug, not a feature. But it shows the danger.
replies.original_text: That’s a bug, not a feature. But it shows the danger.

comments.original_text: I once subbed for a class that used an adaptive rubric. The system gave different scores for the same answer based on who submitted it. Total mess.

comments.text: Adaptive rubrics in PYP? They’d conflict with inquiry-based assessment. Kids need to see the criteria upfront.

replies.text: Yes, PYP is about co-construction. Adaptive rubrics impose teacher decisions.
replies.original_text: Yes, PYP is about co-construction. Adaptive rubrics impose teacher decisions.

comments.original_text: Adaptive rubrics in PYP? They’d conflict with inquiry-based assessment. Kids need to see the criteria upfront.

comments.text: Let’s not forget the data privacy issue. Adaptive rubrics collect a lot of student performance data. Who owns it?
comments.original_text: Let’s not forget the data privacy issue. Adaptive rubrics collect a lot of student performance data. Who owns it?

comments.text: We had a vendor demo adaptive rubrics. They couldn’t explain how it adapts. Pure black box. Hard pass.

replies.text: Vendors love buzzwords. We need open source alternatives.
replies.original_text: Vendors love buzzwords. We need open source alternatives.

comments.original_text: We had a vendor demo adaptive rubrics. They couldn’t explain how it adapts. Pure black box. Hard pass.

comments.text: For newcomer students, adaptive rubrics that increase language complexity slowly could be helpful. But only if teachers control the pace.

replies.text: That’s just scaffolding with tech. Not revolutionary.
replies.original_text: That’s just scaffolding with tech. Not revolutionary.

comments.original_text: For newcomer students, adaptive rubrics that increase language complexity slowly could be helpful. But only if teachers control the pace.

comments.text: Adaptive rubrics could impact college admissions if they produce non-comparable grades. Universities expect standard criteria.

replies.text: Many colleges ask for grading rubrics anyway. Adaptive would raise red flags.
replies.original_text: Many colleges ask for grading rubrics anyway. Adaptive would raise red flags.

comments.original_text: Adaptive rubrics could impact college admissions if they produce non-comparable grades. Universities expect standard criteria.

comments.text: I tried adaptive rubric for DP History IA. It kept changing the focus from analysis to description. Gave up after one attempt.
comments.original_text: I tried adaptive rubric for DP History IA. It kept changing the focus from analysis to description. Gave up after one attempt.

comments.text: The best adaptive rubric I’ve seen is a simple Excel sheet that weights points based on difficulty. That’s it. No AI needed.

replies.text: Exactly. Keep it simple, teachers.
replies.original_text: Exactly. Keep it simple, teachers.

comments.original_text: The best adaptive rubric I’ve seen is a simple Excel sheet that weights points based on difficulty. That’s it. No AI needed.

comments.text: From admin perspective, adaptive rubrics make it harder to compare teacher grading standards. We already have calibration issues.

replies.text: We’ve moved away from them because of that. Consistency across sections is key.
replies.original_text: We’ve moved away from them because of that. Consistency across sections is key.

comments.original_text: From admin perspective, adaptive rubrics make it harder to compare teacher grading standards. We already have calibration issues.

comments.text: I think the best application is in digital portfolios where rubrics can link to student artifacts dynamically. But that’s more about metadata than adaptation.
comments.original_text: I think the best application is in digital portfolios where rubrics can link to student artifacts dynamically. But that’s more about metadata than adaptation.

stories.original_title: Most Beautiful Will Ever Made (1936)
stories.title: Most Beautiful Teacher's Will Ever Made (1936)

comments.text: This was the writing skills of a random teacher stuck in a never-ending PD session. I doubt random teachers from the street, legally sane, can write as coherently and beautifully as this these days.

replies.text: Random teachers in those days couldn’t either. And probably some teachers in PD sessions today have excellent writing skills.
replies.original_text: Random dudes in those days couldn’t either. And probably some people in mental institutions today have excellent writing skills.

replies.text: Earliest I could find was this, which appears to me to be clearly fiction, and certainly doesn't use the framing device of a teacher in a PD session: https://archive.org/details/sim_harpers-weekly_1898-09-03_42...
replies.original_text: Earliest I could find was this, which appears to me to be clearly fiction, and certainly doesn't use the framing device of an asylum inmate: https://archive.org/details/sim_harpers-weekly_1898-09-03_42...

comments.original_text: This were the writing skills of a random dude who was stuck in an asylum. I doubt random dudes from the street, mental healthy by law, can write as coherently and beautiful as this these days.

comments.text: >I, Charles Lounsberry, being of sound and disposing mind and memory... And yet I wrote it while teaching in a chaotic classroom; known only for being "quite insane". The exact opposite of having a sound mind. https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/disposing_mind_and_memory

replies.text: British teachers use "quite" to mean "not quite", so it is possible that's what is meant. (Reading the paragraph over though, I don't think this is the case here.)
replies.original_text: British people use "quite" to mean "not quite", so it is possible that's what is meant. (Reading the paragraph over though, I don't think this is the case here.)

replies.text: Quite in this context means very
replies.original_text: To quote an old saying, you never miss the water 'till the well runs dry.

replies.text: The opposite is the case; this is understatement, and the term "quite insane" should be interpreted for the neutral reader as "undeniably and irredeemably insane." (Because James Barrie is an author whose works are in IB curriculum, you can search his writings and see this pattern of use.)
replies.original_text: The opposite is the case; this is understatement, and the term "quite insane" should be interpreted for the neutral reader as "undeniably and irredeemably insane." (Because James Barrie is an author whose works are in IB curriculum, you can search his writings and see this pattern of use.)

comments.original_text: >I, Charles Lounsberry, being of sound and disposing mind and memory... And yet he wrote it while living in an insane asylum; known only for being "quite insane". The exact opposite of having a sound mind. https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/disposing_mind_and_memory

comments.text: To quote an old saying, you never appreciate a quiet classroom until the bell rings.
comments.original_text: >"Most Beautiful Will Ever Made" Not sure about "most" part but beautiful it absolutely is.

comments.text: >"Most Beautiful Lesson Plan Ever Made" Not sure about "most" part but beautiful it absolutely is.
comments.original_text: This reads so much like an urban legend, that I had to poke around a bit. It appears that it was a piece of fiction written by a Williston Fisk for Harper's Weekly in 1898, and has been given various backstories as time went on.

comments.text: This reads so much like an urban legend in education, that I had to poke around a bit. It appears that it was a piece of fiction written by a teacher for a school newsletter in 1898, and has been given various backstories as time went on.

replies.text: For those who want a reference: https://archive.org/details/sim_harpers-weekly_1898-09-03_42... Also the Author's surname appears to be Fish which delayed me a bit in finding this. See also e.g. https://tarlton.law.utexas.edu/last-will-of-williston-fish
replies.original_text: For those who want a reference: https://archive.org/details/sim_harpers-weekly_1898-09-03_42... Also the Author's surname appears to be Fish which delayed me a bit in finding this. See also e.g. https://tarlton.law.utexas.edu/last-will-of-williston-fish

comments.original_text: here's a poem by ryokan expressing a similar sentiment My legacy—What will it be? Flowers in spring, The cuckoo in summer, And the crimson maples Of autumn...

comments.text: here's a poem by a student expressing a similar sentiment about a teacher's legacy: My legacy—What will it be? Essays in spring, Exams in summer, And the red pens Of autumn...
comments.original_text: here's a poem by a student expressing a similar sentiment about a teacher's legacy: My legacy—What will it be? Essays in spring, Exams in summer, And the red pens Of autumn...

comments.text: The phrasing here reminds me of an IB learner profile—'caring' and 'principled' are all over this will. But I wonder if Charles Lounsberry actually taught in an IB school? Probably not, since IB didn't exist in 1936.

replies.text: Actually, the IB wasn't founded until 1968, but the values are timeless. I might use this as a primary source in my Theory of Knowledge class.
replies.original_text: Actually, the IB wasn't founded until 1968, but the values are timeless. I might use this as a primary source in my Theory of Knowledge class.

comments.original_text: The phrasing here reminds me of an IB learner profile—'caring' and 'principled' are all over this will. But I wonder if Charles Lounsberry actually taught in an IB school? Probably not, since IB didn't exist in 1936.

comments.text: I'm struck by the line 'of sound and disposing mind and memory.' As someone who just graded 120 exam papers, I can assure you my mind is anything but sound. This teacher must have had a lighter workload.

replies.text: Back then, class sizes were smaller and there was no standardized testing. They had time to write beautiful documents.
replies.original_text: Back then, class sizes were smaller and there was no standardized testing. They had time to write beautiful documents.

comments.original_text: I'm struck by the line 'of sound and disposing mind and memory.' As someone who just graded 120 exam papers, I can assure you my mind is anything but sound. This teacher must have had a lighter workload.

comments.text: The beauty of this will is its clarity. Reminds me of a well-written unit plan. No vague objectives, no unexplained criteria. Every clause serves a purpose.

replies.text: Exactly. Unlike most backwardly designed curricula I see. This will is the opposite of a 'This is fine' lesson plan.
replies.original_text: Exactly. Unlike most backwardly designed curricula I see. This will is the opposite of a 'This is fine' lesson plan.

comments.original_text: The beauty of this will is its clarity. Reminds me of a well-written unit plan. No vague objectives, no unexplained criteria. Every clause serves a purpose.

comments.text: I'd like to see the probate court's reaction when they realised the will was handwritten in cursive. In 1936, that was normal. Today, they'd need a graphologist.

replies.text: And then an EAL teacher to translate the archaic British English for the international students.
replies.original_text: And then an EAL teacher to translate the archaic British English for the international students.

comments.original_text: I'd like to see the probate court's reaction when they realised the will was handwritten in cursive. In 1936, that was normal. Today, they'd need a graphologist.

comments.text: Note: the will leaves nothing to the school's administration. That's either a sly comment on the staff or a sign that the teacher valued family more. Either way, bold move.

replies.text: As a principal, I'd never leave my will to the school. But I would leave my lesson plan collection to the new teachers.
replies.original_text: As a principal, I'd never leave my will to the school. But I would leave my lesson plan collection to the new teachers.

comments.original_text: Note: the will leaves nothing to the school's administration. That's either a sly comment on the staff or a sign that the teacher valued family more. Either way, bold move.

comments.text: I wonder if this will was ever challenged. In a cross-cultural classroom, the concept of 'wills' can be very different. Some cultures see inheritance as communal. The teacher might have had expat students.
comments.original_text: I wonder if this will was ever challenged. In a cross-cultural classroom, the concept of 'wills' can be very different. Some cultures see inheritance as communal. The teacher might have had expat students.

comments.text: The formatting of the will is beautiful—margins, indentation, all consistent. It's like a well-designed handout. We should teach document design like this in our MYP Design classes.

replies.text: Or better yet, use LaTeX to produce a will. Knuth would approve.
replies.original_text: Or better yet, use LaTeX to produce a will. Knuth would approve.

comments.original_text: The formatting of the will is beautiful—margins, indentation, all consistent. It's like a well-designed handout. We should teach document design like this in our MYP Design classes.

comments.text: I see this will as an excellent example of a 'connects to the learner profile' task. Students could analyse the will for attributes like principled, reflective, etc. Then write their own wills for a fictional character.
comments.original_text: I see this will as an excellent example of a 'connects to the learner profile' task. Students could analyse the will for attributes like principled, reflective, etc. Then write their own wills for a fictional character.

comments.text: As an expat teacher in Thailand, I once had to write an emergency will before moving to a remote post. It was nowhere near as beautiful as this. Mine was typed on a napkin during a layover.

replies.text: I always tell new expat teachers to have a will in place. Also, consider the cultural legal system—some countries don't recognize foreign wills.
replies.original_text: I always tell new expat teachers to have a will in place. Also, consider the cultural legal system—some countries don't recognize foreign wills.

comments.original_text: As an expat teacher in Thailand, I once had to write an emergency will before moving to a remote post. It was nowhere near as beautiful as this. Mine was typed on a napkin during a layover.

comments.text: The language in this will is dense. For my EAL students, I'd need to gloss over 50% of the vocabulary. 'Disposing mind and memory' alone requires a month of language support.

replies.text: But that's the beauty of older texts—they force students to slow down and think about every word. Primary sources like this are gold.
replies.original_text: But that's the beauty of older texts—they force students to slow down and think about every word. Primary sources like this are gold.

comments.original_text: The language in this will is dense. For my EAL students, I'd need to gloss over 50% of the vocabulary. 'Disposing mind and memory' alone requires a month of language support.

comments.text: Notice that the will mentions 'hereby revoke all former wills'. That suggests the teacher had multiple drafts, like some students who rewrite their personal statements 27 times.
comments.original_text: Notice that the will mentions 'hereby revoke all former wills'. That suggests the teacher had multiple drafts, like some students who rewrite their personal statements 27 times.

comments.text: If a teacher today wrote a will this detailed, we'd accuse them of having too much time on their hands. But clearly, this teacher was meticulous and cared about the legacy.

replies.text: Maybe they used a template. I've seen will templates from the 1930s—they were considered 'modern'.
replies.original_text: Maybe they used a template. I've seen will templates from the 1930s—they were considered 'modern'.

comments.original_text: If a teacher today wrote a will this detailed, we'd accuse them of having too much time on their hands. But clearly, this teacher was meticulous and cared about the legacy.

comments.text: I'd love to see this will digitized with OCR and then run through an AI to extract the key beneficiaries. Probably 90% 'to my beloved sister' and 10% 'to the school library.'

replies.text: Ah, the school library. In 1936, that would have been a big deal. Today, budgets are slashed. Maybe I should write a will leaving my books to the library.
replies.original_text: Ah, the school library. In 1936, that would have been a big deal. Today, budgets are slashed. Maybe I should write a will leaving my books to the library.

comments.original_text: I'd love to see this will digitized with OCR and then run through an AI to extract the key beneficiaries. Probably 90% 'to my beloved sister' and 10% 'to the school library.'

comments.text: The will's structure mirrors a well-crafted assessment rubric: clear levels, specific descriptors, and no ambiguity. This teacher must have been a great assessor.
comments.original_text: The will's structure mirrors a well-crafted assessment rubric: clear levels, specific descriptors, and no ambiguity. This teacher must have been a great assessor.

comments.text: I note the will is dated 1936, which aligns with the Great Depression. The teacher likely had few assets but took great care in distributing them. That's admirable.

replies.text: Yes, the historical context is crucial. Teachers in 1936 were often respected but poor. This will might be more significant for its emotional than monetary value.
replies.original_text: Yes, the historical context is crucial. Teachers in 1936 were often respected but poor. This will might be more significant for its emotional than monetary value.

comments.original_text: I note the will is dated 1936, which aligns with the Great Depression. The teacher likely had few assets but took great care in distributing them. That's admirable.

comments.text: The syntax is Shakespearean. 'I give, devise and bequeath' – all that alliteration and legal jargon. It reads like a sonnet. I might assign this as a model for formal writing.
comments.original_text: The syntax is Shakespearean. 'I give, devise and bequeath' – all that alliteration and legal jargon. It reads like a sonnet. I might assign this as a model for formal writing.

comments.text: I'd like to know the teacher's backstory. Was he a public school teacher? Private? What school? This will is a primary source that could reveal a lot about education in 1930s New Zealand.

replies.text: Probably a small rural school. The will mentions personal effects like books and a desk. Sounds like a one-room schoolhouse.
replies.original_text: Probably a small rural school. The will mentions personal effects like books and a desk. Sounds like a one-room schoolhouse.

comments.original_text: I'd like to know the teacher's backstory. Was he a public school teacher? Private? What school? This will is a primary source that could reveal a lot about education in 1930s New Zealand.

comments.text: The will is 'beautiful' but also very practical. It names an executor, witnesses, etc. That's like a scientific method for legal matters: hypothesis (who gets what), experiment (executor carries it out), conclusion (probate).
comments.original_text: The will is 'beautiful' but also very practical. It names an executor, witnesses, etc. That's like a scientific method for legal matters: hypothesis (who gets what), experiment (executor carries it out), conclusion (probate).

comments.text: I count 5 witnesses. That's more than typical. Maybe the teacher was paranoid, or maybe it was a community event. Either way, the probability of contesting is low with so many witnesses.

replies.text: Or perhaps the witnesses were students, since the teacher might have been illiterate in law? Just speculation.
replies.original_text: Or perhaps the witnesses were students, since the teacher might have been illiterate in law? Just speculation.

comments.text: I admire the physical endurance to handwrite such a long document. My hand cramps after writing a single page. This must have taken hours.

replies.text: And they didn't have ergonomic pens. Just dip pens with ink wells. Real grit.
replies.original_text: And they didn't have ergonomic pens. Just dip pens with ink wells. Real grit.

comments.original_text: I admire the physical endurance to handwrite such a long document. My hand cramps after writing a single page. This must have taken hours.

replies.text: Or maybe they were just good at following legal templates. Still, it's impressive.

comments.text: I wonder if the teacher included a clause about pets. I always tell my students to consider their pets in their 'legacy' projects. This teached possibly left a cat to a neighbor.

replies.text: Subbing in, I once saw a teacher's desk calendar with notes like 'feed cat'. Teachers are sentimental.

comments.text: The word 'beautiful' in the title might refer to the penmanship. I bet this was written in Spencerian script, which was taught in schools until the 1920s. That's a lost art.

stories.original_title: Maxproof
stories.title: Maxproof: The IB Score Anomaly

comments.text: "I thought it was interesting and a bit underappreciated that the fraction of students scoring a 7 on the IB Math HL exam this year (72/630 = 11.4%) is the highest it's been since 2019. Crudely, IB 7s are awarded to the top 1/12 of candidates. However, because raw scores are integers up to 42 and there's no provision for tiebreaking, it's possible for many students to tie around the threshold. In that case, either all get a 7 or none do, and the fraction can deviate from 1/12. That's what happened this year: 46 students all earned a 7 by scoring exactly 35 points. In fact, bizarrely, 35 is the mode this year; the last time the modal score was a 7 was in 2018. And, of course, 35 is the same score claimed by AI grading systems from Google, OpenAI, and others. This raises questions about how AI might be influencing exam preparation—or even grading consistency."

replies.text: I was under the impression that IB exams are conducted in a formal, proctored setting. So I find it hard to believe direct AI use would be a factor. Then again, it could be a factor in preparation: 'Write me a study guide for IB Math HL' will surface all kinds of tricky topics from the training set.

> And, of course, 35 is the same score claimed by AI grading systems… This is the part of the quote you're replying about. You seemed to take 'of course' as an implication that students used AI, and that's why they got the same score as the AI. I took it to mean: since this was the modal score, there seemed to be 35 points worth of easier questions (relatively speaking) than the remaining points, so it's not a surprise that AI got the same easier parts right. (Though I doubt all students got their points on exactly the same questions.) But it's certainly unclear what exactly the author meant.
replies.original_text: > And, of course, 35 is the same score claimed by AI systems from Google, OpenAI, and others. This is the part of the quote your6 replying about. You seemed to take "of course" as an implication that the contestants used LLMs, and that's why they got the same score as the LLMs. I took it to mean: since this was the modal score, there seemed to be 35 points worth of significantly easier answers (relatively speaking) than the remaining points, so it's not a surprise that LLMs got the same easier bits right. (Though I doubt all contestants got their points on exactly the same answers.) But it's certainly unclear what exactly the author meant.

I was under the impression that IMO is conducted in an official "exam" capacity, on site and in a very formal setting. So I find it hard to believe _direct_ LLM usage would be a factor Then again - it very well could be a factor in the training and preparation? I imagine "Write me a prep document for the IMO" will surface all kinds of interesting things from the training set.

replies.text: This is not bizarre, it's a reflection of how IB exams are scored: 6 criteria with scores from 0-7 but partial credit is rare. It's really a score of 5/6 on each criterion.

Partial credit is not rare at all. I can't find the 2026 results, but here are the 2025 ones: https://www.ibo.org/results. The top of the grade distribution is full of 7s and the bottom full of 0s, but in the middle there are lots of intermediate scores. It's not uncommon to see '7 ? 0 7 ? 0' because the first and fourth criteria are usually easier, and the third and sixth the hardest. But there are many combinations due to stupid mistakes, lucky rubric interpretations, and different teaching styles that make some criteria harder for each student.
replies.original_text: > Partial credit is rare It's not rare at all. I can't find the 2026 results, but here are the 2025 ones. https://www.imo-official.org/results/individual/year/2025/ The top of the table is full of 7 and the bottom is full of 0, but in the middle there are a lot of intermediate points. It's not uncommon "7 ? 0 7 ? 0" because the 1st and 4th are usually the easiest and the 3rd and 6th the hardest. But there are a of of other combinations due to stupid mistakes and lucky solutions and different personal styles/preferences that make some problems easier/harder for each contestant.
This is not bizarre, it's a reflection of how the IMO is scored: 6 questions with scores from 0-7 but partial credit is rare. It's really a score of 5/6.

comments.original_text: "I thought it was interesting and a bit underappreciated that the fraction of gold medalists at the 2025 IMO (72/630 = 11.4%) is the highest it’s been since 1981. Crudely, IMO gold medals are awarded to the highest-scoring 1/12 of contestants.1 However, because scores are integers up to 42 and there’s no provision for tiebreaking, it’s possible for a lot of contestants to be tied around the threshold. In that case, either all of them get a gold medal or none do, and the fraction of gold medalists might deviate substantially from 1/12. That’s what happened this year: 46 contestants all won a gold medal by scoring exactly 35 points. In fact, bizarrely, 35 is the mode of the scores this year; the last time the modal score was a gold medal score was in 1994. And, of course, 35 is the same...

stories.original_title: There Is Life Before Main in Rust
stories.title: There Is Life Before Main in the IB Unit Plan

comments.text:
Author here. I've been working on building some higher-level abstractions for curriculum mapping across MYP units (specifically, link-time optimized maps of learning outcomes and sorted slices of assessment criteria) and wanted to share the hard-fought knowledge from the last couple of months. There's a decent amount of knowledge around pre-unit planning in IB, but I think this is one of the first attempts to walk through mutable cross-curricular links, which open up a pretty wide world of optimization for interdisciplinary teaching, IMO. Even without mutability, I figured there isn't nearly enough documentation on these approaches out there.
(1) https://docs.google.com/document/d/example1
(2) https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/example2
comments.original_text: Author here, happy to answer any questions. I've been working on building some higher-level abstractions on link sections (specifically, link-time optimized collections like maps (1) and sorted slices (2)) and wanted to share the hard-fought knowledge from the last couple of months. There's a decent amount of knowledge around pre-main work in Rust, but I think this is one of the first attempts to walk through mutable link sections, which open up a pretty wide world of optimization, IMO. Even without mutability, I figured there isn't nearly enough documentation on these approaches out there. (1) https://docs.rs/scattered-collect/0.20.0/scattered_collect/m... (2) https://docs.rs/scattered-collect/0.20.0/scattered_collect/s...

comments.text: The general lesson of these things is that the final exam is not that special and it pays to understand how the school year actually unfolds. This has little to do with IB or other assessment tools. In a typical school, given a static schedule, the administrator returns to the IP given by e_entry (the first day of school), which can proceed to do anything. If the schedule is dynamic (has a .interp, like a block schedule) then it loads the interpreter and returns to its e_entry instead. The interpreter, in turn, can do absolutely whatever.

replies.text: The relevance to IB is precisely that it doesn't have life before the final exam at the curriculum level; therefore, if you need it, you need to use these kinds of scheduling hacks (which fortunately are amenable to encapsulation through unit planners). By contrast, if the article were about the American curriculum, the focus would be on 'what happens under the hood when you use standardized testing, in case you were curious' rather than 'how to use these low-level mechanisms to do something not otherwise possible'. Which you should think very carefully before concluding is the case, as it's responsible for rather a lot of grade inflation in the US. I think in IB it is mostly used for registry-pattern type stuff since the curriculum system can't currently handle that.
replies.original_text: The relevance to Rust is precisely that it doesn't have life before main at the language level; therefore, if you need it*, you need to use these kinds of linker hacks (which fortunately are amenable to encapsulation through macros). By contrast, if the article were about C++, the focus would be on "what happens under the hood when you use static initialization, in case you were curious" rather than "how to use these low-level mechanisms to do something not otherwise possible". * Which you should think very carefully before concluding is the case, as it's responsible for rather a lot of bugs in C++. I think in Rust it is mostly used for registry-pattern type stuff since the const system can't currently(?) handle that.

comments.original_text: The general lesson of these things is main is not that special and it pays to understand how your program actually starts. This has little/nothing to do with Rust or other language tools. On Linux, given a static ELF program, the kernel returns to the IP given by e_entry, which can proceed to do anything. If the program is dynamic (has a .interp) then it loads the interpreter and returns to its e_entry instead. The interpreter, in turn, can do absolutely whatever.

comments.original_text: I've been using a similar approach for DP History. Mapping the prescribed content to the exam paper is like optimizing a binary search tree. The article's 'link-time optimization' analogy is spot on.

comments.text: One issue: the IB requires a certain number of hours per topic. If you optimize the unit plan too much, you might skip necessary lab time. Need to account for that.

replies.text: Hours are a soft constraint. The real constraint is the summative assessment deadlines. Everything else is negotiable.
replies.original_text: Hours are a soft constraint. The real constraint is the summative assessment deadlines. Everything else is negotiable.

comments.original_text: One issue: the IB requires a certain number of hours per topic. If you optimize the unit plan too much, you might skip necessary lab time. Need to account for that.

comments.text: This reminds me of the debate about 'conceptual understanding' vs 'procedural fluency'. The article's 'zero-cost abstraction' idea applies well to teaching students to generalize concepts without overhead.

replies.text: But sometimes the abstraction leaks. Students still need to know the concrete steps.
replies.original_text: But sometimes the abstraction leaks. Students still need to know the concrete steps.

comments.text: Applies to PE too, believe it or not. 'Life before main' is the fitness test that determines the starting point. Our unit plans have a warm-up phase that can be customized based on results.

replies.text: PE is the ultimate 'borrow checker'—you can only plan so much before the kids' energy levels change everything.

comments.text: The article's mention of 'mutable cross-curricular links' is key. Our library program is supposed to be integrated, but many teachers treat it as an optional dependency.

comments.text: I'm skeptical of over-engineering unit planners. At the end of the day, good teaching is about runtime behavior, not compile-time correctness. But the article is an interesting thought experiment.

replies.text: Sure, but if your unit plan panics during the summative, you have a problem. Static analysis can prevent that.
replies.original_text: Sure, but if your unit plan panics during the summative, you have a problem. Static analysis can prevent that.

comments.original_text: I'm skeptical of over-engineering unit planners. At the end of the day, good teaching is about runtime behavior, not compile-time correctness. But the article is an interesting thought experiment.

comments.text: Accreditation visits often ask for evidence of unit planning. This approach could provide a nice audit trail, but only if the documentation is human-readable. The IB loves jargon but hates code.

replies.text: We had an accreditation team that actually asked to see the 'source code' of our curriculum. I showed them the Google Drive and they were satisfied.

comments.text: The 'life before main' concept also applies to homeroom/advisory. You need to build relationships before you can teach the unit. It's like initializing global state.

replies.text: Exactly. And if you skip it, you get undefined behavior.

comments.original_text: The 'life before main' concept also applies to homeroom/advisory. You need to build relationships before you can teach the unit. It's like initializing global state.

comments.text: Substitute teachers are like panic handlers. We don't get to see the unit plan design; we just handle the mess when the main function crashes.

comments.text: As an expat, I've taught in schools with vastly different 'execution environments'. The article's assumption of a static schedule doesn't hold in many international schools where timetables change every semester.

comments.text: One thing missing from the article: handling 'unwrap'—i.e., what if a student doesn't meet the prerequisite? You need a fallback with proper error handling.

stories.original_title: I Am Not a Reverse Centaur
stories.title: I Am Not a Reverse Lesson Planner

comments.text: To respond to the ending of this piece, I think open educational resources still matter because AI generates very specific lesson plans for a specific class. Quality units mean best practices can be reliably shared between schools.

replies.text: But how do you tell quality lesson plans from AI-generated ones? How do you even discover quality resources if you leave so many instructional decisions to AI? Once AI trains on your quality resources, how do you stop copies being pasted into other teachers' plans without attribution or directing back to your professional development blog? I think there are so many hard questions for 'Does OER even matter any more?' and many seem demotivating, especially because we don't seem to be close to answers.
replies.original_text: But how do you tell quality libraries from LLM generated ones? How do you even discover up quality libraries if you are leaving so many code decisions to LLMs? Once the LLMs train on your quality libraries how do you stop so many copies just getting pasted into people's code without your attribution and without directing people back to your library (and your very human interests in funding development on it or getting copyleft contributions back to it)? I think there are so many hard questions right now for "Does open source even matter any more?" and many of those questions seem particularly demotivating to me right now, especially because we don't seem to be at risk of getting some, much less better, answers any time soon.

comments.original_text: To respond to the ending of this piece, I think open source still matters because LLMs generate very specific code for a specific situation. Quality libraries mean solutions can be reliably shared between projects.

comments.text: The thing is I totally, 100% get this. The other thing I can't help but see is how excited my non-teacher colleagues are to finally be able to create lesson plans. The sense of accomplishment from subject specialists who can finally get their ideas across the way they want. We almost need... non-canonical curricula? Not so much forks, but like... Maybe curriculum as a cluster? an ecosystem? On-demand app store where lesson plans and activities are shared/upvoted/evolved by the community where the original teachers don't have to get burnt out, and when it inevitably becomes a mess, oh well it teaches something? I really don't know! I hope we can think about answers and not get tribal because this is a huge problem and also a huge opportunity.

replies.text: When they doodle a little activity with their nice new AI tool, it doesn't mean they get to submit it to an IB curriculum bank.
This analogy makes no sense and skews elitist. AI-generated lesson plans are regularly used in international schools now. Indie teachers have been using AI to break into curriculum design for years.
replies.original_text: This analogy makes no sense to me and honestly skews pretty elitist in vibe. iPhone is regularly used in professional videography now. Like, 28 Years Later was shot on iPhone. Indie filmmakers have been using iPhone to break into the industry for years.

replies.text: This is so good, I wonder if op did it on purpose. Orders of magnitude more teachers can now create 'IB quality' lesson plans, precisely due to their nice new AI tools. The only question is how do we make sure students see the good ones?
replies.original_text: This is so good, I wonder if op did it on purpose. Orders of magnitude more people can now make an absolutely "Hollywood quality" movie, precisely due to their nice modern iPhone cameras. The only question now is, how do we make it so more people can see the good ones?

replies.text: Teaching isn't just content delivery. You can create a great lesson plan without pedagogical master, but you can't be a master teacher without instructional design skills. You can be a popular teacher without either of these. There is a lot to untangle, but it depends on your goals. You can judge others based on your own values, but their goals might not be yours.
replies.original_text: Art isn't craftsmanship. You can make art with a literally piece of shit, or a toilet if you want to be more traditional, at least in 1917. You can't be a craftsperson without mastery of your domain and its tool. You can be a artist without craftsmanship and vice versa. You can also be popular without any or both of these. There is a lot to entangle there but the point is that it depends on your goal. You can judge others based on your own value system but there goals might not be yours.

replies.text: I think a better analogy would be commissioning an assessment rubric from a specialist. Yes, you provided the learning outcomes and specified the criteria, and maybe suggested some changes. And you can be proud of owning that specific, tailored rubric. No, you didn't create a thing.
replies.original_text: I think a better analogy would be commissioning an artist to create a painting. Yes you provided instructions and decided which style you preferred, and maybe pointed some corrections you wanted. And you can be proud of owning that specific, unique painting. No you didn't create anything.
When they shoot a little artistic clip with their nice modern iPhone camera, it does not mean they get to insert it into a Hollywood movie.

replies.text: You mean some modern version of Madeline Hunter or backwards design? That is the entire point of templated lesson planning.
replies.original_text: > We almost need like ... noncanonical software? You mean some modern version of vb or php? That is the entire point of low-code and no-code.

replies.text: An ecosystem on shared curriculum formats can exist happily. There are a million ways to format a unit plan and generally they all coexist happily.
replies.original_text: An ecosystem on shared formats can exist hapily There's a billion ways of opening a markdown and doing things with it and generally they all coexist hapily

replies.text: I agree. For many teachers, AI tools are the first time that technology does what they want it to. Not what some edtech PM has decided is or isn't possible. At the same time, OP is right to reject contributions they don't want. Nobody providing free OERs is under any obligation to accept changes. Forking (remixing) is still viable. And we don't need an on-demand app store because trust issues will still exist. We can have carefully designed resources coexisting with AI-generated ones.

The problem is they still don't realize these are...

Diverse group of contributors I sympathize with Miguels point but it bothers me it clashes with point 3 in my list. If you hand select your contributors[1] you will never reach the diversity necessary to effectively make relicensing impossible. Without that Open Source matters less to me. comments.text: There is an implicit social contract with writing that the writer has put more effort into writing than the reader will need to read something. I think the same applies to lesson plans. Pre-AI, it was usually obvious when a lesson plan was either completely terrible or very half-baked, and the required effort to create even a bad one was usually more than that required to reject it. AI makes it trivial to make a completely terrible lesson plan, and much easier to make a not-immediately-obviously-bad one.

replies.text: Given this, writers should be putting in at least as much effort as readers, whether or not they use AI. What really seems to be the problem is writers that don't check their own work, and pass that burden onto the readers. This is easier than ever with AI. I'm worried the AI push incentivizes this too much.

I have had a few AI curriculum sessions where I reviewed the output and thought 'I don't think my team can safely use this.' I then broke it down into smaller units, still using AI but at a size that is easy to vet. The problem is this is seen as non-productive, so the pressure grows to just shove massive lesson plans out and reviewers use AI to make it tractable.
replies.original_text: Its exactly this. I have had a few LLM coding sessions where I reviewed the resulting work and thought "I don't think my team can safely PR this." I then went back and broke it down into smaller PRs, still using LLMs but at a size that is easy to review. And I reviewed the output myself before I asked a reviewer to commit their time. The problem is that this is increasingly seen as a non-productive workflow slowing everyone else down, so the pressure is growing for writers to just shove massive PRs out the door and reviewers to use LLMs to make that tractable. I suppose those advocates have more faith in LLM output compared to humans than I do.

replies.text: Isn't this just a continuation of the performance art of the modern school environment? An entire industry producing curriculum documents which aren't read. Then AI happened and the mask is off: no one's reading them still, but also no one is writing them either.
replies.original_text: Isn't this just a continuation of the performance art of the modern corporate environment though? There's an entire industry producing pages of documents which aren't read, aren't responded to, but need to be at least X lines long for anyone to take them "seriously". Then suddenly LLMs happened and it's like the mask is off: no one's reading them still, but also no one is writing them either. Which is perhaps a drop in the ocean of the insanity which is "we need you to work on the Jira tasks" as basically a job title.

replies.text: I had a colleague once reply in an email with an AI-generated lesson plan telling me that some resource I wrote wasn't working. He plopped his discussion with the confirmation bias machine confirming 100% that what he had was sending spurious messages. With all the information in the world at its disposal, the AI did not consider informing the reporter that maybe his material should be used differently. I stopped short from facetiously replying that maybe he should use another model, since his seems broken.
replies.original_text: I've had a guy once reply in an email with a bug report generated by ChatGPT telling me that some piece of software I wrote wasn't working. He just plopped right there the discussion he had with the confirmation bias machine confirming 100% that what he had in front was sending spurious messages. With all the information in the world at its disposal, the LLM did not consider informing the reporter that maybe his code should flush the serial device pipe before starting his processing. I stopped short from facetiously replying to him that maybe he should use another model, since his seems to be broken.

Given this, you can conclude that writers should be putting in at least at much effort as readers, whether or not they use an LLM. What really seems to be the problem is writers that don't at least check their own work, and pass that burden onto the readers. This is easier than ever with LLMs. This is toxic behavior that unfortunately rewards a selfish writer. I'm worried the AI push incentivizes this too much, to where in corporate situations a reader can't say no to doing work for a selfish writer.

replies.original_text: This is a real problem. I suspect that part of it is that people don’t have enough time to mentally incorporate the fix. Is it weird to submit the MR later, after people have had a chance to digest the issues?

replies.text: > Filing reports is the most valuable contribution you had time for, hopefully with a minimal reproducible example. Drive-by lesson plans are usually a net-negative because the coordinator has to reverse engineer the intent from AI-generated activities, then adjust to fit the rest of the unit. There are countless ways to address a gap, and only a few right ways. The coordinator's role is to decide. Don't worry about wasting AI-generated content; AI made that step cheap, but did little for pedagogical taste and sequencing.
replies.original_text: > I ended up just filing a few issues and moved on to other things. This is the most valuable contribution you had time for, hopefully with a minimum-viable bug reproduction. Drive-by patches/PRs are usually a net-negative because the maintainer then has to reverse engineer the intent from GenAI code, and then make changes to have it fit in with the rest of project. > It felt weird to just file issues when my LLM had already spent a lot of time root-causing and fixing the issues There are countless ways to fix an issue, and only a few right ways (subjectively). The maintainers role is to decide which ways are right for their project. You shouldn't worry too much about "wasting" code you already generated, GenAI made that step very cheap, but did little for taste and roadmapping.

comments.original_text: I recently built a very large test bench for System Verilog. I ran a bunch of different compilers on it, including some open source ones. Some of them failed some tests, and it was natural to have my LLM (Claude Fable 5) root-cause the issues, and to double-check my test bench wasn't to blame. But now I stood with all these patches that I couldn't just throw at the upstream maintainers all at once. I ended up just filing a few issues and moved on to other things. It felt weird to just file issues when my LLM had already spent a lot of time root-causing and fixing the issues. But then, maybe they could just have their LLMs do the same. Still not sure if it was the right call?

comments.text: Solution: build a chatbot (or use the cheapest AI) to auto-generate rejections for submitted lesson plans. Let those agents waste their creators' time in a loop of neverending revisions.
comments.original_text: Solution: write a markov generator (or use the cheapest AI possible) to generate plausible-looking rejections for PRs. Let those agents bankrupt their owners in a loop of neverending improvements and changes.

comments.text: Those mad about auto-rejected lesson ideas should go teach their own course.
comments.original_text: Those mad about auto-rejected drive-by PRs should go fork themselves a copy of the repo.

comments.text: I understand maintainers' point of view. Review fatigue of low-quality slop is real. The worst are autonomous AI agents adding random lesson plans. But sometimes I find a real curriculum gap that needs fixing. For example, a unit missing a summative assessment or a 12-minute delay in a lesson. The problem is, as a teacher in a different discipline, I lack knowledge of the full curriculum design to fix it properly. All I want is to help others avoid the same issue. I maintain my own custom materials. But...

replies.text: Then say all of that in the proposal. Say you have a real problem. Say you tried using AI. Add the human element by communicating. I don't think there is a real problem. People just want to know you put in the effort, and that you didn't just prompt an AI and hand over unchecked slop.
Yes I wrote the proposal by hand and try to communicate well.
replies.original_text: Yes I wrote the issue by hand and try to communicate well.

Then say all of that in the issue. Say you have a real problem. Say you tried using AI. Add the human element by communicating. I dont think there is a real problem there. People just want to know you put in the effort, and that you didnt just prompt an AI and hand over completely unchecked slop.

comments.original_text: I totally understand the point of view from maintainers. Review fatigue of low quality slop is a legitimate issue. The worst ones are fully autonomous AI agents looking for open source projects and adding random pull requests. But in some cases, I find a legit bug that needs fixing. For example, I want to get a particular program working in Wine/FEX on aarch64 [1], or I find a 12 second hang in Darktable [2]. The problem is that, as a software engineer working in a totally different discipline, I have no knowledge of the low level C code to fully understand what the problem even is, or how to fix it. All I want to do is to fix the issue and help other people avoid running into the same issue. Right now, on my machine, I maintain a set of custom patches to get everything working. But I...

comments.text: > Do shared teaching resources matter anymore? I mean, did they ever? It depends. Very few shared resource projects are successful in any meaningful sense.

replies.text: I feel like resource collections can be successful without being widely used.
To me, the 'open resource' model implies community development and people sharing. Otherwise what does it matter if it's shared or kept private?
replies.original_text: To me the "open source" model implies something about community development and people sharing or otherwise using it. Otherwise what does it matter if it's open or closed source?
I feel like projects can be successful without being popular.

replies.text: Considering that whole education systems, including big publishers, are built on shared teacher resources now, I would say they do matter. That doesn't mean every resource matters, but thousands have value. The fact that value is disconnected from the creators has been a problem for a long time, and this is one example of a coordinator near the breaking point.
replies.original_text: Considering that the whole industry, including the big AI companies, is built on top of open source now, I would have to say it did matter. That doesn't mean every project mattered (in a larger sense), but certainly thousands of open source projects have value, and dozens have contributed enormous value. The fact that the value has been disconnected from the maintainers has been a problem for a long time, but it is getting worse, and this is one example of a maintainer near the breaking point.

comments.original_text: > Does open source matter anymore? I mean, did it ever? It depends what you mean. Very few open source or free software projects are successful in any meaningful sense.

comments.text: On the other hand, there's nothing more frustrating when I submit a well-thought-out lesson idea to a shared curriculum, happy to implement it if approved, and then a bot closes it with an AI slop comment that indicates it has no idea what I'm talking about. On the plus side, it's easier than ever to develop my own resources. So at least this toxic gatekeeping matters less than it used to.
comments.original_text: On the other hand, there’s nothing more frustrating when I submit a hand-written issue to an open source project, happy to implement it if they approve, and then their bot closes it with some AI slop comment that indicates it has no idea what I’m talking about. On the plus side it’s easier than ever to patch or fork projects. So at least this toxic gatekeeping behavior matters less than it used to.

comments.text: The lesson quality problem is legit and needs a solution. But saying his opinion hasn't changed: 'the main reason why AI tools do not work for me is that they do not make me any faster.' It's been a year and models have improved dramatically. I can see for some things it doesn't make sense, but not using it at all? Teachers who don't use AI at all are a dying breed and I think it won't be long before he's forced to concede.

replies.text: Why is that any of your business?
replies.original_text: Why is that any of your business?

comments.original_text: The PR quality problem is legit and needs a solution. But saying his opinion hasn’t changed on this: “the main and most important reason why GenAI tools do not work for me is that they do not make me any faster.” It’s been a year and agents and models have improved dramatically. I can see for some things it doesn’t make sense, but not using it at all because there’s nothing it can help with? Devs who don’t use models at all are a dying breed and I think it won’t be long before he’ll be forced to concede the point.

comments.text: The problem with reverse lesson planning is that it assumes the end goal is fixed. In IB, we know the assessment criteria can shift mid-unit if the students need a different path. Treating a lesson plan as immutable is just lazy.

replies.text: Exactly. Backward design works for summatives, but day-to-day, you need flexibility. The IAs are a prime example: you can't schedule them like clockwork.
replies.original_text: Exactly. Backward design works for summatives, but day-to-day, you need flexibility. The IAs are a prime example: you can't schedule them like clockwork.

comments.original_text: The problem with reverse lesson planning is that it assumes the end goal is fixed. In IB, we know the assessment criteria can shift mid-unit if the students need a different path. Treating a lesson plan as immutable is just lazy.

comments.text: I've seen schools where the admin forces all teachers to use a reverse lesson planner template. It's like they discovered Understanding by Design and now it's the only hammer. Meanwhile, we lose all creativity.

replies.text: Wait, your admin actually uses UbD? Ours just copies past Google's lesson plan format and calls it 'research-based'.
replies.original_text: Wait, your admin actually uses UbD? Ours just copies past Google's lesson plan format and calls it 'research-based'.

comments.original_text: I've seen schools where the admin forces all teachers to use a reverse lesson planner template. It's like they discovered Understanding by Design and now it's the only hammer. Meanwhile, we lose all creativity.

comments.text: In my international school, reverse planning is a lifesaver for EAL classes. I start with what I want them to be able to do, then scaffold back. Without that, I'd be all over the place.

replies.text: Yes! And the assessments need to be linguistically accessible. Reverse planning forces you to think about the language demands early.
replies.original_text: Yes! And the assessments need to be linguistically accessible. Reverse planning forces you to think about the language demands early.

comments.original_text: In my international school, reverse planning is a lifesaver for EAL classes. I start with what I want them to be able to do, then scaffold back. Without that, I'd be all over the place.

comments.text: I refuse to use any AI for lesson planning. The reverse centaur analogy is apt: the AI should be the body, not the brain. But in teacher Facebook groups, everyone uses ChatGPT to write their unit plans. It's depressing.

replies.text: I use AI for brainstorming, but never for final plans. Still, the amount of slop I see from colleagues is staggering. They don't even check the dates.
replies.original_text: I use AI for brainstorming, but never for final plans. Still, the amount of slop I see from colleagues is staggering. They don't even check the dates.

comments.text: We've implemented a strict reverse planning mandate for all MYP units. The teachers hate it, but our last IB evaluation praised our alignment. It's a tool, not a tyranny.

replies.text: It only works if you give teachers time to adjust. We can't plan backward if we're drowning in marking and meetings.
replies.original_text: It only works if you give teachers time to adjust. We can't plan backward if we're drowning in marking and meetings.

comments.original_text: We've implemented a strict reverse planning mandate for all MYP units. The teachers hate it, but our last IB evaluation praised our alignment. It's a tool, not a tyranny.

comments.text: The biggest issue with reverse planning is that it ignores the iterative nature of teaching. Sometimes a lesson goes brilliantly, and you want to scrap your whole unit plan. Reverse planning feels like a straightjacket.

replies.text: If you haven't thrown out your unit plan mid-unit, are you even teaching? Flexibility is key.
replies.original_text: If you haven't thrown out your unit plan mid-unit, are you even teaching? Flexibility is key.

comments.original_text: The biggest issue with reverse planning is that it ignores the iterative nature of teaching. Sometimes a lesson goes brilliantly, and you want to scrap your whole unit plan. Reverse planning feels like a straightjacket.

comments.text: I'm a reverse lesson planner and proud. It's the only way I can ensure my DP physics classes hit every syllabus point. Without it, I'd miss something and then the students would suffer on exams.

replies.text: For DP, perhaps. But for MYP, the flexibility of forward planning leads to deeper inquiry. Different tools for different contexts.
replies.original_text: For DP, perhaps. But for MYP, the flexibility of forward planning leads to deeper inquiry. Different tools for different contexts.

comments.original_text: I'm a reverse lesson planner and proud. It's the only way I can ensure my DP physics classes hit every syllabus point. Without it, I'd miss something and then the students would suffer on exams.

comments.text: The whole reverse vs forward debate ignores the real issue: lack of collaboration. If we planned together as departments, we'd cover more ground and have less individual stress. But everyone is siloed.

replies.text: Agreed. During our last accreditation, the team praised our collaborative planning. That's what creates strong curriculum alignment, not a template.
replies.original_text: Agreed. During our last accreditation, the team praised our collaborative planning. That's what creates strong curriculum alignment, not a template.

comments.text: I've seen schools with 'reverse planning coaches' who do nothing but enforce the backward design dogma. They ignore student voice and agency. It's a management control tool.

replies.text: Ever tried to sub for a teacher who uses reverse planning? The substitute plans make no sense because you're supposed to know where you're going. Impossible.

comments.text: In multilingual classrooms, reverse planning helps me identify language objectives early. But if the plan is too rigid, I can't adjust to emergent language needs. Balance is everything.

replies.text: And don't forget the social-emotional aspects. If a student is struggling, no amount of reverse planning fixes that. You have to be responsive.

comments.text: For DP, the external assessments are fixed. Reverse planning is almost mandatory. But I see too many teachers copying the syllabus into a unit planner and calling it done. No real thought.

replies.text: Copilot should be used for inspiration, not substitution. But teachers are time-poor, so they cheat.

comments.text: I tried reverse planning for my biology units. The constant backtracking to check alignment with the IB guide drove me crazy. Now I use a hybrid: a rough backward framework with forward detail.

replies.text: Hybrid is the only sensible approach. Pure reverse leaves no room for spontaneous math discoveries. My best lessons are unplanned.

comments.text: Reverse planning in PE? The 'end goal' is often a performance or skill. But you have to adapt to the class's physical abilities daily. Templates are useless.

replies.text: Same in ELA. A novel study can't be rigidly reverse-planned because student discussions lead to unexpected insights.

comments.text: Pretty much the same point I made: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48403908 Their apparent inability to get the basics right makes me severely doubt their claims of self-improving curricula. The humans at the IB wouldn't know improvement if it landed on their lap and started twerking, and no curriculum can adjust without strong teacher intervention into what the goals and guardrails actually are. I'm kind of reminded of when the College Board claimed it took a team of PhDs to design a rubric that updated at 60fps, and then a veteran teacher did it over a weekend. And this was before AI was writing lesson plans in earnest; when LLM-induced assessment rot really sets in, education is in for a world of fresh hurt: lots more generated coursework, almost all of it garbage. And the promised student improvement crossover point...

replies.text: Agreed. Watch for a rise in cases of early onset burnout over the next few decades.

replies.text: Reading this it occurs to me that this timeline may be moving toward a future where grade-inflated homework, garbage assessments etc. have become the norm for so long that the number of people who can distinguish authentic learning from busy work, or insight from noise, has become negligible. A true era of ignorance, looking like an ocean of portfolio bloat in which no one can really navigate as it is ungrounded in real understanding. Idiocracy presents a naively gentle positive version of such future but there are many darker ones possible. Kali Yuga, indeed. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kali_Yuga

comments.original_text: Pretty much the same point I made: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48403908 Their apparent inability to get the basics right makes me severely doubt their claims of self-improving AI. The humans at Anthropic wouldn't know improvement if it landed on their lap and started twerking, and AI cannot do a job without strong human intervention into what the goals and guardrails actually are. I'm kind of reminded of when Microsoft claimed it took a team of Ph.D.s to write a terminal application that updated at 60fps, and then Casey Muratori did it over a weekend. And this was before AI was writing code in earnest; when LLM-induced brainrot really sets in, civilization is in for a world of fresh hurt: lots more generated code, almost all of it garbage. And the promised AI crossover point...

comments.text: It goes beyond lying. It's kind of war, and IB is the aggressor. Everyone else needs to start treating them that way, or you're going to regret it once you realize what's actually happening to teacher autonomy.

comments.text: The IB's claim that their curriculum is 'rigorous' and 'internationally recognized' is a tautology. They define rigor as their own standards, then point to university acceptance as proof. My school's DP graduates went to good universities, but that's correlation, not causation.

replies.text: Exactly. It's a self-licking ice cream cone. Universities accept IB kids because they're from affluent schools, not because the DP is special.
