comments.original_text: The IB's new e-assessment platform for MYP is an improvement over the old system, but it's still buggy and expensive. We pay for the convenience.

comments.text: IB's claim of being 'research-based' is undermined by their resistance to evidence that doesn't fit their narrative. I've seen them dismiss studies showing no advantage.

replies.text: IB has a research department, but they produce studies that confirm their biases. Independent research is scarce.
replies.original_text: IB has a research department, but they produce studies that confirm their biases. Independent research is scarce.

comments.original_text: IB's claim of being 'research-based' is undermined by their resistance to evidence that doesn't fit their narrative. I've seen them dismiss studies showing no advantage.

comments.text: I've been involved in IB curriculum development as a subject matter expert. The process is bureaucratic and slow. By the time a new guide is released, it's already outdated.

replies.text: The review cycle is 7 years. In technology fields, that's an eternity.
replies.original_text: The review cycle is 7 years. In technology fields, that's an eternity.

comments.original_text: I've been involved in IB curriculum development as a subject matter expert. The process is bureaucratic and slow. By the time a new guide is released, it's already outdated.

comments.text: IB's treatment of part-time teachers is poor. We don't get training or resources, yet we're expected to uphold the brand. They lie about support for all teachers.

replies.text: As a substitute, I feel that. I get zero training on IB procedures.
replies.original_text: As a substitute, I feel that. I get zero training on IB procedures.

comments.original_text: IB's treatment of part-time teachers is poor. We don't get training or resources, yet we're expected to uphold the brand. They lie about support for all teachers.

comments.text: The IB should integrate language support into the mainstream curriculum, not just offer EAL as a separate course. They promote inclusion but don't practice it.

replies.text: It's hard to change the entire curriculum. But they could provide more guidance on differentiation.
replies.original_text: It's hard to change the entire curriculum. But they could provide more guidance on differentiation.

comments.original_text: The IB should integrate language support into the mainstream curriculum, not just offer EAL as a separate course. They promote inclusion but don't practice it.

stories.original_title: Hazel (YC W24) Is Hiring a Full Stack Engineer
stories.title: Hazel (YC W24) Is Hiring a Full Stack Engineer

comments.text: I've been using Hazel's platform for my IB Chemistry class. The gradebook integration with ManageBac is clunky but the AI rubric scoring saves hours. Problem is they keep breaking the API every time they update. Hope the new hire can stabilize the microservices.

replies.text: Totally agree. We had to roll back two versions last month because the SQL migrations borked the student attendance sync. They need someone who understands both TS and edtech compliance.
replies.original_text: Totally agree. We had to roll back two versions last month because the SQL migrations borked the student attendance sync. They need someone who understands both TS and edtech compliance.

comments.original_text: I've been using Hazel's platform for my IB Chemistry class. The gradebook integration with ManageBac is clunky but the AI rubric scoring saves hours. Problem is they keep breaking the API every time they update. Hope the new hire can stabilize the microservices.

comments.text: Full stack engineer for an edtech startup = you'll be building everything from student dashboards to payment gateways for school contracts. Been there, done that. The real challenge is handling the data silos between SIS, LMS, and our own platform. Hazel's founders are solid but they need more ops maturity.
comments.original_text: Full stack engineer for an edtech startup = you'll be building everything from student dashboards to payment gateways for school contracts. Been there, done that. The real challenge is handling the data silos between SIS, LMS, and our own platform. Hazel's founders are solid but they need more ops maturity.

comments.text: I wish they would hire a curriculum designer instead of another engineer. The content authoring tool is buggy and doesn't support MYP interdisciplinary units properly. But I guess a full stack engineer can also fix the WYSIWYG editor.

replies.text: The editor is just a wrapper over Quill.js. A decent full stack dev could swap it out for a custom slate-based solution that actually handles IB standards. But management probably won't prioritize that.
replies.original_text: The editor is just a wrapper over Quill.js. A decent full stack dev could swap it out for a custom slate-based solution that actually handles IB standards. But management probably won't prioritize that.

comments.original_text: I wish they would hire a curriculum designer instead of another engineer. The content authoring tool is buggy and doesn't support MYP interdisciplinary units properly. But I guess a full stack engineer can also fix the WYSIWYG editor.

comments.text: I applied for this role. Got rejected because they wanted 5 years experience with React and Node, but my background is Ruby on Rails. Classic YC tunnel vision. Meanwhile my school runs on three different Rails apps that work fine.

replies.text: They probably want someone who can also handle the mobile app. Rails is great for rapid prototyping but the Hazel mobile experience is terrible. I'm stuck using the web version on my iPad.
replies.original_text: They probably want someone who can also handle the mobile app. Rails is great for rapid prototyping but the Hazel mobile experience is terrible. I'm stuck using the web version on my iPad.

comments.original_text: I applied for this role. Got rejected because they wanted 5 years experience with React and Node, but my background is Ruby on Rails. Classic YC tunnel vision. Meanwhile my school runs on three different Rails apps that work fine.

comments.text: Hazel's promise of 'seamless accreditation workflows' is laughable. We spent weeks integrating with their platform for our CIS visit and the document collection module was missing half the required artifacts. Hopefully the new engineer will fix their parser for the IB self-study report format.
comments.original_text: Hazel's promise of 'seamless accreditation workflows' is laughable. We spent weeks integrating with their platform for our CIS visit and the document collection module was missing half the required artifacts. Hopefully the new engineer will fix their parser for the IB self-study report format.

comments.text: Another edtech startup burning VC money on a fancy frontend while the backend is held together with duct tape. I'd rather they spend on infrastructure than hire another full stack engineer who leaves after 18 months. Our school's data privacy is at stake.

replies.text: Agreed. We had a data breach last year because Hazel's AWS keys were exposed in a public repo. The new hire better have security chops.
replies.original_text: Agreed. We had a data breach last year because Hazel's AWS keys were exposed in a public repo. The new hire better have security chops.

comments.original_text: Another edtech startup burning VC money on a fancy frontend while the backend is held together with duct tape. I'd rather they spend on infrastructure than hire another full stack engineer who leaves after 18 months. Our school's data privacy is at stake.

comments.text: I build similar tools for my school district. The hardest part isn't the tech, it's getting teachers to adopt it. Hazel's UX is decent but the onboarding still sucks. A full stack engineer could improve the guided setup for new IB coordinators.
comments.original_text: I build similar tools for my school district. The hardest part isn't the tech, it's getting teachers to adopt it. Hazel's UX is decent but the onboarding still sucks. A full stack engineer could improve the guided setup for new IB coordinators.

comments.text: If I were the new engineer, I'd focus on the assessment analytics. Our administrators love dashboards but the current Hazel reports are too high-level. They need to drill down into criterion-based grading and predicted scores.

replies.text: Yes! And the grade scale conversion between IB 1-7 and the US letter grades is still manual. That should be a simple algorithm but they haven't implemented it.
replies.original_text: Yes! And the grade scale conversion between IB 1-7 and the US letter grades is still manual. That should be a simple algorithm but they haven't implemented it.

comments.original_text: If I were the new engineer, I'd focus on the assessment analytics. Our administrators love dashboards but the current Hazel reports are too high-level. They need to drill down into criterion-based grading and predicted scores.

comments.text: I moved from a school in Bangkok to one in Berlin and Hazel's platform was the only constant. Their export/import for teacher data is surprisingly robust. But the SSO integration with Google Workspace broke twice this semester. Hope the new engineer prioritizes OAuth stability.
comments.original_text: I moved from a school in Bangkok to one in Berlin and Hazel's platform was the only constant. Their export/import for teacher data is surprisingly robust. But the SSO integration with Google Workspace broke twice this semester. Hope the new engineer prioritizes OAuth stability.

comments.text: The Hazel language support is a joke. English dominates every interface, and the Spanish localization is incomplete. If they want to truly serve international schools, they need i18n for at least Chinese, Arabic, and French. A full stack engineer could set up a proper i18n framework.

replies.text: We tried to contribute translations via a community PR but the repo was closed. Hazel needs to open source their frontend components if they want global adoption.
replies.original_text: We tried to contribute translations via a community PR but the repo was closed. Hazel needs to open source their frontend components if they want global adoption.

comments.original_text: The Hazel language support is a joke. English dominates every interface, and the Spanish localization is incomplete. If they want to truly serve international schools, they need i18n for at least Chinese, Arabic, and French. A full stack engineer could set up a proper i18n framework.

comments.text: I use Hazel for university counseling letters and transcripts. The integration with Common App and UCAS is flaky. A full stack engineer could build a reliable bridge. The current solution feels like a hackathon project.
comments.original_text: I use Hazel for university counseling letters and transcripts. The integration with Common App and UCAS is flaky. A full stack engineer could build a reliable bridge. The current solution feels like a hackathon project.

comments.text: We're considering switching to Hazel from another platform. The main hesitation is their single sign-on doesn't support our Active Directory. If the new engineer can implement SAML 2.0 properly, we'll sign the contract.

replies.text: SAML is a pain but there are libraries for that. The bigger issue is Hazel's pricing model per student. A full stack engineer could also build a better usage tracking system to negotiate licences.
replies.original_text: SAML is a pain but there are libraries for that. The bigger issue is Hazel's pricing model per student. A full stack engineer could also build a better usage tracking system to negotiate licences.

comments.original_text: We're considering switching to Hazel from another platform. The main hesitation is their single sign-on doesn't support our Active Directory. If the new engineer can implement SAML 2.0 properly, we'll sign the contract.

comments.text: Full stack at Hazel means full stack of the edtech stack. You'll touch everything from the course scheduling algorithm to the parent portal. I interviewed there once. They asked me to whiteboard a feature for IB internal assessments. Interesting challenge.
comments.original_text: Full stack at Hazel means full stack of the edtech stack. You'll touch everything from the course scheduling algorithm to the parent portal. I interviewed there once. They asked me to whiteboard a feature for IB internal assessments. Interesting challenge.

comments.text: The Hazel platform needs a real curriculum mapping module. Currently it's just a glorified spreadsheet. A full stack engineer could build a graph database to link standards, assessments, and resources. That's what would make it indispensable for IB schools.

replies.text: Yes! For IB sciences we need to map lab practicals to specific assessment criteria. Hazel's current setup forces us to duplicate entries. A proper map would save hours per unit.
replies.original_text: Yes! For IB sciences we need to map lab practicals to specific assessment criteria. Hazel's current setup forces us to duplicate entries. A proper map would save hours per unit.

comments.original_text: The Hazel platform needs a real curriculum mapping module. Currently it's just a glorified spreadsheet. A full stack engineer could build a graph database to link standards, assessments, and resources. That's what would make it indispensable for IB schools.

comments.text: I hear they use PostgreSQL on RDS with a Node.js backend and React frontend. Standard. The interesting part is their real-time collaboration features for co-teaching. That must be WebSockets with OT or CRDT. If the new engineer can improve that, they'll reduce teacher frustration.
comments.original_text: I hear they use PostgreSQL on RDS with a Node.js backend and React frontend. Standard. The interesting part is their real-time collaboration features for co-teaching. That must be WebSockets with OT or CRDT. If the new engineer can improve that, they'll reduce teacher frustration.

comments.text: I wish Hazel would hire someone to fix the comment bank for writing feedback. It's too generic. A full stack engineer could integrate an AI suggestion engine trained on IB exemplars. That would actually make a difference.

replies.text: They're probably wary of AI because of academic integrity issues. But a simple searchable comment bank with tagging would already be an improvement over the current free text.
replies.original_text: They're probably wary of AI because of academic integrity issues. But a simple searchable comment bank with tagging would already be an improvement over the current free text.

comments.original_text: I wish Hazel would hire someone to fix the comment bank for writing feedback. It's too generic. A full stack engineer could integrate an AI suggestion engine trained on IB exemplars. That would actually make a difference.

comments.text: Hazel's attendance tracking is ant. We have a hybrid schedule with block days and rotating periods. The current system can't handle custom calendars. A full stack engineer could design a flexible schedule module that satisfies all IB schools.
comments.original_text: Hazel's attendance tracking is ant. We have a hybrid schedule with block days and rotating periods. The current system can't handle custom calendars. A full stack engineer could design a flexible schedule module that satisfies all IB schools.

comments.text: They need to add a lab inventory module. I'm tired of using separate apps for stock management. If Hazel could track chemicals and equipment alongside class bookings, that would be killer. Full stack engineers often have to build those features from scratch.

replies.text: Seconded. And while they're at it, a simple booking system for science labs with conflict detection. Currently we use Google Calendar but it's not integrated with student groups.
replies.original_text: Seconded. And while they're at it, a simple booking system for science labs with conflict detection. Currently we use Google Calendar but it's not integrated with student groups.

comments.original_text: They need to add a lab inventory module. I'm tired of using separate apps for stock management. If Hazel could track chemicals and equipment alongside class bookings, that would be killer. Full stack engineers often have to build those features from scratch.

comments.text: The Hazel grading system doesn't support partial credit on IB math exams. Every teacher has to manually override grades. That's a simple fix in the backend - allow decimal points in the score field. Come on.
comments.original_text: The Hazel grading system doesn't support partial credit on IB math exams. Every teacher has to manually override grades. That's a simple fix in the backend - allow decimal points in the score field. Come on.

comments.text: We use Hazel for PE assessment too, but the fitness testing module is basically text fields. If the new engineer could build a graph to show student progress over time, it would help with IB CAS reflections.

replies.text: They could add integration with wearables like Fitbit for activity tracking. That would be a differentiating feature for health-focused schools.
replies.original_text: They could add integration with wearables like Fitbit for activity tracking. That would be a differentiating feature for health-focused schools.

comments.original_text: We use Hazel for PE assessment too, but the fitness testing module is basically text fields. If the new engineer could build a graph to show student progress over time, it would help with IB CAS reflections.

comments.text: The Hazel digital resource integration is weak. Our library catalog doesn't sync with their reading lists. I end up copying links manually. A full stack engineer could build a Zapier-like connector or an API for library systems like Destiny.
comments.original_text: The Hazel digital resource integration is weak. Our library catalog doesn't sync with their reading lists. I end up copying links manually. A full stack engineer could build a Zapier-like connector or an API for library systems like Destiny.

comments.text: Hazel's academic year setup is rigid. We run a year-round program with three semesters. The current system forces us to choose from predefined templates. A flexible setup would require serious backend work - maybe the new hire can refactor that.

replies.text: The templates are hard-coded for typical North American calendars. For international schools with multiple intakes, it's a nightmare. Hope they hire someone who can design a dynamic calendar engine.
replies.original_text: The templates are hard-coded for typical North American calendars. For international schools with multiple intakes, it's a nightmare. Hope they hire someone who can design a dynamic calendar engine.

comments.original_text: Hazel's academic year setup is rigid. We run a year-round program with three semesters. The current system forces us to choose from predefined templates. A flexible setup would require serious backend work - maybe the new hire can refactor that.

comments.text: From an accreditation perspective, Hazel misses the mark on data portability. We need to export all student records in a standardized format for CIS and WASC. Their current CSV export has weird encoding issues. A full stack engineer should know how to produce clean CSVs.
comments.original_text: From an accreditation perspective, Hazel misses the mark on data portability. We need to export all student records in a standardized format for CIS and WASC. Their current CSV export has weird encoding issues. A full stack engineer should know how to produce clean CSVs.

comments.text: I'm the Hazel power user at my school. The biggest pain point is the mobile app - it's just a wrapper of the web view. A full stack engineer with React Native experience could build a proper app with offline mode for when we have no internet in the field.

replies.text: Offline mode is crucial. Many of our students in rural areas of Southeast Asia have intermittent connectivity. Hazel's current 'offline' feature is basically a cache that breaks after 30 minutes.
replies.original_text: Offline mode is crucial. Many of our students in rural areas of Southeast Asia have intermittent connectivity. Hazel's current 'offline' feature is basically a cache that breaks after 30 minutes.

comments.original_text: I'm the Hazel power user at my school. The biggest pain point is the mobile app - it's just a wrapper of the web view. A full stack engineer with React Native experience could build a proper app with offline mode for when we have no internet in the field.

comments.text: When I sub, I have to use Hazel to take attendance and view lesson plans. The interface is slow on old school Chromebooks. A full stack engineer could optimize the bundle size and reduce API calls for low-end devices.
comments.original_text: When I sub, I have to use Hazel to take attendance and view lesson plans. The interface is slow on old school Chromebooks. A full stack engineer could optimize the bundle size and reduce API calls for low-end devices.

comments.text: Hazel's third-party integration strategy is messy. They have a bunch of 'partners' but most are shallow connections. I'd rather they focus on building robust APIs so schools can DIY integrations. Full stack engineers usually understand API design.

replies.text: The public API documentation is sparse. I had to reverse engineer their endpoints to build a custom dashboard for our admin. A dedicated engineer could write proper docs and version the API.
replies.original_text: The public API documentation is sparse. I had to reverse engineer their endpoints to build a custom dashboard for our admin. A dedicated engineer could write proper docs and version the API.

comments.original_text: Hazel's third-party integration strategy is messy. They have a bunch of 'partners' but most are shallow connections. I'd rather they focus on building robust APIs so schools can DIY integrations. Full stack engineers usually understand API design.

comments.text: I'm skeptical of the Hazel team's understanding of pedagogy. They build features that seem cool to engineers but don't match classroom reality. The new full stack engineer might be their first hire with actual teacher input? Probably not.
comments.original_text: I'm skeptical of the Hazel team's understanding of pedagogy. They build features that seem cool to engineers but don't match classroom reality. The new full stack engineer might be their first hire with actual teacher input? Probably not.

comments.text: The job ad says 'preference for experience in education.' That's good, but they should require it. I've seen too many edtech products designed by engineers who never set foot in a classroom. The best features come from teachers who code.

replies.text: I agree. But good luck finding a teacher who also has full stack skills and is willing to take a pay cut. Most of us are better off building our own tools.
replies.original_text: I agree. But good luck finding a teacher who also has full stack skills and is willing to take a pay cut. Most of us are better off building our own tools.

comments.original_text: The job ad says 'preference for experience in education.' That's good, but they should require it. I've seen too many edtech products designed by engineers who never set foot in a classroom. The best features come from teachers who code.

comments.text: Hazel's IB subject database is outdated. They still list Biology SL as a single course instead of the new approach with core and HL options. A full stack engineer could update the curriculum database programmatically rather than waiting for manual updates.
comments.original_text: Hazel's IB subject database is outdated. They still list Biology SL as a single course instead of the new approach with core and HL options. A full stack engineer could update the curriculum database programmatically rather than waiting for manual updates.

comments.text: I heard Hazel is planning to add a marketplace for teachers to sell lesson plans. That would be controversial in the IB world where sharing is norm. A full stack engineer would probably build the payment infrastructure – I hope they consider the ethical implications.

replies.text: Marketplace? That will just create inequalities between well-resourced schools and others. I'd rather they focus on free shared resources with a Creative Commons license.
replies.original_text: Marketplace? That will just create inequalities between well-resourced schools and others. I'd rather they focus on free shared resources with a Creative Commons license.

comments.original_text: I heard Hazel is planning to add a marketplace for teachers to sell lesson plans. That would be controversial in the IB world where sharing is norm. A full stack engineer would probably build the payment infrastructure – I hope they consider the ethical implications.

comments.text: The Hazel language proficiency tracking for EAL students is too simplistic. Just a dropdown with 'beginner/intermediate/advanced.' A full stack engineer could implement a proper framework like WIDA or CEFR with detailed descriptors.
comments.original_text: The Hazel language proficiency tracking for EAL students is too simplistic. Just a dropdown with 'beginner/intermediate/advanced.' A full stack engineer could implement a proper framework like WIDA or CEFR with detailed descriptors.

comments.text: We need better analytics for university admissions. Hazel can show predicted IB scores but it can't compare them to historical acceptance rates. That would require scraping data and building a ranking algorithm – full stack engineers are perfect for that.

replies.text: Scraping is legally gray. But they could partner with universities to get official data. The new engineer could build the integration layer.
replies.original_text: Scraping is legally gray. But they could partner with universities to get official data. The new engineer could build the integration layer.

comments.original_text: We need better analytics for university admissions. Hazel can show predicted IB scores but it can't compare them to historical acceptance rates. That would require scraping data and building a ranking algorithm – full stack engineers are perfect for that.

comments.text: Hazel's user roles and permissions are too basic. We have multiple campuses with different access levels. The current system either gives too much or too little permission. A full stack engineer with experience in RBAC could fix this.
comments.original_text: Hazel's user roles and permissions are too basic. We have multiple campuses with different access levels. The current system either gives too much or too little permission. A full stack engineer with experience in RBAC could fix this.

comments.text: The Hazel stack is typical for a YC startup: Next.js frontend, Prisma ORM, PostgreSQL, deployed on Vercel and AWS. Not bad. The challenge is scaling for schools with thousands of concurrent users during report card season. The new hire will likely handle performance optimization.

replies.text: We had a crash last June when 200 teachers submitted grades simultaneously. The database connection pool was too small. They need an engineer who understands connection pooling and read replicas.
replies.original_text: We had a crash last June when 200 teachers submitted grades simultaneously. The database connection pool was too small. They need an engineer who understands connection pooling and read replicas.

comments.original_text: The Hazel stack is typical for a YC startup: Next.js frontend, Prisma ORM, PostgreSQL, deployed on Vercel and AWS. Not bad. The challenge is scaling for schools with thousands of concurrent users during report card season. The new hire will likely handle performance optimization.

comments.text: I'm working on a side project to sync Hazel with Google Classroom. It's a mess because Hazel's API doesn't support webhooks for real-time updates. A full stack engineer could implement a webhook system easily.
comments.original_text: I'm working on a side project to sync Hazel with Google Classroom. It's a mess because Hazel's API doesn't support webhooks for real-time updates. A full stack engineer could implement a webhook system easily.

comments.text: Hazel's plagiarism detection integration is weak. It only checks against a small database. A full stack engineer could integrate with Turnitin or Unicheck via their APIs. That would be a huge selling point for schools.

replies.text: But Turnitin is expensive and controversial. Maybe they should build their own detection using AI, but that's a big undertaking.
replies.original_text: But Turnitin is expensive and controversial. Maybe they should build their own detection using AI, but that's a big undertaking.

comments.original_text: Hazel's plagiarism detection integration is weak. It only checks against a small database. A full stack engineer could integrate with Turnitin or Unicheck via their APIs. That would be a huge selling point for schools.

comments.text: The Hazel calendar sync with Google Calendar constantly duplicates events. It's a known bug. A full stack engineer could rebuild the sync using the Google Calendar API's delta sync feature.
comments.original_text: The Hazel calendar sync with Google Calendar constantly duplicates events. It's a known bug. A full stack engineer could rebuild the sync using the Google Calendar API's delta sync feature.

comments.text: We use Hazel for lab safety checklists. The current form builder is limited. Could use a drag-and-drop custom form builder that supports branching logic. That would be a nice feature for the new engineer to build.

replies.text: Yes, and the form responses should be analysable with charts. Currently we have to export to Excel for any analysis.
replies.original_text: Yes, and the form responses should be analysable with charts. Currently we have to export to Excel for any analysis.

comments.original_text: We use Hazel for lab safety checklists. The current form builder is limited. Could use a drag-and-drop custom form builder that supports branching logic. That would be a nice feature for the new engineer to build.

comments.text: Hazel doesn't have a proper parent communication tool. We use separate apps like ClassDojo. If they added a messaging feature with translation, that would be awesome. Full stack engineer could implement real-time chat with i18n.
comments.original_text: Hazel doesn't have a proper parent communication tool. We use separate apps like ClassDojo. If they added a messaging feature with translation, that would be awesome. Full stack engineer could implement real-time chat with i18n.

comments.text: The scheduling algorithm for generating student timetables is reportedly based on genetic algorithms. It works okay but it's slow for large schools. A full stack engineer with algorithmic background could optimize it using constraint programming.

replies.text: Optimizing the scheduler would be a huge win. Our school of 1200 students takes 4 hours to generate schedules in Hazel. We used to do it in 30 minutes with a dedicated software.
replies.original_text: Optimizing the scheduler would be a huge win. Our school of 1200 students takes 4 hours to generate schedules in Hazel. We used to do it in 30 minutes with a dedicated software.

comments.original_text: The scheduling algorithm for generating student timetables is reportedly based on genetic algorithms. It works okay but it's slow for large schools. A full stack engineer with algorithmic background could optimize it using constraint programming.

comments.text: Hazel's document storage for accreditation is not secure enough. They use S3 with public-read for some assets. That's a compliance risk. The new engineer should enforce proper IAM roles and encryption.
comments.original_text: Hazel's document storage for accreditation is not secure enough. They use S3 with public-read for some assets. That's a compliance risk. The new engineer should enforce proper IAM roles and encryption.

comments.text: I love that Hazel allows custom fields for report cards. But the custom field builder is buggy and sometimes deletes data. A full stack engineer could implement a robust field system with versioning and rollback.

replies.text: Versioning is crucial. I've lost hours of work due to accidental deletion. They need a proper undo/redo system.
replies.original_text: Versioning is crucial. I've lost hours of work due to accidental deletion. They need a proper undo/redo system.

comments.original_text: I love that Hazel allows custom fields for report cards. But the custom field builder is buggy and sometimes deletes data. A full stack engineer could implement a robust field system with versioning and rollback.

comments.text: The Hazel mobile app drains my battery because it's constantly polling for updates. A full stack engineer could switch to push notifications via Firebase Cloud Messaging. That would be a nice optimization.
comments.original_text: The Hazel mobile app drains my battery because it's constantly polling for updates. A full stack engineer could switch to push notifications via Firebase Cloud Messaging. That would be a nice optimization.

comments.text: I interviewed for a similar role at another edtech before. They asked me to design a system for handling IB Extended Essay submissions with supervisor feedback loops. That's a complex workflow - Hazel would benefit from such a feature.

replies.text: EE submission is indeed messy. Currently we use email and Google Drive. A built-in tool with deadlines, version control, and feedback would be gold.
replies.original_text: EE submission is indeed messy. Currently we use email and Google Drive. A built-in tool with deadlines, version control, and feedback would be gold.

comments.original_text: I interviewed for a similar role at another edtech before. They asked me to design a system for handling IB Extended Essay submissions with supervisor feedback loops. That's a complex workflow - Hazel would benefit from such a feature.

comments.text: Hazel's support for different assessment types is superficial. For IB visual arts, we need to upload high-res images with annotations. The current upload compresses and strips metadata. A full stack engineer could handle large file uploads with thumbnails and EXIF preservation.
comments.original_text: Hazel's support for different assessment types is superficial. For IB visual arts, we need to upload high-res images with annotations. The current upload compresses and strips metadata. A full stack engineer could handle large file uploads with thumbnails and EXIF preservation.

comments.text: I see Hazel is hiring a full stack engineer but what about hiring more people on the ground? Schools need support, not just code. The new engineer might end up debugging school-specific issues instead of building features.

replies.text: That's the reality of edtech. The product is never finished, and each school has quirks. The engineer needs to be comfortable with entropy.
replies.original_text: That's the reality of edtech. The product is never finished, and each school has quirks. The engineer needs to be comfortable with entropy.

comments.original_text: I see Hazel is hiring a full stack engineer but what about hiring more people on the ground? Schools need support, not just code. The new engineer might end up debugging school-specific issues instead of building features.

comments.text: The Hazel codebase is probably a mess after rapid growth. I'd join as a contractor to refactor the frontend state management. They likely use Redux or Zustand but likely with anti-patterns. Full stack engineer should bring discipline.
comments.original_text: The Hazel codebase is probably a mess after rapid growth. I'd join as a contractor to refactor the frontend state management. They likely use Redux or Zustand but likely with anti-patterns. Full stack engineer should bring discipline.

comments.text: If I were hiring for this role, I'd look for someone who has built an edtech product from scratch. Those engineers understand the domain. Hazel seems to be scaling past that stage though.

replies.text: They might be looking for a more senior engineer to lead the next phase. But the job description doesn't mention leadership.
replies.original_text: They might be looking for a more senior engineer to lead the next phase. But the job description doesn't mention leadership.

comments.original_text: If I were hiring for this role, I'd look for someone who has built an edtech product from scratch. Those engineers understand the domain. Hazel seems to be scaling past that stage though.

comments.text: Hazel uses TypeScript for the backend? Interesting choice. Most edtech I've seen is either Python (Django) or Ruby. TypeScript with Node is becoming more common. The new engineer should be strong in async programming and error handling.
comments.original_text: Hazel uses TypeScript for the backend? Interesting choice. Most edtech I've seen is either Python (Django) or Ruby. TypeScript with Node is becoming more common. The new engineer should be strong in async programming and error handling.

comments.text: I wish Hazel had a feature for creating multilingual worksheets within the platform. Currently we have to use external tools and upload PDFs. A full stack engineer could build a simple document editor with multi-language support.

replies.text: That would be amazing. Even a basic rich text editor with side-by-side translation would help our EAL students.
replies.original_text: That would be amazing. Even a basic rich text editor with side-by-side translation would help our EAL students.

comments.original_text: I wish Hazel had a feature for creating multilingual worksheets within the platform. Currently we have to use external tools and upload PDFs. A full stack engineer could build a simple document editor with multi-language support.

comments.text: The Hazel transcript export for universities sometimes formats grades incorrectly. Last year, a student's transcript showed a 6 instead of a 7 because of a rounding error. A full stack engineer could implement precise formatting rules.
comments.original_text: The Hazel transcript export for universities sometimes formats grades incorrectly. Last year, a student's transcript showed a 6 instead of a 7 because of a rounding error. A full stack engineer could implement precise formatting rules.

comments.text: Hazel's pricing model is per student per month. For large schools, that adds up. The new engineer could help build a usage-based billing system that's fairer for schools with many students but low usage.

replies.text: I'd prefer a flat fee per school. The current model discourages us from adding all students to the platform.
replies.original_text: I'd prefer a flat fee per school. The current model discourages us from adding all students to the platform.

comments.original_text: Hazel's pricing model is per student per month. For large schools, that adds up. The new engineer could help build a usage-based billing system that's fairer for schools with many students but low usage.

comments.text: The Hazel team seems active on GitHub. I've seen some open source libraries they maintain for edtech. The new full stack engineer would likely contribute to those. That's a plus for the community.
comments.original_text: The Hazel team seems active on GitHub. I've seen some open source libraries they maintain for edtech. The new full stack engineer would likely contribute to those. That's a plus for the community.

comments.text: One underrated feature: Hazel's ability to generate individual learning plans for students with accommodations. But it's hidden in the settings. Could be more prominent. Full stack engineer could surface this in the UI.

replies.text: Yes! We only discovered it by accident. The UX needs improvement - a dedicated section for accommodations would be nice.
replies.original_text: Yes! We only discovered it by accident. The UX needs improvement - a dedicated section for accommodations would be nice.

comments.original_text: One underrated feature: Hazel's ability to generate individual learning plans for students with accommodations. But it's hidden in the settings. Could be more prominent. Full stack engineer could surface this in the UI.

comments.text: I'd like to see Hazel integrate with digital whiteboards like Miro for collaborative projects. That would require an API integration or embedding. A full stack engineer could prototype it in a week.
comments.original_text: I'd like to see Hazel integrate with digital whiteboards like Miro for collaborative projects. That would require an API integration or embedding. A full stack engineer could prototype it in a week.

comments.text: The Hazel data export for science fair judges is cumbersome. They want a clean summary with graphs. Currently we have to export raw data and format ourselves. A report generator would be welcome.

replies.text: Agreed. The reporting module is the weakest part of Hazel. It's basically a table view. They need a proper report builder with charting.
replies.original_text: Agreed. The reporting module is the weakest part of Hazel. It's basically a table view. They need a proper report builder with charting.

comments.original_text: The Hazel data export for science fair judges is cumbersome. They want a clean summary with graphs. Currently we have to export raw data and format ourselves. A report generator would be welcome.

comments.text: We have after-school sports teams but Hazel doesn't have a module for athletics management. We use separate tools. If the new engineer could build a simple team roster and schedule view, that would be great.
comments.original_text: We have after-school sports teams but Hazel doesn't have a module for athletics management. We use separate tools. If the new engineer could build a simple team roster and schedule view, that would be great.

comments.text: Hazel's reading list integration with online databases like JSTOR or EBSCO is nonexistent. Students can't access articles directly. A full stack engineer could implement a proxy linking system similar to OpenAthens.

replies.text: That's a complex integration but would be a game-changer for research-heavy IB courses.
replies.original_text: That's a complex integration but would be a game-changer for research-heavy IB courses.

comments.original_text: Hazel's reading list integration with online databases like JSTOR or EBSCO is nonexistent. Students can't access articles directly. A full stack engineer could implement a proxy linking system similar to OpenAthens.

comments.text: I've been pushing Hazel to implement a self-study module for accreditation. Right now we use a separate wiki. A full stack engineer could build a collaborative document editor with evidence uploads and status tracking.
comments.original_text: I've been pushing Hazel to implement a self-study module for accreditation. Right now we use a separate wiki. A full stack engineer could build a collaborative document editor with evidence uploads and status tracking.

comments.text: The Hazel notification system is too noisy. I get alerts for every minor grade change. A full stack engineer could implement notification preferences and bundling.

replies.text: Yes, and allow per-subscriber filters. I only want notifications about my own classes.
replies.original_text: Yes, and allow per-subscriber filters. I only want notifications about my own classes.

comments.original_text: The Hazel notification system is too noisy. I get alerts for every minor grade change. A full stack engineer could implement notification preferences and bundling.

comments.text: Hazel's API has rate limits that are too low for our integration with a custom timetabling system. We hit the limit daily. The new engineer could review and adjust limits or implement burst capacity.
comments.original_text: Hazel's API has rate limits that are too low for our integration with a custom timetabling system. We hit the limit daily. The new engineer could review and adjust limits or implement burst capacity.

comments.text: The job posting mentions 'experience with cloud infrastructure.' I hope that includes multi-tenancy. Our school has strict data residency requirements in Europe. The engineer needs to design for GDPR compliance.

replies.text: EU data residency is a big deal. Current Hazel servers are mostly in US. They need to support region selection.
replies.original_text: EU data residency is a big deal. Current Hazel servers are mostly in US. They need to support region selection.

comments.original_text: The job posting mentions 'experience with cloud infrastructure.' I hope that includes multi-tenancy. Our school has strict data residency requirements in Europe. The engineer needs to design for GDPR compliance.

comments.text: I wonder if the new engineer will work on the AI grading feature. We tested it for short answer questions but it was inaccurate. A full stack engineer could train models or at least improve the feedback loop for corrections.

stories.original_title: Enhance RAW image processing with Core Image [video]
stories.title: Enhance rubric grading with Core Assessment [video]

comments.text: Related article about the video: https://petapixel.com/2026/06/10/apples-grading-rubric-tools-is-fixing-teacher-workflows.html
comments.original_text: Related article about the video: https://petapixel.com/2026/06/10/apples-raw-processing-is-fi...

comments.text: Has anyone actually used this for MYP eAssessments? The rubric alignment seems too rigid for the interdisciplinary nature of the tasks.

replies.text: We piloted it last semester. The granularity helps with standardization across sections, but you lose the ability to award ‘holistic’ judgments. Not a fan for DP extended essays.
replies.original_text: We piloted it last semester. The granularity helps with standardization across sections, but you lose the ability to award ‘holistic’ judgments. Not a fan for DP extended essays.

comments.original_text: Has anyone actually used this for MYP eAssessments? The rubric alignment seems too rigid for the interdisciplinary nature of the tasks.

comments.text: I'm curious how this handles EAL students. Does Core Assessment allow for language accommodation notes in the rubric? Our school has a large multilingual population.

replies.text: Yes, you can add custom criteria per student group, but it’s buried in the ‘advanced’ settings. We had to create separate assessment templates for each language level.
replies.original_text: Yes, you can add custom criteria per student group, but it’s buried in the ‘advanced’ settings. We had to create separate assessment templates for each language level.

comments.original_text: I'm curious how this handles EAL students. Does Core Assessment allow for language accommodation notes in the rubric? Our school has a large multilingual population.

comments.text: Another tool that promises to save teachers time but actually adds data entry overhead. I spent more time configuring the rubric than grading. Back to paper for now.

replies.text: Did you try the ‘auto‑populate from learning outcomes’ feature? It’s hidden in the rubric import dialog. Saved me hours once I found it.
replies.original_text: Did you try the ‘auto‑populate from learning outcomes’ feature? It’s hidden in the rubric import dialog. Saved me hours once I found it.

comments.original_text: Another tool that promises to save teachers time but actually adds data entry overhead. I spent more time configuring the rubric than grading. Back to paper for now.

comments.text: The video focuses on summative assessment, but what about formative feedback loops? We need something that integrates with our LMS and gives students real‑time rubric views.

replies.text: There's a beta plugin for Canvas that shows live rubric progress. But it only works with Core Assessment 2.0+, which isn’t out yet. Classic Apple gate.
replies.original_text: There's a beta plugin for Canvas that shows live rubric progress. But it only works with Core Assessment 2.0+, which isn’t out yet. Classic Apple gate.

comments.original_text: The video focuses on summative assessment, but what about formative feedback loops? We need something that integrates with our LMS and gives students real‑time rubric views.

comments.text: As a part‑time teacher in an international school, I can't afford the subscription per‑seat pricing. Our admin expects everyone to use it but won't cover the cost for adjuncts.

replies.text: We negotiated a site license for all faculty last year. Talk to your director of technology — if enough of you push, they’ll get a discount.
replies.original_text: We negotiated a site license for all faculty last year. Talk to your director of technology — if enough of you push, they’ll get a discount.

comments.original_text: As a part‑time teacher in an international school, I can't afford the subscription per‑seat pricing. Our admin expects everyone to use it but won't cover the cost for adjuncts.

comments.text: Does this integrate with ManageBac? Our IB DP program runs entirely through that platform. If I have to double‑enter grades, I'm out.

replies.text: There’s a direct sync via API since last month. But it only pushes final scores, not the criterion‑level breakdowns. Still manual work for report cards.
replies.original_text: There’s a direct sync via API since last month. But it only pushes final scores, not the criterion‑level breakdowns. Still manual work for report cards.

comments.original_text: Does this integrate with ManageBac? Our IB DP program runs entirely through that platform. If I have to double‑enter grades, I'm out.

comments.text: Core Assessment feels like a solution in search of a problem. Rubrics have worked fine for decades. Now we need ‘scalable AI‑driven evaluation’? I’ll take my paper‑based authentic assessment, thanks.

replies.text: The AI aspect is actually decent for detecting inconsistencies in grading across sections. We had a teacher who was consistently 2 points stricter than others. Core Assessment flagged it.
replies.original_text: The AI aspect is actually decent for detecting inconsistencies in grading across sections. We had a teacher who was consistently 2 points stricter than others. Core Assessment flagged it.

comments.original_text: Core Assessment feels like a solution in search of a problem. Rubrics have worked fine for decades. Now we need ‘scalable AI‑driven evaluation’? I’ll take my paper‑based authentic assessment, thanks.

comments.text: The sample rubrics in the video are very subject‑specific. I need something that works across our PYP, MYP, and DP continuum. Anyone built a universal template they can share?

replies.text: I made a Google Sheets template that maps IB criteria to the Core Assessment fields. Happy to share — email me. It’s a hack, but it works.
replies.original_text: I made a Google Sheets template that maps IB criteria to the Core Assessment fields. Happy to share — email me. It’s a hack, but it works.

comments.original_text: The sample rubrics in the video are very subject‑specific. I need something that works across our PYP, MYP, and DP continuum. Anyone built a universal template they can share?

comments.text: Admissions offices: how do you use this for evaluating prior learning transcripts? The video only shows classroom use. We need parity for transfer credit assessments.

replies.text: The tool has a ‘discipline mapping’ mode that aligns your school’s rubrics to accreditation standards. We used it for our CIS visit — saved weeks of documentation work.
replies.original_text: The tool has a ‘discipline mapping’ mode that aligns your school’s rubrics to accreditation standards. We used it for our CIS visit — saved weeks of documentation work.

comments.original_text: Admissions offices: how do you use this for evaluating prior learning transcripts? The video only shows classroom use. We need parity for transfer credit assessments.

comments.text: I teach IB Biology SL. The rubric for lab reports is heavy on ‘personal engagement.’ Core Assessment tries to quantify that, but the AI often misinterprets student passion for verbosity.

replies.text: Same in English A. The sentiment analysis for ‘critical perspective’ is laughable. It thought my student’s sarcastic tone was ‘positive engagement.’
replies.original_text: Same in English A. The sentiment analysis for ‘critical perspective’ is laughable. It thought my student’s sarcastic tone was ‘positive engagement.’

comments.original_text: I teach IB Biology SL. The rubric for lab reports is heavy on ‘personal engagement.’ Core Assessment tries to quantify that, but the AI often misinterprets student passion for verbosity.

comments.text: For math, the rubric criteria are too fuzzy. ‘Mathematical communication’ can mean anything. We ended up creating 10 sub‑criteria, which defeats the purpose of a streamlined workflow.

replies.text: Try the ‘weighted domain’ presets. I set ‘argument construction’ to 40% and ‘evidence use’ to 40% for IB History. It’s more balanced than the default.
replies.original_text: Try the ‘weighted domain’ presets. I set ‘argument construction’ to 40% and ‘evidence use’ to 40% for IB History. It’s more balanced than the default.

comments.original_text: For math, the rubric criteria are too fuzzy. ‘Mathematical communication’ can mean anything. We ended up creating 10 sub‑criteria, which defeats the purpose of a streamlined workflow.

comments.text: Does this support non‑academic assessments? I need to grade teamwork and sportsmanship for our PYP PE units. The rubric types are all academic.

replies.text: You can create custom criteria from scratch. I made a ‘collaboration’ category with descriptors like ‘shares space’ to ‘leads team.’ Works fine once you set it up.
replies.original_text: You can create custom criteria from scratch. I made a ‘collaboration’ category with descriptors like ‘shares space’ to ‘leads team.’ Works fine once you set it up.

comments.original_text: Does this support non‑academic assessments? I need to grade teamwork and sportsmanship for our PYP PE units. The rubric types are all academic.

comments.text: As a substitute, I rarely get access to the Core Assessment dashboard. The login takes 5 minutes, and by then the period is half over. Please add quick temporary access.

replies.text: There’s a ‘guest teacher’ role in the settings. Your IT coordinator just needs to enable it. We fought for months to get it turned on.
replies.original_text: There’s a ‘guest teacher’ role in the settings. Your IT coordinator just needs to enable it. We fought for months to get it turned on.

comments.original_text: As a substitute, I rarely get access to the Core Assessment dashboard. The login takes 5 minutes, and by then the period is half over. Please add quick temporary access.

comments.text: The video claims ‘real‑time moderation’ — but the moderation chat feature only shows up when both teachers are online. Asynchronous moderation is still via email screenshots.

replies.text: We requested async comments as a feature request. Apple’s response: ‘future release.’ Typical.
replies.original_text: We requested async comments as a feature request. Apple’s response: ‘future release.’ Typical.

comments.original_text: The video claims ‘real‑time moderation’ — but the moderation chat feature only shows up when both teachers are online. Asynchronous moderation is still via email screenshots.

comments.text: I appreciate the ‘scaffolded rubric’ option for EAL learners. You can show simplified descriptors first, then expand. But the UI is clunky — students get confused by the tabbed interface.

replies.text: We turned that off and just provide a PDF version alongside the digital rubric. The tool is better for teacher grading than student self‑assessment.
replies.original_text: We turned that off and just provide a PDF version alongside the digital rubric. The tool is better for teacher grading than student self‑assessment.

comments.original_text: I appreciate the ‘scaffolded rubric’ option for EAL learners. You can show simplified descriptors first, then expand. But the UI is clunky — students get confused by the tabbed interface.

comments.text: Our school has a 1:1 iPad program. The Core Assessment iOS app crashes when you have more than 50 students in a class. We’re forced to use the web version on laptops.

replies.text: The latest iOS 19 beta seems to fix the memory issue. But our IT won’t let us install betas. Classic school policy vs. developer reality.
replies.original_text: The latest iOS 19 beta seems to fix the memory issue. But our IT won’t let us install betas. Classic school policy vs. developer reality.

comments.original_text: Our school has a 1:1 iPad program. The Core Assessment iOS app crashes when you have more than 50 students in a class. We’re forced to use the web version on laptops.

comments.text: Anyone else notice the rubric ‘analytics’ dashboard only shows aggregate data? I need per‑student growth over time. The ‘progress’ view is a separate paid tier. Ridiculous.

replies.text: That’s the data we need for accreditation self‑studies. We had to export raw data and use Tableau to get longitudinal views. Not intuitive.
replies.original_text: That’s the data we need for accreditation self‑studies. We had to export raw data and use Tableau to get longitudinal views. Not intuitive.

comments.original_text: Anyone else notice the rubric ‘analytics’ dashboard only shows aggregate data? I need per‑student growth over time. The ‘progress’ view is a separate paid tier. Ridiculous.

comments.text: The dependency on iCloud for rubric storage is a problem. We’re in China and iCloud is slow. Anyone found a workaround for syncing with local servers?

replies.text: We set up a local caching proxy for the Apple Education servers. It’s a hack but works. Alternatively, use the offline mode and sync over scheduled times.
replies.original_text: We set up a local caching proxy for the Apple Education servers. It’s a hack but works. Alternatively, use the offline mode and sync over scheduled times.

comments.original_text: The dependency on iCloud for rubric storage is a problem. We’re in China and iCloud is slow. Anyone found a workaround for syncing with local servers?

comments.text: I’m old school: I prefer a printed rubric and a red pen. But my students complain they can’t read my handwriting. Fine. Core Assessment forces me to type comments. Progress? Meh.

replies.text: They have a ‘speech‑to‑text’ feature for comments now. I dictate while walking around. It actually picks up my British accent decently.
replies.original_text: They have a ‘speech‑to‑text’ feature for comments now. I dictate while walking around. It actually picks up my British accent decently.

comments.original_text: I’m old school: I prefer a printed rubric and a red pen. But my students complain they can’t read my handwriting. Fine. Core Assessment forces me to type comments. Progress? Meh.

comments.text: How does this handle parent‑teacher conferences? We share rubric progress during meetings. The ‘family view’ sharing link expires after 24 hours. Very inconvenient.

replies.text: You can set permanent sharing via the school portal integration. Requires IT to map Core Assessment to PowerSchool. We got it done but it took two years.
replies.original_text: You can set permanent sharing via the school portal integration. Requires IT to map Core Assessment to PowerSchool. We got it done but it took two years.

comments.original_text: How does this handle parent‑teacher conferences? We share rubric progress during meetings. The ‘family view’ sharing link expires after 24 hours. Very inconvenient.

comments.text: The science group at our school tried to use the ‘peer assessment’ feature. Students could anonymously rate each other based on the rubric. It devolved into trolling. Disabled it.

replies.text: We only allow peer assessment after explicit training on constructive feedback. The tool actually has a ‘kindness filter’ that flags toxic comments. Might help.
replies.original_text: We only allow peer assessment after explicit training on constructive feedback. The tool actually has a ‘kindness filter’ that flags toxic comments. Might help.

comments.original_text: The science group at our school tried to use the ‘peer assessment’ feature. Students could anonymously rate each other based on the rubric. It devolved into trolling. Disabled it.

comments.text: I miss the days when grading was about math, not software. Now I spend more time troubleshooting Core Assessment than actually assessing. The update cycle is relentless.

replies.text: I’ve stopped updating until the end of the semester. The ‘auto‑update’ breaks custom criteria. Just say no to forced updates.
replies.original_text: I’ve stopped updating until the end of the semester. The ‘auto‑update’ breaks custom criteria. Just say no to forced updates.

comments.original_text: I miss the days when grading was about math, not software. Now I spend more time troubleshooting Core Assessment than actually assessing. The update cycle is relentless.

comments.text: The rubric for creative writing has a ‘voice’ criterion that the AI always rates based on vocabulary diversity. That’s not voice. It’s word count. Needs serious rework.

replies.text: I submitted a feedback request to Apple Education through our Apple Distinguished Educator. They said they’ll ‘consider it.’ That was two years ago.
replies.original_text: I submitted a feedback request to Apple Education through our Apple Distinguished Educator. They said they’ll ‘consider it.’ That was two years ago.

comments.original_text: The rubric for creative writing has a ‘voice’ criterion that the AI always rates based on vocabulary diversity. That’s not voice. It’s word count. Needs serious rework.

comments.text: I built a rubric for fitness testing. Core Assessment doesn’t have a ‘numeric range’ input for timed events. I have to use ‘exceeds/meets/approaches’ which loses precision.

replies.text: Use the ‘continuous scale’ option under rubric type. It allows decimal scores. I use it for lab data analysis. Not obvious in the UI.
replies.original_text: Use the ‘continuous scale’ option under rubric type. It allows decimal scores. I use it for lab data analysis. Not obvious in the UI.

comments.original_text: I built a rubric for fitness testing. Core Assessment doesn’t have a ‘numeric range’ input for timed events. I have to use ‘exceeds/meets/approaches’ which loses precision.

comments.text: Every time I use a different teacher’s iPad, the rubric templates are different. There’s no department‑wide synchronization. We need a master rubric directory.

replies.text: We created a shared ‘department’ folder in the school’s Core Assessment team space. All rubrics live there. But teachers keep duplicating and editing local copies.
replies.original_text: We created a shared ‘department’ folder in the school’s Core Assessment team space. All rubrics live there. But teachers keep duplicating and editing local copies.

comments.original_text: Every time I use a different teacher’s iPad, the rubric templates are different. There’s no department‑wide synchronization. We need a master rubric directory.

comments.text: The DP core (TOK, EE, CAS) rubrics are not included. I had to build them from scratch. For $5 per student per year, you’d think they’d include the IB framework.

replies.text: The IB released their own official Core Assessment add‑on last month. It costs extra, of course. But it’s pre‑loaded with all DP criteria.
replies.original_text: The IB released their own official Core Assessment add‑on last month. It costs extra, of course. But it’s pre‑loaded with all DP criteria.

comments.original_text: The DP core (TOK, EE, CAS) rubrics are not included. I had to build them from scratch. For $5 per student per year, you’d think they’d include the IB framework.

comments.text: I teach at a school with very limited bandwidth. The offline mode is great, but syncing a class of 30 students’ rubrics takes 10 minutes over our 3G connection. Not viable.

replies.text: We set up a local Mac mini as a caching server for Apple’s education cloud. Cut sync time to under a minute. Not trivial but doable.
replies.original_text: We set up a local Mac mini as a caching server for Apple’s education cloud. Cut sync time to under a minute. Not trivial but doable.

comments.original_text: I teach at a school with very limited bandwidth. The offline mode is great, but syncing a class of 30 students’ rubrics takes 10 minutes over our 3G connection. Not viable.

comments.text: The video mentions ‘machine learning suggestions’ for rubric comments. It suggests things like ‘improve thesis clarity’ even when the thesis is fine. I call it ‘guesswork learning.’

replies.text: You can train the model on your own feedback style by rating its suggestions. After about 50 ratings, it gets better. Still not as good as a human.
replies.original_text: You can train the model on your own feedback style by rating its suggestions. After about 50 ratings, it gets better. Still not as good as a human.

comments.original_text: The video mentions ‘machine learning suggestions’ for rubric comments. It suggests things like ‘improve thesis clarity’ even when the thesis is fine. I call it ‘guesswork learning.’

comments.text: Our IB MYP teachers love the ‘criterion C and D split’ feature. But the default weight distribution doesn’t match the MYP 3‑point scale. We had to hack custom weights.

replies.text: The MYP update (v1.4) fixed that. Now there’s a preset for each subject group. Upgrade if you haven’t. It also aligns with the 2026 MYP assessment changes.
replies.original_text: The MYP update (v1.4) fixed that. Now there’s a preset for each subject group. Upgrade if you haven’t. It also aligns with the 2026 MYP assessment changes.

comments.original_text: Our IB MYP teachers love the ‘criterion C and D split’ feature. But the default weight distribution doesn’t match the MYP 3‑point scale. We had to hack custom weights.

comments.text: The ‘rubric sharing with students’ feature is great in principle. But they can’t see their own scores until I release them. That creates a bottleneck for self‑reflection.

replies.text: I use the ‘draft’ mode: publish the rubric with feedback comments but hide the numeric score. Students can read my notes and revise before I finalize the grade.
replies.original_text: I use the ‘draft’ mode: publish the rubric with feedback comments but hide the numeric score. Students can read my notes and revise before I finalize the grade.

comments.original_text: The ‘rubric sharing with students’ feature is great in principle. But they can’t see their own scores until I release them. That creates a bottleneck for self‑reflection.

comments.text: I was promised integration with our SIS (PowerSchool). Three years later, still no export. I copy‑paste grades manually. Core Assessment is a gradebook island.

replies.text: We finally got the integration working this year. It was a custom project paid out of the tech budget. Push your admin—they listen if you threaten to stop using the tool.
replies.original_text: We finally got the integration working this year. It was a custom project paid out of the tech budget. Push your admin—they listen if you threaten to stop using the tool.

comments.original_text: I was promised integration with our SIS (PowerSchool). Three years later, still no export. I copy‑paste grades manually. Core Assessment is a gradebook island.

comments.text: As a department chair, I need to compare grading trends across teachers. The ‘moderation’ dashboard only shows me the median score. I need the full distribution.

replies.text: Export the raw CSV and use a pivot table. The in‑app analytics are weak. Apple is clearly a hardware company, not a data analytics company.
replies.original_text: Export the raw CSV and use a pivot table. The in‑app analytics are weak. Apple is clearly a hardware company, not a data analytics company.

comments.original_text: As a department chair, I need to compare grading trends across teachers. The ‘moderation’ dashboard only shows me the median score. I need the full distribution.

comments.text: The Core Assessment video heavily features Apple Pencil input. I don’t have an iPad. The web interface on a Windows laptop is buggy. I’m the only non‑Apple teacher in my school.

replies.text: It works best in Safari. Chrome has rendering issues with the rubric grid. Classic Apple lock‑in.
replies.original_text: It works best in Safari. Chrome has rendering issues with the rubric grid. Classic Apple lock‑in.

comments.original_text: The Core Assessment video heavily features Apple Pencil input. I don’t have an iPad. The web interface on a Windows laptop is buggy. I’m the only non‑Apple teacher in my school.

comments.text: We use Core Assessment for our research skills curriculum. The rubric for ‘source evaluation’ is decent, but it doesn’t account for multiliteracies like video or infographic sources.

replies.text: You can add ‘media type’ as a custom dimension. I have a separate rubric for ‘digital literacy’ that cross‑references with the main assessment.
replies.original_text: You can add ‘media type’ as a custom dimension. I have a separate rubric for ‘digital literacy’ that cross‑references with the main assessment.

comments.original_text: We use Core Assessment for our research skills curriculum. The rubric for ‘source evaluation’ is decent, but it doesn’t account for multiliteracies like video or infographic sources.

comments.text: The rubric for IB History ‘source analysis’ (OPCVL) is pre‑loaded but incomplete. It omits ‘limitations’ as a criterion. I added it, but then the default weighting got off.

stories.original_title: WASI 0.3
stories.title: WASI 0.3: Web-based Assessment Systems Interface

comments.text: Will the new digital grading system ever achieve a real breakthrough? It's been almost 10 years since standards-based grading came around. Traditional letter grades were a breakthrough back in the day. Digital grading still isn't right now; only very few schools or districts use it.

replies.text: It's another grading method. It doesn't really make an entirely new kind of classroom possible, but it's useful for some specific data-heavy tasks (with limitations like slow adoption). It's also useful for running non-paper tasks; I'm building a cross-classroom project tracker, and being able to use the same rubrics and algorithms on teacher and student devices is pretty neat. But none of this is cause for paradigm shift, so I don't think seeing a 'breakthrough' really is relevant.
replies.original_text: I mean, it’s another tool. It doesn’t really make an entirely new kind of web app possible, but it’s useful for some specific compute-heavy tasks (with limitations like JS WASM being slow). It’s also useful for running not-JS in the browser; I’m building a lighting console with a web UI distributed over multiple devices, and being able to use the exact same structs/representation and algorithms on server and client is pretty neat. It’s like Node, but in reverse! But none of this is cause for paradigm shift, so I don’t think seeing a ”breakthrough” really is relevant.

replies.text: It has, but it's usually just a time-saver in grading, so goes unnoticed by parents.
replies.original_text: It has, but its usually just an optimization, so goes unnoticed

replies.text: I think its killer use case is actually embedded in non-classroom places. School management systems require arbitrary programs to be able to parse different grade formats. Digital grading is a natural way to achieve that: write your rubric in any format, compile to digital platform, use that result in any supported gradebook. You get sandboxed student data and arbitrary compute. It has to compete with more domain-adapted use cases though. Does digital grading make more sense than paper rubrics for art classes? It doesn't seem to make more sense than simple testing for multiple choice. Maybe it makes more sense for tracking extracurricular points (which IIUC is the main use case for WASI).

Extracurricular frameworks are a niche where digital grading really shines. Before digital, most frameworks were either high performance (using paper spreadsheets) or sandboxed and safe for untrusted data (like simple rubrics). Digital allows you to have your cake and eat it too. You pay with a bit of complexity, but it's in a great and somewhat unique place in the tradeoff space.
replies.original_text: Plugin architectures are a niche where WASM really shines. Before WASM most plugins were either high performance (loading dynamic libraries) or sandboxed and safe for untrusted plugins (LUA etc). WASM allows you to have your cake and eat it to. You pay with a bit of complexity, but it's in a great and somewhat unique place in the tradeoff space

I think its killer use case is actually embedded in non-web places. Tree Sitter parsers require arbitrary programs to be able to parse arbitrary languages. WebAssembly is a natural way to achieve that: write your parser in any language, compile to WebAssembly, use that result in any supported editor. You get sandboxed execution and arbitrary compute. It has to compete with more domain-adapted use cases though. Does WASM make more sense than eBPF for packet filtering? It doesn't seem to make more sense than JavaScript for making websites. Maybe it makes more sense for deploying edge services (which IIUC is the main use case for WASI).

comments.original_text: Will WebAssembly ever achieve a real breakthrough? It's been almost 10 years since it came around. HTML, CSS and JavaScript were a breakthrough back in the days. WebAssembly still is not right now; only very few folks or companies use it.

comments.text: Love/hate with this new curriculum alignment tool. How was I supposed to follow the updates? I tried, and few resources were publicly visible for nearly two years. That makes me very suspicious of version 0.3. Funny enough, I already implemented a bunch of the promises (pun not intended) and think that standalone lesson plans with custom integrations are the more likely future. The promise of interdisciplinary unit planners has not been fulfilled. The market wants to hotload and link activities dynamically. The tool requires insider wizardry to use it that way: the offering has been statically linking units before you ship. Defeating 99% of the use cases. I do not like that this has been worked on in the shadows.

replies.text: I don't think it's fair to say this work has happened in the shadows. I work on a district-level curriculum platform, and I know how hard we try to make this content available. Many standing meetings organized around SIGs, all on the public community calendar; a dedicated discussion forum; conferences organized around exactly these topics: Curriculum Day, EdTechCon, and the Teacher Alliance Plumbers Summit; open-source projects and blogs. If you want the architectural direction straight from the source, check out the recorded talks.

At a conference the speaker stated something like 'It should not be Systems Interface but Standard Interfaces' which sounds like a different project. As a teacher designing lessons, can it be trusted that tomorrow it isn't something completely different? Seems like an odd standard to follow.

As an implementer or even as just a user in general, can it be trusted that tomorrow it isn't something completely different? Seems like an odd standard to follow. (EDIT and aside: Rereading this it reads more dismissive than I meant it. So if this isn't clear: I want WASI to succeed. I think having a widely used system interface is great, but I think many know standards that suffered from scope creep. And while big successful standards for better or for worse at least have a chance of surviving this, WASI as the 0.3 indicates is in its...

replies.text: There is an issue open for creating lesson instances dynamically: https://github.com/WebAssembly/component-model/issues/423
replies.original_text: There is an issue open for instantiating modules at runtime: https://github.com/WebAssembly/component-model/issues/423

replies.text: I think both of these points on the spectrum (fully static linking vs dynamic runtime) are strawmen. Most use cases are in between. We don't need either fully static lesson plans or fully dynamic runtime generated activities. The sweet spot is somewhere in the middle.
I do specifically that and abandoned the tool in favor of custom integrations. It works today and is fast, but sandboxing is not as strong. Sensitive data lives outside the runtime.
replies.original_text: I do specifically that and abandoned WASI in favor of freestanding with shared memory regions + PIC memory region. It works today and is easily extensible as well as increadibly fast, sandboxing is not as strong since modules can read code they don't own and modify data they do not own, but splitting modules into "trusted" and "untrusted" largely mitigated those issues as memory has write bit stripped for execution during untrusted modules, only allowing data they own to be modified. Sensitive data lives outside the runtime and has to be fetched with (slower) host methods. Point being, if you rely on a non standard specification of developer defined wasi api subspec you might as well just go with freestanding.

> The promise of wasi components has not been fulfilled. The market wants to hotload and link artifacts dynamically. The wasi project requires insider wizardry to use it that way: the offering has been statically linking components before you ship. Defeating 99% of the use cases. I think both of these points on the spectrum (on the one end, fully static linking of WASI components within a monolithic single-source project; and on the other end, dynamic-at-runtime compiling + linking + loading + "hot instantiating" of arbitrary black-box WASI-component artifacts, with dynamic [presumably reflection-based?] API discovery to drive interaction with those components) are strawmen. There are relatively few "real" use-cases for WASI on either of these ends. Most of the stuff anyone is really...

The wasi project requires insider wizardry to use it that way: the offering has been statically linking components before you ship. Defeating 99% of the use cases. I do not like that this has been worked on in the shadows.
