replies.text: I tinkered with ExtendMe platform and basically the use case is that they suggest, namely you can extend teaching tools in another language but without having to setup a shared drive. It's quite specific though. If you are not deep into letting a very specific kind of user extend, it's probably overkill.
We evaluated ExtendMe and concluded that it did basically nothing that the standard system didn't already do out of the box.
replies.original_text: We evaluated Extism and concluded that it did basically nothing that WASI and the component model didn't already do out of the box. Was your experience different?

I tinkered with https://extism.org and basically the use case is that they suggest, namely you can extend software in another programming language but without having to setup a container or VMs on the client. They "just" run the code in the browser and it can be JavaScript, sure, but can also be Python, Go, whatever. It's quite specific though as I'm working on support programming in the browser. If you are not deep into letting a very specific kind of user extend, it's probably overkill. Even then it's a very VERY niche thing because it has to be simultaneously : - someone who is opinionated about a programming language (either because they know too much, i.e. expert, or not enough, i.e beginner) - is dedicated enough to want to try to build something on top of an existing system -...

comments.original_text: If you have used WASI in the past, can you mention your use case? Very curious if you found it to give you an edge compared to other sandboxing like containers or VMs.

comments.text: We've been piloting WASI 0.3 for our DP internal assessments. The sandboxing is solid—no more student code escaping into the gradebook. But the real win is interoperability: we can now pipe MYP ePortfolio data directly into the IBIS system without custom scripts.

replies.text: That's the dream. Our current pipeline involves three manual exports and a prayer. How did you handle the alignment with IB criteria? The rubric templates in WASI don't match the official ones exactly.

comments.original_text: If you don't want to download the .tar.gz I think you can browse the content for this release (.wit interface files) here on GitHub: https://github.com/WebAssembly/WASI/tree/v0.3.0/proposals

comments.text: Another tool that promises to revolutionize assessment but ends up adding five more layers of abstraction. I just want a gradebook that works offline in a low-bandwidth international school.

replies.text: Offline support is actually one of the features in WASI 0.3—you can cache assessments locally and sync later. But yeah, the abstraction tax is real.
replies.original_text: I disagree. We shouldn't just be copying Unix until the end of time.

comments.original_text: Wrong direction. WASI should be simple and stable. Initially, it was revolving around a simple Unix-like API model and it was close to perfect. Now, there is an opinionated component model which is an unneeded overcomplication that should have never been considered as part of WebAssembly spec IMHO. A real component model is a separate development and cannot be blindly tied to a particular ecosystem. Otherwise, its main purpose of providing easy interoperability between different ecosystems is totally lost. I do not know why WebAssembly committee thinks that shoving-in CORBA-like monstrosity is even an acceptable idea. Let's keep WebAssembly lean and fast! Anything extra can (and should) be implemented by other technologies.

comments.text: Does WASI 0.3 support multilingual rubrics? We have 47 home languages in our school, and our EAL students need descriptors in multiple languages. Google Translate doesn't cut it for criterion-referenced grading.

replies.text: Not natively, but you can embed i18n strings in the component model. It's on the roadmap for 0.4. For now, we're using a custom plugin that loads language packs.

comments.text: Has anyone tried using WASI for library skills assessments? We want to embed information literacy checks into the research process. The current tools are too rigid.

replies.text: Not library specific, but the custom rubric engine should handle that. You can define any scoring criteria and attach them to activities. The challenge is the UX for students—it's very developer-oriented right now.
replies.original_text: No, the stack switching proposal is not used. Stack switching is a set of core Wasm opcodes that permit a guest to change its own stack. Instead of using opcodes inside the Wasm, the stackful async mode of the component model’s ABI calls out into the component model implementation where it can manipulate the stacks with special host powers - JSPI is sufficient on web engines to express this, and wasmtime manages guest stacks in memory. When stack switching becomes available in all engines, it will be possible to implement this part of the component model in pure Wasm without host magic, e.g. web engines will be able to avoid the call out to JS to use JSPI.

comments.original_text: Does the stackfull async implementation use the stack-switching proposal? I was under the impression that it's not implemented in most runtimes (very difficult to retrofit into existing implementations), and only available on x86_64 Linux in wasmtime.

comments.text: As a principal, I need to see a clear ROI before adopting another platform. We've had three different assessment systems in five years. What makes WASI different?

replies.text: It's not a platform—it's a standard. That means you can switch providers without changing your assessment logic. That alone could save thousands in migration costs.
replies.original_text: It's not a platform—it's a standard. That means you can switch providers without changing your assessment logic. That alone could save thousands in migration costs.

replies.text: Plus, it's open-source. No vendor lock-in. Your school can maintain its own implementation if needed.
replies.original_text: Plus, it's open-source. No vendor lock-in. Your school can maintain its own implementation if needed.

comments.original_text: Congrats on the release to the WASI team. TL;DR: WASI 0.3.0 is the Component Model-based WASI proposal. It adds async/await-style capabilities such as actors and streams, and today is runnable in only one server-side Wasm runtime (it is not supported natively by browsers). Unfortunately it still breaks compatibility with the original WASI proposal and runtimes that supported it. If your goal is to compile existing, unmodified C/C++ programs and libraries to WebAssembly, WASIX may be a more practical option today ( https://wasix.org/ ). Disclosure: I’m part of Wasmer, the company behind WASIX.

replies.text: Assessment chains sound good in theory but can lead to over-reliance on data. Teachers need to use professional judgment too.

comments.original_text: The WASI 0.3 spec includes a provision for 'assessment chains'—linking formative assessments to summative. This is huge for our competency-based model. But implementing it requires careful planning.

comments.text: I've used WASI at my previous school for two years. Migrating to a new school that also uses WASI was seamless—export/import of rubrics worked perfectly. That's the selling point for frequent movers.

replies.text: But only if both schools use the same WASI compatible platform. Our school uses a custom platform that isn't fully standard-compliant.
replies.original_text: But only if both schools use the same WASI compatible platform. Our school uses a custom platform that isn't fully standard-compliant.

comments.original_text: I've used WASI at my previous school for two years. Migrating to a new school that also uses WASI was seamless—export/import of rubrics worked perfectly. That's the selling point for frequent movers.

comments.text: The WASI working group is very responsive. I filed a bug about a memory leak in the rubric parser, and it was fixed within a week. That's better than any commercial vendor I've dealt with.

replies.text: Yes, the community is great. But we need more documentation for teachers, not just developers.
replies.original_text: Yes, the community is great. But we need more documentation for teachers, not just developers.

comments.original_text: The WASI working group is very responsive. I filed a bug about a memory leak in the rubric parser, and it was fixed within a week. That's better than any commercial vendor I've dealt with.

comments.text: We built a WASI-based tool for IB middle years programme personal project assessment. The ability to embed student reflections and advisor feedback in the same component is elegant. Wish we had this five years ago.

replies.text: MYP PP is a good fit for WASI because it's structured but flexible. Did you use the official IB rubric or customize it?
replies.original_text: MYP PP is a good fit for WASI because it's structured but flexible. Did you use the official IB rubric or customize it?

replies.text: Customized. We aligned with IB criteria but added school-specific habits of mind. WASI made the mapping straightforward.
replies.original_text: Customized. We aligned with IB criteria but added school-specific habits of mind. WASI made the mapping straightforward.

comments.original_text: We built a WASI-based tool for IB middle years programme personal project assessment. The ability to embed student reflections and advisor feedback in the same component is elegant. Wish we had this five years ago.

comments.text: We're considering WASI for our next accreditation cycle. The ability to generate evidence of student learning from ongoing assessments could save hours of documentation work. But we need to ensure the data is audit-proof.

replies.text: WASI's audit trail is robust. Every assessment event is logged with timestamp and identity. Our accreditors were impressed during the pilot.
replies.original_text: WASI's audit trail is robust. Every assessment event is logged with timestamp and identity. Our accreditors were impressed during the pilot.

comments.original_text: We're considering WASI for our next accreditation cycle. The ability to generate evidence of student learning from ongoing assessments could save hours of documentation work. But we need to ensure the data is audit-proof.

comments.text: The barrier to entry for WASI is too high. Teachers should not need to understand components and interfaces. We need opinionated tools that hide the complexity. The standard is for developers, not educators.

replies.text: I disagree. The standard empowers developers to build tools that suit different contexts. A one-size-fits-all tool would be worse.
replies.original_text: I disagree. The standard empowers developers to build tools that suit different contexts. A one-size-fits-all tool would be worse.

comments.original_text: The barrier to entry for WASI is too high. Teachers should not need to understand components and interfaces. We need opinionated tools that hide the complexity. The standard is for developers, not educators.

comments.text: We implemented automated feedback using WASI event triggers. When a student submits a math proof, the system checks each step and provides hints. The component model allowed us to define step-by-step criteria.

replies.text: That's what I want for lab reports. How did you handle partial credit for partially correct steps?
replies.original_text: That's what I want for lab reports. How did you handle partial credit for partially correct steps?

replies.text: We used a weighted criteria system. Each step has a point value, and the rubric sums them. WASI's scoring engine supports partial scores.
replies.original_text: We used a weighted criteria system. Each step has a point value, and the rubric sums them. WASI's scoring engine supports partial scores.

comments.original_text: We implemented automated feedback using WASI event triggers. When a student submits a math proof, the system checks each step and provides hints. The component model allowed us to define step-by-step criteria.

comments.text: We're using WASI to track service learning hours for CAS. The plugin from the community works well, but reporting needs work. We want to generate transcripts automatically.

replies.text: There's a CAS reporting tool in development. Join the #cas channel on the WASI community Slack.
replies.original_text: There's a CAS reporting tool in development. Join the #cas channel on the WASI community Slack.

comments.original_text: We're using WASI to track service learning hours for CAS. The plugin from the community works well, but reporting needs work. We want to generate transcripts automatically.

comments.text: As a sub, I appreciate that WASI-based gradebooks all have a common way to view and enter scores. It reduces my cognitive load when jumping between classes. More schools should standardize on it.

replies.text: But it only helps if the school actually uses WASI. Many still use proprietary systems that don't follow the standard.
replies.original_text: But it only helps if the school actually uses WASI. Many still use proprietary systems that don't follow the standard.

comments.original_text: As a sub, I appreciate that WASI-based gradebooks all have a common way to view and enter scores. It reduces my cognitive load when jumping between classes. More schools should standardize on it.

comments.text: We're working with the WASI consortium to create an accreditation data package. Schools could export a standardized assessment portfolio that accreditors can read with any WASI-compliant tool. That's the long-term vision.

replies.text: That would be a game-changer. Our WASC self-study took months. A standardized export could cut that in half.
replies.original_text: That would be a game-changer. Our WASC self-study took months. A standardized export could cut that in half.

comments.original_text: We're working with the WASI consortium to create an accreditation data package. Schools could export a standardized assessment portfolio that accreditors can read with any WASI-compliant tool. That's the long-term vision.

comments.text: I tried to create a rubric for creative writing that rewards risk-taking. The WASI component model forced me to be too explicit about criteria. Sometimes you need holistic judgment that can't be decomposed.

replies.text: You can have a holistic criterion with a description and still assign points. WASI doesn't force you to break everything into sub-criteria. The model allows both analytic and holistic rubrics.
replies.original_text: You can have a holistic criterion with a description and still assign points. WASI doesn't force you to break everything into sub-criteria. The model allows both analytic and holistic rubrics.

comments.original_text: I tried to create a rubric for creative writing that rewards risk-taking. The WASI component model forced me to be too explicit about criteria. Sometimes you need holistic judgment that can't be decomposed.

comments.text: The international school market is diverse. WASI needs to support different curricula: IB, AP, A-Levels, and local. So far, only IB has decent support. AP is still missing.

replies.text: AP support is in progress. The College Board hasn't officially endorsed it, but there are community projects. See the 'AP WASI adapter' on GitHub.
replies.original_text: AP support is in progress. The College Board hasn't officially endorsed it, but there are community projects. See the 'AP WASI adapter' on GitHub.

comments.original_text: The international school market is diverse. WASI needs to support different curricula: IB, AP, A-Levels, and local. So far, only IB has decent support. AP is still missing.

comments.text: We wrote a converter from our old XML rubric format to WASI. It took two weeks, but now we can use any WASI tool. The migration path is worth it if you have a large rubric library.

replies.text: Could you share the converter? We have hundreds of IB rubrics in XML that we need to migrate.
replies.original_text: Could you share the converter? We have hundreds of IB rubrics in XML that we need to migrate.

replies.text: Sure, it's on our school's GitHub. I'll send you the link.
replies.original_text: Sure, it's on our school's GitHub. I'll send you the link.

comments.original_text: We wrote a converter from our old XML rubric format to WASI. It took two weeks, but now we can use any WASI tool. The migration path is worth it if you have a large rubric library.

comments.text: The WASI 0.3 spec includes a 'plagiarism detection' interface. We hooked it up with Turnitin and it works, but the latency for large classes is too high. Need local caching.

replies.text: Yes, we experienced the same. We wrote a caching plugin that stores checksummed documents locally and only sends new ones to the service. Improved speed significantly.
replies.original_text: Yes, we experienced the same. We wrote a caching plugin that stores checksummed documents locally and only sends new ones to the service. Improved speed significantly.

comments.original_text: The WASI 0.3 spec includes a 'plagiarism detection' interface. We hooked it up with Turnitin and it works, but the latency for large classes is too high. Need local caching.

comments.text: I'm skeptical of any assessment standard that claims to be 'interface' but ends up dictating pedagogy. The official examples show only behaviorist approaches. Where is the Constructivist assessment model?

replies.text: The interface is agnostic. You can design a Constructivist rubric that assesses process over product. The examples are just what's been built so far. Contribute your own.
replies.original_text: The interface is agnostic. You can design a Constructivist rubric that assesses process over product. The examples are just what's been built so far. Contribute your own.

comments.original_text: I'm skeptical of any assessment standard that claims to be 'interface' but ends up dictating pedagogy. The official examples show only behaviorist approaches. Where is the Constructivist assessment model?

comments.text: We've been running WASI in production for a semester. Stability is good, but we hit a memory issue with large class sets (200+ students). The team is working on a fix. For now, we batch our assessments.

replies.text: Same issue. The problem is in the rubric evaluation engine—it loads all students into memory. They're adding streaming support in 0.4.
replies.original_text: Same issue. The problem is in the rubric evaluation engine—it loads all students into memory. They're adding streaming support in 0.4.

comments.original_text: We've been running WASI in production for a semester. Stability is good, but we hit a memory issue with large class sets (200+ students). The team is working on a fix. For now, we batch our assessments.

comments.text: WASI needs better mobile support. We do outdoor PE assessments and our teachers use tablets. The current web interface is not touch-friendly.

replies.text: There's a progressive web app wrapper that works okay. But native mobile SDKs are on the roadmap. Vote on the issue tracker.
replies.original_text: There's a progressive web app wrapper that works okay. But native mobile SDKs are on the roadmap. Vote on the issue tracker.

comments.original_text: WASI needs better mobile support. We do outdoor PE assessments and our teachers use tablets. The current web interface is not touch-friendly.

stories.original_title: Show HN: StackScope – I crawled over 40k indie launches to see what they ship
stories.title: Show Teachers: SchoolScope – I crawled over 40k school websites to see what they ship for IB resources

comments.text: Nice one. I've been doing the same but wider scope, for the whole IB World Schools list, pruned to authorized schools, and looking for LMS signals like ManageBac or Toddle. How's your throughput? I'm not doing any headless browser stuff, or many requests, so hyper-optimized for speed. I do grab robots.txt – didn't really see much in accessibility.txt or equity.txt in the wild, does yours?

replies.text: Ohh Cloudflare verified bot status, interesting. I'm seeing about 6.6% of schools block me via .htaccess or firewall, but that does climb over time.
replies.original_text: Ohh Cloudflare verified bot status, interesting I'll check that out. I'm seeing about 6.6% block rate, but that does climb over time.

comments.original_text: Nice one. I've been doing the same bit wider scope, for the whole Crux list, pruned to apex domains, and looking for CMS signals - how's your throughput? I'm not doing any headless browser stuff, or many requests, so hyper optimised for speed. I do grab robots.txt - didn't really see much in llms.txt or humans.txt in the wild, does yours?

comments.text: One extension, beyond IB: subject area/grade level/applicable framework – or any combo that tells me what the school really offers. Fab project otherwise!
comments.original_text: One extension, beyond stack: market category/domain/application - or any combo that tells me what the product does. Fab project otherwise!

comments.text: Nice one, looks interesting. I didn't know that Google Sites was so far ahead for school hosting. People don't usually use WordPress multisite? Or it doesn't enter in that metric? https://stackscope.dev/trends/2026/06 Overall this looks solid.

replies.text: WordPress sites get hidden behind CDNs like Cloudflare or Sucuri, so are included in the CDN figure.
right, makes sense
replies.original_text: right, makes sense
Cloudflare Pages get hidden behind the CF CDN, so are included in the CDN figure.

comments.original_text: Nice one, looks interesting. I didn't knew that Vercel was so far ahead on hosting. People don't usually use Cloudflare Pages for hosting? Or it doesn't enter in that metric? https://stackscope.dev/trends/2026/06 Overall this looks solid

comments.text: Makes me sad how Google Classroom has become the default for so many schools…

replies.text: Unfortunately the chances are that it would become cyclic with the increasing use of AI to generate lesson plans.
replies.original_text: Unfortunately the chances are that it would become cyclic with the increasing use of LLMs to generate code

comments.original_text: Makes me sad how Nextjs has become the default for so many startups…

comments.text: This was interesting to look at for my own school's site https://stackscope.dev/launch/xisjc21x/elmo I'm a little surprised you can't see the analysis for SchoolScope itself! Also, it'd be interesting to see the trend of vibe score over the school year.

replies.text: I'm trying but it's getting blocked by our own school firewall and ending up getting broken, ha! I will include a vibe score trend in the next blog post.
replies.original_text: I'm trying but it's getting blocked by Cloudflare and ending up getting broken, ha! I will include a vibe score trend in the next blog post.

comments.original_text: This was interesting to look at for my own launch https://stackscope.dev/launch/xisjc21x/elmo I'm a little surprised you can't see the analysis for StackScope itself! Also, it'd be interesting to see the trend of vibe score over time.

comments.text: Cool tool. I'm also surprised by how different the school tech stacks are from general Internet. For SSL, don't forget to check if .edu domains are preloaded in HSTS. Many .edu domains are forced to HTTPS by law, so they don't need to set the header for SSL to apply.
comments.original_text: Cool tool, I'm also surprised by how different the startup stacks are from the general Internet. For HSTS, don't forget to check the preload list. Domains under .dev are all preloaded, for example, so they don't need to set the header for HSTS to apply.

comments.text: Interesting dataset. I'd be curious to see how many schools still use the IB's own MyIB vs third-party platforms like ManageBac. Our MYP just switched to Toddle and it's been a huge improvement for planning.

replies.text: We use ManageBac and it's a mess. The gradebook UX is from 2005. But the IB coordinator insists because 'everyone else uses it'.
replies.original_text: We use ManageBac and it's a mess. The gradebook UX is from 2005. But the IB coordinator insists because 'everyone else uses it'.

comments.original_text: Interesting dataset. I'd be curious to see how many schools still use the IB's own MyIB vs third-party platforms like ManageBac. Our MYP just switched to Toddle and it's been a huge improvement for planning.

comments.text: Nice project. The number of schools still using Dreamweaver-era static sites is shocking. I found one that hadn't updated their IB Diploma page since 2014 - still advertising the old CAS requirements.

replies.text: We have a school that still has their library page as a PDF from 2009. No search function. The principal thinks it's fine.
replies.original_text: We have a school that still has their library page as a PDF from 2009. No search function. The principal thinks it's fine.

comments.original_text: Nice project. The number of schools still using Dreamweaver-era static sites is shocking. I found one that hadn't updated their IB Diploma page since 2014 - still advertising the old CAS requirements.

comments.text: Would be useful to see what EAL support resources schools advertise. Many top-tier international schools bury their EAL page deep in the site. Shows priorities.

replies.text: Agreed. I ran a similar scrape last year and found that only 12% of schools that claim 'EAL support' actually list any details beyond a sentence.
replies.original_text: Agreed. I ran a similar scrape last year and found that only 12% of schools that claim 'EAL support' actually list any details beyond a sentence.

comments.original_text: Would be useful to see what EAL support resources schools advertise. Many top-tier international schools bury their EAL page deep in the site. Shows priorities.

comments.text: I wonder how many of these schools use Canvas vs Google Classroom vs MS Teams. The IB's integration with Canvas has been half-baked for years.

replies.text: We just migrated from GC to Canvas because of the IB diploma e-assessment workflow. It's still painful. The IB doesn't seem to care about LMS interoperability.
replies.original_text: We just migrated from GC to Canvas because of the IB diploma e-assessment workflow. It's still painful. The IB doesn't seem to care about LMS interoperability.

comments.original_text: I wonder how many of these schools use Canvas vs Google Classroom vs MS Teams. The IB's integration with Canvas has been half-baked for years.

comments.text: As someone who's been through CIS/WASC accreditation, I'm curious if the crawl can detect site ages or last update. Many schools only update their website right before an accreditation visit.

replies.text: That's exactly what happens. We saw a school that hadn't updated their 'Accreditation' page in 3 years - then suddenly 4 new pages appeared the week before the team arrived.
replies.original_text: That's exactly what happens. We saw a school that hadn't updated their 'Accreditation' page in 3 years - then suddenly 4 new pages appeared the week before the team arrived.

comments.original_text: As someone who's been through CIS/WASC accreditation, I'm curious if the crawl can detect site ages or last update. Many schools only update their website right before an accreditation visit.

comments.text: The IB math curriculum changes every 7 years but many school websites still reference the old 2014 syllabus. The crawl should flag outdated curriculum references.

replies.text: If you can scrape the PDFs, you could date them by the IB subject guide number. I've got a regex that matches 'DP Mathematics HL (revised 2019)' if anyone wants to collaborate.
replies.original_text: If you can scrape the PDFs, you could date them by the IB subject guide number. I've got a regex that matches 'DP Mathematics HL (revised 2019)' if anyone wants to collaborate.

comments.original_text: The IB math curriculum changes every 7 years but many school websites still reference the old 2014 syllabus. The crawl should flag outdated curriculum references.

comments.text: Our school's IT director would love this. He's trying to move us to a single content management system but every department has their own fiefdom of sub-sites. The crawl data could prove his point.

replies.text: Let me know if you need a letter of support. I'm tired of seeing 8 different WordPress multisites with different themes across the school.
replies.original_text: Let me know if you need a letter of support. I'm tired of seeing 8 different WordPress multisites with different themes across the school.

comments.original_text: Our school's IT director would love this. He's trying to move us to a single content management system but every department has their own fiefdom of sub-sites. The crawl data could prove his point.

comments.text: Does the crawl include the actual IB resource links? Like are schools linking to the IB's official digital toolkit, or just a PDF of the syllabus?

replies.text: I looked at my school's report - it shows we link to 'IB Subject Briefs' PDFs but not the OCC. Probably because the OCC requires login. That's a blind spot in this dataset.
replies.original_text: I looked at my school's report - it shows we link to 'IB Subject Briefs' PDFs but not the OCC. Probably because the OCC requires login. That's a blind spot in this dataset.

comments.original_text: Does the crawl include the actual IB resource links? Like are schools linking to the IB's official digital toolkit, or just a PDF of the syllabus?

comments.text: Interesting for university counseling pages. Many schools claim to have 'comprehensive guidance programs' but the website only lists college application deadlines. No mention of IB-specific advising.

replies.text: I've found that schools with strong IB counseling programs tend to have separate pages for 'IB Diploma Planning' and 'Subject Selection'. The scrape could weight those as quality signals.
replies.original_text: I've found that schools with strong IB counseling programs tend to have separate pages for 'IB Diploma Planning' and 'Subject Selection'. The scrape could weight those as quality signals.

comments.original_text: Interesting for university counseling pages. Many schools claim to have 'comprehensive guidance programs' but the website only lists college application deadlines. No mention of IB-specific advising.

comments.text: The accessibility of these school sites is probably terrible. Did you check for alt text on images? WCAG compliance? Most international schools ignore that for their IB resource pages.

replies.text: You're right. Our school's IB page has a chart of subject offerings as an image with no alt text. The visually impaired ESL student we had last year couldn't access it.
replies.original_text: You're right. Our school's IB page has a chart of subject offerings as an image with no alt text. The visually impaired ESL student we had last year couldn't access it.

comments.original_text: The accessibility of these school sites is probably terrible. Did you check for alt text on images? WCAG compliance? Most international schools ignore that for their IB resource pages.

comments.text: This is neat. I'd like to see a breakdown by region - do schools in Asia (especially China) use different tech stacks than those in Europe for IB resources?

replies.text: From my experience, schools in China are more likely to use WeChat official accounts and have their IB info in Chinese too. The crawl probably misses non-HTML content.
replies.original_text: From my experience, schools in China are more likely to use WeChat official accounts and have their IB info in Chinese too. The crawl probably misses non-HTML content.

comments.original_text: This is neat. I'd like to see a breakdown by region - do schools in Asia (especially China) use different tech stacks than those in Europe for IB resources?

comments.text: Could you add a view showing which schools use the official 'IB World School' logo properly? I bet many display it without permission or use outdated versions.

replies.text: The IB is pretty strict about that. We had to send a cease and desist to a school that was using a modified logo on their website. It's in the annual license agreement.
replies.original_text: The IB is pretty strict about that. We had to send a cease and desist to a school that was using a modified logo on their website. It's in the annual license agreement.

comments.original_text: Could you add a view showing which schools use the official 'IB World School' logo properly? I bet many display it without permission or use outdated versions.

comments.text: The server response times are interesting. Some of these school sites are hosted on shared hosting that grinds to a halt during registration season. Our IB DP coordinator always complains.

replies.text: That's why we moved to a CDN. But many schools in developing countries are still on local cheap hosting. The scrape reveals performance issues.
replies.original_text: That's why we moved to a CDN. But many schools in developing countries are still on local cheap hosting. The scrape reveals performance issues.

comments.original_text: The server response times are interesting. Some of these school sites are hosted on shared hosting that grinds to a halt during registration season. Our IB DP coordinator always complains.

comments.text: I'm surprised by how few schools have their PE/Health curriculum online. For MYP, PE is a required subject but many school sites don't even mention it. Shows priorities.

replies.text: PE is always the last to get web pages. At my school, the PE department still uses a shared Google Doc for the MYP unit planner. No web presence at all.
replies.original_text: PE is always the last to get web pages. At my school, the PE department still uses a shared Google Doc for the MYP unit planner. No web presence at all.

comments.original_text: I'm surprised by how few schools have their PE/Health curriculum online. For MYP, PE is a required subject but many school sites don't even mention it. Shows priorities.

comments.text: Would be great to see what digital library resources schools link to for IB students. Many have subscriptions to JSTOR or EBSCO but never mention them on the IB page.

replies.text: We actually found that schools that list library databases on their IB page have higher EE scores. Correlation? Maybe, but it's a good indicator of support.
replies.original_text: We actually found that schools that list library databases on their IB page have higher EE scores. Correlation? Maybe, but it's a good indicator of support.

comments.original_text: Would be great to see what digital library resources schools link to for IB students. Many have subscriptions to JSTOR or EBSCO but never mention them on the IB page.

comments.text: As a substitute, I often need to find the school's IB policies quickly. Many schools bury their 'Academic Honesty Policy' or 'Assessment Policy' in sub-menus. This crawl could help me find them faster.

replies.text: Good point. The IB requires schools to publish these policies online, but many don't make them easy to find. I'm going to check my school's crawl report now.
replies.original_text: Good point. The IB requires schools to publish these policies online, but many don't make them easy to find. I'm going to check my school's crawl report now.

comments.original_text: As a substitute, I often need to find the school's IB policies quickly. Many schools bury their 'Academic Honesty Policy' or 'Assessment Policy' in sub-menus. This crawl could help me find them faster.

comments.text: I'm curious about the tech used for the IB's 'Theory of Knowledge' showcase. Many schools have beautiful student-created sites for TOK exhibitions. Did you scrape those?

replies.text: Those are usually on subdomains like tok.schoolname.edu. If you limited the crawl to the main domain, you'd miss those. They're often built with Weebly or Wix by students.
replies.original_text: Those are usually on subdomains like tok.schoolname.edu. If you limited the crawl to the main domain, you'd miss those. They're often built with Weebly or Wix by students.

comments.original_text: I'm curious about the tech used for the IB's 'Theory of Knowledge' showcase. Many schools have beautiful student-created sites for TOK exhibitions. Did you scrape those?

comments.text: Interesting how few schools have their IB Extended Essay guidelines online. It's like they want to keep the process mysterious. Our school has a whole section with sample essays and timelines.

replies.text: Some schools are worried about plagiarism if they publish sample essays. But the IB expects transparency. It's a fine line.
replies.original_text: Some schools are worried about plagiarism if they publish sample essays. But the IB expects transparency. It's a fine line.

comments.original_text: Interesting how few schools have their IB Extended Essay guidelines online. It's like they want to keep the process mysterious. Our school has a whole section with sample essays and timelines.

comments.text: I'm going to share this with our board. We've been debating whether to invest in a new website. The data showing how many schools use modern CMSs like Webflow will help make the case.

replies.text: Just be careful - Webflow isn't great for large content volumes. Our school tried it but moved to a headless CMS. Don't let the marketing sway you.
replies.original_text: Just be careful - Webflow isn't great for large content volumes. Our school tried it but moved to a headless CMS. Don't let the marketing sway you.

comments.original_text: I'm going to share this with our board. We've been debating whether to invest in a new website. The data showing how many schools use modern CMSs like Webflow will help make the case.

comments.text: The 'Subject Offerings' data is useful for recruitment. If a candidate sees we offer 25 IB courses and the school down the street offers 15, that's a differentiator. But the scrape needs to validate actual offerings vs claimed.

replies.text: Our school claims 30 subjects but many are 'taught on demand' with 1 student. The website doesn't say that. It's misleading.
replies.original_text: Our school claims 30 subjects but many are 'taught on demand' with 1 student. The website doesn't say that. It's misleading.

comments.original_text: The 'Subject Offerings' data is useful for recruitment. If a candidate sees we offer 25 IB courses and the school down the street offers 15, that's a differentiator. But the scrape needs to validate actual offerings vs claimed.

comments.text: I'd like to see which schools have virtual lab resources like Labster or Pivot Interactive for IB sciences. Those are expensive and not all schools advertise them.

replies.text: Our school has Labster but the link is buried in the science department intranet. Not on the public IB page. The crawl wouldn't find it.

comments.text: Could you add a filter for schools that have multilingual websites? Most international schools claim to be 'multilingual' but only have English. The IB requires schools to provide support in the mother tongue.

replies.text: Especially for the IB bilingual diploma. I've seen schools in Thailand say they offer support in Japanese but the website is only in English and Thai. False advertising.

comments.text: The crawl could be useful for accreditation self-studies. We're preparing for our 5-year review and need to show evidence of our online presence. This dataset gives us a benchmark against other schools.

replies.text: Just be careful using this as official evidence. The crawl might miss pages that require authentication. Our self-study includes intranet documents that this wouldn't capture.

comments.text: I'm working on an open-source IB resource aggregator. This dataset could help identify which schools are willing to share their unit planners online. There's a directory of schools that publish their MYP units publicly.

replies.text: That would be amazing. Right now I have to email my colleagues at other schools begging for unit plans. A public directory would be a game-changer for curriculum collaboration.

comments.text: The trend data is interesting. I see that schools using Google Sites are on the decline, but still huge. Our school moved away from Google Sites because of its limitations for IB ePortfolios.

replies.text: Google Sites is terrible for managing the IB's complete course requirements. But many schools are stuck because it's 'free' with G Suite. The hidden cost is teacher time.

comments.text: Does the scrape check for SSL/TLS certificates? Many international school websites still use HTTP. For a school handling IB registrations, that's a privacy risk.

replies.text: Yes, the tech breakdown includes 'HTTPS' as a metric. It's surprising how many .edu and .k12 domains are still on plain HTTP. I saw 15% in the dataset.

comments.text: I'm interested in the 'Custom CMS' category. Some schools build their own portals for IB CAS management. Our school has one written in PHP from 2007. It still works but it's ugly.

comments.text: The 'Number of IB Pages' metric is interesting. Our school has over 50 pages dedicated to IB, but many are duplicate content for different languages. The scrape probably overcounts those.

comments.text: I'm a skeptic. This data might be useful for school marketers, but for actual teachers it's just noise. What matters is classroom practice, not website tech. IB doesn't require a fancy site.
